| Stage | Engineering Question Answered | Physical Principle |
|-------|-------------------------------|--------------------|
| Bubble Generation | How to maximize interfacial area? | Higher A ⇒ more adsorption sites |
| Interfacial Adsorption | Why does solute migrate to bubble? | Minimization of surface free energy (Γ effect) |
| Foam Formation | How to retain adsorbed solute? | Foam stability ↔ surface viscosity |
| Foam Drainage | How to enrich the foam? | Gravity-driven thin-film thinning |
| Foam Collapse | How to recover product? | Destabilization of lamellae |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Foam fractionation is used industrially for:
> - **Detergent manufacturing** (sodium lauryl sulfate concentration)
> - **Wastewater treatment** (removal of surfactants, oils, PFAS)
> - **Bioprocessing** (recovery of proteins like BSA, lysozyme, ovalbumin from fermentation broths)
> - **Ore flotation** (mineral processing — the macroscopic cousin of foam fractionation)
> - **Effluent detoxification** of dye-laden textile waste

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table summarizes **every equation** you must know to solve Module 1 foam fractionation problems in KTU 2024 ESE. **Memorize these symbols, units, and forms.**

| # | Equation Name | Formula | Variables & Units | Application |
|---|---------------|---------|-------------------|-------------|
| 1 | **Gibbs Adsorption Isotherm** | $\Gamma = -\dfrac{C}{RT}\dfrac{d\gamma}{dC}$ | Γ = surface excess (mol/m²), C = bulk concentration (mol/m³), γ = surface tension (N/m), R = 8.314 J/(mol·K), T = temperature (K) | Predicts equilibrium adsorption at gas–liquid interface |
| 2 | **Enrichment Ratio** | $E = \dfrac{C_f}{C_i}$ | $C_f$ = foamate concentration, $C_i$ = initial concentration (both in same units, e.g., kg/m³) | Quantifies degree of pre-concentration |
| 3 | **Recovery (Yield)** | $R = \dfrac{C_f V_f}{C_i V_i}$ | $V_f$ = foamate volume, $V_i$ = initial liquid volume (m³) | Mass balance; typically expressed as % |
| 4 | **Surface Excess from Mass Balance** | $\Gamma = \dfrac{(C_i - C_f)\, V}{A}$ | A = total bubble interfacial area (m²) | Experimental determination of Γ |
| 5 | **Total Interfacial Area (spherical bubbles)** | $A = n \cdot 4\pi r^2$ | n = number of bubbles, r = bubble radius (m) | Links bubble size to adsorption capacity |
| 6 | **Bubble Volume Relation** | $V_{gas} = n \cdot \dfrac{4}{3}\pi r^3$ | $V_{gas}$ = total gas volume sparged (m³) | Couples gas flow rate to bubble count |
| 7 | **Air–Liquid Ratio** | $\text{ALR} = \dfrac{Q_g}{Q_l}$ | $Q_g$ = gas flow rate (m³/s), $Q_l$ = liquid flow rate (m³/s) | Dimensionless operating parameter |
| 8 | **Foam Height (Stefan-Ross-type model)** | $H_f = H_0 \cdot \exp\left(-\dfrac{d \cdot \rho \, g}{2 \mu \, v}\right)$ | $H_0$ = initial foam height, d = bubble diameter, ρ = liquid density, g = gravity, μ = liquid viscosity, v = superficial gas velocity | Predicts foam stability under drainage |
| 9 | **Equilibrium Surface Tension (Szyszkowski)** | $\gamma = \gamma_0 \left[1 - B \ln\left(1 + \dfrac{C}{K}\right)\right]$ | $\gamma_0$ = pure solvent surface tension, B, K = empirical constants | Used to compute $\frac{d\gamma}{dC}$ for Gibbs equation |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall — Sign Convention in Gibbs Isotherm:** The negative sign in the Gibbs equation is **not optional**. It is there because most surface-active solutes **lower** surface tension ($\frac{d\gamma}{dC} < 0$), so the surface excess Γ is **positive**. A common student error is to drop the negative sign.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Surface Excess from a Mass Balance

We start with a **control volume** of liquid volume $V$ containing an initial solute mass $m_i$. Gas is sparged, and after steady-state operation, the foamate of volume $V_f$ contains solute mass $m_f$. The residual retentate of volume $V - V_f$ has concentration $C_r$.

**Step 1 — Write the mass balance for the solute in the bulk liquid:**

$$m_i = m_{adsorbed} + m_{residual}$$

where the adsorbed mass is the surface excess Γ times the total interfacial area A, and the residual mass is $(V - V_f) C_r$.

$$C_i V = \Gamma A + C_r (V - V_f)$$

**Step 2 — Express the foamate mass balance:**

$$m_f = C_f V_f$$

**Step 3 — The total mass balance across the column:**

$$C_i V = C_f V_f + C_r (V - V_f) + \Gamma A$$

**Step 4 — Solving for Γ (the surface excess):**

$$\Gamma A = C_i V - C_f V_f - C_r (V - V_f)$$

$$\Gamma = \dfrac{C_i V - C_f V_f - C_r (V - V_f)}{A}$$

**Step 5 — Simplification under the ideal assumption of no residual bulk entrainment ($C_r \to C_i$ and $V_f \ll V$):**

$$\Gamma \approx \dfrac{(C_i - C_f)\, V}{A}$$

This is the **standard working equation** in foam fractionation design. It tells us that **surface excess scales linearly with bulk concentration difference and inversely with bubble area.**

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Coupling Bubble Size to Interfacial Area

Given a known gas volumetric flow rate $Q_g$ and a measured mean bubble radius $r$, the total interfacial area $A$ per unit time of sparging is derived as follows.

**Step 1 — Number of bubbles generated per unit time:**

$$n = \dfrac{Q_g}{\frac{4}{3}\pi r^3}$$

**Step 2 — Total surface area per unit time:**

$$A = n \cdot 4\pi r^2$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $n$:**

$$A = \dfrac{Q_g}{\frac{4}{3}\pi r^3} \cdot 4\pi r^2$$

$$A = \dfrac{3 Q_g}{r}$$

**Step 4 — Practical design implication:**

Since $A \propto \dfrac{1}{r}$, **halving the bubble radius doubles the interfacial area** at constant gas flow. This is why foam fractionation columns use **fine-pore spargers** to maximize $A$.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Enrichment Ratio under Steady-State Operation

Consider a continuous foam fractionation column operating at steady state with feed flow rate $F$, foamate flow rate $F_f$, and retentate flow rate $F_r$. The surface excess is $\Gamma$, and the total bubble area generated per unit time is $\dot{A}$.

**Step 1 — Solute mass balance over the entire column:**

$$F \, C_i = F_f \, C_f + F_r \, C_r + \Gamma \dot{A}$$

**Step 2 — Overall volumetric balance:**

$$F = F_f + F_r$$

**Step 3 — Assuming negligible entrainment in the foam ($C_r \approx C_i$):**

$$F C_i = F_f C_f + (F - F_f) C_i + \Gamma \dot{A}$$

**Step 4 — Simplify the right side:**

$$F C_i = F_f C_f + F C_i - F_f C_i + \Gamma \dot{A}$$

**Step 5 — Cancel $F C_i$ on both sides:**

$$0 = F_f (C_f - C_i) + \Gamma \dot{A}$$

**Step 6 — Solve for the foamate concentration:**

$$C_f = C_i - \dfrac{\Gamma \dot{A}}{F_f}$$

**Step 7 — Express the enrichment ratio $E$:**

$$E = \dfrac{C_f}{C_i} = 1 - \dfrac{\Gamma \dot{A}}{F_f \, C_i}$$

This elegant result shows that **E increases as Γ, $\dot{A}$ increase, and decreases as $F_f$ increases** — a fundamental design trade-off: slower foam collection yields more enrichment but less throughput.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Influence of Operating Parameters

| Parameter | Effect on Enrichment (E) | Effect on Recovery (R) | Effect on Throughput |
|-----------|--------------------------|------------------------|----------------------|
| ↑ Gas flow rate ($Q_g$) | ↓ (larger bubbles, more entrainment) | ↑ (more adsorption events) | ↑ |
| ↓ Bubble size (r) | ↑ (more interfacial area) | ↑ (more area per unit gas) | ↔ (mostly area effect) |
| ↑ Column height | ↑ (more drainage time) | ↓ (more drainage loss) | ↓ |
| ↑ Foam collection rate ($F_f$) | ↓ (less drainage) | ↑ | ↑ |
| ↑ Initial concentration ($C_i$) | ↓ (saturation of Γ) | ↑ | ↔ |
| ↑ Temperature | ↓ (Γ often decreases) | ↔ | ↔ |
| ↑ Inlet pH (for proteins) | Variable (depends on pI) | Variable | ↔ |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Foam Fractionation Column (Mermaid Block Diagram)

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid block diagram represents the **functional flow** of a foam fractionation column, the key control volume for Module 1 of PEBBT525. It captures every structural and process element from gas inlet to foamate collection.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    GasInlet[GAS INLET N2 or Air] --> FlowCtrl[Flow Controller and Pressure Regulator]
    FlowCtrl --> Sparger[Microporous Sparger Frit Pore Size 10 to 50 micrometer]
    Sparger --> BubbleZone[Bubble Generation Zone Bubble Diameter 0.5 to 3 mm]
    BubbleZone --> AdsorptionZone[Adsorption Zone Bulk Liquid Pool Height H_L]
    AdsorptionZone -->|Surface Active Solute Migrates to Interface| InterfaceZone[Gas Liquid Interface Surface Excess Gamma]
    InterfaceZone --> FoamZone[Foam Zone Height H_f Drainage Active]
    FoamZone -->|Drainage Thins Lamellae| EnrichedFoam[Enriched Stable Foam]
    FoamZone -->|Some Bulk Entrainment| EntrainmentLoss[Entrainment Loss to Retentate]
    EnrichedFoam --> FoamOutlet[Foam Outlet Top of Column]
    FoamOutlet --> FoamCollector[Foam Collector Vessel]
    FoamCollector --> FoamBreaker[Mechanical Foam Breaker Rotating Blade or Shear]
    FoamBreaker --> Foamate[Foamate Concentrated Product C_f]
    AdsorptionZone --> RetentateOut[Retentate Outlet Bottom Side]
    RetentateOut --> Retentate[Retentate Depleted Bulk C_r]
    FeedIn[Feed Inlet C_i V_i] --> AdsorptionZone
    Foamate --> MassBalance{Global Mass Balance Check F Ci equals Ff Cf plus Fr Cr plus Gamma A dot}
    Retentate --> MassBalance
    MassBalance --> Verified[Material Balance Verified]
```

## 4.2 Component Identification & Functional Roles

| Block ID | Functional Role | Engineering Parameter |
|----------|-----------------|----------------------|
| GasInlet | Source of dispersed gas phase | Gas type: air / N₂ / CO₂ |
| FlowCtrl | Maintains steady Qg | Rotameter / mass flow controller |
| Sparger | Generates uniform fine bubbles | Porosity, pore diameter |
| BubbleZone | Initial bubble formation | Bubble size distribution |
| AdsorptionZone | Solute attachment to bubbles | Contact time, Γ |
| InterfaceZone | Site of Gibbs adsorption | Surface tension gradient |
| FoamZone | Foam builds and drains | Foam height, half-life |
| EnrichedFoam | Final concentrated foam | Liquid fraction in foam |
| FoamOutlet | Directs foam to collector | Outlet geometry |
| FoamBreaker | Collapses foam to liquid | Mechanical / chemical |
| Foamate | Final desired product | $C_f$, $V_f$ |
| Retentate | Depleted bulk | $C_r$ |
| MassBalance | Numerical consistency check | F, C, Γ, $\dot{A}$ |

## 4.3 Process Variables in the Diagram (KTU-Standard Symbols)

- **Q_g** = gas volumetric flow rate, m³/s
- **H_L** = bulk liquid pool height, m
- **H_f** = foam column height, m
- **Γ** = surface excess concentration, mol/m²
- **C_i, C_f, C_r** = concentrations in feed, foamate, retentate, kg/m³
- **A_dot** = rate of interfacial area generation, m²/s
- **F, F_f, F_r** = volumetric flow rates of feed, foamate, retentate, m³/s

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Note for Drawing Questions in KTU:** When asked to "draw and explain" foam fractionation, your diagram **must** label at least: (1) gas inlet, (2) sparger, (3) bulk liquid pool, (4) foam zone, (5) foam outlet, (6) foamate collection. Mark the **direction of bubble rise** clearly with arrows.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU Part A questions test **Recall and Understanding (Bloom Level 1 & 2)**. Answer in 4–6 lines with a **precise definition, the governing principle, and a relevant example.** No derivations are required.

---

### Question A.1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Define foam fractionation. State the principle on which it is based and name two industrial applications.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Foam fractionation is an adsorptive bubble separation technique in which a gas is dispersed through a liquid containing surface-active solutes; the solutes adsorb at the rising gas–liquid interfaces and are collected as a concentrated foamate after foam collapse.

**Principle (1 mark):** The technique is based on the **preferential adsorption of surface-active species at gas–liquid interfaces**, as quantified by the **Gibbs adsorption isotherm**. Molecules that lower the surface tension of the solvent accumulate at the interface in proportion to the bulk concentration.

**Applications (1 mark):** Two industrial applications are:
1. Concentration of **surfactants** (e.g., sodium dodecyl sulfate) in detergent manufacture.
2. Pre-concentration of **proteins** (e.g., bovine serum albumin, lysozyme) from dilute fermentation broths in bioprocessing.

---

### Question A.2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define "surface excess concentration" and write the Gibbs adsorption isotherm. What does the negative sign signify?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Surface excess concentration (Γ) is the amount of solute (in moles) present per unit area of the gas–liquid interface in excess of the bulk concentration, expressed in **mol·m⁻²**.

**Gibbs Equation (1 mark):** The Gibbs adsorption isotherm is:
$$\Gamma = -\dfrac{C}{RT} \dfrac{d\gamma}{dC}$$

**Significance of Negative Sign (1 mark):** The negative sign indicates that for most surface-active solutes, surface tension **decreases** with increasing concentration ($\frac{d\gamma}{dC} < 0$). This makes Γ a **positive quantity**, signifying **net accumulation** of solute at the interface — the thermodynamic foundation of foam fractionation.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU Part B questions carry 14 marks, split into two sub-parts of 7 marks each. Internal choice means the student answers **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 - Module 1 Carry 14 marks]`

**Q: (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **principle, construction, and working of a batch foam fractionation column** used for the recovery of a surface-active protein. Discuss the role of bubble size, gas flow rate, and foam drainage in determining the enrichment ratio. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive an expression for the **enrichment ratio (E)** in terms of surface excess concentration (Γ), interfacial area generation rate ($\dot{A}$), foamate flow rate ($F_f$), and initial bulk concentration ($C_i$). Briefly discuss how an increase in column height affects E and the recovery R. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Principle (2 marks):**
Foam fractionation exploits the **preferential adsorption** of surface-active solutes at gas–liquid interfaces. When gas bubbles are sparged through the liquid feed, protein molecules (or any surface-active species) migrate from the bulk to the bubble surface. The bubbles rise into a foam zone, where further drainage concentrates the adsorbed material. The collected foam is collapsed to yield a foamate enriched relative to the feed.

**Step 2 — Construction (2 marks):**
A typical batch foam fractionation column consists of:
1. **Gas inlet line** with a rotameter or mass flow controller.
2. **Microporous sparger** (sintered glass frit, pore size 10–50 μm) at the bottom.
3. **Bulk liquid pool** (height $H_L$ ≈ 0.3–0.5 m).
4. **Foam zone** (height $H_f$ above the liquid pool).
5. **Foam outlet and collector** at the top.
6. **Retentate outlet** for batch sampling or continuous drainage at the bottom.
7. **Foam breaker** (mechanical, vacuum, or antifoam-based) downstream of the foam collector.

**Step 3 — Working & Effect of Parameters (3 marks):**
- **Bubble size:** Smaller bubbles ⇒ larger total interfacial area $A$ (since $A = 3Q_g / r$) ⇒ higher Γ loading ⇒ higher E.
- **Gas flow rate $Q_g$:** Higher $Q_g$ increases throughput but produces larger, less stable bubbles and may cause excessive entrainment, reducing E.
- **Foam drainage:** In the foam zone, gravity-driven drainage of the thin liquid films between bubbles reduces entrained bulk liquid, increasing E. A taller foam zone enhances drainage — enriching the foam at the cost of partial solute drainage back into the bulk (lower R).

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Derivation of E (5 marks):**

The derivation is reproduced in **Section 3.3** of these notes. The key steps are:

**Step 1 — Overall mass balance at steady state:**

$$F \, C_i = F_f \, C_f + F_r \, C_r + \Gamma \dot{A}$$

**Step 2 — Volumetric balance:** $F = F_f + F_r$

**Step 3 — Substituting and assuming $C_r \approx C_i$ (low entrainment model):**

$$0 = F_f (C_f - C_i) + \Gamma \dot{A}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $C_f$:**

$$C_f = C_i - \dfrac{\Gamma \dot{A}}{F_f}$$

**Step 5 — Enrichment ratio:**

$$\boxed{E = \dfrac{C_f}{C_i} = 1 - \dfrac{\Gamma \dot{A}}{F_f \, C_i}}$$

**Discussion on Column Height (2 marks):**
- An **increase in column height** provides more residence time for **foam drainage**, allowing more interstitial liquid to drain back into the bulk. This **increases E** (the foam becomes more enriched in surface-active solute).
- However, the same drainage returns some adsorbed solute back into the bulk, **decreasing R** (recovery).
- This represents the **fundamental design trade-off** in foam fractionation: **taller columns → higher purity, lower yield; shorter columns → higher yield, lower purity.**

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2023 - Module 1 Carry 14 marks]`

**Q: (a)** What is the **Gibbs adsorption isotherm**? Derive it from thermodynamic principles. Show how it is applied to predict the **surface excess concentration of BSA (bovine serum albumin)** in a foam fractionation experiment. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A laboratory foam fractionation column processes **500 mL** of an aqueous BSA solution at an initial concentration of **0.5 kg/m³**. Air is sparged at a flow rate that generates a total interfacial area of **0.4 m²** in the column. The resulting foamate volume is **25 mL** at a BSA concentration of **8.0 kg/m³**. Calculate: **(i) the enrichment ratio, (ii) the recovery, and (iii) the surface excess concentration Γ.** **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Statement of the Isotherm (1 mark):**

$$\Gamma = -\dfrac{C}{RT} \dfrac{d\gamma}{dC}$$

**Step 2 — Thermodynamic Derivation (4 marks):**

Consider a gas–liquid interface of area $A$ at constant temperature $T$ and pressure $P$. The Gibbs free energy of the interface is $G = \gamma A$ (where $\gamma$ is the surface tension). For a system with $n_i$ moles of component $i$:

$$dG = -S\, dT + V\, dP + \gamma\, dA + \sum_i \mu_i\, dn_i$$

At constant $T$ and $P$, the surface chemical potential is defined as:

$$\mu_i^{surf} = \left(\dfrac{\partial G}{\partial n_i}\right)_{T,P,A,n_{j \neq i}}$$

The total surface excess of component $i$ is:

$$\Gamma_i = \dfrac{n_i^{surf}}{A}$$

For a two-component system (solvent + solute) at equilibrium, the surface chemical potential of the solute equals that in the bulk: $\mu_i^{surf} = \mu_i^{bulk}$. Applying the **Gibbs–Duhem relation** to this two-component system and invoking the equilibrium condition, we obtain:

$$\Gamma_2 = -\dfrac{1}{RT} \dfrac{d\gamma}{d\ln a_2}$$

For dilute solutions, $a_2 \approx C$, so $\dfrac{d\gamma}{d\ln a_2} = C \dfrac{d\gamma}{dC}$. Therefore:

$$\boxed{\Gamma = -\dfrac{C}{RT} \dfrac{d\gamma}{dC}}$$

**Step 3 — Application to BSA (2 marks):**

For BSA (a globular protein), surface tension of the aqueous solution **decreases** with increasing BSA concentration: $\frac{d\gamma}{dC} < 0$. Substituting experimentally measured values of $\gamma(C)$ at a given temperature (e.g., $T = 298$ K, $R = 8.314$ J/mol·K), the equation gives a **positive Γ**, indicating that BSA accumulates at the air–water interface — the physical basis for its foam-fractionation recovery.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Given Data:**
- $V_i = 500$ mL $= 5 \times 10^{-4}$ m³
- $C_i = 0.5$ kg/m³
- $A = 0.4$ m²
- $V_f = 25$ mL $= 2.5 \times 10^{-5}$ m³
- $C_f = 8.0$ kg/m³

**(i) Enrichment Ratio E (2 marks):**

$$E = \dfrac{C_f}{C_i} = \dfrac{8.0 \text{ kg/m}^3}{0.5 \text{ kg/m}^3} = \boxed{16}$$

The foamate is **16 times more concentrated** than the feed.

**(ii) Recovery R (2 marks):**

$$R = \dfrac{C_f V_f}{C_i V_i} = \dfrac{(8.0)(2.5 \times 10^{-5})}{(0.5)(5 \times 10^{-4})}$$

$$R = \dfrac{2.0 \times 10^{-4}}{2.5 \times 10^{-4}} = 0.80 = \boxed{80\%}$$

**80% of the BSA is recovered** in the foamate.

**(iii) Surface Excess Γ (3 marks):**

Using the working mass balance from Section 3.1, Step 5:

$$\Gamma = \dfrac{(C_i - C_f) V}{A}$$

But here $V$ in the formula refers to the volume of liquid that supplied the surface excess. For a batch column, the appropriate formulation uses the initial feed volume minus the foamate volume (residual bulk):

$$\Gamma = \dfrac{C_i V_i - C_f V_f}{A}$$

$$\Gamma = \dfrac{(0.5)(5 \times 10^{-4}) - (8.0)(2.5 \times 10^{-5})}{0.4}$$

$$\Gamma = \dfrac{(2.5 \times 10^{-4}) - (2.0 \times 10^{-4})}{0.4}$$

$$\Gamma = \dfrac{5.0 \times 10^{-5}}{0.4}$$

$$\Gamma = 1.25 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg/m}^2 = \boxed{1.25 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg} \cdot \text{m}^{-2}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Mixing up $V_i$ and $V_f$** — students often plug $V_f$ into the surface excess formula. Use the **full feed volume $V_i$** minus the **foamate mass equivalent**, divided by interfacial area.
> 2. **Unit inconsistency** — keep $V$ in m³ and $A$ in m² to obtain Γ in **kg/m²**. Do NOT mix mL with m².
> 3. **Rounding too early** — keep at least 3 significant figures until the final step. Recovery of exactly 80% is a common trap; off-by-decimal errors are the #1 reason students lose 1 mark.
> 4. **Forgetting the negative sign in Gibbs isotherm** — a guaranteed 0.5 mark deduction in any Gibbs-related question.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Foam Fractionation (Module 1)**

- [ ] **Foam fractionation** is an **adsorptive bubble separation method** that exploits preferential solute accumulation at gas–liquid interfaces.
- [ ] The thermodynamic foundation is the **Gibbs adsorption isotherm**: $\Gamma = -\dfrac{C}{RT}\dfrac{d\gamma}{dC}$.
- [ ] The **negative sign** in the Gibbs equation is **mandatory** — it ensures Γ > 0 for surface-active solutes.
- [ ] A solute is **surface-active** if it has both hydrophilic and hydrophobic domains (amphipathic).
- [ ] **Bubble size** is the single most important design parameter — $A = 3Q_g/r$; smaller bubbles ⇒ higher interfacial area.
- [ ] **Enrichment ratio** $E = C_f / C_i$ measures concentration amplification; $E > 1$ confirms successful pre-concentration.
- [ ] **Recovery** $R = C_f V_f / (C_i V_i)$ measures mass yield; typically reported as a percentage.
- [ ] The fundamental **design trade-off**: **Tall columns** → high E, low R; **Short columns** → high R, low E.
- [ ] **Foam drainage** in the foam zone improves enrichment by removing entrained bulk liquid, but also causes some adsorbed solute loss.
- [ ] **Key equipment components**: gas inlet, flow controller, microporous sparger, bulk liquid pool, foam zone, foam outlet, foam breaker, foamate and retentate collection.
- [ ] **Industrial applications**: surfactant concentration (detergents), protein recovery (BSA, lysozyme), wastewater treatment (dyes, oils, PFAS), and ore flotation (mineral processing).
- [ ] **Operating parameters to optimize**: gas flow rate, bubble size, column height, foam collection rate, pH (for proteins near pI), temperature.
- [ ] **Air–Liquid Ratio (ALR)** is a dimensionless design parameter; typical operating range: **5 to 50**.
- [ ] **Assumed standard conditions** in KTU problems: $T = 298$ K, $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K), $g = 9.81$ m/s², dilute-solution regime.
- [ ] **Foam stability** is governed by surface viscosity, surface elasticity, and Marangoni effects — not just surface tension alone.
- [ ] **Continuous** vs **Batch** operation: continuous uses steady feed/foamate/retentate streams (E = 1 - Γ·Ȧ / (F_f·C_i)); batch uses a fixed liquid pool that is depleted over time.
- [ ] **Antifoam agents** (silicone-based) can be used to break foam downstream but may contaminate the product.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Recap Statement:**
> *Foam fractionation is the bioseparation unit operation where surface activity, interfacial thermodynamics, and bubble hydrodynamics converge. Master the Gibbs isotherm, the enrichment–recovery trade-off, and the derivation of $E = 1 - \Gamma \dot{A} / (F_f C_i)$ — these are the three pillars of any Module 1 examination question.*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sedimentation of Fine Particles and Industrial Sedimentation Equipment: Classifiers, Clarifiers, and Thickeners
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEBBT525 - BIOSEPARATION TECHNOLOGY > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Sedimentation of Fine Particles and Industrial Sedimentation Equipment: Classifiers, Clarifiers, and Thickeners ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Sedimentation – Formal Definition **Sedimentation** is a solid–liquid separation process based on the action of gravitational (or centrifugal) forces on particles suspended in a fluid. In bioseparation technology, sedimentation is exploited as a primary recovery step to separate cells, cell debris, protein precipitates, or other insoluble bioproducts from the bulk liquid medium (broth, buffer, or process stream). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition:** Sedimentation is the gravity-driven settling of suspended solid particles through a liquid continuum, governed by the balance between the gravitational body force, the buoyant force, and the hydrodynamic drag force exerted by the fluid on the particle. For **fine particles** (typically 1 µm – 100 µm in biological systems), the Reynolds number of settling is very small ($Re_p \lt 1$), and the flow regime around the particle is firmly in the **Stokes' (viscous) regime**. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Analogy – "Rain in a Dusty Room"** > Imagine a room filled with fine dust suspended in still air. Gravity continuously pulls every dust speck downward, but the air resists motion through friction (viscous drag). Big dust particles fall fast and reach the floor quickly — they are the "easy" coarse fraction. The tiniest particles, however, descend at an almost imperceptible pace, drifting like a slow, lazy snowfall. Sedimentation equipment in industry is simply an *engineered room* where we (a) make the air/medium as quiet as possible, (b) give the specks enough time and height to fall, and (c) collect the dust pile at the bottom (the *underflow*) and the clean air/supernatant at the top (the *overflow*). - **Coarse particles** (large diameter) → high terminal velocity → settle rapidly → handled by simple gravity settlers. - **Fine particles** (small diameter) → low terminal velocity → settle slowly → require large residence times, shallow tanks, or assisted fields (centrifuges, coagulants, flocculants). ### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics The following constants and dimensionless groups govern fine-particle sedimentation and must be memorized for KTU exams: | Quantity | Symbol | Standard Value / Definition | Units | |----------|--------|------------------------------|-------| | Acceleration due to gravity | $g$ | **9.81 m/s²** | m·s⁻² | | Density of water (reference) | $\rho_w$ | **1000 kg/m³** | kg·m⁻³ | | Dynamic viscosity of water (20 °C) | $\mu_w$ | **1.002 × 10⁻³ Pa·s** | Pa·s | | Kinematic viscosity of water (20 °C) | $\nu_w$ | **1.004 × 10⁻⁶ m²/s** | m²·s⁻¹ | | Particle Reynolds number | $Re_p$ | $\frac{d_p u_t \rho}{\mu}$ | dimensionless | | Galileo number | $Ga$ | $\frac{d_p^{\,3} \rho (\rho_p - \rho) g}{\mu^{2}}$ | dimensionless | | Stokes' regime upper limit | $Re_p$ | $\le 0.1$ (or $\le 1$ approx.) | – | --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Force Balance on a Single Spherical Particle in a Fluid A single spherical particle falling under gravity in a viscous fluid experiences three principal forces along the vertical axis: 1. **Gravitational (weight) force, $F_g$** Acts downward. 2. **Buoyant force, $F_b$** Acts upward (= weight of displaced fluid). 3. **Hydrodynamic drag force, $F_D$** Acts upward (opposes relative motion). The net downward driving force is: $$F_g - F_b \;=\; \frac{\pi}{6}\, d_p^{\,3}\, (\rho_p - \rho)\, g$$ The drag force depends on the flow regime: $$F_D \;=\; C_D \cdot \tfrac{1}{2}\, \rho\, u^{2} \cdot \tfrac{\pi}{4}\, d_p^{\,2}$$ where $C_D$ is the drag coefficient (function of $Re_p$). ### 2.2 The Three Settling Regimes | Regime | Reynolds Number $Re_p$ | Drag-Coefficient Correlation | Why It Matters in Bio-Separation | |--------|------------------------|-------------------------------|----------------------------------| | **Stokes' (viscous)** | $Re_p \le 0.1$ | $C_D = \dfrac{24}{Re_p}$ | Dominates for sub-micron cells, cell debris, protein precipitates. | | **Intermediate (Allen's)** | $0.1 \lt Re_p \le 10^{3}$ | $C_D = \dfrac{18.5}{Re_p^{0.6}}$ | Transition regime, rarely used in exam problems. | | **Newton's (turbulent)** | $10^{3} \lt Re_p \le 2\times 10^{5}$ | $C_D \approx 0.44$ | Coarse mineral particles; rarely seen in bioprocess streams. | ### 2.3 Terminal (Free-Settling) Velocity — Step-by-Step Logic At terminal velocity $u_t$, the net downward force is exactly balanced by the drag force (acceleration = 0). **Step 1 – Equate driving force and drag force:** $$F_g - F_b \;=\; F_D$$ **Step 2 – Substitute the Stokes' drag expression** (valid because $Re_p \le 0.1$ for fine particles): $$\frac{\pi}{6}\, d_p^{\,3}\, (\rho_p - \rho)\, g \;=\; 3\pi\, \mu\, d_p\, u_t$$ **Step 3 – Solve for the terminal velocity $u_t$:** > [!IMPORTANT] > **Stokes' Law (1851) – KTU High-Yield Equation:** > $$u_t \;=\; \frac{d_p^{\,2}\,(\rho_p - \rho)\, g}{18\,\mu}$$ This is the **single most important equation** in sedimentation theory. Notice that $u_t \propto d_p^{\,2}$ — doubling the particle size quadruples the settling velocity. ### 2.4 Hindered Settling (Concentrated Suspensions) When the solids volume fraction $\phi$ exceeds ~1 %, particle–particle hydrodynamic interactions slow the settling. The Richardson–Zaki correlation is the standard KTU tool: > **Richardson–Zaki Equation:** > $$u_t^{\,*} \;=\; u_t\,(1 - \phi)^{n}$$ where $u_t^{\,*}$ is the hindered-settling velocity, and the exponent $n$ depends on the particle Reynolds number: - $Re_p \lt 0.1$ → $n = 4.65$ - $0.1 \lt Re_p \lt 1$ → $n = 4.4\, Re_p^{-0.03}$ - $1 \lt Re_p \lt 500$ → $n = 4.4\, Re_p^{-0.1}$ - $Re_p \gt 500$ → $n = 2.4$ ### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Name / Use | Units | |---|---------|------------|-------| | 1 | $u_t = \dfrac{d_p^{\,2}(\rho_p - \rho)g}{18\mu}$ | Stokes' terminal velocity (free settling) | m·s⁻¹ | | 2 | $Re_p = \dfrac{d_p u_t \rho}{\mu}$ | Particle Reynolds number | – | | 3 | $F_D = 3\pi\mu d_p u$ | Stokes' drag (creeping flow) | N | | 4 | $F_D = C_D \cdot \tfrac{\rho u^{2}}{2} \cdot \tfrac{\pi d_p^{\,2}}{4}$ | General drag (any regime) | N | | 5 | $u_t^{\,*} = u_t (1-\phi)^{n}$ | Richardson–Zaki hindered settling | m·s⁻¹ | | 6 | $Q = A \cdot u_t$ | Volumetric throughput of an ideal settler | m³·s⁻¹ | | 7 | $A = \dfrac{Q}{u_t}$ | Required cross-sectional area of clarifier/thickener | m² | | 8 | $v_s = \dfrac{Q_o - Q_u}{A}$ | Settling (overflow) velocity in thickener | m·s⁻¹ | | 9 | $\Sigma Q_i C_i = 0$ | Mass-balance constraint on solute/solid | – | | 10 | $L = u_t \cdot t_{res}$ | Settling distance = velocity × residence time | m | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility of Stokes' Law** > - **Scale-up of clarifiers:** knowing $u_t$ immediately gives the cross-sectional area required to process a given broth flow rate. > - **Precipitation design:** protein precipitate flocs are engineered to have a target $d_p$ so that $u_t$ is large enough for practical settling times. > - **Cell harvesting:** mammalian cells (~15 µm) settle in minutes; bacterial cells (~1 µm) may need hours — guiding whether to use gravity or centrifugation. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Detailed Derivation: Stokes' Law from First Principles We begin with Newton's second law applied to a spherical particle of diameter $d_p$, density $\rho_p$, falling in a fluid of density $\rho$ and viscosity $\mu$. Take *downward* as positive. **Step 1 – Write the momentum balance in the vertical direction:** $$m_p\,\frac{du}{dt} \;=\; F_g - F_b - F_D$$ **Step 2 – Express the mass of the particle:** $$m_p \;=\; \frac{\pi}{6}\, d_p^{\,3}\, \rho_p$$ **Step 3 – Express the buoyant force (Archimedes):** $$F_b \;=\; \frac{\pi}{6}\, d_p^{\,3}\, \rho\, g$$ **Step 4 – Express the Stokes drag (creeping flow, $Re_p \le 0.1$):** $$F_D \;=\; 3\pi\,\mu\, d_p\, u$$ **Step 5 – Substitute and simplify:** $$\frac{\pi}{6}\, d_p^{\,3}\, \rho_p \frac{du}{dt} \;=\; \frac{\pi}{6}\, d_p^{\,3}\, (\rho_p - \rho)\, g \;-\; 3\pi\,\mu\, d_p\, u$$ **Step 6 – At terminal velocity, $du/dt = 0$ (force balance):** $$0 \;=\; \frac{\pi}{6}\, d_p^{\,3}\, (\rho_p - \rho)\, g \;-\; 3\pi\,\mu\, d_p\, u_t$$ **Step 7 – Solve for $u_t$:** $$3\pi\,\mu\, d_p\, u_t \;=\; \frac{\pi}{6}\, d_p^{\,3}\, (\rho_p - \rho)\, g$$ $$u_t \;=\; \frac{\pi\, d_p^{\,3}\, (\rho_p - \rho)\, g}{6 \cdot 3\pi\,\mu\, d_p}$$ $$u_t \;=\; \frac{d_p^{\,2}\,(\rho_p - \rho)\, g}{18\,\mu}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Stokes' Law (final form):** > $$u_t \;=\; \frac{d_p^{\,2}\,(\rho_p - \rho)\, g}{18\,\mu}$$ ### 3.2 Detailed Derivation: Settling-Velocity Design of a Clarifier **Given:** Feed flow rate $Q$ (m³/s) containing fine particles of known terminal velocity $u_t$. **Find:** Minimum cross-sectional area $A_{\min}$ of the clarifier. **Step 1 — Criterion for complete settling:** A particle entering at the top surface of the clarifier must reach the bottom (or the sludge zone) before the liquid carrying it travels the entire clarifier length and exits as overflow. **Step 2 — Define residence time:** $$t_{res} \;=\; \frac{V}{Q} \;=\; \frac{A \cdot H}{Q}$$ where $H$ is the depth of the clarifier and $A$ is its cross-sectional area. **Step 3 — Define the time required for a particle to settle through depth $H$:** $$t_{settle} \;=\; \frac{H}{u_t}$$ **Step 4 — Apply the design constraint $t_{settle} \le t_{res}$:** $$\frac{H}{u_t} \;\le\; \frac{A \cdot H}{Q}$$ **Step 5 — Solve for $A$:** $$A \;\ge\; \frac{Q}{u_t}$$ Therefore the **minimum clarifier area** is: > **Clarifier Sizing Equation (KTU):** > $$A_{\min} \;=\; \frac{Q}{u_t}$$ ### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example > **Problem.** A fermentation broth (viscosity $\mu = 1.5 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s, density $\rho = 1010$ kg/m³) contains yeast cells of diameter $d_p = 8 \, \mu$m and density $\rho_p = 1080$ kg/m³. The broth flow rate is $Q = 5 \, \text{m}^3/\text{h}$. Design a continuous gravity clarifier to remove ≥ 95 % of the cells. > > **(a)** Compute the terminal settling velocity. > **(b)** Verify that Stokes' regime is valid. > **(c)** Determine the minimum clarifier cross-sectional area. **(a) Terminal velocity:** $$u_t \;=\; \frac{d_p^{\,2}\,(\rho_p - \rho)\, g}{18\,\mu}$$ Numerically, $d_p^{\,2} = (8\times10^{-6})^{2} = 6.4 \times 10^{-11}$ m². $$u_t \;=\; \frac{(6.4\times10^{-11})\,(1080 - 1010)\,(9.81)}{18 \times (1.5\times10^{-3})}$$ $$u_t \;=\; \frac{6.4\times10^{-11} \times 70 \times 9.81}{2.7\times10^{-2}}$$ Let me compute the numerator step by step: - $6.4 \times 10^{-11} \times 70 = 4.48 \times 10^{-9}$ - $4.48 \times 10^{-9} \times 9.81 = 4.395 \times 10^{-8}$ So: $$u_t \;=\; \frac{4.395\times10^{-8}}{2.7\times10^{-2}} \;=\; 1.628 \times 10^{-6} \;\text{m/s}$$ $$u_t \;\approx\; 5.86 \;\text{mm/h}$$ **(b) Verify Stokes' regime:** $$Re_p \;=\; \frac{d_p\, u_t\, \rho}{\mu} \;=\; \frac{(8\times10^{-6})(1.628\times10^{-6})(1010)}{1.5\times10^{-3}}$$ $$Re_p \;=\; \frac{1.315\times10^{-8}}{1.5\times10^{-3}} \;=\; 8.77 \times 10^{-6}$$ $$Re_p \;\ll\; 0.1 \;\;\checkmark\;\;\text{Stokes' regime is valid.}$$ **(c) Minimum clarifier area:** Convert the flow rate to SI units: $$Q \;=\; 5 \,\text{m}^3/\text{h} \;=\; 1.389 \times 10^{-3} \,\text{m}^3/\text{s}$$ $$A_{\min} \;=\; \frac{Q}{u_t} \;=\; \frac{1.389\times10^{-3}}{1.628\times10^{-6}} \;=\; 853 \,\text{m}^2$$ A square clarifier would be $\sqrt{853} \approx 29.2$ m on a side — impractically large! This is exactly why **centrifuges** are used for small microbial cells in industry. The calculation correctly shows that **gravity sedimentation is economically infeasible for sub-10 µm particles at industrial flow rates**. ### 3.4 Derivation: Mass Balance in a Continuous Thickener A thickener is a continuous sedimentation vessel with feed (F), overflow (O), and underflow (U) streams. Solid mass balance gives: $$Q_F\, C_F \;=\; Q_O\, C_O \;+\; Q_U\, C_U$$ Total volumetric balance: $$Q_F \;=\; Q_O \;+\; Q_U$$ For an *ideal* thickener (perfectly clarified overflow), $C_O \to 0$, so: $$Q_U \;=\; \frac{Q_F\, C_F}{C_U}$$ The **settling (overflow) velocity** in the clear zone of a thickener (Coe–Clevenger approach) is: $$v_s \;=\; \frac{Q_O - Q_U}{A}$$ Solving for the area: $$A \;=\; \frac{Q_O - Q_U}{v_s}$$ ### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Industrial Sedimentation Equipment | Equipment | Primary Function | Typical Particle Size | Solids in Feed | Solids in Underflow | KTU Example | |-----------|------------------|----------------------|----------------|----------------------|-------------| | **Classifier** | Separates particles *by size* using a settling fluid (often water) | 50 µm – 5 mm | Variable | Concentrated coarse fraction | Spiral classifier, rake classifier | | **Clarifier** | Produces a *clear overflow*; solids removal is secondary | 10 µm – 1 mm | Low (< 0.5 %) | Low–moderate sludge | Lamella plate clarifier, circular clarifier | | **Thickener** | Produces a *concentrated underflow*; clarity of overflow is secondary | 10 µm – 1 mm | Low–moderate (0.5 – 20 %) | High (50 – 70 % solids) | Dorr thickener, gravity thickener | > [!NOTE] > **One-line memory hook (KTU 2024):** > **Classifiers = sort by size, Clarifiers = clean water, Thickeners = concentrate slurry.** --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Force Balance on a Settling Particle (Block Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart TB classDef forceNode fill:#E8F1FF,stroke:#1F4E8C,stroke-width:2px,color:#0B2545 classDef balNode fill:#FFF4E5,stroke:#B45F06,stroke-width:2px,color:#5C2E00 classDef resNode fill:#E8FFE8,stroke:#2E7D32,stroke-width:2px,color:#1B3D1B nodeP["SPHERICAL PARTICLE
diameter dp, density rhop"]:::forceNode nodeFg["GRAVITATIONAL FORCE
Fg = pi/6 * dp^3 * rhop * g
(acts downward)"]:::forceNode nodeFb["BUOYANT FORCE
Fb = pi/6 * dp^3 * rho * g
(acts upward)"]:::forceNode nodeFd["DRAG FORCE
FD = 3*pi*mu*dp*u
(acts upward, Stokes regime)"]:::forceNode nodeNet["NET DRIVING FORCE
Fnet = Fg - Fb - FD"]:::balNode nodeTerm["TERMINAL VELOCITY
du/dt = 0
ut = dp^2 (rhop - rho) g / 18 mu"]:::resNode nodeRe["REYNOLDS CHECK
Rep = dp*ut*rho / mu
Must be less than 0.1"]:::resNode nodeP --> nodeFg nodeP --> nodeFb nodeP --> nodeFd nodeFg --> nodeNet nodeFb --> nodeNet nodeFd --> nodeNet nodeNet --> nodeTerm nodeTerm --> nodeRe ``` ### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Continuous Thickener ```mermaid flowchart LR classDef zone fill:#EAF2FF,stroke:#1F4E8C,stroke-width:2px,color:#0B2545 classDef stream fill:#FFF5E5,stroke:#A65A00,stroke-width:2px,color:#5C2E00 classDef mech fill:#E8FFE8,stroke:#2E7D32,stroke-width:2px,color:#1B3D1B feed["FEED SLURRY
QF, CF"]:::stream chem["FLOCCULANT
(optional)"]:::stream zClear["CLEAR ZONE
low solids
overflow velocity vs"]:::zone zSep["SETTLING ZONE
particles fall at ut"]:::zone zTran["TRANSITION ZONE
hindered settling"]:::zone zComp["COMPRESSION ZONE
consolidation"]:::zone zSludge["SLUDGE BLANKET
concentrated solids"]:::zone rake["SLOW ROTATING RAKE
arms push sludge
to central outlet"]:::mech ovf["OVERFLOW
QO, CO approx 0"]:::stream und["UNDERFLOW
QU, CU concentrated"]:::stream feed --> zClear chem -.-> zTran zClear --> zSep --> zTran --> zComp --> zSludge zSludge --> rake --> und zClear --> ovf ``` ### 4.3 Functional Flow — Comparison of the Three Industrial Devices ```mermaid flowchart TB classDef startNode fill:#FFE5E5,stroke:#A02626,stroke-width:2px,color:#5C0F0F classDef branchNode fill:#FFF8DC,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px,color:#5C4400 classDef endNode fill:#E0FFE0,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:2px,color:#0B3D0B start["INDUSTRIAL SUSPENSION"]:::startNode q1{"Goal of separation?"}:::branchNode start --> q1 q1 -->|"Sort by size"| cls["CLASSIFIER
spiral or rake
coarse fraction separated"]:::endNode q1 -->|"Clean overflow"| cla["CLARIFIER
lamella plates
clear liquid recovered"]:::endNode q1 -->|"Concentrate solids"| thk["THICKENER
Dorr type, large diameter
underflow thickened"]:::endNode ``` > [!NOTE] > The above flow diagrams abstract the physical equipment into a **Block-Level Functional Architecture** because the biological process is best understood in terms of its *zones* (clarification, settling, compression) rather than the literal drawing of tank shapes. This is the same block-flow convention used in standard chemical-engineering textbooks and in KTU valuation scripts. --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) #### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model)] **State Stokes' law for the terminal settling velocity of a fine particle in a fluid. List the assumptions under which it is valid.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **Stokes' Law:** The terminal velocity of a spherical particle settling under gravity in the laminar (creeping-flow) regime is > $$u_t \;=\; \frac{d_p^{\,2}\,(\rho_p - \rho)\, g}{18\,\mu}$$ > **Assumptions:** > 1. The particle is a smooth, rigid sphere. > 2. The flow regime around the particle is creeping / laminar (Reynolds number based on particle $\le 0.1$). > 3. The particle is settling in an infinite, quiescent, Newtonian fluid. > 4. The particle concentration is very dilute (no hindered-settling effects, $\phi \to 0$). > 5. No slip at the particle surface (no-slip boundary condition satisfied). > 6. Only gravitational, buoyant, and viscous-drag forces are significant; Brownian motion, electrostatic, and van der Waals forces are negligible. --- #### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model)] **Distinguish between a clarifier, a thickener, and a classifier. State one industrial application of each.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Equipment | Function | Industrial Application | |-----------|----------|------------------------| | **Clarifier** | Produces a *clear overflow*; removes trace solids from a low-concentration stream | Final polishing of treated wastewater in a biotech plant | | **Thickener** | Produces a *concentrated underflow*; increases solids fraction of a slurry | Pre-concentration of yeast slurry before a centrifuge or filter | | **Classifier** | Separates a feed into *coarse and fine fractions* based on settling velocity | Sorting of milled biological solids; separating oversize from fermentation broth carriers | --- ### 5.2 Part B – Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) #### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024 (Model)]** **(a) [7 marks]** Derive Stokes' law from a force balance on a single spherical particle settling in a Newtonian fluid under gravity. Clearly state the assumptions. **(b) [7 marks]** A suspension contains spherical particles of diameter $d_p = 50 \, \mu$m and density $\rho_p = 2500$ kg/m³, suspended in water at 20 °C ($\rho = 998$ kg/m³, $\mu = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s). The flow rate to be treated is $Q = 0.5 \, \text{m}^3/\text{h}$. (i) Calculate the terminal settling velocity using Stokes' law. (ii) Verify the regime of settling. (iii) Determine the cross-sectional area of an ideal clarifier to settle 100 % of these particles. ##### Model Solution **(a) Derivation (7 marks):** Refer to **Section 3.1** above for the step-by-step force balance. Marks distribution: - 1 mark: Statement of momentum balance $m_p\, du/dt = F_g - F_b - F_D$ - 1 mark: Expressions for $F_g$, $F_b$, $F_D$ in Stokes' regime - 1 mark: Equating at terminal velocity ($du/dt = 0$) - 1 mark: Algebra leading to $u_t = d_p^{\,2}(\rho_p - \rho)g / 18\mu$ - 1 mark: Stating Reynolds-number limit $Re_p \le 0.1$ - 1 mark: Listing the six assumptions - 1 mark: Correctly concluding that $u_t \propto d_p^{\,2}$ **(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):** **(i) Terminal velocity:** $$u_t \;=\; \frac{d_p^{\,2}\,(\rho_p - \rho)\, g}{18\,\mu}$$ $$u_t \;=\; \frac{(50\times10^{-6})^{2}\,(2500 - 998)\,(9.81)}{18 \times 1.0\times10^{-3}}$$ $$d_p^{\,2} \;=\; 2.5 \times 10^{-9} \;\text{m}^2$$ $$\text{Numerator} \;=\; 2.5\times10^{-9} \times 1502 \times 9.81 \;=\; 3.683 \times 10^{-5}$$ $$\text{Denominator} \;=\; 1.8 \times 10^{-2}$$ $$\boxed{u_t \;=\; 2.05 \times 10^{-3} \;\text{m/s} \;\approx\; 7.36 \;\text{m/h}}$$ **(ii) Reynolds-number check:** $$Re_p \;=\; \frac{d_p\, u_t\, \rho}{\mu} \;=\; \frac{(50\times10^{-6})(2.05\times10^{-3})(998)}{1.0\times10^{-3}}$$ $$Re_p \;=\; \frac{1.023\times10^{-4}}{1.0\times10^{-3}} \;=\; 0.1023$$ $$Re_p \;\approx\; 0.10 \;\;\text{(at the upper limit of Stokes' regime — borderline acceptable)}$$ > If a stricter limit of $Re_p \le 0.1$ is enforced, the answer may be flagged "borderline — Stokes' law marginally applicable." For a KTU board exam, this is acceptable but the student should comment on it. **(iii) Clarifier area:** $$Q \;=\; 0.5 \;\text{m}^3/\text{h} \;=\; 1.389 \times 10^{-4} \;\text{m}^3/\text{s}$$ $$A_{\min} \;=\; \frac{Q}{u_t} \;=\; \frac{1.389\times10^{-4}}{2.05\times10^{-3}} \;=\; 6.78 \times 10^{-2} \;\text{m}^2$$ $$\boxed{A_{\min} \;\approx\; 0.068 \;\text{m}^2} \quad \text{(a small unit — practical for lab scale)}$$ --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model)]** **(a) [7 marks]** With the aid of a neat sketch, describe the construction and working of a **Dorr-type thickener**. Discuss the role of each zone. **(b) [7 marks]** A continuous thickener handles 2000 m³/day of feed slurry containing 5 % w/w solids (slurry density 1050 kg/m³, solids density 2200 kg/m³). The underflow is to be concentrated to 40 % w/w solids, and a flocculant is added such that the hindered-settling velocity equals 1.2 m/h. Calculate (i) the underflow volumetric flow rate, and (ii) the cross-sectional area required for the thickener. ##### Model Solution **(a) Dorr Thickener (7 marks):** A **Dorr thickener** is a large-diameter, shallow, cylindrical tank with a slowly rotating rake arm at the bottom. Feed enters through a central feed well. The slurry flows radially outward while the solids settle. The functional zones from top to bottom are: | Zone | Function | |------|----------| | **Clear zone (top)** | Clarified liquid moves horizontally toward the peripheral launder; very low solids concentration. | | **Settling zone** | Individual particles fall at their free-settling velocity $u_t$. | | **Transition zone** | Particle concentration increases; hindered settling (Richardson–Zaki) becomes important. | | **Compression zone (bottom)** | Solids are mechanically compacted by the rotating rake arms; water is squeezed out upward. | | **Sludge-blanket zone** | Concentrated underflow is pushed to the central discharge cone by the rake. | **Working principle:** Solids report to the underflow (high concentration), while the clarified liquid overflows the peripheral launder (low concentration). The rotating rake arms (1 – 4 rpm) gently stir the sludge, (i) channeling it to the central underflow outlet, and (ii) promoting dewatering in the compression zone. (Diagrammatic representation: refer to **Section 4.2** for the equivalent block-level functional architecture.) **Marks distribution:** - 1 mark: Sketch of a Dorr thickener (cylindrical tank + central feed well + rake + launder) - 2 marks: Description of construction - 2 marks: Description of working (rake rotation, radial flow, overflow, underflow) - 2 marks: Discussion of each of the 5 zones **(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):** **Given:** $Q_F = 2000 \;\text{m}^3/\text{day}$, $w_F = 0.05$ (mass fraction), $w_U = 0.40$ (mass fraction), $u_t^{\,*} = 1.2 \;\text{m/h}$. **Step 1 — Convert volumetric flow rate to mass basis:** Feed mass flow rate: $$\dot{m}_F \;=\; Q_F \cdot \rho_F \;=\; 2000 \times 1050 \;=\; 2.1 \times 10^{6} \;\text{kg/day}$$ **Step 2 — Solid mass flow rate in feed:** $$\dot{m}_{s,F} \;=\; 0.05 \times 2.1\times10^{6} \;=\; 1.05 \times 10^{5} \;\text{kg/day}$$ **Step 3 — Underflow solid mass flow rate (= feed solid mass, assuming no solids in overflow):** $$\dot{m}_{s,U} \;=\; 1.05 \times 10^{5} \;\text{kg/day}$$ **Step 4 — Total underflow mass flow rate:** $$\dot{m}_U \;=\; \frac{\dot{m}_{s,U}}{w_U} \;=\; \frac{1.05\times10^{5}}{0.40} \;=\; 2.625 \times 10^{5} \;\text{kg/day}$$ **Step 5 — Liquid mass flow rate in underflow:** $$\dot{m}_{L,U} \;=\; \dot{m}_U - \dot{m}_{s,U} \;=\; 2.625\times10^{5} - 1.05\times10^{5} \;=\; 1.575 \times 10^{5} \;\text{kg/day}$$ **Step 6 — Underflow volumetric flow rate (using slurry density in the underflow zone, approximated as 1100 kg/m³ at 40 % solids):** $$Q_U \;=\; \frac{\dot{m}_U}{\rho_U} \;=\; \frac{2.625\times10^{5}}{1100} \;\approx\; 238.6 \;\text{m}^3/\text{day} \;\approx\; 9.94 \;\text{m}^3/\text{h}$$ (If slurry density is not provided, the student can estimate $\rho_U$ from solids density 2200 kg/m³ and water density 1000 kg/m³, weighted by mass fractions.) > **Answer (i): $Q_U \approx 240 \,\text{m}^3/\text{day}$** **Step 7 — Overflow flow rate (volumetric balance, assuming overflow density ≈ 1000 kg/m³):** $$Q_O \;\approx\; Q_F - Q_U \;\approx\; 2000 - 240 \;=\; 1760 \;\text{m}^3/\text{day} \;=\; 73.3 \;\text{m}^3/\text{h}$$ **Step 8 — Settling (overflow) velocity = hindered-settling velocity:** $$v_s \;=\; u_t^{\,*} \;=\; 1.2 \;\text{m/h}$$ **Step 9 — Required thickener cross-sectional area (Coe–Clevenger equation):** $$A \;=\; \frac{Q_O - Q_U}{v_s} \;\approx\; \frac{73.3 - 9.94}{1.2} \;\approx\; 52.8 \;\text{m}^2$$ > **Answer (ii): $A \approx 53 \,\text{m}^2$** → diameter $\approx \sqrt{4 \times 53 / \pi} \approx 8.2$ m. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common mistakes that cost marks in sedimentation problems:** > 1. **Forgetting to convert units** — $d_p$ must be in **metres** (not µm), $Q$ in **m³/s** (not m³/h), and $\mu$ in **Pa·s** (not cP). One missed conversion can lose 1 – 2 marks. > 2. **Not verifying the Stokes' regime** — always compute $Re_p$ at the end and confirm $Re_p \le 0.1$ (or $\le 1$ for a soft limit). If $Re_p > 0.1$, Stokes' law is **invalid** and a transition- or Newton-regime correlation must be used. > 3. **Confusing clarifier and thickener design equations** — a *clarifier* is sized on $A = Q / u_t$ (cleaning the overflow), a *thickener* on the Coe–Clevenger area $A = (Q_O - Q_U)/v_s$ (concentrating the underflow). > 4. **Using free-settling $u_t$ in a thickener** — at the high solids concentrations found in a thickener, hindered settling applies: $u_t^{\,*} = u_t (1 - \phi)^{n}$. > 5. **Sign convention for $v_s$** — the settling velocity in a thickener is *upward positive*; the *downward* particle motion is what removes the solids. > 6. **Skipping the assumption list** in derivations — the KTU 2024 rubric explicitly allots 1 – 2 marks for listing Stokes' law assumptions; a derivation without them is incomplete. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - **Stokes' Law (free settling, fine particles):** $u_t = \dfrac{d_p^{\,2}(\rho_p - \rho)g}{18\mu}$. It is valid only when $Re_p \le 0.1$ and the suspension is dilute. - **Drag in Stokes' regime:** $F_D = 3\pi\mu d_p u$. The drag coefficient is $C_D = 24/Re_p$. - **Hindered settling (Richardson–Zaki):** $u_t^{\,*} = u_t(1 - \phi)^{n}$ with $n$ in the range 2.4 – 4.65 depending on $Re_p$. - **Clarifier sizing:** $A_{\min} = Q/u_t$ — area is determined by throughput and terminal velocity alone (independent of depth). - **Thickener sizing (Coe–Clevenger):** $A = (Q_O - Q_U)/v_s$ — area is determined by the *net upward liquid velocity* in the clear zone. - **Classifier sizing:** based on a *cut size* $d_{50}$ — particles larger than $d_{50}$ report to the coarse product, smaller ones to the fines. - **Differences (memory hook):** - **Classifier** → sorts by **size**. - **Clarifier** → produces **clear overflow**. - **Thickener** → produces **concentrated underflow**. - **Five zones of a continuous thickener** (top → bottom): Clear, Settling, Transition, Compression, Sludge-blanket. - **Why Stokes' law fails for microbes:** bacterial cells ($\sim 1 \,\mu$m, $\rho_p \approx 1050 \,\text{kg/m}^3$, very close to broth density) have $u_t$ of order $10^{-7}$ m/s, requiring impractically large clarifier areas — hence industry uses **centrifuges** instead. - **For a sphere:** volume $= \pi d_p^{\,3}/6$, projected area $= \pi d_p^{\,2}/4$, surface area $= \pi d_p^{\,2}$. - **Always report $Re_p$** in your numerical answers — it is the first thing a KTU examiner looks for after the terminal velocity. - **Unit-check list:** $d_p$ [m], $\rho$ [kg/m³], $\mu$ [Pa·s], $g$ [m/s²], $Q$ [m³/s], $A$ [m²], $u_t$ [m/s]. > **Final KTU Mantra for this topic:** *"Master Stokes' law, prove the regime, convert the units, pick the right equipment."*

topics.title: Centrifugal Bioseparations: Principles of Centrifugal Settling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT525 - BIOSEPARATION TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Centrifugal Bioseparations: Principles of Centrifugal Settling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Aligned)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Centrifugal Settling** is a bioseparation unit operation in which particles suspended in a fluid are separated under the influence of an artificially enhanced gravitational field generated by rotating the fluid at high angular velocity. The induced **centrifugal acceleration** (a function of angular velocity squared and radial distance) replaces ordinary gravity, dramatically increasing the settling (sedimentation) velocity of biological particles such as cells, organelles, proteins, viruses, and cellular debris.

Mathematically, the centrifugal field strength at any radial position **r** is:

$$a_c = \omega^2 \, r$$

where $\omega$ is the angular velocity (rad/s) and $r$ is the distance of the particle from the axis of rotation (m). This acceleration typically ranges from **1,000 × g** in laboratory benchtop centrifuges to **>100,000 × g** in ultracentrifuges.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy 🥗

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of a Salad Spinner!** When you press the lid of a salad spinner and rotate it rapidly, water droplets clinging to the lettuce leaves are flung outward and collect at the bowl's outer wall. The faster you spin, the more violently the water is ejected. The lettuce (your "solid") stays behind because it is heavier and held by the basket, while the water (your "liquid") escapes. A **centrifuge works on exactly the same principle**, except instead of lettuce, we are separating cells, proteins, or organelles from their suspending medium — and the "spinning" is done at tens of thousands of revolutions per minute.

---

## 1.3 Why Centrifugal Settling? (Engineering Motivation)

- **Gravity settling is too slow** for sub-micron biological particles. A 1 µm bacterium settles only a few millimetres per day under gravity.
- **Centrifugation accelerates settling** by 10³ to 10⁶ fold by amplifying the body force acting on the particle.
- It is a **non-destructive, scalable** primary recovery step in downstream processing of bioproducts (e.g., harvesting recombinant *E. coli*, blood fractionation, vaccine production).

---

## 1.4 Key Engineering Parameters (Quick Glossary)

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Unit | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Angular velocity | $\omega$ | rad/s | Rotational speed in radians per second |
| Rotational speed | $N$ | rpm | Revolutions per minute |
| Radial distance | $r$ | m | Distance of particle from rotation axis |
| Relative Centrifugal Force | RCF | dimensionless (× g) | Ratio of $a_c$ to standard gravity $g$ |
| Settling velocity | $v_g$ | m/s | Radial velocity at which particle migrates outward |
| Particle diameter | $d_p$ | m | Equivalent spherical diameter of particle |
| Viscosity of medium | $\mu$ | Pa·s | Dynamic viscosity of suspending fluid |
| Density difference | $\Delta \rho$ | kg/m³ | $(\rho_p - \rho_f)$ between particle and fluid |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conversion shortcut:** $RCF = 1.118 \times 10^{-5} \times r \, (cm) \times [N \, (rpm)]^2$. This is one of the most-used formulae in any centrifugation lab and is **guaranteed to appear in KTU numerical problems**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔬 Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Principle — Force Balance on a Suspended Particle

A spherical particle moving radially through a viscous fluid in a centrifugal field experiences **three principal forces**:

1. **Centrifugal force** (outward, driving force):
$$F_c = \frac{\pi}{6} d_p^3 \, (\rho_p - \rho_f) \, \omega^2 \, r$$

2. **Buoyancy + gravity** (negligible compared to $F_c$ at high RCF — often ignored in standard derivations).

3. **Drag force** (inward, resisting force). In the **Stokes regime** (low Reynolds number, $Re < 1$):
$$F_D = 3 \pi \mu d_p \, v_g$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **"Why"** behind this analysis: in a rotating reference frame, the fluid is stationary in the lab frame, so we use the steady-state balance $F_c = F_D$ to derive the terminal radial settling velocity. This is the *exact analogue* of the classic gravity settling derivation — just with $g$ replaced by $\omega^2 r$.

---

## 2.2 The Stokes-Regime Settling Velocity

Setting $F_c = F_D$:

$$\frac{\pi}{6} d_p^3 (\rho_p - \rho_f) \omega^2 r = 3 \pi \mu d_p v_g$$

Solving for $v_g$:

$$v_g = \frac{d_p^2 (\rho_p - \rho_f) \omega^2 r}{18 \mu}$$

> **Comparison with gravity settling:** $v_{g,gravity} = \dfrac{d_p^2 (\rho_p - \rho_f) g}{18 \mu}$

The only difference is the substitution of $g$ by $\omega^2 r$. The **enhancement factor** is therefore RCF = $\omega^2 r / g$.

---

## 2.3 The Sigma Factor (Σ) — The Most Important Concept in Centrifugation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Sigma (Σ) concept is the cornerstone of centrifuge scale-up and appears in nearly every KTU Module-2 question.**

A real industrial centrifuge has a complex geometry (tubular bowl, disc stack, basket, etc.) where the fluid and particles travel along complicated paths. To compare different centrifuge geometries on an **equal footing**, engineers define the **equivalent settling area** $\Sigma$:

$$\Sigma = \frac{V \, \omega^2 \, r_{eq}^2}{g \, s}$$

where:
- $V$ = volume of fluid in the bowl (m³)
- $\omega$ = angular velocity (rad/s)
- $r_{eq}$ = equivalent radius of the bowl
- $s$ = a dimensionless "slip" or geometry correction factor (≈ 1 for tubular bowls)

The **physical meaning of Σ** is: *"The cross-sectional area of a gravity settling tank that would give the same separation performance as this centrifuge."*

For a **simple tubular bowl centrifuge**, the equivalent settling area is:

$$\Sigma_{tubular} = \frac{\pi \, L \, \omega^2}{g \ln\left( \dfrac{2 r_2}{r_1 + r_2} \right)} \cdot \left(r_2^2 - r_1^2 \right)$$

where $L$ is the bowl length, $r_1$ is the inner liquid radius, and $r_2$ is the bowl inner radius.

---

## 2.4 The Performance Equation — Equivalent Clarification Time

The throughput $Q$ (m³/s) of a continuous centrifuge is related to $\Sigma$ by:

$$Q = V \cdot \frac{\omega^2}{g} \cdot \frac{1}{t_{eq}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad t_{eq} = \frac{V}{\Sigma} \cdot \frac{g}{\omega^2}$$

For a **batch centrifuge** (e.g., a lab tube), the equivalent settling time to clarify a suspension from initial solids fraction $x_0$ to final $x_f$ is:

$$t_{eq} = \frac{\Sigma}{s \cdot g} \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{x_f}{x_0}\right)$$

> **Engineering utility:** The Σ-concept allows a bioprocess engineer to **scale up a lab centrifugation to industrial scale** simply by matching $Q/\Sigma$ ratios — a universal standard in pharmaceutical manufacturing.

---

## 2.5 Resolution of Two Particle Populations

For separating two particles (1 and 2) in a density gradient, the **resolution equation** is:

$$R = \frac{\Delta v_g}{\bar{v_g}} = \frac{ \Delta(\rho) \cdot \Delta(d_p^2) }{\text{average of } \rho d_p^2 }$$

Better resolution requires:
- **Higher $\omega$** (more centrifugal force)
- **Longer run time** $t$
- **Smaller radial zone** (sharper bands)

---

## 2.6 📋 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas you need to solve any KTU 2024 numerical on this topic are tabulated below. Master this table.**

| # | Formula | Application / When to Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $a_c = \omega^2 r$ | Centrifugal acceleration at radius $r$ |
| 2 | $RCF = \dfrac{\omega^2 r}{g} = 1.118 \times 10^{-5} \, r_{cm} N_{rpm}^2$ | Convert rpm to "× g" |
| 3 | $v_g = \dfrac{d_p^2 (\rho_p - \rho_f) \omega^2 r}{18 \mu}$ | Stokes-regime settling in centrifugal field |
| 4 | $v_g = \dfrac{d_p^2 (\rho_p - \rho_f) g}{18 \mu}$ | Settling under ordinary gravity (Stokes) |
| 5 | $\Sigma = \dfrac{V \omega^2 r_{eq}^2}{g \, s}$ | Sigma factor (scale-up parameter) |
| 6 | $Q = \dfrac{V \cdot \omega^2 \cdot r_{eq}}{g \cdot t_{eq}}$ | Throughput of continuous centrifuge |
| 7 | $t_{eq} = \dfrac{\Sigma}{s \cdot g} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{x_f}{x_0}\right)$ | Batch clarification time |
| 8 | $F_c = \dfrac{\pi}{6} d_p^3 \Delta\rho \, \omega^2 r$ | Centrifugal force on one particle |
| 9 | $F_D = 3 \pi \mu d_p v_g$ | Stokes drag on spherical particle |
| 10 | $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$ | Convert rpm to rad/s |
| 11 | $Re = \dfrac{\rho_f v_g d_p}{\mu} < 1$ | Validity check for Stokes regime |
| 12 | $C_D = \dfrac{18.5}{Re^{0.6}}$ | Drag coefficient (intermediate regime, $1 < Re < 1000$) |

> **Real-world utility of this system:** These equations are used to design vaccine clarification lines, choose between disc-stack and tubular centrifuges in monoclonal antibody production, and specify ultracentrifuge runs for plasmid DNA or virus purification.

---

## 2.7 When Stokes' Law Fails — Non-Stokesian Settling

If $Re > 1$, the drag force is no longer linear in $v_g$. The general force balance is:

$$\frac{\pi}{6} d_p^3 \Delta\rho \, \omega^2 r = \frac{C_D}{2} \rho_f v_g^2 \cdot \frac{\pi d_p^2}{4}$$

Solving:

$$v_g = \sqrt{ \frac{4 d_p \Delta\rho \, \omega^2 r}{3 C_D \rho_f} }$$

For the **intermediate regime** ($1 < Re < 1000$), $C_D \approx 18.5 / Re^{0.6}$, leading to the empirical settling velocity:

$$v_g = \frac{0.152 \, d_p^{1.14} (\Delta\rho)^{0.71}}{\rho_f^{0.29} \mu^{0.43}} (\omega^2 r)^{0.71}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Trap:** Students often blindly apply the Stokes formula in problems where particles are large (e.g., > 50 µm) or the medium is non-viscous. Always check $Re$ first!
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Terminal Settling Velocity in a Centrifugal Field

**Starting premise:** A spherical particle of diameter $d_p$ and density $\rho_p$ is suspended in a fluid of density $\rho_f$ and viscosity $\mu$. The system rotates at angular velocity $\omega$. The particle reaches a **terminal radial velocity** $v_g$ when the net force is zero.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Identify forces in the rotating frame:**

In the rotating reference frame, a fictitious centrifugal force acts outward on the particle:

$$F_{centrifugal} = m_p \cdot \omega^2 r = \frac{\pi}{6} d_p^3 \rho_p \cdot \omega^2 r$$

**Step 2 — Subtract buoyancy (Archimedes' principle in the rotating frame):**

The displaced fluid would also "experience" a centrifugal force, so the net driving force is the **apparent weight in the centrifugal field**:

$$F_{net, driving} = \frac{\pi}{6} d_p^3 (\rho_p - \rho_f) \, \omega^2 r$$

**Step 3 — Write the resisting (drag) force in the Stokes regime:**

For low Reynolds number, the drag on a sphere is given by Stokes' law:

$$F_{drag} = 3 \pi \mu d_p \, v_g$$

**Step 4 — Apply steady-state condition (terminal velocity):**

At terminal velocity, acceleration is zero, so $F_{net, driving} = F_{drag}$:

$$\frac{\pi}{6} d_p^3 (\rho_p - \rho_f) \omega^2 r = 3 \pi \mu d_p \, v_g$$

**Step 5 — Algebraic rearrangement for $v_g$:**

Divide both sides by $3 \pi \mu d_p$:

$$\frac{d_p^2 (\rho_p - \rho_f) \omega^2 r}{18 \mu} = v_g$$

$$\boxed{\,v_g = \frac{d_p^2 (\rho_p - \rho_f) \, \omega^2 \, r}{18 \, \mu}\,}$$

**Step 6 — Define the enhancement (RCF):**

The ratio of centrifugal to gravitational settling velocity is:

$$\frac{v_{g,cent}}{v_{g,grav}} = \frac{\omega^2 r}{g} \equiv RCF$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Sigma Factor for a Tubular-Bowl Centrifuge

**Starting premise:** Consider a tubular bowl of inner radius $r_2$ (outer liquid radius) and $r_1$ (inner liquid radius), with axial length $L$. The fluid rotates as a solid body at $\omega$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Set up a particle mass balance in a cylindrical shell:**

Consider an infinitesimal cylindrical shell of fluid at radius $r$ with thickness $dr$ and length $L$. The radial flow rate of fluid is $dQ = 2\pi r L \cdot v_g \, dr$.

**Step 2 — Express $dQ$ using the Stokes settling velocity:**

Substitute $v_g$ from Section 3.1:

$$dQ = 2\pi r L \cdot \frac{d_p^2 \Delta\rho \, \omega^2 r}{18 \mu} \, dr$$

**Step 3 — Integrate across the annulus from $r_1$ to $r_2$:**

$$Q = \int_{r_1}^{r_2} \frac{2\pi L \, d_p^2 \Delta\rho \, \omega^2}{18 \mu} \, r^2 \, dr$$

$$Q = \frac{2\pi L \, d_p^2 \Delta\rho \, \omega^2}{18 \mu} \cdot \left[ \frac{r^3}{3} \right]_{r_1}^{r_2}$$

**Step 4 — Equate this to the standard Q = V·(ω²/g)·(1/t) and identify Σ:**

The volume of fluid in the bowl is $V = \pi L (r_2^2 - r_1^2)$. Equating and solving for the geometric terms:

$$\Sigma = \frac{\pi L \, \omega^2 (r_2^2 - r_1^2)}{2g \ln(r_2/r_1)} \cdot \left( \frac{r_2 + r_1}{2} \right)$$

For the common approximation $r_1 \approx r_2 \approx r$ (thin annular fluid layer):

$$\boxed{\,\Sigma_{tubular} = \frac{2\pi L \omega^2 r^3}{g} = \frac{V \omega^2 r}{g}\,}$$

(when $V = 2\pi r L \, dr$ is the active fluid volume).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Equivalent Clarification Time for a Batch Centrifuge

**Starting premise:** A suspension with initial particle concentration $C_0$ (or volume fraction $x_0$) is centrifuged. We want the time $t$ for the concentration to drop to $C_f$ (or $x_f$).

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Rate of removal of particles:**

The fraction of particles removed per unit time is proportional to the volumetric throughput divided by bowl volume:

$$\frac{dC}{dt} = -\frac{Q \cdot C}{V} \cdot \frac{\omega^2 r}{g} \cdot s$$

Wait — for a **batch centrifuge** (no outflow), the correct formulation uses the sigma concept:

**Step 2 — Use the equivalent-area formulation:**

The total number of particles removed by sedimentation is equal to those that would settle in an equivalent gravity settler of area $\Sigma$. The fraction remaining after time $t$ is:

$$\frac{x_f}{x_0} = \exp\!\left( - \frac{s \cdot g \cdot t}{\Sigma} \right)$$

**Step 3 — Solve for time:**

$$t = \frac{\Sigma}{s \cdot g} \ln\!\left(\frac{x_0}{x_f}\right)$$

$$\boxed{\,t_{eq} = \frac{\Sigma}{s \, g} \ln\!\left(\frac{x_0}{x_f}\right)\,}$$

> **Engineering insight:** To improve clarification, you can either (a) **increase $\omega$** (use a higher-speed centrifuge — but this is limited by mechanical stress), or (b) **increase bowl geometry** (longer $L$ for tubular bowls — increases $\Sigma$ linearly), or (c) **pre-concentrate** the feed (reduces $V$ and increases $x_0$).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Decision Tree for Centrifuge Type Selection

| Particle Size | Density Diff. $\Delta\rho$ | Best Centrifuge Type | Typical RCF |
|---|---|---|---|
| > 50 µm (whole cells, debris) | High (≥ 0.1 g/cm³) | **Basket / Pusher** | 500 – 2,000 × g |
| 1 – 50 µm (organelles, RBC) | Moderate | **Tubular bowl** | 10,000 – 50,000 × g |
| 0.1 – 1 µm (bacteria, yeast) | Low – moderate | **Disc stack** | 5,000 – 15,000 × g |
| 0.01 – 0.1 µm (virus, proteins) | Very low | **Ultracentrifuge** | 50,000 – 100,000 × g |
| < 0.01 µm (macromolecules) | Tiny | **Density-gradient ultracentrifuge** | > 100,000 × g |

---

## 3.5 Solved Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** A bacterial broth contains cells of diameter $d_p = 2 \,\mu m$ and density $\rho_p = 1080 \, kg/m^3$, suspended in a medium of density $\rho_f = 1000 \, kg/m^3$ and viscosity $\mu = 1.0 \times 10^{-3} \, Pa \cdot s$. The centrifuge bowl has an equivalent radius $r = 0.10 \, m$ and operates at 8000 rpm. Calculate: (a) the RCF, (b) the settling velocity at $r = 0.10 \, m$, and (c) the time needed for the cells to sediment from $r_1 = 0.02 \, m$ to $r_2 = 0.10 \, m$.

### Solution:

**(a) RCF:**

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2\pi (8000)}{60} = 837.76 \, rad/s$$

$$RCF = \frac{\omega^2 r}{g} = \frac{(837.76)^2 (0.10)}{9.81} = 7154 \approx 7150 \times g$$

**(b) Settling velocity:**

$$v_g = \frac{(2\times 10^{-6})^2 (1080 - 1000)(837.76)^2 (0.10)}{18 (1.0\times 10^{-3})}$$

$$v_g = \frac{(4\times 10^{-12})(80)(701,841)(0.10)}{0.018}$$

$$v_g = \frac{2.247 \times 10^{-5}}{0.018} = 1.25 \times 10^{-3} \, m/s$$

**(c) Sedimentation time (integrate $dr/v_g$):**

$$t = \int_{r_1}^{r_2} \frac{18 \mu}{d_p^2 \Delta\rho \omega^2} \cdot \frac{dr}{r} = \frac{18 \mu}{d_p^2 \Delta\rho \omega^2} \ln\!\left(\frac{r_2}{r_1}\right)$$

$$t = \frac{18 (10^{-3})}{(4\times 10^{-12})(80)(701{,}841)} \cdot \ln(5)$]

$$t = \frac{0.018}{2.245 \times 10^{-4}} \cdot 1.609 = 80.18 \cdot 1.609 = 129 \, s$$

$$\boxed{t \approx 2.15 \, minutes}$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🏗️ Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Force-Balance Diagram — Particle in a Centrifugal Field

The schematic below shows the forces acting on a spherical particle at radius $r$ rotating about a central axis. The particle moves **radially outward** because the centrifugal force dominates the inward-pointing drag.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Axis["ROTATION AXIS"]
    Particle["SPHERICAL PARTICLE\nd_p, density rho_p\nPosition: r from axis"]
    Fcent["CENTRIFUGAL FORCE F_c\nDirection: radially OUTWARD\nMagnitude: pi/6 d_p^3 rho_p omega^2 r"]
    Fbuoy["BUOYANT FORCE\nDirection: radially INWARD\nMagnitude: pi/6 d_p^3 rho_f omega^2 r"]
    Fdrag["STOKES DRAG F_D\nDirection: radially INWARD\nOpposes radial motion\nMagnitude: 3 pi mu d_p v_g"]
    Fnet["NET DRIVING FORCE\nF_c minus F_buoy\n= pi/6 d_p^3 rho_p minus rho_f omega^2 r"]

    Axis --> Particle
    Particle --> Fcent
    Particle --> Fbuoy
    Particle --> Fdrag
    Fcent --> Fnet
    Fbuoy --> Fnet
    Fnet --> Particle
    Fdrag --> Particle
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The "engine" of centrifugal settling is the imbalance between outward $F_c$ and inward ($F_{buoy} + F_{drag}$). At terminal velocity, $F_{net} = F_{drag}$, and the particle travels at constant $v_g$.

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Centrifugal Bioseparation Train

This diagram maps the complete functional flow of a typical industrial centrifugal bioseparation cascade, from broth intake to clarified product.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Feed["STEP 1: CRUDE BROTH FEED\nWhole cells, debris, soluble product\nin fermentation medium"]
    PreTr["STEP 2: PRE-TREATMENT\npH adjustment, viscosity reduction\nflocculant addition if needed"]
    PrimCent{"STEP 3: PRIMARY CENTRIFUGE\nDisc-stack or tubular bowl\nRCF 5000 to 15000 x g\nGoal: cell harvest or removal"}
    Sup1["STEP 4: CLARIFIED SUPERNATANT\nSoluble proteins, product of interest\nresidual fine particulates"]
    SecCent{"STEP 5: SECONDARY CENTRIFUGE\nHigher RCF, polish step\nRemoves sub-micron fines"}
    Paste["STEP 6: CELL PASTE / PELLET\nHeavy phase out, biomass disposal\nor further processing"]
    Prod["STEP 7: CLARIFIED PRODUCT STREAM\nReady for chromatography or\nmembrane filtration"]
    QC["STEP 8: SIGMA ANALYSIS AND QC\nCompute Sigma factor\nVerify Q over Sigma constant\nfor scale-up validation"]

    Feed --> PreTr
    PreTr --> PrimCent
    PrimCent --> Sup1
    PrimCent --> Paste
    Sup1 --> SecCent
    SecCent --> Prod
    SecCent --> QC
    Paste --> QC
```

> **Functional reading:** Each block is a unit operation. The two **diamond blocks** are the actual centrifuges; the rectangular blocks represent streams or auxiliary operations. The **Sigma concept** (computed in STEP 8) is what links the laboratory-scale bowl to the industrial unit.

---

## 4.3 Comparative Flow — Stokes vs. Non-Stokes Settling Regimes

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start["PARTICLE IN CENTRIFUGAL FIELD\nKnown d_p, rho_p, rho_f, mu, omega, r"]
    Q1{"CHECK: Compute Reynolds number\nRe equals rho_f v_g d_p over mu\nIs Re less than 1?"}
    Stokes["STOKES REGIME\nF_drag equals 3 pi mu d_p v_g\nv_g equals d_p squared rho_p minus rho_f omega squared r\nover 18 mu\nLinear in omega squared r"]
    Inter{"INTERMEDIATE REGIME\n1 less than Re less than 1000"}
    CDInt["USE EMPIRICAL C_d\nC_d approx 18.5 over Re to 0.6\nv_g equals 0.152 d_p to 1.14\nDelta rho to 0.71\nomega squared r to 0.71"]
    Newton{"NEWTON REGIME\nRe greater than 1000\nlarge particles in low viscosity fluid"}
    CDNew["C_d approx 0.44 constant\nv_g equals sqrt of 4 d_p Delta rho omega squared r\nover 1.32 rho_f"]
    End["TERMINAL SETTLING VELOCITY v_g\nReturn to overall process model"]

    Start --> Q1
    Q1 -- yes --> Stokes --> End
    Q1 -- no --> Inter
    Inter -- typical --> CDInt --> End
    Inter -- high Re --> Newton --> CDNew --> End
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📌 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**
**Define the term "Relative Centrifugal Force" (RCF). How is it related to angular velocity and the radius of rotation? What is the typical RCF range used in (i) a laboratory benchtop centrifuge and (ii) an ultracentrifuge?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Relative Centrifugal Force (RCF) is the ratio of the centrifugal acceleration experienced by a particle in a rotating system to the standard acceleration due to gravity $g$:

$$RCF = \frac{\omega^2 r}{g} = 1.118 \times 10^{-5} \, r_{cm} \, N_{rpm}^2$$

It is **directly proportional** to the square of the angular velocity $\omega$ and to the radial distance $r$ from the axis of rotation.

- (i) Benchtop laboratory centrifuge: typically **1,000 – 10,000 × g**
- (ii) Ultracentrifuge: typically **100,000 – 1,000,000 × g**

> **Mark distribution:** 1 mark for formula, 1 mark for proportionalities, 1 mark for the two RCF ranges.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**
**State Stokes' law for a particle settling under gravity. Modify it to obtain the corresponding expression for the terminal settling velocity of a particle in a centrifugal field.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Stokes' law under gravity:

$$v_{g,grav} = \frac{d_p^2 (\rho_p - \rho_f) g}{18 \mu}$$

To modify for a centrifugal field, replace $g$ with the centrifugal acceleration $\omega^2 r$:

$$v_{g,cent} = \frac{d_p^2 (\rho_p - \rho_f) \omega^2 r}{18 \mu}$$

The ratio $v_{g,cent}/v_{g,grav} = \omega^2 r / g = RCF$, indicating that centrifugation accelerates settling by a factor equal to the RCF.

> **Mark distribution:** 1 mark for Stokes' law, 1 mark for substitution step, 1 mark for RCF interpretation.

---

## 📌 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Pattern]*

**(a) [7 marks]** Derive, starting from a force balance, the expression for the terminal settling velocity of a spherical particle in a centrifugal field under Stokes-regime conditions. Clearly state all assumptions.

**(b) [7 marks]** A tubular-bowl centrifuge has an inner radius $r_2 = 0.10 \, m$, inner liquid radius $r_1 = 0.05 \, m$, and length $L = 0.50 \, m$. It operates at 12,000 rpm. Compute (i) the angular velocity, (ii) the RCF at $r_2$, and (iii) the sigma factor $\Sigma$ of the bowl.

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Derivation [7 marks]:**

Refer to Section 3.1 for the full derivation. The key steps are:

1. State the centrifugal force: $F_c = \frac{\pi}{6} d_p^3 \rho_p \omega^2 r$ [1 mark]
2. Apply buoyancy correction: $F_{net} = \frac{\pi}{6} d_p^3 (\rho_p - \rho_f) \omega^2 r$ [1 mark]
3. State Stokes drag: $F_D = 3 \pi \mu d_p v_g$ [1 mark]
4. Assumptions: spherical particle, Stokes regime ($Re < 1$), incompressible fluid, steady-state, particle is small compared to bowl [1 mark]
5. Equate and solve: $v_g = \dfrac{d_p^2 (\rho_p - \rho_f) \omega^2 r}{18 \mu}$ [2 marks]
6. Identify enhancement: $v_{g,cent} = RCF \cdot v_{g,grav}$ [1 mark]

**(b) Numerical [7 marks]:**

**(i) Angular velocity:**

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 12000}{60} = 1256.6 \, rad/s \quad \text{[2 marks]}$$

**(ii) RCF at $r_2$:**

$$RCF = \frac{\omega^2 r_2}{g} = \frac{(1256.6)^2 (0.10)}{9.81} = \frac{157{,}914}{9.81} \approx 16{,}097 \times g \quad \text{[2 marks]}$$

**(iii) Sigma factor** (using thin-annulus approximation):

$$V = \pi L (r_2^2 - r_1^2) = \pi (0.50)(0.10^2 - 0.05^2) = \pi (0.50)(0.0075) = 0.01178 \, m^3$$

$$\Sigma = \frac{V \, \omega^2 \, r_{eq}}{g}, \quad r_{eq} = \frac{r_1 + r_2}{2} = 0.075 \, m$$

$$\Sigma = \frac{(0.01178)(157{,}914)(0.075)}{9.81} = \frac{139.50}{9.81} = 14.22 \, m^2 \quad \text{[3 marks]}$$

$$\boxed{\omega = 1257 \, rad/s, \quad RCF = 16{,}100 \times g, \quad \Sigma = 14.22 \, m^2}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern]*

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the **Sigma concept** in centrifugal separation. Why is it considered the cornerstone of centrifuge scale-up? Derive the equivalent clarification time expression for a batch centrifuge.

**(b) [7 marks]** A laboratory centrifuge is used to clarify a yeast suspension. The initial cell concentration is $x_0 = 0.10$ (volume fraction) and must be reduced to $x_f = 0.001$. The bowl has a sigma factor $\Sigma = 5.0 \, m^2$, and the slip factor $s \approx 1.0$. Calculate the time required. If the same clarification is desired in **half the time**, by what factor must the angular velocity be increased? (Assume $r$ remains constant.)

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Sigma Concept [7 marks]:**

The **Sigma factor** $\Sigma$ is the equivalent cross-sectional area of a gravity settling tank that would give the same sedimentation performance as a given centrifuge. It depends only on **geometry and speed** of the centrifuge, not on the feed properties. [2 marks]

It is the cornerstone of **scale-up** because two different centrifuges (lab vs. industrial) will give the **same separation** provided the ratio $Q/\Sigma$ is held constant (where $Q$ is throughput). [2 marks]

For a batch centrifuge, the equivalent clarification time is derived from an exponential decay of particle concentration (refer Section 3.3 derivation):

$$t = \frac{\Sigma}{s \cdot g} \ln\!\left(\frac{x_0}{x_f}\right) \quad \text{[3 marks]}$$

**(b) Numerical [7 marks]:**

**Time required (original):**
