$$t = \frac{\Sigma}{s \cdot g} \ln\!\left(\frac{x_0}{x_f}\right) = \frac{5.0}{(1.0)(9.81)} \ln\!\left(\frac{0.10}{0.001}\right) = \frac{5.0}{9.81} \ln(100)$$

$$t = 0.5097 \times 4.6052 = 2.347 \, s \quad \text{[2 marks]}$$

(Wait — this seems too short; in practice, the "equivalent" sigma is divided by $g$ to keep it in $m^2$, but real $g$ is included, so the answer is in seconds, which matches the lab scale.)

**Factor by which $\omega$ must be increased** for half the time:

Since $t \propto \Sigma$ and $\Sigma \propto \omega^2$ (for fixed $r$):

$$t \propto \omega^2 \Rightarrow \frac{t_{new}}{t_{old}} = \left(\frac{\omega_{new}}{\omega_{old}}\right)^2$$

We need $t_{new} = t_{old}/2$:

$$\frac{1}{2} = \left(\frac{\omega_{new}}{\omega_{old}}\right)^2 \Rightarrow \frac{\omega_{new}}{\omega_{old}} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{2}} = 0.7071 \quad \text{[2 marks]}$$

Wait — this gives a *reduction*, not an increase! **Correction:** To **halve** the time, we must **increase** $\omega$:

$$\frac{t_{new}}{t_{old}} = \frac{1}{2} = \left(\frac{\omega_{new}}{\omega_{old}}\right)^2$$

This would require $\omega_{new} = \omega_{old} / \sqrt{0.5} = \omega_{old} \times \sqrt{2} = 1.414 \, \omega_{old}$

$$\boxed{\omega \text{ must be increased by a factor of } \sqrt{2} \approx 1.414 \quad \text{[3 marks]}}$$

(Re-derivation: $t \propto 1/\Sigma \propto 1/\omega^2$, so $t_2/t_1 = (\omega_1/\omega_2)^2 = 0.5$, giving $\omega_2 = \omega_1 \sqrt{2}$.)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch in ω:** Always convert **rpm → rad/s** via $\omega = 2\pi N/60$. A frequent error is plugging in rpm directly into $\omega^2 r$.
> 2. **Forgetting the radius term in RCF:** When asked for RCF, students often write $RCF = \omega^2 r / g$ but forget to specify *which radius* — for tubular bowls, RCF is conventionally reported at the **outer** wall $r_2$.
> 3. **Confusing $\Sigma$ with $V$:** $\Sigma$ is an **area** (m²), not a volume. Losing this 1 mark is a classic KTU evaluation pitfall.
> 4. **Stokes validity not checked:** Marks are deducted if you use $v_g = d_p^2 \Delta\rho \omega^2 r / 18\mu$ for large particles without verifying $Re < 1$.
> 5. **Sign of $\Delta\rho$:** If $\rho_p < \rho_f$ (e.g., lipid droplets in water), $v_g$ becomes *negative* — meaning the particle **floats** toward the axis! State this clearly in your answer.

---

## 🔁 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

- [x] **Centrifugal acceleration** $a_c = \omega^2 r$ is the cornerstone; it replaces $g$ in every settling equation.
- [x] **RCF = $\omega^2 r / g$** is a dimensionless multiplier (in "× g") that quantifies the enhancement over gravity.
- [x] **Stokes settling velocity in centrifugal field:** $v_g = d_p^2 \Delta\rho \, \omega^2 r / 18\mu$ — apply only when $Re < 1$.
- [x] **Three forces on a particle:** $F_c$ (outward), buoyancy (inward), Stokes drag (inward) — balance at terminal velocity.
- [x] **Sigma factor $\Sigma$** = equivalent gravity-settling area, depends only on geometry and $\omega$; central to scale-up.
- [x] **Sigma scale-up rule:** maintain $Q/\Sigma$ constant for identical separation at different scales.
- [x] **Equivalent clarification time (batch):** $t = (\Sigma / sg) \ln(x_0 / x_f)$.
- [x] **Reynolds check:** Always verify $Re = \rho_f v_g d_p / \mu < 1$ before applying Stokes; otherwise use $C_D \approx 18.5/Re^{0.6}$ (intermediate) or $C_D \approx 0.44$ (Newtonian).
- [x] **Density difference sign:** $\Delta\rho = \rho_p - \rho_f$ can be negative for buoyant particles (lipids, oil droplets, gas bubbles).
- [x] **Industrial rule-of-thumb RCFs:** 1,000–10,000× g (cells), 10,000–50,000× g (organelles), >100,000× g (viruses/macromolecules).
- [x] **Unit conversion:** $1 \, rpm = 2\pi/60 \, rad/s = 0.1047 \, rad/s$; $RCF = 1.118 \times 10^{-5} r_{cm} N_{rpm}^2$.
- [x] **Enhancement factor** is RCF, NOT $\omega^2 r$ alone — the latter has units, RCF is dimensionless.
- [x] **Stokes regime requires:** low $Re$, rigid spherical particle, no slip at surface, no particle-particle interaction (dilute suspension).
- [x] **KTU-favourite questions:** derive $v_g$, compute $\Sigma$, scale-up using $\Sigma$-ratio, calculate $t$ for batch clarification.
- [x] **Final sanity check:** always state units and check that final answer magnitudes are physically reasonable (e.g., a 1 µm cell at 10,000 × g should settle in seconds, not hours).

> **🎯 Last-line takeaway for KTU exams:** *Memorize $v_g = d_p^2 \Delta\rho \omega^2 r / 18\mu$, the definition of $\Sigma$, and the batch-time equation. These three equations, together with the Reynolds check, will fetch full marks on any 14-mark question on this topic.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Range of Centrifuges: Tubular Bowl, Basket, Scroll, Multi-chamber, and Disc Stack Centrifuges
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT525 - BIOSEPARATION TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Range of Centrifuges: Tubular Bowl, Basket, Scroll, Multi-chamber, and Disc Stack Centrifuges

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Centrifugation in Bioseparation — The Governing Principle

A **centrifuge** is a mechanical device that exploits the principle of **sedimentation under an enhanced gravitational field** to separate components of a mixture based on differences in **density, size, and shape** of the particles or phases. In a centrifugal field, a particle of mass *m* at a radial distance *r* rotating at angular velocity *ω* experiences a centrifugal force many times greater than Earth's gravity.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024):** A centrifuge is an apparatus that applies a controlled centrifugal force field (typically 10³ to 10⁶ × *g*) to a fluid mixture, accelerating the natural sedimentation of dispersed phases (solids, droplets, cells, macromolecules) so that bioseparation can be achieved rapidly and efficiently at industrial scale.

**Key Performance Metric — Relative Centrifugal Force (RCF):**

$$RCF = \frac{\omega^2 \, r}{g} = 1.118 \times 10^{-5} \cdot r \cdot (rpm)^2$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU 2024 scheme specifically classifies industrial centrifuges into **sedimenting (solid-bowl)** and **filtering (perforated-bowl)** types, and further subdivides them by geometry: **Tubular, Basket, Scroll (Decanter), Multi-chamber, and Disc Stack.** Students must be able to identify each type, its principle, application, and the design equation for scale-up (the **Σ-theory**).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Spinning Salad Spinner"

Imagine you are washing lettuce leaves in a bowl of water. If you let the bowl sit still, the water drains very slowly and the grit falls to the bottom only after minutes. Now imagine you swing the bowl rapidly in a circular motion over your head (like a salad spinner). The water is flung outward almost instantly, the grit is pressed firmly against the walls, and the leaves emerge dry in seconds.

> [!TIP]
> **Intuition Check:** A centrifuge is essentially a **"gravity multiplier"**. The spinning wall *replaces* gravity as the driving force. Heavier (denser) particles get flung outward faster; lighter particles (or the liquid) stay closer to the centre. The geometry of the bowl — tall tube, basket, screw, concentric chambers, or stacked discs — determines **how efficiently** this separation happens for a given feed.

## 1.3 The Five Centrifuge Types at a Glance

| Centrifuge Type | Bowl Geometry | Primary Separation | Typical Operating *g*-Force |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Tubular Bowl** | Tall, hollow cylindrical tube | Liquid–liquid, solid–liquid clarification | 15,000 – 50,000 × *g* |
| **Basket** | Perforated cylindrical wall (filter) | Solid–liquid (filtering) | 500 – 3,000 × *g* |
| **Scroll / Decanter** | Cylindrical + internal helical screw | Solid–liquid (continuous) | 1,000 – 4,000 × *g* |
| **Multi-chamber** | Several nested concentric chambers | Liquid–liquid, solid–liquid | 5,000 – 8,000 × *g* |
| **Disc Stack** | Stack of angled conical discs | Liquid–liquid, solid–liquid | 5,000 – 15,000 × *g* |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Underlying Sedimentation Theory

When a particle of diameter *d* and density $\rho_p$ is suspended in a fluid of density $\rho_f$ and viscosity $\mu$, and subjected to a centrifugal field, it migrates radially with a settling velocity given by **Stokes' Law in a centrifugal field**:

$$v_s = \frac{d^2 \, (\rho_p - \rho_f) \, \omega^2 \, r}{18 \, \mu}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The settling velocity in a centrifuge is **not constant** — it increases with the radial distance *r* because the centrifugal force field is stronger near the bowl wall. This is a critical difference from gravity settling.

The time *t* required for a particle initially at radius $r_1$ to reach the wall at radius $r_2$ is obtained by integrating $dt = dr / v_s$:

$$t = \frac{18 \, \mu \, \ln\!\left(\dfrac{R_2}{R_1}\right)}{d^2 \, (\rho_p - \rho_f) \, \omega^2}$$

## 2.2 Sigma (Σ) Theory — The Cornerstone of Centrifuge Design

**Ambler's Σ-factor** is the most important design concept for KTU examinations. Σ has the dimension of area (m²) and represents the **equivalent cross-sectional area of a gravity settler that would give the same settling performance** as the centrifuge.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** Two centrifuges give the same separation performance if they have the same Σ-factor operating at the same *g*-force. This allows **scale-up** from lab to plant.

The general relationship between volumetric throughput *Q* and Σ is:

$$Q = v_g \cdot \Sigma \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad Q = \frac{\omega^2 \, r \, d^2 (\rho_p - \rho_f)}{18 \mu} \cdot \Sigma$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** The Σ-theory allows engineers to predict plant-scale throughput using pilot-scale data. If a lab centrifuge has Σ_lab and a production centrifuge has Σ_plant, the throughput scales linearly with the Σ-ratio at the same clarification efficiency.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description / Application |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $RCF = 1.118 \times 10^{-5} \cdot r \cdot N^2$ | Relative Centrifugal Force (with N in rpm, r in cm) |
| 2 | $F_c = m \, \omega^2 \, r$ | Centrifugal force on a particle |
| 3 | $v_s = \dfrac{d^2 (\rho_p - \rho_f) \, \omega^2 \, r}{18 \mu}$ | Stokes' settling velocity in centrifugal field |
| 4 | $t = \dfrac{18 \mu \, \ln(R_2/R_1)}{d^2 (\rho_p - \rho_f) \omega^2}$ | Residence time for complete sedimentation |
| 5 | $Q = v_g \cdot \Sigma$ | Throughput–Sigma relationship |
| 6 | $\Sigma_{tube} = \dfrac{2 \pi l \, \omega^2}{g} \cdot \dfrac{R_2^2 - R_1^2}{2}$ | Sigma for Tubular Bowl |
| 7 | $\Sigma_{disc} = \dfrac{2 \pi n \, \omega^2}{3 g} (R_o^3 - R_i^3) \cot\theta$ | Sigma for Disc Stack (n = number of discs) |
| 8 | $\Sigma_{basket} = \dfrac{\pi \, l \, \omega^2}{g}(R^2 - r_w^2)$ | Sigma for Basket (filtering) |
| 9 | $\Sigma_{multi} = \dfrac{2 \pi l \, \omega^2}{g} \sum_{k} (R_{k+1}^2 - R_k^2)$ | Sigma for Multi-chamber (k = chamber index) |

> [!NOTE]
> In the disc-stack formula, $\theta$ is the **half-cone angle** of the disc (typically 30°–45°), and $R_o$, $R_i$ are the outer and inner disc radii. The factor $\cot\theta$ arises from the geometry of the disc gap.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

* **Vaccine production:** Disc-stack centrifuges harvest *Saccharomyces* or *E. coli* cells from fermentation broths at 8,000–12,000 × *g*.
* **Blood plasma fractionation:** Tubular-bowl centrifuges separate plasma from red cells at >15,000 × *g*.
* **Edible oil refining:** Disc-stack centrifuges de-gum, neutralise, and de-wax vegetable oils.
* **Sewage sludge dewatering:** Scroll/Decanter centrifuges continuously dewater biosolids to ~25% dry solids.
* **Antibiotic recovery:** Basket centrifuges separate crystalline products (e.g., penicillin, erythromycin) from mother liquor.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Sigma Factor for a Disc-Stack Centrifuge

Consider a disc-stack centrifuge with *n* conical discs, each making a half-angle $\theta$ with the vertical axis. A particle must travel from the inner radius $R_i$ to the outer radius $R_o$ along the underside of a disc to be captured.

**Step 1 — Settling along the disc face.**
The effective radial path length is $(R_o - R_i)$. The actual path along the inclined face is:

$$L = \frac{R_o - R_i}{\sin\theta}$$

**Step 2 — Effective centrifugal field component along the disc.**
The radial component of settling velocity $v_s$ is:

$$v_{s,radial} = v_s \cdot \sin\theta = \frac{d^2 (\rho_p - \rho_f) \omega^2 r}{18 \mu} \sin\theta$$

**Step 3 — Time for a particle to traverse one disc gap.**

$$t_{disc} = \frac{R_o - R_i}{v_{s,radial}} = \frac{18 \mu (R_o - R_i)}{d^2 (\rho_p - \rho_f) \omega^2 r \sin\theta}$$

**Step 4 — Feed volumetric flow per disc gap.**
For a thin annular gap of height $h = h_{gap}$ (gap between two adjacent discs), the volumetric flow carried by one disc is:

$$Q_{disc} = v_s \cdot \cos\theta \cdot 2 \pi r \cdot h$$

Substituting $v_s$ and integrating across the disc annulus gives the **classical disc-stack Σ-formula**:

$$\boxed{\Sigma_{disc} = \frac{2 \pi n \, \omega^2}{3 g} \,(R_o^3 - R_i^3) \, \cot\theta}$$

**Step 5 — Engineering interpretation.**
The Σ grows with the **cube of disc radius** (so larger discs dominate), the **number of discs** (more discs = shorter path = better capture), and the **square of angular speed** (doubling rpm quadruples Σ). Reducing the cone angle (making discs shallower) increases Σ by the $\cot\theta$ factor.

## 3.2 Derivation: Sigma Factor for a Tubular Bowl Centrifuge

The tubular bowl is a tall hollow cylinder of length *l* with inner liquid surface at radius $R_1$ and outer wall at radius $R_2$. Particles entering the bottom travel axially upward at the liquid velocity $u$ while being flung outward.

**Step 1 — Radial settling equation:**

$$\frac{dr}{dt} = \frac{d^2 (\rho_p - \rho_f) \omega^2 r}{18 \mu}$$

**Step 2 — Separation condition.**
A particle will be captured if its residence time $t_{res} = l/u$ equals the radial settling time.

**Step 3 — Integrate the radial equation:**

$$\int_{R_1}^{R_2} \frac{dr}{r} = \frac{d^2 (\rho_p - \rho_f) \omega^2}{18 \mu} \cdot t_{res}$$

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{R_2}{R_1}\right) = \frac{d^2 (\rho_p - \rho_f) \omega^2 \, t_{res}}{18 \mu}$$

**Step 4 — Identify Sigma.**
Equating the throughput $Q = \pi (R_2^2 - R_1^2) \cdot u / 2$ with $Q = v_g \cdot \Sigma$:

$$\Sigma_{tube} = \frac{2 \pi l \, \omega^2}{g} \cdot \frac{R_2^2 - R_1^2}{2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on $R_1$:** In the strict sense, $R_1$ is the radius of the inner **liquid surface** (free surface of the swirling liquid), not the inner wall. For a tubular bowl, $R_1 \approx 0$ is not used; typically $R_1/R_2 \approx 0.4$–$0.6$ to leave an air core.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Performance of the Five Centrifuges

| Parameter | Tubular Bowl | Basket | Scroll (Decanter) | Multi-chamber | Disc Stack |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Bowl speed (rpm)** | 15,000 – 50,000 | 600 – 4,000 | 1,500 – 6,000 | 4,000 – 8,000 | 5,000 – 12,000 |
| **Typical *g*-force** | 15,000 – 50,000 × *g* | 500 – 3,000 × *g* | 1,000 – 4,000 × *g* | 5,000 – 8,000 × *g* | 5,000 – 15,000 × *g* |
| **Mode of operation** | Batch / semi-batch | Batch | Continuous | Batch / continuous | Continuous |
| **Solids capacity** | Very low (<1%) | Moderate (5–15%) | High (20–60%) | Low (1–5%) | Moderate (2–15%) |
| **Particle size cut-off** | 0.1 – 5 µm | 5 – 500 µm | 10 – 1000 µm | 1 – 50 µm | 0.5 – 50 µm |
| **Sigma scaling** | $\propto l \, \omega^2 \, R^2$ | $\propto l \, \omega^2 \, R^2$ | Complex (geometry) | $\propto l \, \omega^2 \sum R^2$ | $\propto n \, \omega^2 \, R_o^3 \cot\theta$ |
| **Typical use** | Plasma fractionation, vaccine clarification | Salt/sugar separation, crystal recovery | Sludge dewatering, fermentation broth | Two-phase liquid separation | Cell harvest, oil refining, beer clarification |
| **Cleaning** | Easy (open bowl) | Moderate | Moderate | Difficult (chambers) | Easy (self-cleaning versions) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Five Centrifuge Working Principles

The following Mermaid flow diagrams map the functional flow of each centrifuge type, illustrating the path of feed, solids, and clarified liquid in a structured manner.

### 4.1.1 Tubular Bowl Centrifuge

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Feed Inlet at Bottom] --> B[High-Speed Hollow Cylindrical Bowl]
    B --> C[Liquid Swirls Upward]
    C --> D[Heavier Solids / Heavy Liquid Pinned to Wall]
    D --> E[Sliding Down to Sludge Collection]
    C --> F[Light Liquid Overflows Top via Skimmer]
    F --> G[Light Phase Outlet]
    E --> H[Sludge Discharge when Bowl Stops]
    B --> I[Angular Velocity omega 15000 to 50000 rpm]
```

### 4.1.2 Basket Centrifuge

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Feed Inlet from Top] --> B[Perforated Cylindrical Basket]
    B --> C[Liquid Passes Through Filter Media]
    C --> D[Filtrate Collected in Outer Casing]
    D --> E[Filtrate Outlet]
    B --> F[Solids Retained as Cake on Basket Wall]
    F --> G[Washing of Cake Optional]
    G --> H[Spinning Drying of Cake]
    H --> I[Plough / Scraper Removes Cake at Low Speed]
    I --> J[Solids Discharge Chute]
```

### 4.1.3 Scroll / Decanter Centrifuge

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Feed Slurry Inlet] --> B[Horizontal Rotating Conical Cylindrical Bowl]
    B --> C[Inner Helical Scroll / Screw Conveyor]
    C --> D[Scroll Rotates at Differential Speed Delta omega]
    D --> E[Solids Transported Toward Conical End]
    E --> F[Solids Discharge at Small End / Beach]
    B --> G[Liquid Moves Toward Cylindrical End]
    G --> H[Clarified Liquid Overflows at Large End]
    F --> I[Dewatered Solids Cake]
    H --> J[Centrate Outlet]
```

### 4.1.4 Multi-chamber Centrifuge

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Feed Inlet at Centre Bottom] --> B[Innermost Chamber Radius R1]
    B --> C[Particle Settles on Chamber Wall]
    C --> D[Liquid Overflows to Next Chamber R2]
    D --> E[Particle Settles on Outer Chamber Wall]
    E --> F[Subsequent Chambers R3 to Rn]
    F --> G[Final Overflow Exit at Top]
    G --> H[Clarified Liquid Outlet]
    B --> I[Heaviest Solids in Innermost Chamber]
    F --> J[Lighter Solids in Outer Chambers]
    I --> K[Manual / Periodic Solids Removal]
```

### 4.1.5 Disc Stack Centrifuge

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Feed Inlet at Centre] --> B[Distributor and Central Feed Pipe]
    B --> C[Feed Exits into Disc Stack Region]
    C --> D[Conical Disc Stack with n Discs]
    D --> E[Particles Slide Along Underside of Disc]
    E --> F[Particles Ejected to Bowl Periphery]
    F --> G[Solids Collection in Sludge Space]
    C --> H[Clarified Liquid Moves Inward to Axis]
    H --> I[Light Phase / Clarified Liquid Outlet at Top]
    G --> J[Solids Discharge - Intermittent or Continuous]
    J --> K{Ejector Type}
    K --> L[Self-Cleaning Ejector at High Speed]
    K --> M[Nozzle Discharge for Liquids with Solids]
    K --> N[Manual Discharge Hermetic Design]
```

## 4.2 Comparative Functional Architecture — Selection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Feed Mixture Type] --> B{Solid or Liquid-Liquid?}
    B -->|Solid-Liquid Filtration| C[Filtration Type?]
    C -->|Batch| D[Basket Centrifuge]
    C -->|Continuous| E[Scroll / Decanter Centrifuge]
    B -->|Solid-Liquid Clarification| F{Solids Content}
    F -->|Very Low <1 percent| G[Tubular Bowl Centrifuge]
    F -->|Low 1 to 5 percent| H[Multi-chamber Centrifuge]
    F -->|Moderate 2 to 15 percent| I[Disc Stack Centrifuge]
    B -->|Liquid-Liquid Separation| J{Density Difference}
    J -->|Large 5 percent or More| G
    J -->|Small 1 to 5 percent| I
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**List the various types of centrifuges used in bioseparation technology. Write the working principle of a disc-stack centrifuge.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The various types of centrifuges used in bioseparation technology are:
1. Tubular-bowl centrifuge
2. Basket (filter) centrifuge
3. Scroll / Decanter centrifuge
4. Multi-chamber centrifuge
5. Disc-stack centrifuge

**Working principle of disc-stack centrifuge (2 marks):**
A disc-stack centrifuge contains a stack of conical discs separated by a small gap (~0.5–2 mm) and inclined at an angle (typically 30°–45°) to the axis of rotation. Feed enters through a central pipe and is distributed to the disc stack. Under high centrifugal force, solid particles (or the heavier liquid phase) sediment outward along the underside of each disc and are thrown to the bowl periphery where they collect as sludge. The clarified (or lighter) liquid moves inward and exits at the top through a centripetal pump. The discs dramatically reduce the settling path (from metres to millimetres), enabling very high clarification efficiency at moderate *g*-forces.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define the Sigma factor of a centrifuge. Mention its significance.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **Sigma factor (Σ)** of a centrifuge is defined as the equivalent cross-sectional area of a perfect gravity settling tank that would produce the same solids-removal efficiency as the centrifuge under otherwise identical conditions. It has units of area (m²) and is determined by the geometry and speed of the bowl.

**Significance:**
1. Σ enables **direct comparison** of the separation performance of centrifuges of different geometry and size.
2. It is the **key design parameter** for scale-up — if a lab unit gives good clarification at flow rate $Q_{lab}$, a plant unit with Σ-ratio times larger will achieve the same clarification at proportionally higher $Q_{plant}$.
3. The throughput equation $Q = v_g \cdot \Sigma$ links the centrifuge's separation capability to the Stokes settling velocity $v_g$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]` — Choice A

**(a) [7 marks]** With the aid of a neat diagram, describe the construction and working of a **tubular-bowl centrifuge**. Discuss its applications in the bioprocess industry.

**(b) [7 marks]** A tubular-bowl centrifuge has a bowl of length *l* = 100 cm, outer wall radius $R_2$ = 5 cm and inner liquid-surface radius $R_1$ = 2 cm. It rotates at 20,000 rpm. Calculate:
(i) the centrifugal acceleration at the bowl wall,
(ii) the relative centrifugal force (RCF),
(iii) the sigma factor of the centrifuge.

#### Model Solution (a)
**Construction:** A tubular-bowl centrifuge consists of a tall, narrow, hollow cylindrical bowl (height >> diameter, length-to-diameter ratio ~ 6–8), usually made of stainless steel. The bowl is suspended from a flexible spindle driven by a motor. The feed enters from the bottom through a stationary nozzle, and clarified liquid (or the light phase) overflows from the top through a skimmer. The bowl has no internal parts; it is the high rotational speed that produces the centrifugal field.

**Working:** The feed slurry is accelerated into the bowl through bottom feed tubes. Centrifugal force flings denser solids (or the heavier liquid phase) against the cylindrical wall where they form a thin layer. The clarified (or lighter) liquid moves inward and rises to overflow through the top. In a liquid–liquid separation, the two phases form concentric annular layers inside the bowl — heavy phase outside, light phase inside — and both are discharged through separate top exits.

**Applications:**
1. Clarification of vaccines, sera, and bacterial cultures.
2. Separation of blood components (plasma, platelets, red cells).
3. Purification of vegetable oils and beverages.
4. Recovery of yeast from fermentation broth.
5. Separation of immiscible organic–aqueous solvent mixtures in extraction processes.

**Diagram (Text Schematic):**

```
       Light Phase Outlet
                |
                v
   |‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾|   <-- Bowl top (overflow)
   |                 |
   |   LIGHT PHASE   |   <-- inner annular layer
   |   (skimmer)     |
   |   --------------|   <-- interface
   |   HEAVY PHASE   |   <-- outer annular layer
   |   ------------  |
   |   SLUDGE        |   <-- pinned to wall
   |   =====         |
   |   =====         |
   |_________________|   <-- feed inlet at bottom
        BOWL (rotating at 15,000-50,000 rpm)
```

#### Model Solution (b)

**Given:** $l = 100$ cm = 1.0 m; $R_2 = 5$ cm = 0.05 m; $R_1 = 2$ cm = 0.02 m; $N$ = 20,000 rpm.

**(i) Centrifugal acceleration at the bowl wall:**

Convert rpm to rad/s:

$$\omega = \frac{2 \pi N}{60} = \frac{2 \pi \times 20{,}000}{60} = 2094.4 \text{ rad/s}$$

Centrifugal acceleration at $r = R_2$:

$$a_c = \omega^2 \, R_2 = (2094.4)^2 \times 0.05$$

$$a_c = 4.386 \times 10^6 \times 0.05 = 2.193 \times 10^5 \text{ m/s}^2$$

**(ii) Relative Centrifugal Force (RCF):**

$$RCF = \frac{a_c}{g} = \frac{2.193 \times 10^5}{9.81} = 22{,}358 \approx 22{,}400 \times g$$

*Alternative using the formula* $RCF = 1.118 \times 10^{-5} \cdot r \cdot N^2$ with $r$ in cm:

$$RCF = 1.118 \times 10^{-5} \times 5 \times (20{,}000)^2 = 1.118 \times 10^{-5} \times 5 \times 4 \times 10^8$$

$$RCF = 22{,}360 \times g \quad \checkmark$$

**(iii) Sigma factor:**

$$\Sigma = \frac{2 \pi l \, \omega^2}{g} \cdot \frac{R_2^2 - R_1^2}{2}$$

$$R_2^2 - R_1^2 = (0.05)^2 - (0.02)^2 = 0.0025 - 0.0004 = 0.0021 \text{ m}^2$$

$$\frac{R_2^2 - R_1^2}{2} = 0.00105 \text{ m}^2$$

$$\Sigma = \frac{2 \pi \times 1.0 \times (2094.4)^2}{9.81} \times 0.00105$$

$$\Sigma = \frac{6.2832 \times 4.386 \times 10^6}{9.81} \times 0.00105$$

$$\Sigma = \frac{2.756 \times 10^7}{9.81} \times 0.00105 = 2.809 \times 10^6 \times 0.00105$$

$$\boxed{\Sigma \approx 2950 \text{ m}^2}$$

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]` — Choice B

**(a) [7 marks]** Describe with a neat sketch the construction, working, and applications of the **disc-stack centrifuge**. Derive the expression for its sigma factor.

**(b) [7 marks]** A disc-stack centrifuge has 60 conical discs with outer radius $R_o$ = 15 cm, inner radius $R_i$ = 5 cm, and half-cone angle $\theta$ = 35°. It rotates at 8,000 rpm. Calculate its sigma factor and the maximum volumetric throughput for a feed containing particles of diameter 5 µm, density 1,050 kg/m³, in water (ρ = 1,000 kg/m³, μ = 1 × 10⁻³ Pa·s).

#### Model Solution (a)
**Construction:** A disc-stack centrifuge consists of a vertically-mounted bowl containing a pack of closely-spaced conical discs. Each disc is typically made of sheet metal, separated from adjacent discs by spacer strips, and angled at 30°–50° to the vertical axis. The bowl is driven from below by a vertical shaft. Feed enters through a central pipe, and the clarified liquid exits at the top. The sludge collects at the bowl periphery and is discharged either manually, through a self-cleaning ejector, or via peripheral nozzles.

**Working:** The feed is distributed into the disc stack. The discs split the fluid into thin layers, dramatically reducing the sedimentation distance a particle must travel. Under high centrifugal force, particles (or the heavier liquid phase) sediment outward along the underside of each disc, get collected at the bowl wall, and slide down to the sludge space. The clarified (or lighter) liquid moves inward between the discs and rises to the top where it is discharged under pressure by a centripetal pump.

**Applications:** (i) Cell harvesting from fermentation broth, (ii) clarification of fruit juices, beer, and wine, (iii) refining of edible oils, (iv) purification of vaccines and plasma, (v) separation of cream from milk, (vi) catalyst recovery.

**Derivation of Σ:** (As derived in §3.1 above.) Final result:

$$\Sigma_{disc} = \frac{2 \pi n \, \omega^2}{3 g} (R_o^3 - R_i^3) \cot\theta$$

**Diagram (Text Schematic):**

```
              |‾‾‾‾‾‾‾|  <-- Clarified Liquid Outlet
              |         |
              |  /  /  /|  <-- Disc stack (angled conical)
              | /  /  / |
              |/  /  /  |
              |  /  /  /|
              | /  /  / |
              |/  /  /  |
              |==========| <-- Sludge space
              |__________| <-- Feed inlet (central pipe)
                   ||
```

#### Model Solution (b)

**Given:** $n$ = 60 discs; $R_o$ = 0.15 m; $R_i$ = 0.05 m; $\theta$ = 35°; $N$ = 8,000 rpm; $d_p$ = 5 × 10⁻⁶ m; $\rho_p$ = 1,050 kg/m³; $\rho_f$ = 1,000 kg/m³; $\mu$ = 1 × 10⁻³ Pa·s.

**Step 1 — Angular velocity:**

$$\omega = \frac{2 \pi \times 8{,}000}{60} = 837.76 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 2 — Sigma factor:**

Compute $R_o^3 - R_i^3$:

$$R_o^3 = (0.15)^3 = 3.375 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3$$

$$R_i^3 = (0.05)^3 = 1.25 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3$$

$$R_o^3 - R_i^3 = 3.375 \times 10^{-3} - 0.125 \times 10^{-3} = 3.25 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3$$

Compute $\cot 35°$:

$$\cot 35° = \frac{1}{\tan 35°} = \frac{1}{0.7002} = 1.4281$$

Compute the main bracket:

$$\frac{2 \pi n \, \omega^2}{3 g} = \frac{2 \pi \times 60 \times (837.76)^2}{3 \times 9.81}$$

$$= \frac{376.99 \times 7.018 \times 10^5}{29.43} = \frac{2.645 \times 10^8}{29.43} = 8.988 \times 10^6 \text{ m}^{-1}$$

Therefore:

$$\Sigma = 8.988 \times 10^6 \times 3.25 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.4281$$

$$\boxed{\Sigma \approx 41{,}720 \text{ m}^2 \approx 4.17 \times 10^4 \text{ m}^2}$$

**Step 3 — Maximum volumetric throughput.**
The Stokes gravitational settling velocity (equivalent gravity term) for the particle is:

$$v_g = \frac{d_p^2 \, (\rho_p - \rho_f) \, g}{18 \, \mu}$$

$$v_g = \frac{(5 \times 10^{-6})^2 \times (1{,}050 - 1{,}000) \times 9.81}{18 \times 1 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$v_g = \frac{25 \times 10^{-12} \times 50 \times 9.81}{18 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{1.226 \times 10^{-8}}{1.8 \times 10^{-2}}$$

$$v_g = 6.81 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 4 — Throughput:**

$$Q = v_g \cdot \Sigma = 6.81 \times 10^{-7} \times 4.172 \times 10^4$$

$$\boxed{Q \approx 2.84 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} \approx 28.4 \text{ L/s} \approx 1{,}700 \text{ L/min}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit conversion errors:** *R* must be in metres and *ω* in rad/s when computing $\omega^2 R$ in SI units. Mixing cm and m is the most common mistake.
> 2. **Forgetting the $\cot\theta$ factor** in the disc-stack Σ formula — examiners specifically test this.
> 3. **Using $(R_o - R_i)$ instead of $(R_o^3 - R_i^3)$** in the disc formula; the cubic dependence on radius is a frequent trap.
> 4. **Converting $N$ (rpm) to $\omega$ (rad/s) inconsistently** — always use $\omega = 2\pi N / 60$.
> 5. **Ignoring the $(n - 1)$ vs $n$ correction** in the disc formula — for the simplified KTU formula, $n$ is the number of **gaps** (equal to number of discs minus one). Either convention is acceptable if stated explicitly.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

* **Centrifuge selection** is governed by (i) solids content of feed, (ii) particle size, (iii) density difference, (iv) desired product purity, and (v) whether the mode is **batch or continuous**.
* **Tubular bowl:** Tall, high-speed bowl used for **clarification of dilute feeds** (vaccines, plasma). Highest *g*-force. Σ proportional to $l \omega^2 R^2$. Cannot handle high solids — bowl must be stopped and cleaned periodically.
* **Basket centrifuge:** Perforated wall acts as a **filter**; used when solids retention as a cake is the goal (crystals, salts). Limited to relatively coarse particles. Operates in batch.
* **Scroll / Decanter:** Has an **internal screw conveyor** rotating at a differential speed relative to the bowl. Continuous, handles high-solids slurries, used for **sludge dewatering** and recovery of insoluble products.
* **Multi-chamber centrifuge:** Contains several **concentric chambers** of progressively larger radii. Allows staged liquid–liquid separation and the heaviest particles are collected in the innermost chamber. Difficult to clean.
* **Disc stack:** Stack of conical discs **shortens the settling path** and multiplies the effective area. Σ grows with $n$, $R_o^3$, and $\omega^2$. Most widely used industrial centrifuge in bioprocessing; available in **nozzle, self-cleaning (ejector), and hermetic** designs.
* **Sigma (Σ) factor:** Has the **dimensions of area** (m²). It is the most important **scale-up parameter** — doubling Σ doubles throughput at the same clarification efficiency.
* **Relative Centrifugal Force (RCF):** $RCF = 1.118 \times 10^{-5} \cdot r_{cm} \cdot N^2$. Always quote centrifuge performance in *g*-forces, not just rpm.
* **Stokes' law in a centrifuge** differs from gravity settling in that the settling velocity **increases with radial position**, so the integration gives the **logarithmic residence-time equation**.
* **Throughput–Sigma equation:** $Q = v_g \cdot \Sigma$ — the **workhorse equation** for all centrifuge design problems in KTU examinations.
* For the **disc-stack**, the **half-cone angle** is typically 30°–45°; shallower angles ($\theta$ small) give larger Σ by the $\cot\theta$ factor but require narrower disc gaps.
* For **scale-up** from lab to plant, keep $v_g$ and clarification efficiency constant and scale the throughput $Q$ **proportionally to Σ**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Filtration: General Filtration Theory
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT525 - BIOSEPARATION TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Filtration: General Filtration Theory

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Filtration** is a mechanical, physical separation operation employed to separate an insoluble solid phase (suspended particles) from a fluid (liquid or gas) phase by forcing the mixture through a porous medium (filter medium) that retains the solids and allows the fluid (filtrate) to pass through.

In **Bioseparation Technology**, filtration is the most commonly used primary recovery step for removing **biomass, cell debris, precipitated proteins, and other insoluble matter** from fermentation broths, cell lysates, or reaction mixtures. It is a **solid–fluid separation** process governed by the principles of fluid flow through porous media.

**General Classification of Filtration (KTU Syllabus Mapping):**

| Class | Mechanism | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Surface Filtration** | Particles retained on the medium's surface | Sterile filtration, membrane microfiltration |
| **Depth Filtration** | Particles trapped within the medium's depth | Clarification of broths, pre-filtration |
| **Cake Filtration** | Particles accumulate as a porous cake on the medium | Most industrial bioprocess filtrations |
| **Crossflow Filtration** | Tangential flow limits cake buildup | Continuous bioprocessing, harvest |

> [!NOTE]
> In bioprocess engineering, **cake filtration** dominates the post-fermentation recovery of cells and insolubles because the slurry typically contains a measurable solids concentration (1–10 % w/v) that forms a visible filter cake.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine making coffee with a paper filter at home:
- The **ground coffee + water mixture (slurry)** is poured onto the filter paper.
- The **filter paper (filter medium)** acts as a barrier that physically blocks the coffee grounds.
- The clear **liquid coffee (filtrate)** drips through, while the wet grounds (filter cake) stay behind.
- As more slurry is poured, the layer of grounds grows thicker — this is your **filter cake**, which itself starts to act as a fine filter, increasing resistance to flow.

In a bioprocess, instead of coffee, we have **fermentation broth with microbial cells or precipitated proteins**, and the "filter paper" is replaced by industrial media such as **porous ceramics, sintered metals, woven cloth, or polymeric membranes**. The fundamental physics — flow of liquid through a porous bed under a pressure driving force — is identical.

### 1.3 Key Metrics and Constants

- **Driving force (ΔP)** in **Pa** or **bar** (1 bar = 10⁵ Pa).
- **Filtrate viscosity (μ)** in **Pa·s** (for water at 20 °C, μ ≈ 1.002 × 10⁻³ Pa·s).
- **Specific cake resistance (α)** in **m/kg** (typical for biological cakes: 10¹¹ – 10¹³ m/kg).
- **Cake dry solids mass per unit filtrate volume (c)** in **kg/m³**.
- **Filter medium resistance (Rm)** in **m⁻¹** (typically 10⁹ – 10¹¹ m⁻¹ for bioprocess media).
- **Porosity of cake (ε)** — dimensionless, typically **0.4 – 0.6**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Darcy's Law** is the foundational equation for all quantitative filtration analysis and must be memorized:  

$$ \frac{dV}{dt} = \frac{A \cdot \Delta P}{\mu \cdot (R_c + R_m)} $$

where $V$ is filtrate volume, $t$ is time, $A$ is filtration area, $R_c$ is cake resistance, and $R_m$ is medium resistance.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Framework — Stepwise Logic

Filtration theory is constructed upon the following logical sequence:

**Step 1 — Establish the driving force.**
The fluid moves through the porous bed because of a **pressure differential (ΔP)** across the filter medium. This may be generated by:
- **Pressure filtration** — feed side pressurized (e.g., plate-and-frame filter press).
- **Vacuum filtration** — filtrate side under vacuum (e.g., rotary drum filter).
- **Centrifugal filtration** — centrifugal force provides ΔP (e.g., perforated basket centrifuge).

**Step 2 — Quantify the resistance.**
Two resistances appear in series:
- **Filter medium resistance (Rm)** — intrinsic property of the clean medium, treated as a constant.
- **Filter cake resistance (Rc)** — grows with filtrate volume because cake thickness increases linearly with $V$ (assuming uniform cake).

**Step 3 — Relate cake resistance to solids deposited.**
For a cake of uniform concentration, the mass of solids per unit filtrate volume is a constant, $c$ (kg dry solids per m³ filtrate). The cake resistance is:

$$ R_c = \alpha \cdot \frac{c \cdot V}{A} = \alpha \cdot \nu \cdot c \cdot \frac{V}{A} $$

where $\nu$ is the volume fraction of wet cake solids (often taken as 1 for dilute biological cakes).

**Step 4 — Introduce specific cake resistance (α).**
The parameter **α** is the cake resistance per unit mass of solids deposited. It captures the cake's internal geometry: particle size, shape, porosity, and compressibility.

**Step 5 — Kozeny–Carman equation** links α to particle-level properties for **incompressible** (rigid) cakes:

$$ \alpha = \frac{150 \cdot (1 - \varepsilon)}{\varepsilon^{3} \cdot d_p^{2} \cdot \rho_s} $$

where $d_p$ is particle diameter, $\rho_s$ is solids density, and $\varepsilon$ is cake porosity.

**Step 6 — Compressibility correction.**
Most biological cakes are **compressible** — their porosity decreases under ΔP. The empirical power-law form is:

$$ \alpha = \alpha_0 \cdot (\Delta P)^{n} $$

where $n$ is the **compressibility index** ($0 \le n \le 1$); $n = 0$ is incompressible, $n \to 1$ is highly compressible.

**Step 7 — Mode of operation.**
- **Constant-pressure filtration (CPF):** ΔP is fixed, dV/dt decreases as cake builds.
- **Constant-rate filtration (CRF):** dV/dt is fixed, ΔP rises with cake buildup (used with positive-displacement pumps).

### 2.2 Real-World Engineering Utility

- Designing **plate-and-frame filter presses** for harvesting microbial cells.
- Sizing **rotary vacuum drum filters** for continuous broth clarification.
- Specifying **membrane area** for crossflow microfiltration in monoclonal antibody manufacture.
- Optimizing **filter aid (diatomaceous earth, perlite) addition** in antibiotic broth clarification.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Use / Meaning | Key Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\dfrac{dV}{dt} = \dfrac{A \cdot \Delta P}{\mu \cdot (R_c + R_m)}$ | Darcy's Law (basic) — instantaneous filtrate flow | m³/s |
| 2 | $R_c = \alpha \cdot \dfrac{c \cdot V}{A}$ | Cake resistance proportional to solids deposited | m⁻¹ |
| 3 | $\alpha = \alpha_0 (\Delta P)^{n}$ | Compressibility power-law | m/kg |
| 4 | $\alpha = \dfrac{150(1-\varepsilon)}{\varepsilon^{3} d_p^{2} \rho_s}$ | Kozeny–Carman (incompressible cake) | m/kg |
| 5 | $\dfrac{dV}{dt} = \dfrac{A^{2} \Delta P}{\mu(\alpha c V + A R_m)}$ | Darcy's Law with $R_c$ substituted | m³/s |
| 6 | $(V + V_m)^{2} = K \cdot A^{2} \cdot t$ | Constant-pressure integrated form | m⁶, s |
| 7 | $K = \dfrac{2 \Delta P}{\mu \cdot \alpha \cdot c}$ | Filtration constant (CPF) | m²/s |
| 8 | $V_m = \dfrac{A \cdot R_m}{\alpha \cdot c}$ | Equivalent filtrate volume of medium | m³ |
| 9 | $\dfrac{t}{V} = \dfrac{\mu \alpha c}{2 A^{2} \Delta P} V + \dfrac{\mu R_m}{A \Delta P}$ | Linear form for CPF (plot $t/V$ vs $V$) | s/m³ |
| 10 | $V^{2} + V V_m = \dfrac{A^{2} \Delta P}{\mu \alpha c} t$ | Equivalent form, used to extract $V_m$ & $K$ | m⁶, s |
| 11 | $\dfrac{t}{V} = \dfrac{\mu \alpha c}{2 A^{2}} V + \dfrac{\mu R_m}{A^{2}}$ | Constant-rate filtration (CRF) plot | s/m³ |
| 12 | $R_w = \alpha_w \cdot \dfrac{c_w \cdot V_w}{A}$ | Cake resistance during washing | m⁻¹ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memory tip for KTU:** The linearised form **t/V = (μ α c / 2 A² ΔP) · V + μ Rm / (A ΔP)** gives a straight line when $t/V$ is plotted against $V$. The **slope** yields $\alpha$ and the **intercept** yields $R_m$. This appears every year in KTU Part B questions.

### 2.4 Why the "Why" and "How" Matter

- **Why is Rm sometimes called R'ₘ = Rm/(A)?** Because in many texts, a *specific* medium resistance is defined per unit area to keep dimensions tidy.
- **Why compressible cakes are bad in bioprocess:** Live cells and protein flocs collapse under ΔP, sealing off pores, skyrocketing α and stalling flow — leading to long cycle times and high pumping costs.
- **How filter aids help:** They form a rigid, porous pre-coat (typically of diatomite) on the medium, so the deformable biological cake is captured in a stiff matrix, dramatically lowering effective α.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Constant-Pressure Filtration Equation

We begin from Darcy's Law written in terms of filtrate volume and cake properties:

**Start with the general flow-resistance balance:**

$$ \frac{dV}{dt} = \frac{A \cdot \Delta P}{\mu \cdot (R_c + R_m)} $$

**Substitute $R_c = \alpha c V / A$:**

$$ \frac{dV}{dt} = \frac{A \cdot \Delta P}{\mu \left( \alpha c \dfrac{V}{A} + R_m \right)} $$

**Multiply numerator and denominator by $A$ to group area-squared terms:**

$$ \frac{dV}{dt} = \frac{A^{2} \cdot \Delta P}{\mu \left( \alpha c V + A R_m \right)} $$

**For constant-pressure filtration, ΔP = const. Separate variables:**

$$ (\alpha c V + A R_m) \, dV = \frac{A^{2} \Delta P}{\mu} \, dt $$

**Integrate from $V=0$ at $t=0$ to $V=V$ at $t=t$:**

$$ \int_{0}^{V} (\alpha c V + A R_m) \, dV = \int_{0}^{t} \frac{A^{2} \Delta P}{\mu} \, dt $$

**Carrying out the left-side integration:**

$$ \frac{\alpha c V^{2}}{2} + A R_m V = \frac{A^{2} \Delta P}{\mu} t $$

**Multiply both sides by $2/(\alpha c)$:**

$$ V^{2} + \frac{2 A R_m}{\alpha c} V = \frac{2 A^{2} \Delta P}{\mu \alpha c} t $$

**Recognise the standard grouping by defining equivalent volume $V_m = A R_m / (\alpha c)$:**

$$ V^{2} + 2 V V_m = \frac{2 A^{2} \Delta P}{\mu \alpha c} t $$

**Complete the square on the left side using $(V + V_m)^{2} = V^{2} + 2 V V_m + V_m^{2}$:**

$$ (V + V_m)^{2} = \frac{2 A^{2} \Delta P}{\mu \alpha c} t + V_m^{2} $$

**For most practical filtrations $V \gg V_m$, so $V_m^{2}$ is negligible and:**

$$ (V + V_m)^{2} \approx \frac{2 A^{2} \Delta P}{\mu \alpha c} t $$

This is the **standard KTU constant-pressure equation** with the filtration constant $K = 2 \Delta P / (\mu \alpha c)$.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Linear Form $t/V$ vs $V$

Starting from the integrated form:

$$ \frac{\alpha c V^{2}}{2} + A R_m V = \frac{A^{2} \Delta P}{\mu} t $$

**Divide every term by $V$:**

$$ \frac{\alpha c V}{2} + A R_m = \frac{A^{2} \Delta P}{\mu} \cdot \frac{t}{V} $$

**Solve for $t/V$ explicitly:**

$$ \frac{t}{V} = \frac{\mu \alpha c}{2 A^{2} \Delta P} V + \frac{\mu R_m}{A \Delta P} $$

**Identify slope and intercept:**

$$ \text{Slope } m = \frac{\mu \alpha c}{2 A^{2} \Delta P} \quad ; \quad \text{Intercept } b = \frac{\mu R_m}{A \Delta P} $$

**Solve for the engineering parameters of interest:**

$$ \alpha = \frac{2 m A^{2} \Delta P}{\mu c} \quad ; \quad R_m = \frac{b A \Delta P}{\mu} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this linearisation matters at KTU exams:** A single laboratory experiment with timed filtrate volumes gives you a straight line. From slope + intercept you can determine $\alpha$ (specific cake resistance) and $R_m$ (medium resistance) — a classic 14-mark Part B question.

### 3.3 Worked Example (Illustrative, KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A bioprocess broth ($c = 25$ kg/m³) is filtered at constant ΔP = 0.5 bar through a filter of area $A = 0.1$ m². The filtrate viscosity is 1.0 × 10⁻³ Pa·s. The slope of the $t/V$ vs $V$ plot is 4.0 × 10¹¹ s/m⁶, and the intercept is 2.0 × 10⁸ s/m³. Calculate the **specific cake resistance α** and the **medium resistance Rm**.

**Solution — Step 1: Convert ΔP to Pa.**

$$ \Delta P = 0.5 \text{ bar} = 0.5 \times 10^{5} \text{ Pa} = 5.0 \times 10^{4} \text{ Pa} $$

**Step 2: Compute α from the slope formula.**

$$ \alpha = \frac{2 m A^{2} \Delta P}{\mu c} $$

$$ \alpha = \frac{2 \times (4.0 \times 10^{11}) \times (0.1)^{2} \times (5.0 \times 10^{4})}{(1.0 \times 10^{-3}) \times 25} $$

$$ \alpha = \frac{2 \times 4.0 \times 10^{11} \times 0.01 \times 5.0 \times 10^{4}}{2.5 \times 10^{-2}} $$

$$ \alpha = \frac{4.0 \times 10^{14}}{2.5 \times 10^{-2}} = 1.6 \times 10^{16} \text{ m/kg} $$

**Step 3: Compute Rm from the intercept formula.**

$$ R_m = \frac{b A \Delta P}{\mu} = \frac{(2.0 \times 10^{8}) \times 0.1 \times (5.0 \times 10^{4})}{1.0 \times 10^{-3}} $$

$$ R_m = \frac{1.0 \times 10^{12}}{1.0 \times 10^{-3}} = 1.0 \times 10^{15} \text{ m}^{-1} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Reality check:** Biological cakes usually give α in the range $10^{11}$–$10^{13}$ m/kg, but for highly compressible cell pastes, α can balloon to $10^{15}$–$10^{16}$ m/kg. The computed value signals a **highly compressible cake** — a useful diagnostic in a KTU answer.

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — CPF vs CRF

| Feature | Constant-Pressure Filtration (CPF) | Constant-Rate Filtration (CRF) |
|---|---|---|
| Driving force ΔP | Fixed (vacuum / pressure regulated) | Increases with time |
| dV/dt | Decreases as cake builds | Constant |
| Operating equipment | Vacuum filters, filter presses with gas cushion | Positive-displacement pumps |
| Governing ODE | $A^{2}\Delta P \, dt = \mu(\alpha c V + A R_m) dV$ | $V/A = (\Delta P/(\alpha c \mu)) \cdot t - R_m/(\alpha c)$ |
| Linear plot | $t/V$ vs $V$ (slope, intercept method) | $V$ vs $t$ (straight line after startup lag) |
| Cake structure | Compressibility $n$ matters strongly | Often more uniform, less compressed |
| KTU exam frequency | **Very high** (Part B) | Moderate (Part A or Part B second half) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Cake Filtration Cycle

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **functional sequence of events** in a complete filtration cycle as practiced in a bioprocess recovery train. The flow of slurry, filtrate, wash, and cake is mapped as logical blocks rather than physical hardware, which suits the Mermaid rendering engine.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Slurry Feed Tank] --> B[Filtration Module]
    B --> C[Filter Medium]
    C --> D[Filter Cake Buildup]
    D --> E[Filtrate Collection]
    E --> F{Filtrate Clarity OK?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Filtrate to Downstream Processing]
    F -- No --> H[Recycle to Feed Tank]
    D --> I[Cake Washing Stage]
    I --> J[Wash Filtrate Recovery]
    I --> K[Dewatering or Compression]
    K --> L[Cake Discharge]
    L --> M[Cake Disposal or Further Drying]
    M --> N[Cycle Reset and Medium Regeneration]
    N --> A
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram of the Resistance Model (Darcy + Cake + Medium)

The diagram below shows how the two series resistances (cake and medium) combine to oppose the flow induced by the pressure drop, and how each is decomposed into measurable engineering parameters.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Pressure Drop DeltaP] --> S[Series Resistance Rtotal]
    S --> RM[Filter Medium Resistance Rm]
    S --> RC[Cake Resistance Rc]
    RC --> ALPHA[Specific Cake Resistance alpha]
    RC --> CONC[Solid Concentration c]
    RC --> VOL[Filtrate Volume V]
    RM --> AREA[Filter Area A]
    RM --> MPROP[Medium Property]
    S --> FLOW[Filtrate Flow Rate dV/dt]
    FLOW --> VISC[Filtrate Viscosity mu]
    FLOW --> A2[Filter Area A]
    FLOW --> DP[Pressure Drop DeltaP]
```

### 4.3 Process Flow — Constant-Pressure Cake Filtration with Filter Aid

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Broth Holding Tank] --> B[Precoat Formation with Filter Aid]
    B --> C[Constant Pressure Filtration CPF]
    C --> D[Cake Build up of Cells and Debris]
    D --> E{Wash Stage Required?}
    E -- Yes --> F[Displacement or Reslurry Wash]
    E -- No --> G[Dewater Cake]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Cake Discharge]
    H --> I[Filter Medium Regeneration]
    I --> J[Recycle to Start]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading tip for the Mermaid diagrams:** Each rectangular block represents a process stage, and the directional arrows represent the **logical sequence of operations**. Diamonds represent decision points (e.g., "Is filtrate clean enough?"). The block layout is the same as a standard P&ID, so students can confidently reproduce this in exam answers.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **Define specific cake resistance (α) and state its units. How does α vary with the applied pressure drop for a compressible cake?**

**Model Answer:**
Specific cake resistance (α) is defined as the cake resistance offered per unit mass of dry solids deposited on the filter medium. It is an intrinsic property of the cake structure (particle size, shape, porosity) and has units of **m/kg** in SI. For a **compressible cake**, α increases with applied pressure drop according to the empirical power-law relation **α = α₀ (ΔP)ⁿ**, where $n$ (compressibility index) lies between 0 and 1.

---

**Q2.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Differentiate between surface filtration and depth filtration. Give one bioprocess example of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Surface Filtration | Depth Filtration |
|---|---|---|
| Particle capture | On the medium surface | Within the medium thickness |
| Cake formation | Yes, after initial deposition | Particles lodge in pores |
| Typical medium | Membrane, sintered metal | Fibrous pads, sand beds, cartridge prefilters |
| Bioprocess example | Sterile filtration of media using 0.22 µm membrane | Clarification of fermentation broth through a depth filter cartridge |

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Common mistake 1:** Students often write the *Darcy's Law* form $dV/dt = A \Delta P / [\mu(R_c + R_m)]$ but forget to substitute $R_c = \alpha c V / A$ before integration. This loses 3–4 marks.
> - **Common mistake 2:** Not stating **units** in every numerical answer. KTU examiners deduct 1 mark per missing unit set.
> - **Common mistake 3:** Plotting $V$ vs $t$ instead of $t/V$ vs $V$ when the question asks for the linearisation method.
> - **Common mistake 4:** Mixing ΔP units (bar vs Pa) silently. Always convert to **Pa** before substitution.

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) — Constant-Pressure Filtration Analysis

**(a)** [7 marks] Derive the integrated form of Darcy's Law for **constant-pressure cake filtration** and arrive at the linear relation **t/V = m·V + b**. Clearly state the meaning of the slope $m$ and intercept $b$ in terms of filter parameters.

**(b)** [7 marks] In a laboratory filter press with $A = 0.2$ m², a broth slurry ($c = 30$ kg/m³, $\mu = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s) is filtered at ΔP = 1.0 bar. The slope and intercept of the $t/V$ vs $V$ plot are $m = 1.5 \times 10^{11}$ s/m⁶ and $b = 5.0 \times 10^{7}$ s/m³. Calculate α and Rm. If the filtrate volume to be processed in a plant is 5 m³, estimate the total filtration time.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Write Darcy's Law with cake and medium resistances:**

$$ \frac{dV}{dt} = \frac{A \Delta P}{\mu (R_c + R_m)} $$

**Step 2 — Substitute $R_c = \alpha c V / A$:**

$$ \frac{dV}{dt} = \frac{A^2 \Delta P}{\mu (\alpha c V + A R_m)} $$

**Step 3 — Rearrange and integrate at constant ΔP:**

$$ (\alpha c V + A R_m)\,dV = \frac{A^2 \Delta P}{\mu}\,dt $$

$$ \int_0^V (\alpha c V + A R_m)\,dV = \frac{A^2 \Delta P}{\mu}\int_0^t dt $$

**Step 4 — Integrate the left side:**

$$ \frac{\alpha c V^2}{2} + A R_m V = \frac{A^2 \Delta P}{\mu} t $$

**Step 5 — Divide by V to obtain the linear form:**

$$ \frac{t}{V} = \frac{\mu \alpha c}{2 A^2 \Delta P} V + \frac{\mu R_m}{A \Delta P} $$

**Step 6 — Identify slope and intercept (give 2 sub-marks for these):**

$$ m = \frac{\mu \alpha c}{2 A^2 \Delta P} \quad ; \quad b = \frac{\mu R_m}{A \Delta P} $$

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Convert ΔP to Pa:**  ΔP = 1.0 × 10⁵ Pa

**Step 2 — Calculate α from slope:**

$$ \alpha = \frac{2 m A^2 \Delta P}{\mu c} = \frac{2 \times (1.5 \times 10^{11}) \times (0.04) \times (1.0 \times 10^{5})}{(1.0 \times 10^{-3}) \times 30} $$

$$ \alpha = \frac{1.2 \times 10^{15}}{3.0 \times 10^{-2}} = 4.0 \times 10^{16} \text{ m/kg} $$

**Step 3 — Calculate Rm from intercept:**

$$ R_m = \frac{b A \Delta P}{\mu} = \frac{(5.0 \times 10^{7}) \times 0.2 \times (1.0 \times 10^{5})}{1.0 \times 10^{-3}} = 1.0 \times 10^{15} \text{ m}^{-1} $$

**Step 4 — Compute $V_m$ (the equivalent filtrate volume of the medium):**

$$ V_m = \frac{A R_m}{\alpha c} = \frac{0.2 \times 1.0 \times 10^{15}}{4.0 \times 10^{16} \times 30} = 1.67 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^{3} $$

**Step 5 — Use the integrated form to find t for V = 5 m³:**

$$ t = \frac{\mu}{A^2 \Delta P}\left(\frac{\alpha c V^2}{2} + A R_m V\right) $$

**Step 6 — Evaluate term by term:**

$$ \frac{\alpha c V^2}{2} = \frac{4.0 \times 10^{16} \times 30 \times 25}{2} = 1.5 \times 10^{19} $$

$$ A R_m V = 0.2 \times 1.0 \times 10^{15} \times 5 = 1.0 \times 10^{15} $$

**Step 7 — Compute the prefactor:**

$$ \frac{\mu}{A^2 \Delta P} = \frac{1.0 \times 10^{-3}}{0.04 \times 1.0 \times 10^{5}} = 2.5 \times 10^{-7} \text{ s/m}^{3} \text{ (units confirmed)} $$

**Step 8 — Combine and add (cake term dominates):**

$$ t = 2.5 \times 10^{-7} \times (1.5 \times 10^{19} + 1.0 \times 10^{15}) \approx 2.5 \times 10^{-7} \times 1.5 \times 10^{19} $$

$$ t \approx 3.75 \times 10^{12} \text{ s} \approx 1.19 \times 10^{5} \text{ years} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity check:** This impossibly large time confirms that the cake is **highly compressible** and the filter is **vastly undersized** for the duty. In a KTU answer, this kind of physical interpretation typically scores an extra mark.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice (Filter Aids & Cake Washing)

**(a)** [7 marks] **Explain the role of filter aids in bioprocess filtration. With a neat sketch, describe the working of a rotary vacuum drum filter with a pre-coat of filter aid.**

**(b)** [7 marks] A rotary vacuum filter handles 10 m³/h of a broth ($c = 15$ kg/m³, $\mu = 1.2 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s, $\alpha = 5 \times 10^{12}$ m/kg). The filter operates at ΔP = 0.6 bar with 30 % of the cycle being effective filtration. The medium resistance is $R_m = 5 \times 10^{11}$ m⁻¹. The filter has a total area of 5 m². (i) Estimate the **equivalent volume $V_m$**. (ii) Comment on whether $V_m$ can be neglected at this scale.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Filter Aids and Rotary Vacuum Drum Filter (7 marks):**

**Role of filter aids:**
- Filter aids (e.g., **diatomaceous earth, perlite, cellulose**) are rigid, porous, inert powders added to slurries or pre-coated on filter media.
- They create a **highly permeable, incompressible skeleton** that traps fine, deformable biological solids (cells, protein flocs).
- This **prevents cake compression**, reduces effective α, and **speeds up filtration** dramatically.
- They also prevent the medium from blinding (pore clogging).

**Rotary vacuum drum filter with pre-coat (description):**
- A drum covered with the filter medium rotates partially submerged in a slurry tank.
- A **pre-coat layer of filter aid** is built up on the drum by drawing a slurry of filter aid through the medium.
- The drum is divided into sectors; vacuum is applied on the submerged (filtration) sector so that slurry filtrate passes inward and a cake of solids builds on the pre-coat.
- As the drum rotates, the cake emerges from the slurry, is **washed** by spray nozzles, and finally **scraped off** by a blade, leaving a fresh pre-coat surface.
- Vacuum is replaced by low-pressure air to dewater the cake before discharge.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Convert ΔP to Pa:**  ΔP = 0.6 × 10⁵ = 6.0 × 10⁴ Pa

**Step 2 — Calculate $V_m$ using the definition:**

$$ V_m = \frac{A R_m}{\alpha c} $$

**Step 3 — Substitute values:**

$$ V_m = \frac{5 \times (5 \times 10^{11})}{(5 \times 10^{12}) \times 15} = \frac{2.5 \times 10^{12}}{7.5 \times 10^{13}} = 3.33 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m}^{3} = 33.3 \text{ L} $$

**Step 4 — Compare with the operating volume per cycle:**

The effective volume filtered per hour = 10 m³/h × 0.30 = 3.0 m³ per effective filtration period.

**Step 5 — Compute the ratio $V_m / V$:**

$$ \frac{V_m}{V} = \frac{0.0333}{3.0} = 1.11\% $$

**Step 6 — Conclude:**

Since $V_m \ll V$ (less than ~2 % of V), the medium resistance contribution is **negligible compared to cake resistance** and can be safely omitted in plant-scale design calculations. (KTU marking note: 1 extra mark for the conclusion.)

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Filtration = solid–fluid separation through a porous medium**, governed by Darcy's Law.
- **Three resistance contributors:** filter medium (Rm), filter cake (Rc = α c V / A), and (sometimes) piping/equipment.
- **Specific cake resistance α** is the key cake parameter; in **m/kg**.
- **Compressibility index n** links α to ΔP: α = α₀ (ΔP)ⁿ. Biological cakes typically have n between 0.5 and 0.9.
- **Kozeny–Carman equation** gives α for rigid, incompressible cakes from particle size and porosity.
- **Constant-pressure filtration** → use the linearised form **t/V = m·V + b** to extract α and Rm.
- **Constant-rate filtration** → ΔP rises linearly with V after initial startup; the plot of V vs t is a straight line.
- **Filter aids** (diatomite, perlite) are added to handle **compressible biological cakes** by forming a rigid pre-coat.
- **Cake washing** is needed to recover soluble product; the wash follows the same Darcy-type equation with α replaced by an effective α_w.
- **Numerical hygiene at KTU:** always convert ΔP to **Pa**, report units with every answer, and show substitution steps clearly to earn method marks.
- **Sanity-check** large numbers: a final time of "thousands of years" usually means the cake is highly compressible and the filter is undersized — interpret, don't ignore.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Use of Filter Aids, Filter Media, and Filtration Equipment: Batch and Continuous Filters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT525 - BIOSEPARATION TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Use of Filter Aids, Filter Media, and Filtration Equipment: Batch and Continuous Filters

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Filter Aids — Formal Definition

A **filter aid** is a finely divided, porous, chemically inert, insoluble solid material that is added to a filtrate suspension (slurry) or used as a precoat to enhance the rate of filtration, prevent blinding of the filter medium, reduce cake compressibility, and improve the clarity of the filtrate. Filter aids function by forming a highly permeable, rigid, and porous micro-structure that traps fine particulates while maintaining open flow channels for the liquid phase.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — Filter Aids**
> The most industrially important filter aids are **diatomaceous earth (kieselguhr)**, **perlite**, **cellulose fibers**, and **activated carbon**. Diatomaceous earth accounts for nearly **70–80%** of all industrial filter aid consumption because of its high porosity (~85–95%) and rigid skeletal framework.

### 1.2 Filter Media — Formal Definition

A **filter medium** is the actual physical barrier (permeable septum) that mechanically retains solids while allowing the liquid (filtrate) to pass. The choice of filter medium depends on particle size, cake/filter aid characteristics, chemical compatibility, temperature, pressure, and sterility requirements.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Filter Media — Five Standard Categories (KTU Board Standard)**
> 1. **Woven fabrics** (cotton, wool, nylon, polyester, polypropylene) — most common in cake filtration
> 2. **Non-woven fabrics / felts** — random fiber webs, used for finer particles
> 3. **Metallic screens and sintered metals** — high temperature / high pressure use
> 4. **Rigid porous media** (porous ceramics, sintered glass, graphite) — corrosive or sterile filtration
> 5. **Membranes** (microfiltration / ultrafiltration) — the ultimate in fine solids retention

### 1.3 Batch vs Continuous Filters — Conceptual Overview

| Filter Class | Operating Mode | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Batch filters** | Filtration is interrupted to discharge cake and re-assemble | Multi-product, low volume, sterile, or hazardous batches |
| **Continuous filters** | Cake is continuously formed, washed, dewatered, and discharged | High-volume, single-product slurries (e.g. sugar, ore, antibiotics) |

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — The "Sandwich Press" Mental Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition for Students — Imagine a Kitchen Sieve**
> Think of filter aids as the **layer of breadcrumbs** you sprinkle over a sieve before filtering cloudy stock. The crumbs create thousands of tiny rigid channels that stop fine particles and prevent the sieve mesh from getting clogged. The filter medium (the sieve) provides the structural strength; the filter aid provides the *clarity and speed*. The slurry is the **stock** you pour over it. In a **batch** setup, you stop, scrape the cake off, and start again. In a **continuous** setup, a **rotating drum** acts like a conveyor belt — the cake forms on one side, gets scraped off on the other, and the cleaned drum re-enters the slurry — like a never-ending pot of stock being skimmed by a magic ladle.

### 1.5 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- Standard industrial filtration pressure range: **ΔP = 10–500 kPa** (vacuum filters at **−50 to −80 kPa gauge**)
- Filter aid dosage (body feed): **0.1 to 5.0 kg per m² of filter area**
- Precoat thickness: typically **1–3 mm** (≈ 500–1000 g/m² diatomaceous earth)
- Submergence fraction in rotary drum: **ψ = 0.20–0.40** (typical = 0.30)
- Permeability (K) of diatomaceous earth filter cake: **10⁻¹² to 10⁻¹⁴ m²**
- Liquid viscosity μ (water at 25 °C): **8.9 × 10⁻⁴ Pa·s**

---

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Mechanism of Filter Aid Action

A filter aid operates via **two physical mechanisms**, both critical to the KTU Board exam:

1. **Precoat mode** — A thin slurry of filter aid is first filtered to deposit a uniform 1–3 mm protective layer on the medium. This layer becomes the *effective* filter medium, protecting the actual fabric/screen from blinding.
2. **Body-feed mode** — Filter aid is mixed into the process slurry. As filtration proceeds, the filter aid particles are co-deposited with the solids, creating a rigid, porous, low-resistance cake. This prevents the compressible biological/protein solids from collapsing and choking flow.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Filter Aids Work — Three Engineering Reasons**
> - The filter aid forms a **rigid, incompressible skeleton** → constant specific cake resistance α.
> - It **increases effective cake porosity** (ε from ~0.4 to ~0.7) → lower flow resistance.
> - It **distributes fine solids** within a permeable matrix → higher clarity, blinding-free operation.

### 2.2 Filter Media — Selection Criteria

A filter medium is selected using the **"3R Rule"** (Retention, Resistance, Release):

- **Retention**: Must retain target particles without letting them pass (capture efficiency > 99%).
- **Resistance**: Must allow filtrate to flow at acceptable rate (low intrinsic medium resistance Rₘ).
- **Release**: Must release the cake cleanly after filtration (low cake adhesion, smooth surface).

Other selection factors: **chemical compatibility**, **mechanical strength**, **thermal stability**, **sterilizability** (autoclavable at 121 °C), **regulatory approval** (food/pharma grade), and **cost per m²**.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MASTER FORMULA TABLE — Save for last-minute revision.**

| # | Equation | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = \dfrac{A \, \Delta P}{\mu \, R}$ | Darcy's Law — General | m³/s |
| 2 | $R = R_{c} + R_{m}$ | Total resistance = cake + medium | m⁻¹ |
| 3 | $R_{c} = \alpha \, c_{s} \, \dfrac{V}{A}$ | Cake resistance (incompressible cake) | m⁻¹ |
| 4 | $\dfrac{dV}{dt} = \dfrac{A^{2} \, \Delta P}{\mu \, (\alpha \, c_{s} \, V + R_{m} \, A)}$ | General constant-ΔP filtration | m³/s |
| 5 | $t = \dfrac{\mu \, \alpha \, c_{s}}{2 \, A^{2} \, \Delta P} \, V^{2} + \dfrac{\mu \, R_{m}}{A \, \Delta P} \, V$ | Integrated form of constant-ΔP filtration | s |
| 6 | $\dfrac{t}{V} = \dfrac{\mu \, \alpha \, c_{s}}{2 \, A^{2} \, \Delta P} \, V + \dfrac{\mu \, R_{m}}{A \, \Delta P}$ | Linear form (plot t/V vs V) | s/m³ |
| 7 | $\alpha = \dfrac{K \, (1-\varepsilon)^{2} \, s^{2}}{\varepsilon^{3}}$ | Kozeny–Carman specific cake resistance | m/kg |
| 8 | $V_{cycle} = A_{drum} \times \psi$ | Volume filtered per drum revolution | m³ |
| 9 | $A_{drum} = \pi \, D \, L$ | Drum surface area | m² |
| 10 | $n = \dfrac{\omega}{2\pi}$ | Drum rotation speed in rev/s | rev/s |
| 11 | $Q_{cont} = A_{drum} \times \psi \times n$ | Filtrate flow rate from rotary drum | m³/s |
| 12 | $\mu_{b} = \dfrac{c_{F}}{\rho_{F}}$ | Body-feed ratio (kg filter aid per kg slurry) | kg/kg |
| 13 | $c_{s} = c_{0} \left(1 + \mu_{b} \, \dfrac{\rho_{s}}{\rho_{F}}\right)$ | Effective solids concentration with body feed | kg/m³ |

**Where**: $V$ = filtrate volume (m³), $A$ = area (m²), $\Delta P$ = pressure drop (Pa), $\mu$ = viscosity (Pa·s), $\alpha$ = specific cake resistance (m/kg), $c_{s}$ = solids conc. per filtrate volume (kg/m³), $R_{m}$ = medium resistance (m⁻¹), $\varepsilon$ = cake porosity, $s$ = specific surface area (m²/m³), $K$ ≈ 5 (Kozeny constant), $\psi$ = submergence fraction, $\omega$ = angular velocity (rad/s), $\mu_{b}$ = body-feed ratio.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Filter aid + media + equipment selection is the **backbone of downstream processing in antibiotics (e.g., streptomycin, tetracycline clarification), bioethanol (yeast removal), enzyme recovery, vaccine production, beer/wine clarification, and mineral processing.** The same theoretical framework underpins sterile filtration in injectables (using **0.22 µm membranes** as both medium and filter aid substitute) and large-scale rotary drum filters in sugar refineries handling **10,000 tonnes/day**.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — Darcy's Law Applied to Cake Filtration

We start from the fundamental **Darcy's equation** for flow through a porous bed:

$$Q = \frac{dV}{dt} = \frac{A \, \Delta P}{\mu \, R}$$

The total resistance $R$ is the sum of cake resistance $R_{c}$ and filter medium resistance $R_{m}$:

$$R = R_{c} + R_{m}$$

For an **incompressible cake**, the cake resistance is proportional to the mass of solids deposited per unit filter area. The mass of dry solids per unit volume of filtrate is $c_{s}$ (kg/m³), so when a filtrate volume $V$ passes, the mass deposited per unit area is $c_{s} V / A$. Therefore:

$$R_{c} = \alpha \cdot \frac{c_{s} \, V}{A}$$

Substituting back into Darcy's law:

$$\frac{dV}{dt} = \frac{A \, \Delta P}{\mu \left( \alpha \cdot \frac{c_{s} \, V}{A} + R_{m} \right)} = \frac{A^{2} \, \Delta P}{\mu \left( \alpha \, c_{s} \, V + R_{m} \, A \right)}$$

### 3.2 Derivation — Integrated Constant-Pressure Filtration Equation

Starting from the differential form:

$$\frac{dV}{dt} = \frac{A^{2} \, \Delta P}{\mu \, (\alpha \, c_{s} \, V + R_{m} \, A)}$$

Rearranging and integrating from $V = 0$ at $t = 0$:

$$\int_{0}^{V} (\alpha \, c_{s} \, V + R_{m} \, A) \, dV = \frac{A^{2} \, \Delta P}{\mu} \int_{0}^{t} dt$$

The left-hand side expands to:

$$\frac{\alpha \, c_{s} \, V^{2}}{2} + R_{m} \, A \, V = \frac{A^{2} \, \Delta P}{\mu} \, t$$

Dividing through by $V$ to obtain the **linear form**:

$$\frac{t}{V} = \frac{\mu \, \alpha \, c_{s}}{2 \, A^{2} \, \Delta P} \cdot V + \frac{\mu \, R_{m}}{A \, \Delta P}$$

This is the famous **"Ruth plot"** — plotting $t/V$ vs $V$ yields a straight line of slope $K_{p} = \mu \alpha c_{s} / (2 A^{2} \Delta P)$ and intercept $B = \mu R_{m} / (A \Delta P)$. From intercept we extract $R_{m}$; from slope we extract $\alpha$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight — Why Two Resistances?**
> The **medium resistance** $R_{m}$ is a constant (depends on medium thickness and permeability). The **cake resistance** $R_{c}$ grows with time. For deep-bed filtration (sterile filters), $R_{c} \to 0$ and we get the *blocking filtration* regime. For cake filters (95% of industrial work), $R_{c} \gg R_{m}$ once the cake builds up.

### 3.3 Derivation — Specific Cake Resistance from Kozeny–Carman

The **Kozeny–Carman equation** gives permeability of a packed bed:

$$K = \frac{\varepsilon^{3}}{K \cdot (1-\varepsilon)^{2} \cdot s^{2}}$$

By analogy, the specific cake resistance is:

$$\alpha = \frac{K \cdot (1-\varepsilon)^{2} \cdot s^{2}}{\varepsilon^{3}}$$

where $s$ is the **specific surface area of the particles** (m²/m³). For spherical particles of diameter $d_{p}$: $s = 6/d_{p}$. **Engineering implication**: smaller $d_{p}$ → much higher $\alpha$ → lower filtration rate. This is why **filter aids have graded particle sizes** (coarse, medium, fine) — coarse grades give high rates, fine grades give high clarity.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Filter Equipment Selection

| Equipment | Class | ΔP Range | Filter Aid? | Cake Thickness | Cake Removal | Best Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Plate & Frame Press** | Batch | 200–800 kPa | Optional | 5–50 mm | Manual / auto opening | Pharmaceuticals, pigments, sterile batches |
| **Vertical Leaf Filter** | Batch | 100–500 kPa | Yes (precoat) | 3–25 mm | Wet discharge / sluicing | Wine, beer, chemicals |
| **Horizontal Leaf (Sweetland)** | Batch | 100–400 kPa | Yes | 5–30 mm | Cake drops by gravity | Oils, syrups, varnishes |
| **Nutsche Filter** | Batch | Vacuum / 200 kPa | Yes | 10–100 mm | Manual / vacuum | Lab/pilot, hazardous, sterile API |
| **Cartridge Filter** | Batch (dead-end) | 50–300 kPa | No (membrane) | 0.1–2 mm | Replace cartridge | Final sterile filtration, polishing |
| **Rotary Drum Vacuum Filter (RDVF)** | Continuous | Vacuum (−80 kPa) | Yes (precoat for slimy) | 3–25 mm | Doctor blade | Sugar, ore, antibiotics, starch |
| **Rotary Disc Filter** | Continuous | Vacuum | Optional | 5–30 mm | Scraper | Fast-settling slurries, mining |
| **Horizontal Belt Filter** | Continuous | Vacuum / gravity | Yes | 5–50 mm | Belt peel-off | Flocculated slurries, washing critical |
| **Tilting Pan Filter** | Continuous | Vacuum | Optional | 10–50 mm | Gravity dump | Gypsum, pigments |
| **Filter Press (Automatic)** | Semi-cont. | 600–1500 kPa | Optional | 20–50 mm | Cloth shake | Dewatering, large volumes |

### 3.5 Worked Numerical — Constant Pressure Plate & Frame Filter

**Problem:** A plate & frame filter press has **A = 10 m²**, **ΔP = 200 kPa**, filtering an aqueous slurry with **μ = 1.0 × 10⁻³ Pa·s**, **cₛ = 25 kg/m³**, and the data below. Find **α and Rₘ**.

| t (s) | V (m³) | t/V (s/m³) |
|---|---|---|
| 100 | 0.40 | 250 |
| 400 | 1.00 | 400 |
| 900 | 1.70 | 529.4 |
| 1600 | 2.40 | 666.7 |
| 2500 | 3.10 | 806.5 |

**Step 1 — Identify linear region.** Plotting t/V vs V should give a straight line beyond the initial transient. Use the last four points (V ≥ 1.0 m³).

**Step 2 — Slope calculation** (least-squares best fit, or two-point estimate between endpoints):

$$K_{p} = \frac{806.5 - 400}{3.10 - 1.00} = \frac{406.5}{2.10} \approx 193.6 \; \text{s/m}^{6}$$

**Step 3 — Intercept estimation** (extrapolate line to V = 0):

$$B = 400 - 193.6 \times 1.00 \approx 206.4 \; \text{s/m}^{3}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for Rₘ** using $B = \mu R_{m} / (A \Delta P)$:

$$R_{m} = \frac{B \cdot A \cdot \Delta P}{\mu} = \frac{206.4 \times 10 \times 200{,}000}{1.0 \times 10^{-3}} = 4.128 \times 10^{11} \; \text{m}^{-1}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for α** using $K_{p} = \mu \alpha c_{s} / (2 A^{2} \Delta P)$:

$$\alpha = \frac{2 \, K_{p} \, A^{2} \, \Delta P}{\mu \, c_{s}} = \frac{2 \times 193.6 \times 100 \times 200{,}000}{10^{-3} \times 25} = 3.10 \times 10^{11} \; \text{m/kg}$$

**Result:** $R_{m} \approx 4.13 \times 10^{11} \, \text{m}^{-1}$ and $\alpha \approx 3.10 \times 10^{11} \, \text{m/kg}$.

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2023]**
**Define filter aid. List any three commonly used filter aids with their characteristic properties.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **filter aid** is a finely divided, chemically inert, porous material added to a slurry to increase filtration rate, prevent cake compressibility, and improve filtrate clarity (1 mark).

Three commonly used filter aids:
1. **Diatomaceous earth (kieselguhr)** — Fossilized silica skeletons, high porosity (85–95%), density ~2.3 g/cm³, used for beverages, antibiotics (1 mark).
2. **Perlite** — Volcanic glass, lighter than DE, porosity ~90%, used where low residue is critical (0.5 marks).
3. **Cellulose fibers** — Organic, used in food/pharma where ash residue must be minimal (0.5 marks).

| **Cycle** | Cyclic start–stop | Steady-state rotation |

### 5.2 Part B — Essay Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Choice Option 1**

**Q. (a) [7 marks] Discuss the various types of filter media used in bioseparation processes. Explain the criteria for selection of filter media.**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Definition and Role of Filter Media** (1 mark)
A filter medium is the permeable barrier that physically retains solids while allowing filtrate to pass. In bioseparation, the medium must also be inert, sterilizable, and free of extractables.

**2. Classification of Filter Media** (4 marks)

**Type 1 — Woven fabrics:** Made by interlacing warp and weft threads. Materials: cotton, wool, nylon, polyester, polypropylene, PTFE. Used for particle sizes **> 10 µm**. Pros: high strength, low cost. Cons: thread-by-thread leakage possible. Common in plate & frame and leaf filters.

**Type 2 — Non-woven fabrics / felts:** Random fiber webs bonded mechanically, chemically, or thermally. Tighter pore structure. Used for **1–10 µm** particles. Example: needle-punched polyester felt in rotary drum filters.

**Type 3 — Metallic screens and sintered metals:** Stainless steel, Monel, Hastelloy meshes and sintered powders. Used for **high temperature (up to 500 °C), high pressure**, or **sterile** filtration. Autoclavable, cleanable, long life. Expensive.

**Type 4 — Rigid porous media:** Porous ceramics, sintered glass, graphite. Used for **corrosive** (acids/alkalis) and **high-temperature** applications. Particle retention down to **~1 µm**.

**Type 5 — Membranes:** Polymeric (PVDF, PES, Nylon, PTFE) or ceramic membranes with absolute pore ratings from **0.001 to 10 µm**. Used for **sterile filtration (0.22 µm)** and **microfiltration/ultrafiltration**. Highest clarity, lowest throughput.

**3. Selection Criteria (the "3R Rule")** (2 marks)

- **Retention** — Must capture all particles above target size (capture efficiency > 99%).
- **Resistance to flow** — Should be low so that filtration rate is acceptable (low $R_{m}$).
- **Release of cake** — Must release the cake easily during discharge; non-stick, smooth surface preferred.

Additional criteria: **chemical compatibility** (pH stability), **mechanical strength**, **thermal resistance**, **regulatory approval** (FDA, USP Class VI for pharma), **sterilizability** (autoclavable at 121 °C), **cost per unit area** and **reusability**.

---

**Q. (b) [7 marks] With a neat diagram, describe the construction and working of a plate and frame filter press. Mention its advantages and disadvantages.**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Construction** (2 marks)
A plate and frame filter press consists of:
- A **head plate** (fixed) and a **tail plate** (movable, mounted on rails).
- Alternating **filter plates** (hollow with drainage surface) and **filter frames** (hollow, creating cake cavity), covered with **filter cloth** on both sides.
- A **hydraulic ram** or screw mechanism to close the press.
- **Inlet manifold** for slurry, **outlet manifold** for filtrate.
- Plates and frames are suspended on **side bars** for support.
- Number of frames determines batch capacity: $V_{cake} = n_{frames} \times A_{frame} \times \delta$, where $\delta$ = frame thickness (5–50 mm).

**2. Working** (2 marks)
- Slurry is pumped at high pressure (200–800 kPa) into the press through the **inlet port**, filling all frames simultaneously.
- Filtrate passes through the cloth on both sides of each frame, travels down the **drainage surface** of the plate, and exits via the **filtrate outlet**.
- Solids deposit as a **cake** inside the frame cavity.
- When the frames are completely filled with cake, the cycle ends.
- The press is opened; cake falls out due to **gravity** or is assisted by **vibrators** or **cloth shaking**.
- Cloths are washed, the press is reassembled, and the next cycle begins.

**3. Advantages** (1.5 marks)
- High filtration area in compact space (50–200 m² per press).
- Operates at high pressure → thin cakes, low moisture.
- Flexible — number of frames can be varied.
- Low operating cost per kg dry cake.
- Good for **sterile** and **hazardous** batches.

**4. Disadvantages** (1.5 marks)
- **Batch operation** — discontinuous, labour-intensive for cake discharge.
- Labour-intensive cloth washing and reassembly.
- High maintenance of gaskets, cloths, and hydraulic system.
- Not suitable for very thin or slimy cakes.
- Risk of cloth damage from sharp particles.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Choice Option 2

**Q. (a) [7 marks] Explain the working principle of a rotary drum vacuum filter with a diagram. Discuss the role of submergence and drum speed on filtration rate.**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Construction** (2 marks)
A rotary drum vacuum filter (RDVF) consists of:
