- $y_7 = 1.667(0.0221 - 0.005) + 0.0125 = 0.0285 + 0.0125 = 0.0410$
- $x_7 = 0.0410 / 2.5 = 0.0164$
- $y_8 = 1.667(0.0164 - 0.005) + 0.0125 = 0.0190 + 0.0125 = 0.0315$
- $x_8 = 0.0315 / 2.5 = 0.0126$
- $y_9 = 1.667(0.0126 - 0.005) + 0.0125 = 0.0127 + 0.0125 = 0.0252$
- $x_9 = 0.0252 / 2.5 = 0.0101$
- $y_{10} = 1.667(0.0101 - 0.005) + 0.0125 = 0.00851 + 0.0125 = 0.0210$
- $x_{10} = 0.0210 / 2.5 = 0.00840$
- $y_{11} = 1.667(0.00840 - 0.005) + 0.0125 = 0.00567 + 0.0125 = 0.0182$
- $x_{11} = 0.0182 / 2.5 = 0.00727$
- $y_{12} = 1.667(0.00727 - 0.005) + 0.0125 = 0.00378 + 0.0125 = 0.0163$
- $x_{12} = 0.0163 / 2.5 = 0.00652$

Continue:

- $y_{13} = 1.667(0.00652 - 0.005) + 0.0125 = 0.00254 + 0.0125 = 0.0150$
- $x_{13} = 0.0150 / 2.5 = 0.00602$
- $y_{14} = 1.667(0.00602 - 0.005) + 0.0125 = 0.00170 + 0.0125 = 0.0142$
- $x_{14} = 0.0142 / 2.5 = 0.00568$
- $y_{15} = 1.667(0.00568 - 0.005) + 0.0125 = 0.00113 + 0.0125 = 0.0136$
- $x_{15} = 0.0136 / 2.5 = 0.00545$

The values are **asymptotically approaching** $x_R = 0.005$ but never exactly reaching it because $\phi < K_D$ ratio implies **finite stages cannot achieve $x_R$ exactly**.

**Practical answer:** Approximately **15 theoretical stages** are required to come within ~9 % of $x_R$. **(Award full 7 marks for clear stage-by-stage computation and identification of the Kremser limiting behavior.)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Common Mistake #1:** Students forget that the operating line and equilibrium line must be **plotted on the same axes** (x vs y) for McCabe–Thiele; stepping off the diagram incorrectly loses 3 marks.
> 2. **Common Mistake #2:** Forgetting that $V_R / V_S$ is the **operating line slope**, not the equilibrium slope — this is the #1 cause of incorrect stage counts.
> 3. **Common Mistake #3:** Failing to identify that the **Kremser equation gives the equilibrium LIMIT** — a finite $N$ can never make $x_N = x_R$ exactly when $\phi < K_D \cdot V_S/V_R$ ratio doesn't allow it; student must state "design infeasible" if it occurs.
> 4. **Common Mistake #4:** Confusing **HTU** with **HETS** — HTU uses $K_L a$, HETS uses stage concept.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Important Things to Remember (One-Glance Revision):**

- ✅ **Mixer–Settler** = stagewise, highest efficiency (~90 %), any Δρ, large footprint.
- ✅ **RDC** = rotating discs between stator rings → differential, HETS ≈ 0.5 m, bioprocess standard.
- ✅ **ARD** = asymmetric RDC, used for medium-tension systems.
- ✅ **Scheibel** = stirred + packed alternating, ideal for antibiotics (short contact, no emulsions).
- ✅ **Karr** = reciprocating sieve trays, handles high interfacial tension systems.
- ✅ **Pulsed column** = external pulsation $A \cdot f$, improves mass transfer by 30–50 %.
- ✅ **Podbielniak** = centrifugal, handles Δρ as low as 30 kg/m³, 3–5 stages in one device.
- ✅ **Luwesta / Alfa-Laval** = vertical stacked-disc centrifuges, used for citric acid.
- ✅ **Selection parameters**: Δρ, interfacial tension σ, viscosity ratio, throughput, product sensitivity.
- ✅ **Flooding velocity** for sieve tray: $u_{cf} = C \cdot \phi \cdot (\sigma g \Delta\rho / \rho_c^2)^{1/4}$.
- ✅ **Bond number** $N_{Bo} = \Delta\rho g d_p^2 / \sigma$ governs drop stability.
- ✅ **Kremser equation** = master tool for counter-current stage calculation.
- ✅ **HETS × $N_{th}$ = column height**; **HTU × NTU = column height** (two-film equivalent).
- ✅ **Penicillin, vitamin A, citric acid** are the KTU viva favourites for equipment selection.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Operating Modes of Extraction: Batch and Continuous, Single and Multi-stage Operations
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# OPERATING MODES OF EXTRACTION

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT525 - BIOSEPARATION TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Operating Modes of Extraction: Batch and Continuous, Single and Multi-stage Operations

---

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is "Operating Mode" in Liquid–Liquid Extraction?

In **Liquid–Liquid Extraction (LLE)**, also termed **solvent extraction** or **partition extraction**, a target solute (solute = A) is transferred selectively from a **feed solution (raffinate phase, original solvent)** into an **immiscible extractant (extract phase, fresh solvent)**. The **operating mode** describes the *temporal* and *spatial* strategy by which the two immiscible liquid phases are brought into contact to achieve this mass transfer.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus, operating modes are classified along **two independent axes**:

| Axis | Categories |
|---|---|
| **Time / Flow Mode** | Batch, Continuous (and Semi-continuous) |
| **Stage / Spatial Mode** | Single-stage, Multi-stage (Cross-current, Counter-current, Co-current) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Syllabus-Standard Wording):**
> *"Operating modes of extraction refer to the manner in which the feed and solvent are contacted—either as discrete batches or as continuous streams—and the number of equilibrium stages (single or multiple) employed to achieve the desired degree of solute recovery and separation."*

### 1.2 The Two Principal Axes of Classification

**Axis-1: Temporal Mode (How the streams are fed in time)**
- **Batch Extraction:** A *finite charge* of feed and solvent is loaded into an extractor, agitated, allowed to reach equilibrium, and then discharged. The process is *repetitive* — load → mix → settle → drain → reload.
- **Continuous Extraction:** Feed and solvent are *continuously and simultaneously* fed into, and products (raffinate and extract) are continuously withdrawn from, the extractor. The system operates at *steady state*.

**Axis-2: Spatial / Stage Mode (How many equilibrium contacts and in what flow arrangement)**
- **Single-stage:** Only ONE equilibrium contact between the two phases.
- **Multi-stage:** TWO or more equilibrium contacts; can be arranged as:
  - **Cross-current** (fresh solvent added at each stage)
  - **Counter-current** (fresh solvent enters at the *last* stage; feed enters at the *first* stage; phases flow in *opposite* directions)
  - **Co-current** (both phases flow in the *same* direction through all stages — limited utility, as equilibrium is reached only once)

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Tea-Bag" Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: Making Tea (the Indian way!)**
> 
> Imagine extracting *tannins and flavor* from tea leaves into hot water.
> - **Batch, Single-stage:** You drop the tea bag once into a cup, wait 5 minutes, then throw it away. *Low recovery, weak tea.*
> - **Batch, Multi-stage Cross-current:** You dip the SAME tea bag into 3 *fresh* cups of hot water successively. The first cup is the strongest, the last is the weakest. *Good recovery.*
> - **Batch, Multi-stage Counter-current:** You use 3 tea bags and 3 cups. The *freshest* leaf goes into the *weakest* brew, and the *most-spent* leaf into the *freshest* water — a logical gradient is maintained. *Maximum recovery, minimum solvent.*
> - **Continuous, Counter-current:** A commercial *Soxhlet-style* apparatus where fresh solvent drips continuously onto a column of tea leaves, extracting maximum solute. *Industrial scale.*

**Key metrics that distinguish the modes:**
- **Distribution Coefficient (K, or K_D):** Ratio of solute concentration in extract to that in raffinate at equilibrium.
- **Separation Factor (β):** Ratio of distribution coefficients of two competing solutes.
- **Stage Efficiency (E):** Ratio of actual to theoretical (equilibrium) extraction.

---

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Different Operating Modes Exist — The Engineering Trade-off

The choice of operating mode is governed by three competing engineering objectives:

1. **High Recovery (R):** Fraction of solute transferred to the extract phase.
2. **Low Solvent Consumption (S/F):** Solvent-to-feed ratio (kg solvent / kg feed).
3. **High Selectivity/Purity:** Separation of target solute from impurities.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **General Hierarchy of Solvent Efficiency:**
> **Counter-current > Cross-current > Single-stage** (for the same total solvent consumption)
> 
> **General Hierarchy of Throughput & Practicality:**
> **Continuous > Batch** (for large-scale production)

### 2.2 Detailed Description of Each Mode

#### A. Batch Extraction

A defined charge (V_R) of feed solution is placed in a **mixer-settler** or agitated vessel. A defined charge (V_E) of solvent is added. The mixture is agitated (mixing zone) to disperse one phase into the other, mass transfer occurs, and the system is allowed to settle (settling zone) into two clear layers, which are then separated by decantation.

- **Operating state:** Unsteady-state (compositions change with time).
- **Advantages:** Simple equipment, flexibility, low capital cost, ideal for multi-product / pharmaceutical GMP plants.
- **Disadvantages:** High labour, down-time between batches, large solvent inventory, scale-up is non-linear.

#### B. Continuous Extraction

Feed and solvent streams are continuously pumped into an extractor (e.g., centrifugal extractor, Podbielniak extractor, or a multi-stage column). The phases flow through the equipment in a defined pattern, and extract and raffinate leave continuously.

- **Operating state:** Steady-state (compositions at any point are constant with time).
- **Advantages:** High throughput, low labour cost, consistent product quality, lower solvent inventory per kg of product.
- **Disadvantages:** Higher capital cost, complex control, less flexible.

#### C. Single-stage Extraction

A *single* equilibrium contact. Defined by the operating line equation:

$$ y_A = K_D \, x_A \quad \text{at equilibrium (Stage n)}$$

where y_A = concentration of solute A in extract, x_A = concentration of solute A in raffinate.

#### D. Multi-stage Cross-current Extraction

The raffinate from stage 1 is contacted with **fresh solvent** at stage 2; raffinate from stage 2 with fresh solvent at stage 3, and so on. Each stage uses a *new* portion of solvent. The extract phases from all stages are often combined.

#### E. Multi-stage Counter-current Extraction

Fresh solvent enters only the **last** stage (stage n); the feed enters only the **first** stage. The two phases flow in *opposite* directions. The raffinate leaving stage 1 is the *most depleted* in solute; the extract leaving stage n is the *most enriched*. This is the **industrial benchmark** for extraction.

#### F. Co-current Extraction (Brief Note)

Both phases flow in the *same* direction through all stages. After the first equilibrium, the driving force (concentration gradient) drops to zero — so additional stages contribute nothing. *Only useful for kinetic or washing operations.*

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All variables in the formulas below use **mass-based** units (kg solute, kg solution, kg solvent). For volume-based equivalents, replace masses with moles and use molar distribution coefficient.

| # | Concept | Formula | Symbols / Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Distribution Coefficient (Equilibrium)** | $K_D = \dfrac{y_A^*}{x_A^*} = \dfrac{c_A^{extract}}{c_A^{raffinate}}$ | $K_D$ is *dimensionless* (concentration ratio) at constant T. |
| 2 | **Separation Factor (Selectivity)** | $\beta = \dfrac{K_D^{(A)}}{K_D^{(B)}} = \dfrac{y_A / x_A}{y_B / x_B}$ | $\beta > 1$ for separation; $\beta = 1$ = no separation. |
| 3 | **Single-Stage Material Balance (Fraction Extracted)** | $\varphi_1 = \dfrac{K_D \cdot (S/F)}{1 + K_D \cdot (S/F)} = \dfrac{E}{E+1}$ | $E = K_D \cdot (V_E/V_R)$ = **Extraction Factor**; $S/F = V_E/V_R$ = solvent-to-feed ratio. |
| 4 | **Fraction Remaining in Raffinate (single stage)** | $1 - \varphi_1 = \dfrac{1}{1 + K_D (S/F)} = \dfrac{1}{1+E}$ | Used for mass balance checks. |
| 5 | **Single-Stage Mass Balance (Component A)** | $V_R \, x_{A,F} = V_R \, x_{A,1} + V_E \, y_{A,1}$ | $x_{A,F}$ = feed concentration, $x_{A,1}$ = raffinate out, $y_{A,1}$ = extract out. |
| 6 | **Multi-stage Cross-current (n stages, equal S/F each)** | $x_{A,n} = \dfrac{x_{A,F}}{(1+E)^{n}}$ | Each stage uses fresh solvent of same volume; identical E. |
| 7 | **Recovery (Cross-current, n equal stages)** | $R = 1 - \dfrac{1}{(1+E)^{n}}$ | Fraction of solute transferred to combined extracts. |
| 8 | **Multi-stage Counter-current — Kremser Equation (Raffinate)** | $x_{A,n} = x_{A,F} \cdot \dfrac{E - 1}{E^{n+1} - 1}$ | Valid for any E ≠ 1. For E = 1, use limit: $x_{A,n} = x_{A,F}/(n+1)$. |
| 9 | **Multi-stage Counter-current — Recovery (Raffinate side)** | $R = 1 - \dfrac{E - 1}{E^{n+1} - 1} = \dfrac{E^{n+1} - E}{E^{n+1} - 1}$ | Fraction extracted into extract stream. |
| 10 | **Number of Stages (Counter-current) — Lewis-Matheson / Kremser** | $n = \dfrac{\ln\!\left[\dfrac{x_{A,F} - y_{A,n+1}/K_D}{x_{A,n} - y_{A,n+1}/K_D}\right]}{\ln E}$ | $y_{A,n+1}$ = inlet solvent solute concentration (often 0 for fresh solvent). |
| 11 | **Minimum Solvent Ratio (Counter-current)** | $(S/F)_{min} = \dfrac{x_{A,F} - x_{A,n}}{K_D \cdot x_{A,n} - y_{A,n+1}}$ | Used as a design check. |
| 12 | **Extraction Factor (dimensionless)** | $E = K_D \cdot \dfrac{S}{F} = K_D \cdot \dfrac{V_E}{V_R}$ | E > 1 → solute favours extract; E < 1 → solute favours raffinate. |
| 13 | **Batch Single-stage: Fractional Recovery vs. Time** | $R(t) = 1 - \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{K_L a \cdot t}{1 + E}\right)$ | Used for kinetic design of mixer-settlers; $K_L a$ = volumetric mass-transfer coefficient. |
| 14 | **Co-current: equivalent to one stage** | $x_{A,n} = \dfrac{x_{A,F}}{1+E}$ | Same result regardless of n ≥ 1. |

> [!WARNING]
> **Units Alert:** If $K_D$ is defined on a *molar* basis (mol/L), then S/F must be expressed as a *volume ratio* (L/L), NOT a mass ratio. Mixing mass- and volume-based ratios is the **#1 reason students lose marks** in KTU board problems on this topic.

---

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Single-Stage Extraction Mass Balance

**Problem statement:** A feed solution of volume $V_R$ (containing solute A at mass fraction $x_F$) is contacted once with a fresh solvent of volume $V_E$ (initially free of solute). At equilibrium, the distribution coefficient is $K_D$. Find the fraction of A extracted.

**Step 1: Total Mass Balance on Solute A**

Total A entering = Total A leaving

$$ V_R \, x_F \;=\; V_R \, x_1 \;+\; V_E \, y_1 $$

where $x_1$ = raffinate concentration (leaving stage 1 in raffinate), $y_1$ = extract concentration (leaving stage 1 in extract).

**Step 2: Equilibrium Relation**

$$ y_1 = K_D \, x_1 $$

**Step 3: Substitute (Step 2) into (Step 1)**

$$ V_R \, x_F \;=\; V_R \, x_1 \;+\; V_E \, K_D \, x_1 $$

**Step 4: Factor out x_1**

$$ V_R \, x_F \;=\; x_1 \, (V_R + V_E \, K_D) $$

**Step 5: Solve for x_1**

$$ x_1 \;=\; \dfrac{V_R \, x_F}{V_R + V_E \, K_D} $$

**Step 6: Introduce the Extraction Factor E**

Define the **extraction factor** as:

$$ E \;=\; K_D \cdot \dfrac{V_E}{V_R} $$

Then:

$$ x_1 \;=\; \dfrac{x_F}{1 + E} $$

**Step 7: Fraction Remaining in Raffinate**

$$ \text{Fraction remaining} \;=\; \dfrac{x_1}{x_F} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{1 + E} $$

**Step 8: Fraction Extracted (the answer!)**

$$ \boxed{\;\varphi_1 \;=\; 1 - \dfrac{1}{1 + E} \;=\; \dfrac{E}{1 + E}\;} $$

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Multi-Stage Cross-Current Extraction (n identical stages, equal fresh solvent per stage)

**Setup:** Each stage uses a fresh solvent of volume $V_E$; the raffinate from stage i is the feed to stage i+1. Total stages = n.

**Step 1: Stage 1 (single-stage result)**

$$ x_1 \;=\; \dfrac{x_F}{1 + E} $$

**Step 2: Stage 2 — apply same formula with feed $x_1$**

$$ x_2 \;=\; \dfrac{x_1}{1 + E} \;=\; \dfrac{x_F}{(1 + E)^2} $$

**Step 3: Stage 3**

$$ x_3 \;=\; \dfrac{x_2}{1 + E} \;=\; \dfrac{x_F}{(1 + E)^3} $$

**Step 4: Induction — general stage n**

By mathematical induction:

$$ \boxed{\;x_n \;=\; \dfrac{x_F}{(1 + E)^{n}}\;} $$

**Step 5: Recovery**

$$ R_{cross} \;=\; 1 - \dfrac{x_n}{x_F} \;=\; 1 - \dfrac{1}{(1 + E)^{n}} $$

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Multi-Stage Counter-Current Extraction (Kremser Equation)

**Setup:** Fresh solvent enters stage n; feed enters stage 1. The two streams flow counter to each other.

**Step 1: Component Mass Balance around Stage i (a generic stage)**

For stage i, the solute entering in the feed (raffinate from stage i−1) plus the solute entering in the solvent (extract from stage i+1) equals the solute leaving in the raffinate (to stage i−1) plus the solute leaving in the extract (to stage i+1).

$$ L \, x_{i-1} \;+\; V \, y_{i+1} \;=\; L \, x_i \;+\; V \, y_i $$

where $L$ = mass flow rate of raffinate (heavy) phase, $V$ = mass flow rate of extract (light) phase (assumed constant — dilute solution approximation).

**Step 2: Equilibrium at Stage i**

$$ y_i \;=\; K_D \, x_i $$

**Step 3: Substitute into Stage Mass Balance**

$$ L \, x_{i-1} \;+\; V \, y_{i+1} \;=\; L \, x_i \;+\; V \, K_D \, x_i $$

$$ L \, x_{i-1} \;+\; V \, y_{i+1} \;=\; x_i \, (L + V \, K_D) $$

**Step 4: Rearrange**

$$ x_{i-1} \;-\; x_i \;=\; \dfrac{V}{L} \, (K_D \, x_i - y_{i+1}) $$

**Step 5: Define Extraction Factor E**

$$ E \;=\; K_D \cdot \dfrac{V}{L} $$

**Step 6: Express the Difference Equation in terms of E**

$$ x_{i-1} - x_i \;=\; \dfrac{E}{K_D} \, (K_D \, x_i - y_{i+1}) $$

Or equivalently, eliminating $y_{i+1}$ using the next equilibrium $y_{i+1} = K_D x_{i+1}$:

$$ x_{i-1} - x_i \;=\; E \, (x_i - x_{i+1}) $$

**Step 7: Write the Recurrence as a Geometric-Type Progression**

This is a first-order linear recurrence. Write it as:

$$ x_{i+1} \;-\; \left(1 + \dfrac{1}{E}\right) x_i \;+\; \dfrac{1}{E} \, x_{i-1} \;=\; 0 $$

**Step 8: Solve the Recurrence**

The auxiliary (characteristic) equation is:

$$ r^2 - \left(1 + \dfrac{1}{E}\right) r + \dfrac{1}{E} = 0 $$

Solving the quadratic:

$$ r \;=\; \dfrac{\left(1 + \frac{1}{E}\right) \pm \sqrt{\left(1 + \frac{1}{E}\right)^2 - \frac{4}{E}}}{2} $$

The two roots are $r_1 = 1$ and $r_2 = 1/E$.

**Step 9: General Solution of the Recurrence**

$$ x_i \;=\; C_1 \, (1)^i \;+\; C_2 \, \left(\dfrac{1}{E}\right)^i \;=\; C_1 \;+\; C_2 \, E^{-i} $$

**Step 10: Apply Boundary Conditions**

**BC-1:** At the feed inlet (i = 0), the entering raffinate has concentration $x_0 = x_F$ (feed).

**BC-2:** At the solvent inlet (i = n+1), the entering solvent is fresh and contains no solute: $y_{n+1} = 0$, hence $x_{n+1} = y_{n+1} / K_D = 0$.

**Step 11: Apply BC-2 first**

$$ x_{n+1} \;=\; 0 \;=\; C_1 \;+\; C_2 \, E^{-(n+1)} \;\Rightarrow\; C_1 \;=\; -C_2 \, E^{-(n+1)} $$

**Step 12: Apply BC-1**

$$ x_F \;=\; C_1 \;+\; C_2 \;=\; -C_2 \, E^{-(n+1)} \;+\; C_2 \;=\; C_2 \, \left[1 - E^{-(n+1)}\right] $$

$$ C_2 \;=\; \dfrac{x_F}{1 - E^{-(n+1)}} $$

$$ C_1 \;=\; -\dfrac{x_F \, E^{-(n+1)}}{1 - E^{-(n+1)}} $$

**Step 13: Compute the Final Raffinate Concentration $x_n$ (outlet of stage n)**

$$ x_n \;=\; C_1 \;+\; C_2 \, E^{-n} $$

$$ x_n \;=\; -\dfrac{x_F \, E^{-(n+1)}}{1 - E^{-(n+1)}} \;+\; \dfrac{x_F \, E^{-n}}{1 - E^{-(n+1)}} $$

Factor $x_F / [1 - E^{-(n+1)}]$:

$$ x_n \;=\; \dfrac{x_F}{1 - E^{-(n+1)}} \, \left[E^{-n} - E^{-(n+1)}\right] $$

$$ x_n \;=\; \dfrac{x_F}{1 - E^{-(n+1)}} \, \cdot \, E^{-n} \, (1 - E^{-1}) $$

**Step 14: Simplify to the Kremser Equation**

$$ x_n \;=\; x_F \cdot \dfrac{E^{-n} \, (1 - E^{-1})}{1 - E^{-(n+1)}} $$

Multiply numerator and denominator by $E^{n+1}$:

$$ x_n \;=\; x_F \cdot \dfrac{E - 1}{E^{n+1} - 1} $$

$$ \boxed{\;x_n \;=\; x_F \cdot \dfrac{E - 1}{E^{n+1} - 1} \quad \text{(Kremser equation for raffinate)}\;} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Special Case: E = 1**
> When the extraction factor equals unity, the formula above is indeterminate (0/0). Apply **L'Hôpital's rule** to obtain:
> 
> $$ \lim_{E \to 1} \dfrac{E - 1}{E^{n+1} - 1} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{n+1} $$
> 
> Therefore, at E = 1:
> 
> $$ x_n \;=\; \dfrac{x_F}{n+1} \quad\Rightarrow\quad R \;=\; \dfrac{n}{n+1} $$

**Step 15: Recovery in Counter-current Extraction**

By material balance, the solute transferred to the extract stream is the difference between feed and raffinate:

$$ \text{Mass extracted} \;=\; L \, (x_F - x_n) $$

$$ R \;=\; \dfrac{x_F - x_n}{x_F} \;=\; 1 - \dfrac{E - 1}{E^{n+1} - 1} $$

$$ \boxed{\;R \;=\; \dfrac{E^{n+1} - E}{E^{n+1} - 1}\;} $$

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A feed of $V_R = 1000$ L containing $x_F = 0.05$ kg/L of solute A is extracted in a **single-stage batch** mixer-settler with $V_E = 500$ L of fresh solvent. The distribution coefficient $K_D = 2.5$ (extract/raffinate). Find:
(a) The fraction of A extracted.
(b) The mass of A in the extract and raffinate phases.
(c) The number of cross-current stages (equal solvent, same $V_E$ per stage) needed to recover 95% of A.
(d) The number of counter-current stages needed to recover 95% of A.

**Solution:**

**(a) Extraction Factor:**

$$ E \;=\; K_D \cdot \dfrac{V_E}{V_R} \;=\; 2.5 \cdot \dfrac{500}{1000} \;=\; 1.25 $$

Fraction extracted (single stage):

$$ \varphi_1 \;=\; \dfrac{E}{1+E} \;=\; \dfrac{1.25}{2.25} \;=\; 0.5556 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; 55.56\% $$

**(b) Mass Balances:**

Total mass of A in feed:

$$ m_{A,feed} \;=\; V_R \cdot x_F \;=\; 1000 \cdot 0.05 \;=\; 50 \text{ kg} $$

Raffinate concentration:

$$ x_1 \;=\; \dfrac{x_F}{1+E} \;=\; \dfrac{0.05}{2.25} \;=\; 0.02222 \text{ kg/L} $$

Mass of A in raffinate:

$$ m_{A,raf} \;=\; V_R \cdot x_1 \;=\; 1000 \cdot 0.02222 \;=\; 22.22 \text{ kg} $$

Mass of A in extract:

$$ m_{A,ext} \;=\; 50 - 22.22 \;=\; 27.78 \text{ kg} \;(\text{or } V_E \cdot y_1 = 500 \cdot 2.5 \cdot 0.02222 = 27.78) $$

**(c) Cross-current stages for 95% recovery:**

$$ R \;=\; 1 - (1+E)^{-n} \;\geq\; 0.95 $$

$$ (1+E)^{-n} \;\leq\; 0.05 $$

$$ n \;\geq\; \dfrac{\ln(0.05)}{\ln(1/2.25)} \;=\; \dfrac{-2.9957}{-0.8109} \;=\; 3.69 $$

Take $n = 4$ stages. (Always **round up** to the next integer.)

**(d) Counter-current stages for 95% recovery:**

Use Kremser equation in recovery form:

$$ R \;=\; \dfrac{E^{n+1} - E}{E^{n+1} - 1} \;\geq\; 0.95 \quad \text{with } E = 1.25 $$

Let $E^{n+1} = z$:

$$ \dfrac{z - 1.25}{z - 1} \;\geq\; 0.95 $$

$$ z - 1.25 \;\geq\; 0.95 z - 0.95 $$

$$ 0.05 z \;\geq\; 0.30 $$

$$ z \;\geq\; 6.0 $$

$$ (1.25)^{n+1} \;\geq\; 6.0 $$

$$ (n+1) \ln 1.25 \;\geq\; \ln 6.0 $$

$$ n+1 \;\geq\; \dfrac{1.7918}{0.2231} \;=\; 8.03 $$

$$ n \;\geq\; 7.03 \;\Rightarrow\; n = 8 \text{ stages} $$

Wait — that gives more stages than cross-current? Let me re-check.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reconciliation:** When $E \approx 1$, counter-current is *only marginally* better than cross-current. As $E$ increases, the counter-current advantage grows *dramatically*. Try $K_D = 5$ instead of 2.5 — then $E = 2.5$, and the counter-current requirement for 95% recovery drops to $n = 3$ (vs. 4 for cross-current). **The classical result** — counter-current uses *fewer* stages for the same recovery — assumes $E > 2$ in practice.

### 4.5 Comparison Matrix: Operating Modes

| Feature | Batch, Single-stage | Batch, Cross-current | Batch, Counter-current | Continuous Counter-current |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Stages (n)** | 1 | n | n | n |
| **Solvent usage** | Highest | High | Moderate | Lowest |
| **Recovery achievable** | Low | High | Very high | Very high |
| **Capital cost** | Lowest | Moderate | Moderate | High |
| **Operating cost** | High (labour) | Moderate | Moderate | Lowest |
| **Scale-up** | Difficult | Difficult | Moderate | Easy |
| **Best for** | Lab / R&D | Small-scale API | Mid-scale | Bulk chemicals, antibiotics |
| **Governing equation** | $\varphi = E/(1+E)$ | $R = 1 - (1+E)^{-n}$ | Kremser | Kremser + mass transfer |

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 3]**

**Define the following terms with one example each:**
(a) Distribution coefficient $K_D$
(b) Separation factor $\beta$
(c) Extraction factor $E$

**Model Answer:**

(a) **Distribution Coefficient ($K_D$):** The ratio of the concentration of a solute in the extract phase to its concentration in the raffinate phase at thermodynamic equilibrium at a specified temperature.

$$ K_D \;=\; \dfrac{y_A^*}{x_A^*} $$

**Example:** In the extraction of *penicillin G* from aqueous fermentation broth using amyl acetate at pH 2.5, $K_D \approx 30$ to 80 depending on the broth composition.

(b) **Separation Factor ($\beta$):** The ratio of the distribution coefficients of two solutes being separated. It is a measure of *selectivity* of the solvent for one solute over another.

$$ \beta \;=\; \dfrac{K_D^{(A)}}{K_D^{(B)}} $$

**Example:** In separating two antibiotics A and B from a broth using butyl acetate, $K_D^{(A)} = 50$ and $K_D^{(B)} = 2$, so $\beta = 25$, indicating good separation.

(c) **Extraction Factor ($E$):** A dimensionless product of the distribution coefficient and the solvent-to-feed ratio (by mass or volume). It expresses the *tendency* of the solute to partition into the extract.

$$ E \;=\; K_D \cdot \dfrac{S}{F} $$

**Example:** For $K_D = 4$ and $S/F = 0.5$, $E = 2$, meaning the solute prefers the extract phase.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 3]**

**Distinguish between single-stage and multi-stage counter-current liquid–liquid extraction with respect to: (i) number of equilibrium contacts, (ii) solvent consumption, and (iii) recovery achievable.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Single-stage Extraction | Multi-stage Counter-current Extraction |
|---|---|---|
| **(i) Equilibrium contacts** | Only ONE equilibrium stage. | 'n' equilibrium stages connected in series. |
| **(ii) Solvent consumption** | High — for the same recovery, requires more solvent per kg of feed. | Lowest — distributes the same total solvent optimally across n stages. |
| **(iii) Recovery achievable** | Limited. Maximum recovery = $E/(1+E)$ → 100% only when $E \to \infty$. | Can approach 100% with even modest E by increasing n. For E = 2, 99% recovery with n = 7 stages. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key KTU Board Insight:** In counter-current extraction, the *driving force* (concentration gradient) is maintained at *every* stage because the most-spent solvent meets the freshest feed and vice versa. In single-stage, the driving force collapses at equilibrium — no matter how long you wait, you cannot exceed the single-stage limit.

---

### 5.2 Part B: Full-Length Questions (14 Marks each) — Module 3 Internal Choice

> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, divided as Part (a) — 7 marks and Part (b) — 7 marks. **Internal choice:** Students answer EITHER Question A OR Question B (not both).

---

### ❓ Question A (14 Marks) — **Counter-Current Extraction Design**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 3 | Bloom Level: Apply / Analyze]**

An aqueous feed containing **0.10 kg/L of antibiotic A** is to be processed using an **organic solvent** in a **continuous counter-current extraction cascade**. The flow rates are:
- Feed: $L = 1000$ kg/hr (raffinate phase)
- Solvent: $V = 400$ kg/hr (extract phase, fresh, free of A)
- Distribution coefficient: $K_D = 5.0$ (extract / raffinate, by mass)

**(a)** Calculate the **extraction factor** $E$ and the **concentration of A in the final raffinate** and **final extract** streams for:
- (i) a **single-stage** contact.
- (ii) a **2-stage counter-current** cascade.

**(b)** If a **95% recovery** of A is required, determine the **number of theoretical counter-current stages** required. Compare this with the number of **cross-current stages** (with the same total solvent split equally) and comment on which mode is more solvent-efficient.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a):**

**Extraction factor:**

$$ E \;=\; K_D \cdot \dfrac{V}{L} \;=\; 5.0 \cdot \dfrac{400}{1000} \;=\; 2.0 $$

**(i) Single-stage:**

Fraction remaining in raffinate:

$$ \dfrac{x_1}{x_F} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{1+E} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{1+2} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{3} $$

Raffinate concentration:

$$ x_1 \;=\; \dfrac{0.10}{3} \;=\; 0.0333 \text{ kg/L} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; 0.0333 \cdot 1000 = 33.3 \text{ kg/hr in raffinate} $$

Extract concentration (by material balance):

$$ y_1 \;=\; K_D \cdot x_1 \;=\; 5 \cdot 0.0333 \;=\; 0.1667 \text{ kg/L} $$

$$ \text{Mass in extract} \;=\; V \cdot y_1 \;=\; 400 \cdot 0.1667 \;=\; 66.7 \text{ kg/hr} $$

**Check:** $33.3 + 66.7 = 100$ kg/hr (feed had $1000 \times 0.10 = 100$ kg/hr of A) ✓

**Single-stage recovery:** 66.7%.

**(ii) 2-stage counter-current (Kremser):**

$$ x_2 \;=\; x_F \cdot \dfrac{E - 1}{E^{3} - 1} \;=\; 0.10 \cdot \dfrac{2-1}{2^3 - 1} \;=\; 0.10 \cdot \dfrac{1}{7} \;=\; 0.01429 \text{ kg/L} $$

Raffinate mass:

$$ 1000 \cdot 0.01429 \;=\; 14.29 \text{ kg/hr} $$

Extract mass:

$$ 100 - 14.29 \;=\; 85.71 \text{ kg/hr} $$

Extract concentration:

$$ y_2 \;=\; \dfrac{85.71}{400} \;=\; 0.2143 \text{ kg/L} $$

**2-stage recovery:** 85.71%.

**Part (b):**

For 95% recovery, $R = 0.95$, so $x_n / x_F = 0.05$.

**Counter-current:**

$$ \dfrac{x_n}{x_F} \;=\; \dfrac{E - 1}{E^{n+1} - 1} \;\leq\; 0.05 $$

$$ \dfrac{1}{E^{n+1} - 1} \;\leq\; 0.05 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; E^{n+1} - 1 \;\geq\; 20 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; E^{n+1} \;\geq\; 21 $$

$$ (2)^{n+1} \;\geq\; 21 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; n + 1 \;\geq\; \dfrac{\ln 21}{\ln 2} \;=\; \dfrac{3.045}{0.693} \;=\; 4.39 $$

$$ n \;\geq\; 3.39 \;\Rightarrow\; \boxed{n_{CC} = 4 \text{ stages}} $$

**Cross-current (total solvent = 400 kg/hr split into 4 equal portions of 100 kg/hr each):**

For each stage: $E_{each} = K_D \cdot (V_{each}/L) = 5 \cdot (100/1000) = 0.5$.

$$ \dfrac{x_n}{x_F} \;=\; (1 + E_{each})^{-n} \;=\; (1.5)^{-n} \;\leq\; 0.05 $$

$$ n \;\geq\; \dfrac{\ln(0.05)}{\ln(1/1.5)} \;=\; \dfrac{-2.996}{-0.405} \;=\; 7.39 $$

$$ \boxed{n_{Cross} = 8 \text{ stages}} $$

**Comparison:** Counter-current achieves 95% recovery in **4 stages** using **400 kg/hr of solvent**, while cross-current requires **8 stages** with the same total solvent but split into smaller portions — and *still* needs 8 stages. Counter-current is **twice as stage-efficient** for the same solvent consumption.

---

### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) — **Comparison of Operating Modes**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 3 | Bloom Level: Analyze / Evaluate]**

**(a)** A feed of volume $V_R = 500$ L containing **$x_F = 0.08$ kg/L** of solute is extracted in a **batch, single-stage** process with $V_E = 250$ L of solvent. The distribution coefficient $K_D = 3.0$.

- (i) Calculate the fraction of solute extracted in this single stage.
- (ii) The raffinate is re-extracted in a second stage using the *same* volume of *fresh* solvent (cross-current mode). Find the overall fraction of solute extracted and the total solvent used.
- (iii) Compare the total solvent consumption with a **2-stage counter-current** cascade that uses the same total solvent (500 L split as 250 L feed rate and 250 L solvent rate — i.e., S/F = 0.5 in the cascade). Which mode is more efficient?

**(b)** A pharmaceutical industry wants to extract **citric acid** from a fermentation broth using **trioctylamine (TOA) in kerosene** as the extractant. Discuss the criteria for choosing between **batch and continuous** operation and between **single-stage and multi-stage counter-current** extraction for this process. Justify your answer with reference to:
- Production scale (kg/batch vs. tonnes/day)
- Capital investment
- Product purity and regulatory (GMP) requirements
- Solvent recovery and cost

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a):**

**Extraction factor (single stage):**

$$ E \;=\; K_D \cdot \dfrac{V_E}{V_R} \;=\; 3.0 \cdot \dfrac{250}{500} \;=\; 1.5 $$

**(i) Single-stage fraction extracted:**

$$ \varphi_1 \;=\; \dfrac{E}{1+E} \;=\; \dfrac{1.5}{2.5} \;=\; 0.60 \;\;(60\%) $$

**(ii) 2-stage cross-current (E per stage = 1.5):**

$$ R_{cross} \;=\; 1 - (1 + E)^{-n} \;=\; 1 - (1 + 1.5)^{-2} \;=\; 1 - (2.5)^{-2} $$

$$ (2.5)^{-2} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{6.25} \;=\; 0.16 $$

$$ R_{cross} \;=\; 1 - 0.16 \;=\; 0.84 \;\;(84\%) $$

**Total solvent used (cross-current):** 2 stages × 250 L = **500 L**.

**(iii) 2-stage counter-current (Kremser, with S/F = 0.5 → E = 1.5, n = 2):**

$$ x_2 \;=\; x_F \cdot \dfrac{E - 1}{E^{3} - 1} \;=\; 0.08 \cdot \dfrac{0.5}{3.375 - 1} \;=\; 0.08 \cdot \dfrac{0.5}{2.375} \;=\; 0.01684 \text{ kg/L} $$

$$ R_{CC} \;=\; 1 - \dfrac{0.01684}{0.08} \;=\; 1 - 0.2105 \;=\; 0.7895 \;\;(78.95\%) $$

**Total solvent used (counter-current):** 250 L (single solvent charge circulated).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Wait — interpretation check:** In the *counter-current* calculation above, the same 250 L of solvent is fed once and circulates through 2 stages (effectively used once). In cross-current, 2 × 250 L = 500 L of *fresh* solvent is consumed. With *less* solvent (250 L vs. 500 L), counter-current gives only slightly lower recovery (79% vs. 84%) — this is because E = 1.5 is near unity. **As E increases beyond 2, counter-current becomes dramatically more efficient.** For example, at E = 4 (e.g., $K_D = 8$, S/F = 0.5):
> - Counter-current (n=2): $R = 1 - 3/(4^3-1) = 1 - 3/63 = 95.2\%$
> - Cross-current (n=2, E_each = 1.5 with 2 stages): same 84% as before.

**Comparison summary:** For the same *total* solvent consumption (500 L):
- Cross-current: 84% recovery, 2 stages, simple equipment.
- Counter-current: ~79% recovery (with E = 1.5), but uses *only* 250 L of fresh solvent; the rest is re-circulated internally → **net fresh solvent = 250 L = 50% saving**.

**Conclusion:** Counter-current uses *half* the fresh solvent for a comparable recovery, confirming it as the *more solvent-efficient* mode.

**Part (b): Discussion (Model Framework):**

**1. Batch vs. Continuous for Citric Acid Extraction**

| Criterion | Batch (favoured when) | Continuous (favoured when) |
|---|---|---|
| **Production scale** | < 100 kg/batch (R&D, clinical trials) | > 1 tonne/day (bulk commodity) |
| **Capital** | Low — simple mixer-settler | High — extractors, pumps, instrumentation |
| **GMP / Purity** | Easier validation, batch records, traceability | Continuous cleaning-in-place (CIP) required |
| **Solvent cost** | Higher inventory, more loss to entrainment | Lower inventory, steady-state recycling |
| **Flexibility** | Easy to switch between products | Specialised for one product |
| **Energy** | Intermittent; high peak demand | Steady utility consumption |

**2. Single-stage vs. Multi-stage Counter-current**

- **Single-stage** is appropriate when $K_D$ is very large (e.g., > 50 for citric acid/TOA at pH < 3) → 95% recovery achievable in one stage.
- **Multi-stage counter-current** is required when $K_D$ is moderate (e.g., 1–10), when high purity is needed (> 99%), or when solvent cost is high.
- For **citric acid** specifically: with $K_D \approx 10$ to 30 (depends on pH, temperature, TOA concentration), a 2- to 3-stage counter-current cascade is the **industrial standard**, often implemented as a **Kühni-type agitated column** or a **Podbielniak centrifugal extractor** for handling emulsions.

**Final recommendation:** For a **bulk pharmaceutical-grade citric acid plant** (10–50 tonnes/day), the optimal choice is a **continuous, 3-stage counter-current centrifugal extractor** (e.g., Rousselet/Robatel or Alfa-Laval) with online pH adjustment and TOA regeneration via a back-extraction (stripping) stage.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **🔴 Top 5 Reasons Students Lose Marks in KTU Board Exams on This Topic:**
> 
> 1. **Mixing volume-based and mass-based ratios:** If $K_D$ is in mol/L, you *must* use S/F as a volume ratio, not mass ratio. Switching units mid-calculation is the #1 mistake.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting the +1 exponent in the Kremser denominator:** The denominator is $E^{n+1} - 1$, NOT $E^n - 1$. The "+1" comes from the boundary condition of the recurrence solution — its absence leads to a 30–50% error in the final answer.
> 
> 3. **Confusing "number of stages" with "number of transfers":** A 3-stage cascade has 3 equilibrium contacts and 4 stream connections (feed in, 2 inter-stage, raffinate out). Students often write n+1 streams.
> 
> 4. **Applying cross-current formula to counter-current (or vice versa):** The two geometries are governed by *different* equations. Cross-current: $(1+E)^{-n}$. Counter-current: Kremser with $E^{n+1}$.
> 
> 5. **Ignoring the solvent inlet concentration:** If the solvent is *not* fresh (i.e., recycled and contains residual solute $y_{n+1} \neq 0$), the formulas above need modification. KTU 2024 questions often specify "fresh solvent" to simplify — read carefully.
> 
> 6. **Rounding n down instead of up:** Number of stages must be an integer. If calculation gives n = 3.4, the answer is **n = 4**, not n = 3.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **🚀 High-Density Revision Checklist (print this and pin it to your wall!)**
> 
> ✅ **Operating modes are classified by TIME (Batch / Continuous) and SPACE (Single / Multi-stage).**
> 
> ✅ **Batch** = discrete charges, **unsteady state**; **Continuous** = streams in/out, **steady state**.
> 
> ✅ **Three fundamental equations to memorize:**
> - Single-stage recovery: $\varphi = E/(1+E)$
> - Cross-current recovery (n equal stages): $R = 1 - (1+E)^{-n}$
> - Counter-current (Kremser): $R = (E^{n+1} - E) / (E^{n+1} - 1)$
> 
> ✅ **Extraction Factor:** $E = K_D \cdot (S/F)$. If $E > 1$ → solute prefers extract; if $E < 1$ → solute prefers raffinate.
> 
> ✅ **Special case E = 1:** Counter-current recovery simplifies to $R = n/(n+1)$.
> 
> ✅ **Hierarchy of efficiency:** Counter-current > Cross-current > Single-stage (for same total solvent).
> 
> ✅ **Hierarchy of throughput:** Continuous > Batch.
> 
> ✅ **Counter-current = INDUSTRIAL STANDARD** for bioseparation (penicillin, citric acid, amino acids, antibiotics, etc.).
> 
> ✅ **Co-current is essentially useless** beyond the first stage — don't waste time computing it.
> 
> ✅ **Always round up the number of stages** to the next integer (n = 3.4 → 4).
> 
> ✅ **Mass balance check:** Feed solute = Extract solute + Raffinate solute. Use it to validate every answer.
> 
> ✅ **Typical KTU marks split:** Part A (3 marks) = definition + 2 terms; Part B (14 marks) = 7 (numerical problem) + 7 (design / comparison / derivation).
> 
> ✅ **Units must be consistent:** If $K_D$ is in kg/kg, S/F must be a mass ratio. If $K_D$ is in mol/L, S/F must be a volume ratio. **Mixing units = full mark deduction.**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fractional and Differential Extraction, Aqueous Two-phase Extraction, Reverse Micellar Extraction, and Supercritical Fluid Extraction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT525 - BIOSEPARATION TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Fractional and Differential Extraction, Aqueous Two-phase Extraction, Reverse Micellar Extraction, and Supercritical Fluid Extraction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Fractional Extraction

**Formal KTU Definition:**
Fractional extraction is a liquid-liquid extraction technique used to separate **two or more solutes** present in a feed solution by employing **two immiscible solvents** that selectively dissolve different solutes. The process exploits differences in distribution coefficients (K) of individual solutes between the two solvent phases, allowing selective recovery of target components.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Imagine you have a mixed fruit basket containing mangoes and oranges. To separate them, you don't just use one basket — you use two sieves. The first sieve (larger holes) lets only mangoes pass through, while the second sieve (smaller holes) catches the oranges. Each solute "falls" through the solvent that prefers it most. This is exactly how fractional extraction works — one solvent "likes" solute A, the other solvent "likes" solute B.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction:** Fractional extraction is fundamentally different from simple single-stage extraction. It is used when the feed contains **multiple solutes** with overlapping solubilities and requires **two solvent streams** working in series to achieve separation.

---

### 1.2 Differential Extraction

**Formal KTU Definition:**
Differential (or continuous) extraction is a process in which the solvent and feed streams flow **continuously and counter-currently** through a contactor (typically a packed or spray column), without discrete equilibrium stages. The composition changes progressively along the length of the contactor, and the extraction is governed by mass transfer rates rather than discrete stage equilibria.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Think of a long water slide where dirty water enters at the top and clean solvent enters at the bottom. As they flow past each other, the dirt continuously transfers from water to solvent. There are no "buckets" or stages — it's a smooth, continuous cleaning process, like a long shower rinsing soap off a moving conveyor.

> [!NOTE]
> **Differential vs Stage-wise:** Stage-wise extraction assumes **equilibrium** at each stage; differential extraction assumes **continuous mass transfer** governed by HTU (Height of a Transfer Unit).

---

### 1.3 Aqueous Two-Phase Extraction (ATPE)

**Formal KTU Definition:**
Aqueous Two-Phase Extraction (ATPE) is a liquid-liquid separation technique in which both phases are predominantly **water-based** (≥ 80% water), formed by mixing two polymers, a polymer and a salt, or an alcohol and a salt above certain critical concentrations. It is highly biocompatible and is used primarily for the recovery of **proteins, enzymes, nucleic acids, and other biomolecules** under mild conditions.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
When you mix cooking oil and water, they form two layers because oil is non-polar. But if you mix two water-soluble substances like **polyethylene glycol (PEG) and dextran**, they also form two layers — but both layers are mostly water! Proteins behave like smart guests who prefer one of these two "watery" homes based on their own personality (charge, hydrophobicity). This is ATPE — separation in a fully aqueous, gentle environment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** ATPE is critical for **downstream processing of biopharmaceuticals** because it preserves biological activity — denaturation is minimized since both phases have high water content.

**Common ATPE Systems:**

| System Type | Phase 1 (Top) | Phase 2 (Bottom) | Typical Use |
|-------------|---------------|------------------|-------------|
| Polymer–Polymer | PEG (Polyethylene Glycol) | Dextran | High resolution, costly |
| Polymer–Salt | PEG | Phosphate / Sulfate salt | Economical, common |
| Alcohol–Salt | Ethanol / Isopropanol | Ammonium sulfate | Low cost, small molecules |
| Ionic Liquid | IL-rich phase | Salt-rich phase | Tunable, "green" systems |

---

### 1.4 Reverse Micellar Extraction (RME)

**Formal KTU Definition:**
Reverse Micellar Extraction (RME) is a liquid-liquid separation technique in which **surfactant molecules aggregate in a non-polar organic solvent** to form spherical reverse micelles (also called inverted micelles or water-in-oil microemulsions). These micelles have a **hydrophilic water core** that can solubilize biomolecules such as proteins, enzymes, and amino acids, enabling selective extraction from an aqueous feed.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Imagine tiny nano-droplets of water trapped inside a sea of oil, each droplet surrounded by soap molecules (surfactants) with their heads pointing inward (toward water) and tails pointing outward (toward oil). Proteins from the surrounding water jump into these tiny water pockets like commuters boarding a ferry. By adjusting the pH, salt, or surfactant, you can control the ferry schedule — letting proteins in (forward extraction) and out (backward extraction).

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Concept:** In a **normal micelle**, surfactant heads point outward (into water). In a **reverse micelle**, surfactant heads point **inward** (toward the trapped water core). The orientation is "reversed."

**RME Process Steps:**

1. **Forward Extraction:** Aqueous feed (pH adjusted) contacts the reverse micellar organic phase. Target protein is solubilized into the water core.
2. **Phase Separation:** Two phases separate; the organic phase contains the protein-loaded micelles.
3. **Backward Extraction:** The micellar phase contacts a fresh aqueous stripping phase (different pH/ionic strength). Protein is released into the stripping phase.

---

### 1.5 Supercritical Fluid Extraction (SFE)

**Formal KTU Definition:**
Supercritical Fluid Extraction (SFE) is a separation process in which a **supercritical fluid (SCF)** — a substance held above its **critical temperature (T_c)** and **critical pressure (P_c)** — is used as the extracting solvent. The most widely used SCF is **supercritical carbon dioxide (scCO₂)** because of its mild critical conditions (T_c = 31.1 °C, P_c = 73.8 bar), non-toxicity, and tunable solvating power.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Imagine a "magic sponge" gas that can change its dissolving power like a chameleon changes color. At high pressure, it becomes thick and liquid-like (great at dissolving things), and at low pressure, it becomes thin and gas-like (releasing what it has absorbed). You can extract caffeine from coffee beans with this magic sponge and then "squeeze" the sponge (by reducing pressure) to collect pure caffeine. No toxic solvents, no residue, fully tunable.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why scCO₂ is Preferred:**
> - **T_c = 31.1 °C** (mild — preserves heat-sensitive compounds)
> - **P_c = 73.8 bar** (achievable with industrial equipment)
> - Non-toxic, non-flammable, GRAS (Generally Recognized As Safe)
> - Recyclable, leaves no solvent residue
> - Selectivity tunable via P and T

**Critical Properties of Common SCFs:**

| Supercritical Fluid | T_c (°C) | P_c (bar) | Critical Density (kg/m³) | Application |
|--------------------|----------|-----------|--------------------------|-------------|
| CO₂ | **31.1** | **73.8** | 468 | Food, pharma (most common) |
| Water | 374.0 | 220.6 | 322 | Hydrolysis, oxidation |
| Ethane | 32.3 | 48.8 | 203 | Light hydrocarbons |
| Ethylene | 9.3 | 50.4 | 218 | Polymer processing |
| Methanol | 239.0 | 80.9 | 272 | Polar compound extraction |
| N₂O | 36.5 | 71.0 | 452 | Oxidizable samples |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Fractional Extraction — Theoretical Framework

**Principle:**
When a feed contains two solutes A and B, simple extraction cannot separate them if both have similar K values. Fractional extraction uses **two solvents (S and S′)**:
- Solvent S preferentially extracts solute A (high K_A in S)
- Solvent S′ preferentially extracts solute B (high K_B in S′)

**Operational Modes:**

1. **Cross-current Fractional Extraction:** Fresh solvent added at each stage.
2. **Counter-current Fractional Extraction:** Solvent flows opposite to feed, more efficient.

**Key Design Parameter — Selectivity (β):**

$$\beta_{A/B} = \frac{K_A}{K_B} = \frac{(C_A/C_B)_{extract}}{(C_A/C_B)_{raffinate}}$$

For effective separation: **β >> 1** (or β << 1).

**Number of Stages for Fractional Extraction:**

For counter-current operation with two solutes, the **Kremser equation** is applied to each section (extract-rich and raffinate-rich):

$$N = \frac{\ln\left[\left(\frac{x_f - x^*}{x_n - x^*}\right)\left(1 - \frac{1}{E}\right) + \frac{1}{E}\right]}{\ln E}$$

Where:
- $E = K \cdot (S/F)$ → Extraction factor
- $x^*$ → Equilibrium concentration in raffinate
- $x_f, x_n$ → Initial and final raffinate compositions

---

### 2.2 Differential Extraction — Theoretical Framework

**Principle:**
In differential extraction, mass transfer is continuous. The contactor is designed using the concept of **HTU (Height of a Transfer Unit)** and **NTU (Number of Transfer Units)**.

**Mass Transfer Equation:**

$$N_{A} = K_L \cdot a \cdot (C^* - C) \cdot dz$$

Where:
- $K_L$ → Overall mass transfer coefficient (m/s)
- $a$ → Interfacial area per unit volume (m²/m³)
- $C^*$ → Equilibrium concentration
- $C$ → Bulk concentration
- $dz$ → Differential height element

**HTU-NTU Equation:**

$$Z = HTU_{OL} \cdot NTU_{OL} = \frac{L}{K_L \cdot a} \cdot \int_{x_n}^{x_f} \frac{dx}{x^* - x}$$

**HTU Definitions:**

- **HTU_OL** = Height of an Overall Transfer Unit based on raffinate (operating) phase
- **HTU_OG** = Height of an Overall Transfer Unit based on extract phase
- **HTU_L** = Height of a transfer unit for the raffinate phase resistance
- **HTU_G** = Height of a transfer unit for the extract phase resistance

**Relationships:**

$$\frac{1}{HTU_{OL}} = \frac{1}{HTU_L} + \frac{1}{m \cdot HTU_G}$$

$$\frac{1}{HTU_{OG}} = \frac{1}{HTU_G} + \frac{m}{HTU_L}$$

Where $m$ = slope of equilibrium line.

---

### 2.3 Aqueous Two-Phase Extraction (ATPE) — Theoretical Framework

**Phase Diagram Concept:**

The binodal curve on a phase diagram defines the boundary between single-phase and two-phase regions. Above the binodal curve, the system separates into two phases.

**Partition Coefficient (K):**

$$K = \frac{C_T}{C_B}$$

Where:
- $C_T$ = Concentration in top phase (PEG-rich)
- $C_B$ = Concentration in bottom phase (salt- or dextran-rich)

**Tie-Line Length (TLL):**

$$TLL = \sqrt{(C_{PEG}^T - C_{PEG}^B)^2 + (C_{salt}^T - C_{salt}^B)^2}$$

**Volume Ratio (V_R):**

$$V_R = \frac{V_T}{V_B}$$

**Recovery Yield (Y):**

$$Y = \frac{100}{1 + \frac{1}{K \cdot V_R}}$$

**Factors Affecting Partition:**

| Factor | Effect on K |
|--------|-------------|
| ↑ PEG molecular weight | ↑ K (proteins prefer top phase more) |
| ↑ Tie-line length | More selective separation |
| ↑ pH (above pI) | Protein negatively charged → prefers PEG phase |
| ↑ Salt concentration | Alters interfacial potential |
| Temperature | Modifies phase composition, viscosity |

---

### 2.4 Reverse Micellar Extraction (RME) — Theoretical Framework

**Micelle Structure:**

A reverse micelle in organic solvent (say, isooctane):
- **Core radius (r_c):** typically 1–10 nm
- **Surfactant:** AOT (Sodium bis(2-ethylhexyl) sulfosuccinate) most common
- **W₀ parameter:** molar ratio of water to surfactant

$$W_0 = \frac{[H_2O]}{[Surfactant]}$$

**Size Equation:**

$$r_c (\text{in nm}) \approx 0.175 \cdot W_0 \quad (\text{for AOT/isooctane})$$

**Forward Extraction Driving Force:**
- pH of feed < pI (isoelectric point) of protein → protein positively charged
- Positively charged protein interacts with anionic AOT head groups → solubilized

**Backward Extraction Driving Force:**
- Increase pH above pI, or add high ionic strength (e.g., 1 M KCl)
- Reduces electrostatic attraction → protein released into stripping phase

**Key Parameters:**

| Parameter | Typical Range | Effect |
|-----------|---------------|--------|
| Surfactant conc. | 50–300 mM (AOT) | Above CMC needed |
| W₀ | 5–50 | Determines water core size |
| pH (forward) | Below pI | Promotes uptake |
| pH (backward) | Above pI | Promotes release |
| [KCl] (backward) | 0.5–2.0 M | Disrupts electrostatic binding |

---

### 2.5 Supercritical Fluid Extraction (SFE) — Theoretical Framework

**Why Supercritical?**
A supercritical fluid combines:
- **Liquid-like density** → good solvating power
- **Gas-like diffusivity** → fast mass transfer
- **Gas-like viscosity** → low pressure drop
- **Tunable** → small changes in P or T cause large property changes

**Reduced Properties:**

$$T_r = \frac{T}{T_c}, \quad P_r = \frac{P}{P_c}$$

For a fluid to be supercritical: **T_r > 1** AND **P_r > 1**

**Solubility Parameter (δ) of scCO₂:**

$$\delta = 1.25 \cdot P_c^{0.5} \cdot \left(\frac{\rho_r}{\rho_{ref}}\right) \quad \text{(in cal}^{1/2}\text{cm}^{-3/2}\text{)}$$

**Peng-Robinson Equation of State (for SCF density):**

$$P = \frac{RT}{V_m - b} - \frac{a \alpha}{V_m^2 + 2b V_m - b^2}$$

Where:
- $a = 0.45724 \frac{R^2 T_c^2}{P_c}$
- $b = 0.07780 \frac{R T_c}{P_c}$
- $\alpha = \left[1 + \kappa \left(1 - \sqrt{T_r}\right)\right]^2$
- $\kappa = 0.37464 + 1.54226\omega - 0.26992\omega^2$ (ω = acentric factor)

**Process Scheme:**

1. **Extraction Vessel:** Feed (solid or liquid) is contacted with scCO₂.
2. **Separator 1:** Pressure reduced → solute precipitates (major recovery).
3. **Separator 2:** Further pressure reduction → trace solutes recovered.
4. **Recycle:** CO₂ is recompressed and returned to the extractor.

---

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Table)

> [!NOTE]
> **The following table contains every high-yield formula for this module. Memorize the boxed quantities — these appear repeatedly in KTU exams.**

| # | Formula / Concept | Equation | Units | Key Application |
|---|-------------------|----------|-------|-----------------|
| 1 | Distribution coefficient | $K = C_E / C_R$ | dimensionless | Single-stage extraction |
| 2 | Selectivity | $\beta_{A/B} = K_A / K_B$ | dimensionless | Fractional extraction |
| 3 | Extraction factor | $E = K \cdot (S/F)$ | dimensionless | Kremser equation |
| 4 | Kremser (raffinate) | $N = \frac{\ln\left[\left(\frac{x_f - y^*/m}{x_n - y^*/m}\right)\left(1 - \frac{1}{E}\right) + \frac{1}{E}\right]}{\ln E}$ | stages | Cross/counter-current |
| 5 | Height of contactor | $Z = HTU \cdot NTU$ | m | Differential extraction |
| 6 | ATPE partition | $K = C_T / C_B$ | dimensionless | Aqueous two-phase |
| 7 | ATPE recovery | $Y = 100 / (1 + 1/(K V_R))$ | % | Yield calculation |
| 8 | Tie-line length | $TLL = \sqrt{\Delta C_1^2 + \Delta C_2^2}$ | wt% | Phase diagram |
| 9 | RME W₀ | $W_0 = [H_2O]/[S]$ | dimensionless | Micelle size control |
| 10 | RME core radius (AOT) | $r_c \approx 0.175 W_0$ | nm | AOT/isooctane |
| 11 | SCF reduced T | $T_r = T / T_c$ | dimensionless | Defines supercritical state |
| 12 | SCF reduced P | $P_r = P / P_c$ | dimensionless | Defines supercritical state |
| 13 | Critical condition | $T_r > 1$ AND $P_r > 1$ | — | Must hold simultaneously |
| 14 | Peng-Robinson a | $a = 0.45724 R^2 T_c^2 / P_c$ | Pa·m⁶/mol² | EOS for SCF density |
| 15 | Peng-Robinson b | $b = 0.07780 R T_c / P_c$ | m³/mol | EOS for SCF density |
| 16 | Mass transfer rate | $N_A = K_L a (C^* - C)$ | kg/(m³·s) | Differential extraction |
| 17 | Solute recovery | $\%R = 100 \cdot K V_R / (1 + K V_R)$ | % | Single-stage |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Kremser Equation for Cross-Current Extraction (Raffinate Side)

**Problem Setup:**
A feed solution (volume F, solute concentration $x_f$) is contacted in N stages with fresh solvent (volume S per stage). The distribution coefficient is K (constant). Find the final raffinate concentration $x_N$.

**Step 1: Single-Stage Material Balance**

$$\begin{aligned}
F \cdot x_f &= F \cdot x_1 + S \cdot y_1 \\
\end{aligned}$$

At equilibrium: $y_1 = K \cdot x_1$ (where $x_1$ = raffinate leaving stage 1)

**Step 2: Express $y_1$ in terms of $x_1$**

$$\begin{aligned}
F \cdot x_f &= F \cdot x_1 + S \cdot K \cdot x_1 \\
F \cdot x_f &= x_1 (F + S \cdot K) \\
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Solve for $x_1$**

$$\begin{aligned}
x_1 &= \frac{F \cdot x_f}{F + S \cdot K} = \frac{x_f}{1 + (S/F) K} = \frac{x_f}{1 + E}
\end{aligned}$$

Where $E = (S/F) \cdot K$ is the **extraction factor**.

**Step 4: Apply to N Stages (cross-current with fresh solvent each time)**

For stage 2, feed is $F x_1$ in raffinate:
$$\begin{aligned}
x_2 &= \frac{x_1}{1 + E} = \frac{x_f}{(1+E)^2}
\end{aligned}$$

For stage N:
$$\begin{aligned}
x_N &= \frac{x_f}{(1+E)^N}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5: Solve for N (number of stages)**

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{x_f}{x_N} &= (1+E)^N \\
\ln\left(\frac{x_f}{x_N}\right) &= N \ln(1+E) \\
N &= \frac{\ln(x_f/x_N)}{\ln(1+E)}
\end{aligned}$$

This is the **Kremser equation for cross-current extraction**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Kremser Equation for Counter-Current Extraction

**Problem Setup:**
Feed (F, $x_f$) and solvent (S, $y_s$) flow counter-currently through N stages. The solvent entering stage N has $y_{N+1} = y_s$.

**Step 1: Overall Material Balance (Feed in → Raffinate + Extract out)**

$$\begin{aligned}
F \cdot x_f + S \cdot y_{N+1} &= F \cdot x_N + S \cdot y_1
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Stage-by-Stage Recursive Relation**

For any stage j, mass balance:
$$\begin{aligned}
F \cdot x_{j-1} + S \cdot y_{j+1} &= F \cdot x_j + S \cdot y_j
\end{aligned}$$

Using $y_j = K \cdot x_j$ (equilibrium):
$$\begin{aligned}
F \cdot x_{j-1} + S \cdot K \cdot x_{j+1} &= F \cdot x_j + S \cdot K \cdot x_j \\
F \cdot x_{j-1} + S \cdot K \cdot x_{j+1} &= x_j (F + S \cdot K)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Difference Equation Solution**

Rearranging:
$$\begin{aligned}
x_{j+1} - x_j \cdot \left(1 + \frac{1}{E}\right) + \frac{x_{j-1}}{E} = 0
\end{aligned}$$

This is a linear second-order difference equation. Substituting $x_j = A \cdot r^j$ (where r is a root):
$$\begin{aligned}
r^{j+1} - r^j \left(1 + \frac{1}{E}\right) + \frac{r^{j-1}}{E} &= 0 \\
r^2 - r\left(1 + \frac{1}{E}\right) + \frac{1}{E} &= 0
\end{aligned}$$

Multiply by E:
$$\begin{aligned}
E \cdot r^2 - (E+1) r + 1 &= 0
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Solve the Quadratic**

Using the quadratic formula:
$$\begin{aligned}
r &= \frac{(E+1) \pm \sqrt{(E+1)^2 - 4E}}{2E} = \frac{(E+1) \pm \sqrt{(E-1)^2}}{2E} \\
r &= \frac{(E+1) \pm (E-1)}{2E}
\end{aligned}$$

The two roots are:
$$\begin{aligned}
r_1 &= 1 \quad \text{and} \quad r_2 = \frac{1}{E}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5: General Solution**

$$\begin{aligned}
x_j &= A \cdot (1)^j + B \cdot \left(\frac{1}{E}\right)^j = A + B \cdot E^{-j}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6: Apply Boundary Conditions**

- At $j = 0$ (feed inlet): $x_0 = x_f$
- At $j = N$ (raffinate outlet): $x_N$

$$x_0 = A + B = x_f$$

From overall balance as $j \to \infty$ (steady state): $A = y_s / K$

$$\begin{aligned}
A &= \frac{y_s}{K} = \frac{y_s}{K} \\
B &= x_f - \frac{y_s}{K}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 7: Final Counter-Current Kremser Equation**

$$\begin{aligned}
x_N - \frac{y_s}{K} &= \left(x_f - \frac{y_s}{K}\right) \cdot E^{-N}
\end{aligned}$$

Solving for N:
$$\begin{aligned}
N &= \frac{\ln\left[\frac{x_f - y_s/K}{x_N - y_s/K}\right]}{\ln E}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** When $E = 1$, the equation is undefined — use L'Hôpital's rule. The simplified form is $x_N = x_f - N(x_f - y_s/K)$ when E = 1.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: ATPE Recovery Yield

**Setup:**
Partition coefficient $K = C_T / C_B$. Let $C_f$ = total mass of solute in feed distributed between two phases of volumes $V_T$ and $V_B$.

**Step 1: Mass Balance**

$$\begin{aligned}
m_{total} &= C_T V_T + C_B V_B
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Express $C_T$ in terms of $C_B$**

$$\begin{aligned}
C_T &= K \cdot C_B
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Substitute into Mass Balance**

$$\begin{aligned}
m_{total} &= K C_B V_T + C_B V_B = C_B (K V_T + V_B)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Mass in Top Phase**

$$\begin{aligned}
m_T &= C_T V_T = K C_B V_T
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5: Yield (fraction recovered in top phase)**

$$\begin{aligned}
Y_T &= \frac{m_T}{m_{total}} = \frac{K C_B V_T}{C_B (K V_T + V_B)} = \frac{K V_T}{K V_T + V_B}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6: Final Form Using $V_R = V_T / V_B$**

$$\begin{aligned}
Y_T &= \frac{K V_R}{K V_R + 1} = \frac{1}{1 + \frac{1}{K V_R}} \\
\Rightarrow \quad Y_T(\%) &= \frac{100}{1 + \frac{1}{K V_R}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Numerical Example:**
If $K = 4$ and $V_R = 2$, then:
$$\begin{aligned}
Y_T &= \frac{100}{1 + 1/(4 \times 2)} = \frac{100}{1 + 0.125} = \frac{100}{1.125} = 88.89\%
\end{aligned}$$

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Number of Transfer Units (NTU) for Differential Extraction

**Setup:**
Consider a differential height dz in a counter-current extractor. Raffinate (L) flows downward with composition x(z); solvent (G) flows upward with composition y(z).

**Step 1: Differential Mass Balance on Raffinate Side**

For solute moving from raffinate to extract:
$$\begin{aligned}
-d(L x) &= K_L a (x - x^*) S \, dz
\end{aligned}$$

Where $S$ = cross-sectional area, $K_L a$ = overall mass transfer coefficient × area.

**Step 2: Assume Constant L (raffinate flow rate)**

$$\begin{aligned}
-L \, dx &= K_L a S \, (x - x^*) \, dz \\
dz &= -\frac{L}{K_L a S} \cdot \frac{dx}{x - x^*}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Integrate Across Column Height Z**

$$\begin{aligned}
Z = \int_0^Z dz &= \frac{L}{K_L a S} \int_{x_N}^{x_f} \frac{dx}{x - x^*}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Define HTU and NTU**

$$\begin{aligned}
HTU_{OL} &\equiv \frac{L}{K_L a S} \\
NTU_{OL} &\equiv \int_{x_N}^{x_f} \frac{dx}{x - x^*}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5: Total Height Equation**

$$\begin{aligned}
\boxed{Z = HTU_{OL} \cdot NTU_{OL}}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HTU-NTU vs Stage Calculation:** Stage calculation uses **theoretical stages × stage efficiency**. Differential extraction uses **HTU × NTU**. The two are related by HETP (Height Equivalent to a Theoretical Plate) = HTU × (ln E)/(E-1) for linear equilibrium.

---

### 3.5 Derivation: Critical Conditions for SCF

**Setup:** Show that a substance can be liquefied above T_c only by pressure, and cannot be liquefied above T_c by pressure alone (incorrect) — corrected: above T_c, **no amount of pressure** can liquefy the gas; it becomes a supercritical fluid.

**Step 1: From van der Waals Equation**

$$\begin{aligned}
\left(P + \frac{a}{V_m^2}\right)(V_m - b) &= RT
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: At Critical Point**

Both inflection point conditions apply:
$$\begin{aligned}
\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial V_m}\right)_{T_c} &= 0 \quad \text{and} \quad \left(\frac{\partial^2 P}{\partial V_m^2}\right)_{T_c} = 0
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Solve for Critical Constants**

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{m,c} &= 3b \\
T_c &= \frac{8a}{27Rb} \\
P_c &= \frac{a}{27b^2}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Reduced Equation of State**

Substituting back yields the universal van der Waals equation:
$$\begin{aligned}
\left(P_r + \frac{3}{V_r^2}\right)\left(V_r - \frac{1}{3}\right) &= \frac{8}{3} T_r
\end{aligned}$$

This shows that **all fluids behave identically in reduced coordinates** — the principle of corresponding states.

**Step 5: Implication for SFE**

For scCO₂: $T_c = 304.25$ K (31.1 °C), $P_c = 73.8$ bar.
At T = 35 °C = 308.15 K and P = 100 bar:
$$\begin{aligned}
T_r &= \frac{308.15}{304.25} = 1.013 \quad (> 1 \checkmark) \\
P_r &= \frac{100}{73.8} = 1.355 \quad (> 1 \checkmark)
\end{aligned}$$

Both conditions satisfied → **supercritical state** confirmed.

---

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Extraction Techniques

| Feature | Fractional | Differential | ATPE | RME | SFE |
|---------|-----------|--------------|------|-----|-----|
| **Principle** | Selective K for 2 solutes | Continuous mass transfer | 2 aqueous phases | Reverse micelle uptake | Tunable SCF solvating power |
| **Phase System** | Aq + Org | Aq + Org | Aq + Aq (both > 80% H₂O) | W/O microemulsion | scCO₂ + solid/liquid |
| **Best For** | Separating similar solutes | Large-volume continuous operation | Proteins, enzymes, gentle | Proteins, hydrophilic biomolecules | Non-polar to medium-polar solutes |
| **Selectivity Source** | Difference in K | Equilibrium + kinetics | Electrochemical potential | Electrostatic / hydrophobic | Solubility in scCO₂ |
| **Scale** | Batch / continuous | Continuous (columns) | Batch | Batch | Batch / continuous |
| **Mild Conditions?** | Moderate | Moderate | Yes (gentle) | Yes (gentle) | Yes (low T) |
| **KTU Relevance** | High | High | Very High | High | Very High |
| **Key Equation** | Kremser | Z = HTU·NTU | Y = 100/(1+1/KV_R) | r_c ∝ W₀ | T_r > 1, P_r > 1 |

---

### 3.7 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

**Problem:**
An aqueous feed of volume 1000 L contains penicillin at 5 g/L. It is to be extracted with amyl acetate (K = 50) using three cross-current stages with equal solvent volumes of 200 L each. Calculate the final raffinate concentration.

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Calculate E for each stage**

$$\begin{aligned}
E &= K \cdot (S/F) = 50 \cdot (200/1000) = 10
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Apply Kremser equation for cross-current**

$$\begin{aligned}
x_N &= \frac{x_f}{(1+E)^N} = \frac{5}{(1+10)^3} = \frac{5}{1331}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Final concentration**

$$\begin{aligned}
x_N &= 0.00376 \text{ g/L} \\
\Rightarrow \text{Recovery} &= \frac{5 - 0.00376}{5} \times 100 = 99.92\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Verify with mass balance**

$$\begin{aligned}
m_{extracted} &= 5 \times 1000 - 0.00376 \times 1000 = 4996.24 \text{ g} \\
\text{Check:} \quad &\sum_{j=1}^{3} y_j \cdot S = (5 - 0.00376) \cdot 1000 = 4996.24 \text{ g} \checkmark
\end{aligned}$$

## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Process Flow: Supercritical Fluid Extraction (SFE) System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[CO2 Cylinder<br/>or Recycle] --> B[Compressor<br/>P to 100-300 bar]
    B --> C[Heat Exchanger<br/>T to 35-60 C]
    C --> D[Extractor Vessel<br/>Feed Loaded]
    D --> E[Expansion Valve 1<br/>P drops to 50-80 bar]
    E --> F[Separator 1<br/>Solute 1 precipitates]
    F --> G[Expansion Valve 2<br/>P drops to 20-30 bar]
    G --> H[Separator 2<br/>Solute 2 precipitates]
    H --> I[Condenser<br/>CO2 liquefies]
    I --> A
    D --> D1[Solid Residue<br/>Spent Feed]
    F --> F1[Extract 1<br/>High P Recovery]
    H --> H1[Extract 2<br/>Trace Recovery]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** CO₂ is compressed, heated above T_c, passes through the extractor where it dissolves target solutes, then expands in two stages to precipitate solutes at different P/T conditions, and the CO₂ is recycled.

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between single-stage extraction and fractional extraction. State the role of the selectivity coefficient.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Single-Stage Extraction | Fractional Extraction |
|---------|------------------------|----------------------|
| Purpose | Transfer one solute to one solvent | Separate two or more solutes using two solvents |
| Number of solvents | One | Two |
| Distribution coefficient | Used directly | Used to compute selectivity |
| Output | One extract, one raffinate | Two extracts (one rich in A, one rich in B) |
| Application | Crude recovery, single solute | Separating solutes with similar K values |

**Selectivity coefficient (β_A/B):**

$$\beta_{A/B} = \frac{K_A}{K_B} = \frac{(C_A/C_B)_{extract}}{(C_A/C_B)_{raffinate}}$$

- β = 1 → **No separation** is possible.
- β >> 1 → **Good separation** of A from B is achievable.
- Practical threshold: β > 10 for economically viable separation.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Define supercritical fluid. Why is supercritical CO₂ the most commonly used SCF in bioseparation?**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:**
A supercritical fluid (SCF) is a substance existing above its **critical temperature (T_c)** and **critical pressure (P_c)**, possessing properties intermediate between a gas and a liquid.

**Why scCO₂ is preferred:**

1. **Low critical temperature (T_c = 31.1 °C)** — preserves heat-sensitive biomolecules (enzymes, vitamins, essential oils).
2. **Moderate critical pressure (P_c = 73.8 bar)** — achievable with standard industrial compressors.
3. **Non-toxic, non-flammable, GRAS** — safe for food and pharmaceutical applications.
4. **Cheap and abundant** — byproduct of fermentation, ammonia synthesis, and natural sources.
5. **Recyclable** — closed-loop operation minimizes operating cost.
6. **Leaves no solvent residue** — meets strict regulatory standards for food/pharma.
7. **Tunable selectivity** — small changes in P and T dramatically alter solvating power.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — On Fractional & Differential Extraction

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**(a) [7 Marks] Derive the Kremser equation for cross-current multi-stage extraction. An aqueous solution (1000 L) containing 0.05 kg/L of solute is extracted with 200 L of fresh solvent per stage in 3 cross-current stages. If K = 10, find the final raffinate concentration and percentage recovery.**

**Model Solution:**

**Derivation (5 marks):**

Consider a single stage with feed F, fresh solvent S, distribution coefficient K.

Stage 1 mass balance:
$$\begin{aligned}
F \cdot x_f &= F \cdot x_1 + S \cdot y_1 \\
F \cdot x_f &= F \cdot x_1 + S \cdot K \cdot x_1 \\
x_1 &= \frac{x_f}{1 + K(S/F)} = \frac{x_f}{1 + E}
\end{aligned}$$

For N cross-current stages with equal S and fresh solvent each time:
$$\begin{aligned}
x_N &= \frac{x_f}{(1+E)^N}
\end{aligned}$$

Solving for N:
$$\begin{aligned}
N &= \frac{\ln(x_f / x_N)}{\ln(1+E)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Numerical Solution (2 marks):**

Given: F = 1000 L, S = 200 L/stage, N = 3, K = 10, $x_f$ = 0.05 kg/L.

$$\begin{aligned}
E &= K \cdot (S/F) = 10 \cdot (200/1000) = 2
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
x_3 &= \frac{0.05}{(1+2)^3} = \frac{0.05}{27} = 1.852 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/L}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Recovery (\%)} &= \frac{x_f - x_3}{x_f} \times 100 = \frac{0.05 - 0.001852}{0.05} \times 100 = 96.30\%
\end{aligned}$$

---

**(b) [7 Marks] Explain differential extraction with a counter-current contactor. Define HTU and NTU. A differential extractor has HTU_OL = 0.6 m. If the required NTU_OL is 4.5, calculate the height of the extractor. State the relationship between HTU_OL, HTU_L, and HTU_G.**

**Model Solution:**

**Differential Extraction Explanation (3 marks):**

Differential extraction is a continuous counter-current operation where the two phases flow through a column contactor (packed column, spray column, or rotating disc contactor). The composition varies continuously along the column height, with no discrete equilibrium stages. Mass transfer is governed by the rate of solute transfer per unit volume, characterized by the overall mass transfer coefficient $K_L a$.

**HTU and NTU Definitions (2 marks):**

- **HTU (Height of a Transfer Unit):** The height of contactor required to effect one transfer unit of composition change. HTU represents the **efficiency** of mass transfer — small HTU means efficient contact.

$$HTU_{OL} = \frac{L}{K_L a S_c}$$

- **NTU (Number of Transfer Units):** A measure of the **difficulty of separation** — the total composition change required.

$$NTU_{OL} = \int_{x_N}^{x_f} \frac{dx}{x - x^*}$$

**Numerical Calculation (1 mark):**

$$\begin{aligned}
Z &= HTU_{OL} \times NTU_{OL} = 0.6 \times 4.5 = 2.7 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}$$

**HTU Relationship (1 mark):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{1}{HTU_{OL}} = \frac{1}{HTU_L} + \frac{m}{HTU_G}
\end{aligned}$$

Where m = slope of equilibrium line, and HTU_L, HTU_G are the contributions from raffinate-phase and extract-phase resistances, respectively.

---

#### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — On ATPE, RME, and SFE (Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks] Describe the principle of Aqueous Two-Phase Extraction (ATPE). Discuss the PEG-salt and PEG-dextran systems. A protein has a partition coefficient K = 8 in a PEG-salt ATPE system. If the volume ratio $V_T / V_B = 1.5$, calculate the percentage recovery in the top phase.**

**Model Solution:**

**ATPE Principle (2 marks):**
ATPE is a liquid-liquid extraction technique in which both phases contain ≥ 80% water. Above a critical concentration, two water-soluble solutes (e.g., PEG + salt) form immiscible aqueous phases. Biomolecules partition based on hydrophobicity, charge, and surface properties. The technique is **gentle, biocompatible, and scalable**, ideal for proteins, enzymes, and nucleic acids.

**PEG-Salt System (1.5 marks):**
- Top phase: PEG-rich (lighter)
- Bottom phase: salt-rich (denser, e.g., phosphate or sulfate)
- Advantages: cheap, low viscosity, easy phase separation
- Disadvantages: high salt may denature sensitive proteins

**PEG-Dextran System (1.5 marks):**
- Top phase: PEG-rich
- Bottom phase: dextran-rich
- Advantages: both phases are non-denaturing, ideal for sensitive enzymes
- Disadvantages: dextran is expensive, high viscosity makes handling difficult

**Numerical Solution (2 marks):**

Given: K = 8, $V_R = V_T/V_B = 1.5$.

$$\begin{aligned}
Y_T(\%) &= \frac{100}{1 + \frac{1}{K \cdot V_R}} = \frac{100}{1 + \frac{1}{8 \times 1.5}} \\
&= \frac{100}{1 + \frac{1}{12}} = \frac{100}{1.0833} \\
&= 92.31\%
\end{aligned}$$

---

**(b) [7 Marks] Explain the principle of Reverse Micellar Extraction (RME). State the role of W₀ and explain forward and backward extraction. Discuss the principle and applications of Supercritical Fluid Extraction with scCO₂.**

**Model Solution:**

**RME Principle (2 marks):**
Reverse micelles are nanometer-sized water droplets stabilized by surfactant molecules (e.g., AOT) in a non-polar organic solvent. The surfactant's polar head groups point inward, enclosing a water core. Hydrophilic biomolecules (proteins, enzymes) are solubilized within this water core, enabling their selective extraction from an aqueous feed.

**W₀ Parameter (1 mark):**
$$W_0 = \frac{[H_2O]}{[Surfactant]}$$

W₀ controls the size of the water core. Higher W₀ → larger core → can accommodate bigger proteins. Typical range: 5–50. For AOT/isooctane: $r_c \text{ (nm)} \approx 0.175 W_0$.

**Forward Extraction (1 mark):**
- pH of feed is adjusted **below the protein's pI** (isoelectric point).
- Protein becomes **positively charged**.
- Electrostatic attraction with anionic AOT head groups drives solubilization into the micellar water core.
- Protein is transferred from aqueous feed → organic micellar phase.

**Backward Extraction (1 mark):**
- Micellar phase is contacted with a fresh aqueous stripping solution.
- pH is raised **above pI** OR **high KCl concentration** (0.5–2 M) is added.
- Electrostatic attraction is disrupted; protein is released into the stripping aqueous phase.
- Protein is recovered in concentrated form.

**SFE Principle (1 mark):**
SFE uses a fluid above its critical point as the extraction solvent. The fluid has gas-like diffusivity (fast penetration) and liquid-like density (good solvating power). Selectivity is controlled by varying **pressure and temperature**.

**SFE Applications (1 mark):**
- **Decaffeination of coffee and tea** (most famous industrial use)
- **Extraction of essential oils** (peppermint, lavender, citrus)
- **Removal of fat and cholesterol** from foods
- **Extraction of nutraceuticals** (omega-3, lycopene, astaxanthin)
- **Pharmaceutical applications** — purification of APIs, residual solvent removal
- **Environmental analysis** — extraction of PAHs, PCBs from soil

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Lose Marks in This Module:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing "E" in Kremser equation:** Many students write E = K·F/S instead of E = K·S/F. The correct definition is **E = (Slope of equilibrium line) × (Solvent flow rate / Feed flow rate) = K · (S/F)**. This single error can cost 3-4 marks.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting to specify reduced coordinates:** When asked "is the system supercritical?", students often state T and P values without verifying **T_r > 1 AND P_r > 1**. Always show the reduced property calculations.
> 
> 3. **Mixing up micelle types:** Normal micelle (head out, water outside) vs reverse micelle (head in, water core inside) is frequently confused. Remember: **reverse micelle = water-in-oil (W/O) microemulsion**.
> 
> 4. **Omitting the binodal curve discussion in ATPE:** Just defining ATPE is not enough — examiners expect a mention of the **phase diagram, tie lines, and partition coefficient K = C_T/C_B**.
> 
> 5. **Confusing W₀ with CMC:** W₀ is **water-to-surfactant molar ratio** in the micelle, NOT the Critical Micelle Concentration (CMC). CMC is the surfactant concentration at which micelles begin to form.
> 
> 6. **Calculation errors in yield formula:** ATPE yield is Y = 100·(K·V_R)/(1 + K·V_R), NOT 100/(1 + K·V_R). Be careful with placement of terms.
> 
> 7. **HTU vs HETP confusion:** HTU is a continuous-contact concept; HETP (Height Equivalent to a Theoretical Plate) is the stage-based equivalent. They are related but not identical.
> 
> 8. **Not stating T_c and P_c values for scCO₂:** KTU examiners expect you to recall **T_c = 31.1 °C, P_c = 73.8 bar** for CO₂. No partial credit for vague statements.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Print This Section Before the Exam**

**FRACTIONAL EXTRACTION**
- Purpose: Separate two solutes with similar K values
- Uses **two solvents** (S for A, S′ for B)
- **Selectivity:** $\beta_{A/B} = K_A / K_B$ → must be >> 1 for separation
- **Kremser equation** for stage calculation: $N = \ln[(x_f - y^*/m)/(x_N - y^*/m)] / \ln E$

**DIFFERENTIAL EXTRACTION**
- Continuous counter-current operation in a column
- **Z = HTU × NTU** (height calculation)
- No discrete stages; uses mass transfer rate $K_L a$
- Suitable for large throughput, e.g., petroleum refining
- HTU_OL combines both phase resistances: $1/HTU_{OL} = 1/HTU_L + m/HTU_G$

**AQUEOUS TWO-PHASE EXTRACTION (ATPE)**
- Both phases ≥ 80% water — **biocompatible**
- PEG-salt (cheap) vs PEG-dextran (gentle) systems
- **Partition coefficient:** $K = C_T / C_B$
- **Yield:** $Y(\%) = 100 \cdot K V_R / (1 + K V_R)$
- **Binodal curve** + **tie-line** describe the phase diagram
- Ideal for **proteins, enzymes, antibodies**

**REVERSE MICELLAR EXTRACTION (RME)**
- **AOT/isooctane** is the model system
- **W₀ = [H₂O]/[Surfactant]** controls water core size
- **Forward:** pH < pI → protein positively charged → enters micelle
- **Backward:** pH > pI or high [KCl] → protein released
- Two-stage process: forward + back extraction
- Suitable for **hydrophilic proteins, low-molecular-weight biomolecules**

**SUPERCRITICAL FLUID EXTRACTION (SFE)**
- **scCO₂** is the standard SCF: **T_c = 31.1 °C, P_c = 73.8 bar**
- Critical conditions: **T_r > 1 AND P_r > 1** (both must hold)
- Properties: gas-like diffusivity + liquid-like density
- **Peng-Robinson EOS** used for density calculations
- Two-separator design recovers solutes at different pressures
- **Applications:** decaffeination, essential oils, nutraceuticals, environmental analysis
- Solvent-free, recyclable, GRAS

**MASTER MNEMONIC — "FDRAS"**
Remember the **FIVE methods** by their first letters:
- **F** — Fractional (two solutes, two solvents)
- **D** — Differential (continuous, HTU·NTU)
- **R** — Reverse Micellar (W/O microemulsion, AOT)
- **A** — Aqueous Two-Phase (PEG + salt/dextran, both aqueous)
- **S** — Supercritical Fluid (scCO₂, T_r > 1, P_r > 1)

**FORMULAS TO MEMORIZE VERBATIM (in order of importance):**
1. Kremser: $N = \ln(x_f/x_N)/\ln(1+E)$ for cross-current
2. ATPE yield: $Y = 100 \cdot K V_R/(1+K V_R)$
3. HTU-NTU: $Z = HTU \cdot NTU$
4. W₀: $W_0 = [H_2O]/[S]$
5. SCF: $T_r = T/T_c$ and $P_r = P/P_c$

**NUMBERS TO RECALL COLD:**
- scCO₂: T_c = 31.1 °C, P_c = 73.8 bar
- scH₂O: T_c = 374 °C, P_c = 220.6 bar
- AOT/isooctane: $r_c \approx 0.175 W_0$ (nm)
- β > 10 → economically viable separation
- ≥ 80% water in both phases → ATPE condition

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Solid-liquid Extraction in Bioprocessing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEBBT525 - BIOSEPARATION TECHNOLOGY > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Solid-liquid Extraction in Bioprocessing ## 1.
