Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Solid-Liquid Extraction (Leaching)** is a downstream bioseparation operation in which a soluble fraction (the **solute**) is separated from an insoluble solid phase (the **inert matrix/raffinate**) by selectively dissolving the solute in a suitable **liquid solvent (extractant)**. The product-rich liquid leaving the extractor is termed the **extract**, while the solute-depleted solid residue is called the **spent solid / marc / raffinate**. In bioprocessing, solid-liquid extraction is a critical **primary recovery step** used to isolate: - Intracellular metabolites (enzymes, proteins, antibiotics) from microbial biomass. - Bioactive phytochemicals (alkaloids, flavonoids, phenolics) from plant tissues. - Edible oils from oilseeds (soybean, sunflower, groundnut). - Polysaccharides and sugars from lignocellulosic biomass. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Tea Bag" Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Analogy — Making a cup of tea:** A tea bag is essentially a porous solid matrix (tea leaves) holding soluble compounds (tannins, theaflavins, caffeine) trapped inside the cells. When you dip the bag in hot water, the solutes **diffuse out** along a concentration gradient, leaving behind the spent, flavorless leaves. The water turns brown because the solutes have transferred from the **solid phase** to the **liquid phase**. That is solid-liquid extraction, observed over a teacup. In industrial bioprocessing, we simply replace the tea bag with a **biomass cake, oilseed meal, or ground plant material**, and the cup of water with a process-grade solvent like hexane, ethanol, or water at controlled pH and temperature. ### 1.3 Key Terminology at a Glance | Term | Engineering Meaning | |---|---| | **Solute** | Desired bioproduct locked in the solid (e.g., oil, enzyme, pigment). | | **Solvent / Extractant** | Liquid used to dissolve and carry the solute. | | **Solid (Inert)** | Insoluble matrix retaining the solute before extraction. | | **Extract** | Solvent-rich solution leaving the extractor carrying the solute. | | **Raffinate / Spent Solid** | Solute-depleted solid leaving the extractor. | | **Underflow** | Solid + entrained liquid exiting a stage (carries residual solvent + solute). | | **Overflow** | Clear liquid extract leaving a stage. | | **Slurry** | Suspension of solid particles in solvent. | ### 1.4 Standard Metrics Used - **S/F ratio** — mass of solvent fed per mass of solid fed. - **Y_N** — mass of underflow per mass of solute-free inert solid (key for stage design). - **x_N** — mass fraction of solute in the underflow liquid. - **X_N** — mass ratio of solute to inert solid in the underflow. - **Stage efficiency E_M** — typically **70–90%** for industrial extractors. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The "Why" Behind Solid-Liquid Extraction In many bioprocesses, the product is **intracellular** or **trapped within a solid matrix**. Mechanical disruption (homogenization, bead milling) releases the contents, but to recover the target biomolecule in a usable, concentrated form, we rely on **selective dissolution** into a chosen solvent. The driving force is the **chemical potential gradient (concentration gradient)** of the solute between the solid pores and the bulk solvent. ### 2.2 Mechanism — Three Sequential Steps Solid-liquid extraction proceeds via three coupled mass-transfer steps: 1. **Solvent Penetration & Pore Diffusion** — Solvent enters the porous solid matrix; the solute dissolves into the retained liquid film coating the solid particles. 2. **Internal Diffusion** — The dissolved solute migrates from the interior of the particle to the particle surface, governed by **Fick's Law of Diffusion**: $$J = -D_{eff} \cdot \frac{dC}{dx}$$ where $D_{eff}$ is the effective diffusivity (m²/s), and $dC/dx$ is the concentration gradient. 3. **External Mass Transfer (Convective)** — The solute transfers from the particle surface into the bulk solvent stream, driven by turbulence and concentration difference. > [!NOTE] > **Rate-Limiting Step:** In most bioprocess extractions, **internal diffusion** is the slowest (rate-controlling) step, because the cell wall or lignocellulosic matrix offers significant resistance. Hence, **particle size reduction** is the single most powerful operational lever to accelerate extraction. ### 2.3 Classification of Extraction Operations Solid-liquid extraction can be classified by contact pattern: - **Single-Stage Extraction** — Solid and solvent contact in one vessel, then separate. Simple, but extraction efficiency is limited. - **Multi-Stage Co-current** — Solvent and solid pass through several stages in the same direction. Improvement is modest after 2–3 stages. - **Multi-Stage Counter-current** — Solvent and solid flow in **opposite directions** through multiple stages. **This is the industrial standard** because it yields the highest recovery with the lowest solvent consumption. - **Continuous Counter-current Extractors** — Used at industrial scale (e.g., **Bollman, Hildebrandt, Rotocel, Kennedy, de Smet, Bonotto** extractors for oils; **Soxhlet apparatus** in the laboratory). ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master these equations — they are the highest-yield formulas for KTU exams on this module.** | # | Equation | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $J = -D_{eff} \dfrac{dC}{dx}$ | Fick's First Law of Diffusion | mol·m⁻²·s⁻¹ | | 2 | $\dfrac{M_t}{M_\infty} = 1 - \dfrac{6}{\pi^2} \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \dfrac{1}{n^2} \exp\left(-D_{eff}\,n^2\pi^2 t \,/\, r^2\right)$ | Crank's unsteady diffusion solution (extraction kinetics in spherical particles) | dimensionless | | 3 | $L_0 + V_{N+1} = L_N + V_1$ | Overall mass balance in counter-current extraction (N stages) | kg/h | | 4 | $L_0 \, x_0 + V_{N+1} \, y_{N+1} = L_N \, x_N + V_1 \, y_1$ | Solute mass balance across N stages | kg solute/h | | 5 | $Y_N = \dfrac{\text{mass of underflow}}{\text{mass of solute-free inert solid}}$ | Underflow-to-inert ratio (constant in constant-underflow operation) | kg/kg | | 6 | $R = \dfrac{V_1}{L_0}$ | Solvent-to-solid mass ratio (S/F) | kg/kg | | 7 | $\eta_{stage} = \dfrac{y_{n+1} - y_n}{y^* - y_n}$ | Murphree stage efficiency | dimensionless | | 8 | $N_{ideal} = \dfrac{\ln\left[\dfrac{x_0 - y_{N+1}/K}{x_N - y_{N+1}/K}\right]}{\ln K \cdot (V/L)}$ | Number of ideal stages (Kremser equation) | dimensionless | | 9 | $Re_p = \dfrac{\rho \, d_p \, u}{\mu}$ | Particle Reynolds number (for agitator design) | dimensionless | | 10 | $Sh = 2.0 + 0.6 \, Re_p^{1/2} \, Sc^{1/3}$ | Ranz-Marshall correlation for external mass transfer | dimensionless | > **Note on Units:** In the formulas above, $L$ denotes underflow (solid + entrained liquid) mass flow rate, $V$ denotes overflow (clear extract) mass flow rate, $x$ is solute mass fraction in the underflow, and $y$ is solute mass fraction in the overflow. $K$ is the partition/distribution coefficient. ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility Solid-liquid extraction is the **backbone of several bioprocess industries**: - **Edible oil industry** — Hexane extraction of soybean, canola, and sunflower oil. - **Pharmaceutical industry** — Recovery of artemisinin, taxol, and morphine alkaloids from plant biomass. - **Fermentation downstream** — Primary recovery of intracellular antibiotics (e.g., erythromycin, tetracycline) after cell disruption. - **Food industry** — Sugar extraction from sugar beet, pectin from citrus peel, caffeine from coffee beans. - **Biofuel industry** — Pretreatment and saccharification of lignocellulose. > [!NOTE] > **Green Solvent Trend:** Modern bioprocessing increasingly favors **supercritical CO₂, subcritical water, ionic liquids, and deep eutectic solvents (DES)** to replace petrochemical solvents like hexane — driven by sustainability and food-grade safety requirements. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Single-Stage Solid-Liquid Extraction Mass Balance > **Problem Setup:** $L_0$ kg/h of solid feed (containing solute at mass fraction $x_0$) is contacted with $V_1$ kg/h of pure solvent. After equilibrium, the underflow $L_1$ contains the spent solid plus entrained solution of mass fraction $x_1$, and the overflow $V_1$ (clean extract) has solute mass fraction $y_1$. **Step 1 — Total Mass Balance** Mass entering the stage must equal mass leaving: $$L_0 + V_1 = L_1 + V_1 \quad \text{(if we assume solvent-free feed)}$$ Simplifying for the case of solvent-free feed and pure solvent: $$L_0 = L_1 \quad \text{(constant solids throughput)}$$ This means the **mass of inert solid + the entrained liquid remains constant** in each stage. **Step 2 — Solute Mass Balance** The solute entering with the solid feed plus the solute entering with fresh solvent equals the solute leaving in underflow and overflow: $$L_0 \, x_0 + V_1 \, y_{in} = L_1 \, x_1 + V_1 \, y_1$$ For **fresh (pure) solvent**, $y_{in} = 0$, so: $$L_0 \, x_0 = L_1 \, x_1 + V_1 \, y_1$$ **Step 3 — Introducing the Constant Underflow Assumption** If we assume that for every 1 kg of inert solid, the underflow carries $Y$ kg of solution (where $Y$ is constant), then: $$L = \text{inert solid} \times (1 + Y)$$ Define: - $A$ = mass of inert (solute-free) solid = $L_0 (1 - x_0)$ (per unit time) - $X$ = mass ratio of solute to inert solid in the underflow = $x / (1 - x)$ (approximately $x$ for dilute systems) - $Y$ = mass of solution per mass of inert solid (constant in **constant-underflow** operation) Then: $$L_0 = A(1 + Y), \quad L_1 = A(1 + Y), \quad V_1 = V_1$$ **Step 4 — Solute Balance in Terms of $A$, $X$, $Y$, and $V$** $$A \cdot X_0 + V_N \, y_N = A \cdot X_N + V_1 \, y_1$$ Rearranging for the exit solute concentration in the extract: $$y_1 = \dfrac{A(X_0 - X_N) + V_N \, y_N}{V_1}$$ **Step 5 — Equilibrium Tie Line** At equilibrium, the overflow and underflow are related by the **partition coefficient K**: $$y^* = K \cdot x_N \quad \text{(where } x_N \text{ is the mass fraction in the underflow)}$$ For **constant-underflow operation**, since $Y$ is constant and $K$ is constant, the **operating line and equilibrium line are straight**, which greatly simplifies the graphical McCabe-Thiele-type solution for multi-stage extraction. ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Counter-Current Multi-Stage Stage Requirement (Kremser Equation) For a counter-current extractor with $N$ ideal stages: **Overall Solute Balance across the Train:** $$L_0 \, X_0 + V_{N+1} \, y_{N+1} = L_N \, X_N + V_1 \, y_1$$ **Per-Stage Operating Line Equation** (for stage $n$): $$y_{n+1} = \dfrac{L}{V} \, X_n + \left( y_1 - \dfrac{L}{V} X_0 \right)$$ **Iterative substitution through $N$ stages** (Kremser, 1930) gives the closed-form solution: $$N = \dfrac{\ln\left[\dfrac{X_0 - y_{N+1}/K}{X_N - y_{N+1}/K}\right]}{\ln(A)}$$ where the **absorption factor** $A$ is defined as: $$A = \dfrac{L}{K \cdot V}$$ **Derivation sketch:** For a single ideal stage, the operating point and equilibrium point satisfy: $$y_n = K \cdot X_n \quad \text{and} \quad y_{n+1} = \dfrac{L}{V} X_n + b$$ Substituting the equilibrium relation into the operating line: $$y_{n+1} = \dfrac{L}{KV} y_n + b = A \cdot y_n + b$$ Recursively applying this through $N$ stages and applying the boundary condition $y_{N+1} = y_{N+1}$ (the entering solvent) yields the **Kremser equation** quoted above. ### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Single-Stage Extraction > **Problem:** A ground oilseed meal containing **0.20 mass fraction oil** is to be leached with pure hexane in a single-stage extractor. If the solvent-to-solid mass ratio (S/F) is **1.5**, and the underflow retains **0.5 kg solution per kg inert solid**, determine the **fractional recovery of oil** assuming equilibrium with $K = 2$ (mass fraction basis). **Solution — Step 1: Define basis** Take basis: **1 kg of oilseed meal feed ($L_0$)**. - Oil mass = $0.20 \times 1 = 0.20$ kg - Inert solid mass $A = 1 - 0.20 = 0.80$ kg **Solution — Step 2: Solvent fed** $$V_1 = (S/F) \times L_0 = 1.5 \times 1 = 1.5 \text{ kg hexane}$$ **Solution — Step 3: Underflow mass** $$L_1 = A(1 + Y) = 0.80 \times (1 + 0.5) = 1.20 \text{ kg}$$ **Solution — Step 4: Overflow (clean extract) mass** $$V_1 = L_0 + V_{in} - L_1 = 1 + 1.5 - 1.2 = 1.3 \text{ kg}$$ **Solution — Step 5: Solute balance** $$L_0 x_0 + V_{in} \cdot 0 = L_1 x_1 + V_1 y_1$$ $$(1)(0.20) + 0 = (1.2)(x_1) + (1.3)(y_1)$$ $$0.20 = 1.2 x_1 + 1.3 y_1 \quad \text{...(i)}$$ **Solution — Step 6: Equilibrium relation** $$y_1 = K \cdot x_1 = 2 x_1 \quad \text{...(ii)}$$ **Solution — Step 7: Substitute (ii) into (i)** $$0.20 = 1.2 x_1 + 1.3(2 x_1) = 1.2 x_1 + 2.6 x_1 = 3.8 x_1$$ $$x_1 = \dfrac{0.20}{3.8} = 0.0526$$ **Solution — Step 8: Oil in extract** $$y_1 = 2 \times 0.0526 = 0.1053$$ Oil recovered in overflow = $V_1 \times y_1 = 1.3 \times 0.1053 = 0.1369$ kg **Solution — Step 9: Fractional recovery** $$\eta = \dfrac{0.1369}{0.20} = 0.6845 = \mathbf{68.45\%}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Interpretation:** A single-stage extractor recovers only **68.45%** of the oil. To achieve **>95%** recovery (the industrial target for edible oil extraction), a **counter-current extractor with 4–5 stages** is necessary, as predicted by the Kremser equation. This is exactly why **counter-current multi-stage extraction is the industrial standard**. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram — Solid-Liquid Extraction Process Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Biomass Feed
Plant Material] --> B[Size Reduction
Crushing or Milling] B --> C[Conditioning
Drying or Moisture Adjustment] C --> D[Solid Liquid Extractor
Multi Stage Counter Current] E[Fresh Solvent
Hexane or Ethanol] --> D D --> F[Extract Overflow
Solvent plus Solute] D --> G[Spent Solid Raffinate
Underflow] F --> H[Solvent Recovery
Distillation or Evaporation] H --> I[Crude Bioproduct
Final Concentrate] H --> J[Recycled Solvent
Back to Extractor] G --> K[Solid Waste Disposal
or Composting] style D fill:#FFD580,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style F fill:#B5E6A2,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style G fill:#D3D3D3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style I fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` ### 4.2 Block Diagram — Multi-Stage Counter-Current Extractor Topology ```mermaid flowchart LR SolidFeed[Solid Feed L0] --> Stage1 Stage1[Stage 1] --> Stage2[Stage 2] Stage2 --> Stage3[Stage 3] Stage3 --> StageN[Stage N] StageN --> Raffinate[Spent Solid LN] SolventFresh[Fresh Solvent VN plus 1] --> StageN StageN --> StageMid2[Stage N minus 1] StageMid2 --> StageMid1[Stage 2] StageMid1 --> ExtractFinal[Rich Extract V1] ExtractFinal --> SolventRec[Solvent Recovery Unit] SolventRec --> Bioproduct[Concentrated Bioproduct] SolventRec --> SolventFresh style Stage1 fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#333 style Stage2 fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#333 style Stage3 fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#333 style StageN fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#333 style ExtractFinal fill:#A2D9CE,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style Bioproduct fill:#F1948A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` ### 4.3 Block Diagram — Equipment Classification Hierarchy ```mermaid flowchart TD SLE[Solid Liquid Extraction Equipment] --> B1[Batch Extractors] SLE --> C1[Continuous Extractors] B1 --> B1a[Stirred Tank] B1 --> B1b[Soxhlet Apparatus] B1 --> B1c[Percolation Vessel] C1 --> C1a[Immersion Type] C1 --> C1b[Percolation Type] C1a --> C1a1[Agitated Vessel Train] C1a --> C1a2[Bollman Extractor] C1a --> C1a3[Bonotto Extractor] C1b --> C1b1[Rotocel Extractor] C1b --> C1b2[Hildebrandt Extractor] C1b --> C1b3[Kennedy Extractor] C1b --> C1b4[de Smet Extractor] style SLE fill:#FFD580,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style C1 fill:#B5E6A2,stroke:#333 style B1 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333 ``` ### 4.4 Block Diagram — Mass Transfer Mechanism in a Solid Particle ```mermaid flowchart LR A[External Solvent Bulk
Low Concentration] --> B[External Boundary Layer
External Mass Transfer] B --> C[Particle Surface
Solute Concentration Cs] C --> D[Internal Pore Diffusion
Fick Law Governed] D --> E[Particle Core
High Solute Concentration C0] F[Agitation and Temperature] --> B F --> D G[Particle Size Reduction] --> D style A fill:#A2D9CE,stroke:#333 style E fill:#F1948A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style C fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#333 style D fill:#D3D3D3,stroke:#333 ``` ### 4.5 Selection Decision Flowchart ```mermaid flowchart TD Start[Select Extractor Type] --> Q1{Feed is solid
or slurry?} Q1 -->|Solid Particles| Q2{Continuous or
Batch Operation?} Q1 -->|Slurry| Q3[Use Immersion Type Extractor] Q2 -->|Batch| Q4[Stirred Tank or Soxhlet] Q2 -->|Continuous| Q5{Solvent retention
in solids tolerable?} Q5 -->|Yes| Q6[Percolation Type Extractor] Q5 -->|No| Q3 Q6 --> Q6a[Rotocel or Hildebrandt] Q3 --> Q3a[Bollman or Bonotto] style Start fill:#FFD580,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style Q6a fill:#A2D9CE,stroke:#333 style Q3a fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333 ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question Paper]** **Define solid-liquid extraction. List any two industrial applications in bioprocessing.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **Definition (2 marks):** Solid-liquid extraction, also called **leaching**, is a separation process in which a soluble fraction (the solute) is selectively removed from a solid phase by dissolution into a suitable liquid solvent. The solvent-rich liquid leaving the extractor is termed the **extract**, and the solute-depleted solid is called the **raffinate** or **spent solid**. > **Applications (1 mark — any two):** > 1. Recovery of **edible oils** from oilseeds using hexane (soybean, sunflower, canola). > 2. Extraction of **antibiotics** such as erythromycin or tetracycline from fermentation biomass. > 3. Recovery of **bioactive phytochemicals** like artemisinin from *Artemisia annua* using ethanol. > 4. Sugar extraction from **sugar beet** using hot water. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Supplementary Paper]** **Differentiate between single-stage and multi-stage counter-current solid-liquid extraction. Why is counter-current operation industrially preferred?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Feature | Single-Stage | Multi-Stage Counter-Current | |---|---|---| | Contact vessels | One | Many (typically 4–8) | | Solvent flow | Once-through | Fresh solvent enters the last stage; extract leaves the first | | Recovery efficiency | Low (~60–70%) | High (>95%) | | Solvent consumption | High | Low | | Industrial use | Lab scale / Soxhlet | Oil, sugar, pharmaceutical industry | > **Why counter-current is preferred (1 mark):** In counter-current operation, the **freshest solvent contacts the most extracted (solute-lean) solid**, while the **richest solvent contacts the freshest solid**. This maintains a **high concentration gradient** at every stage, maximizing driving force and yielding **higher recovery with less solvent** than single-stage or co-current operation. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** > **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Adapted from Past Pattern]** > **(a)** A ground oilseed meal containing **25% by mass oil** is to be leached with pure hexane in a **single-stage extractor**. The solvent-to-solid feed mass ratio is **2.0**, and the underflow carries **0.4 kg solution per kg of inert solid**. Assuming an equilibrium distribution coefficient of $K = 1.8$ (mass fraction basis), determine the **mass of oil recovered in the extract** and the **fractional oil recovery**. Assume the feed is solvent-free. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the construction and working of a **Bollman extractor**. List two advantages and one limitation. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution to Question A(a) — 7 Marks **Given Data:** - Feed $L_0 = 1$ kg basis (solvent-free) - Oil mass fraction $x_0 = 0.25$ - Inert solid $A = 0.75$ kg - Solvent-to-solid ratio $S/F = 2.0 \Rightarrow V_{in} = 2.0$ kg - Underflow ratio $Y = 0.4$ kg solution/kg inert - $K = 1.8$ **Step 1 (1 mark): Compute underflow and overflow masses** $$L_1 = A(1 + Y) = 0.75 \times (1 + 0.4) = 0.75 \times 1.4 = 1.05 \text{ kg}$$ $$V_1 = L_0 + V_{in} - L_1 = 1 + 2 - 1.05 = 1.95 \text{ kg}$$ **Step 2 (1 mark): Set up solute mass balance** $$L_0 x_0 = L_1 x_1 + V_1 y_1$$ $$(1)(0.25) = 1.05 x_1 + 1.95 y_1$$ $$0.25 = 1.05 x_1 + 1.95 y_1 \quad \text{...(i)}$$ **Step 3 (1 mark): Apply equilibrium** $$y_1 = K x_1 = 1.8 x_1 \quad \text{...(ii)}$$ **Step 4 (2 marks): Solve simultaneously** Substituting (ii) into (i): $$0.25 = 1.05 x_1 + 1.95 (1.8 x_1) = 1.05 x_1 + 3.51 x_1 = 4.56 x_1$$ $$x_1 = \dfrac{0.25}{4.56} = 0.0548$$ $$y_1 = 1.8 \times 0.0548 = 0.0987$$ **Step 5 (1 mark): Oil in extract** $$m_{oil,extract} = V_1 \times y_1 = 1.95 \times 0.0987 = \mathbf{0.1925 \text{ kg}}$$ **Step 6 (1 mark): Fractional recovery** $$\eta = \dfrac{0.1925}{0.25} = \mathbf{77.0\%}$$ > **Result:** Only **77%** oil is recovered. To achieve >95% recovery, a **counter-current multi-stage extractor is required** — this motivates Part (b). --- #### Model Solution to Question A(b) — 7 Marks **Bollman Extractor (4 marks):** > A Bollman extractor is a **continuous, basket-type, percolation extractor** consisting of a series of **perforated baskets** mounted on an **endless chain or conveyor** inside a vertical, hermetically sealed vessel. The conveyor moves the baskets **slowly and continuously** through the extractor. **Working Principle (2 marks):** - **Solid feed** (crushed oilseed) is loaded into the baskets at the top of one side. - The baskets descend through a **down-flowing stream of miscella** (solvent + dissolved oil) on one leg — this is the **immersion leg**. - The baskets then ascend through a **counter-current stream of fresh solvent** sprayed from the top of the other leg — this is the **percolation leg**. - The **rich miscella** (extract) is collected at the bottom of the immersion leg and sent for solvent recovery. - The **spent solid** is dumped from the baskets at the top of the percolation leg. **Advantages (0.5 + 0.5 = 1 mark):** 1. Continuous operation with **high throughput**, ideal for large-scale oilseed processing. 2. **Counter-current contact** yields high oil recovery (~95%) with moderate solvent usage. **Limitation (0.5 mark):** 1. Moving mechanical parts (chains, baskets) inside the extractor are prone to wear; maintenance is intensive and the unit is **not ideal for very fine or sticky solids** that may clog basket perforations. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** > **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]** > **(a)** Derive the **Kremser equation** for the number of ideal stages in a counter-current solid-liquid extractor. State clearly all assumptions. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** Discuss the **factors affecting the rate of solid-liquid extraction** in bioprocessing. How does particle size influence the extraction kinetics? **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution to Question B(a) — 7 Marks **Assumptions (1 mark):** 1. Constant underflow ($Y$ = constant) in each stage. 2. Constant partition coefficient $K$. 3. Operating and equilibrium lines are **straight**. 4. Solvent is solute-free at entry. **Derivation (6 marks):** **Step 1 — Stage-wise operating line (1 mark):** For stage $n$, solute balance: $$L \cdot X_n + V \cdot y_{n+1} = L \cdot X_{n+1} + V \cdot y_n$$ Rearranging: $$y_{n+1} = \dfrac{L}{V} X_n + y_n - \dfrac{L}{V} X_{n+1}$$ For a linear equilibrium $y = K X$ and constant $L/V$, the operating line becomes: $$y_{n+1} = \dfrac{L}{V} X_n + b$$ **Step 2 — Single-stage relation (2 marks):** For one ideal stage, the overflow leaves in equilibrium with the underflow: $$y_n = K \cdot X_n \quad \text{and} \quad y_{n+1} = K \cdot X_{n+1}$$ Combining with operating line: $$X_{n+1} = \dfrac{1}{A} X_n + \dfrac{b}{K A}$$ where $A = L/(KV)$ is the **absorption factor**. **Step 3 — Telescoping through N stages (2 marks):** Recursively applying and using the geometric series: $$X_{N+1} = \left(\dfrac{1}{A}\right)^N X_1 + \dfrac{b}{K A} \cdot \sum_{i=0}^{N-1} A^{-i}$$ Applying the boundary condition $X_{N+1} = y_{N+1}/K$ (solvent entering) and rearranging: $$\boxed{N = \dfrac{\ln\left[\dfrac{X_0 - y_{N+1}/K}{X_N - y_{N+1}/K}\right]}{\ln(1/A)}}$$ Or equivalently with $A = L/(KV)$: $$N = \dfrac{\ln\left[\dfrac{X_0 - y_{N+1}/K}{X_N - y_{N+1}/K}\right]}{\ln(KV/L)}$$ **Step 4 — Interpretation (1 mark):** The Kremser equation gives the number of ideal stages required to reduce the solute concentration in the solid from $X_0$ to $X_N$, given the partition coefficient and the $L/V$ ratio. Real stages are obtained by dividing $N$ by the **Murphree stage efficiency** $E_M$. --- #### Model Solution to Question B(b) — 7 Marks **Factor 1 — Particle Size (2 marks):** > Per Fick's law and Crank's solution, the characteristic time for internal diffusion scales as $t \sim r^2/D_{eff}$. Reducing particle size (e.g., from 5 mm to 0.5 mm) reduces the diffusion path by 10×, accelerating extraction by **~100×**. However, excessively fine particles cause **filtration problems, channeling, and compaction** in percolation extractors. **Factor 2 — Temperature (1 mark):** > Higher temperature increases $D_{eff}$ (via the Stokes-Einstein relation $D \propto T/\mu$) and also increases **solute solubility** in the solvent. Most industrial extractions operate at **40–60°C** (above this, thermally labile bioproducts like enzymes or vitamins degrade). **Factor 3 — Solvent Choice (1 mark):** > The solvent must have **high selectivity** for the solute, low viscosity, chemical inertness, and be **recoverable** (low boiling point for distillation). Example: hexane for oils, ethanol for phenolics, water for sugars. **Factor 4 — Agitation / Mixing Intensity (1 mark):** > Increased agitation enhances the **external mass transfer coefficient** $k_c$ (per the Ranz-Marshall correlation), reducing the external boundary layer resistance. Typical agitator speeds are **50–200 RPM** in stirred tanks. **Factor 5 — Solid-to-Solvent Ratio (1 mark):** > A higher S/F ratio improves recovery but increases solvent recovery costs. An **optimal S/F ratio** minimizes total operating cost. **Factor 6 — pH and Ionic Strength (1 mark):** > For ionizable solutes (e.g., amino acids, alkaloids), pH drastically alters solubility. Adjusting pH to the **pKa ± 2 rule** can improve extraction efficiency by an order of magnitude. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting to subtract the underflow** when computing the overflow mass — a classic 2-mark error in numerical problems. > 2. **Confusing mass fraction $x$ with mass ratio $X$** — they are related by $X = x/(1-x)$. Use the correct one as required by the formula. > 3. **Mixing up "extract" and "raffinate"** definitions in short-answer questions — examiners deduct 1 mark immediately. > 4. **Omitting the constant-underflow assumption** when applying the Kremser equation — without it, the operating line is non-linear and the equation is invalid. > 5. **Saying "leaching is the same as washing"** in definitions — washing removes surface solutes; leaching extracts solutes from within the porous solid matrix. The distinction is worth 1 mark. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - [x] **Solid-liquid extraction = Leaching** — separates solute from inert solid using a liquid solvent. - [x] Three mass transfer steps: **solvent penetration → internal diffusion → external mass transfer**. - [x] **Fick's law** governs internal diffusion: $J = -D_{eff}(dC/dx)$. - [x] **Counter-current multi-stage extraction** is the industrial standard; gives >95% recovery. - [x] **Constant underflow assumption** linearizes the operating line and enables the Kremser equation. - [x] **Kremser equation** gives the number of ideal stages: $N = \ln[(X_0 - y_{N+1}/K)/(X_N - y_{N+1}/K)] / \ln(KV/L)$. - [x] **Single-stage recovery** is typically 60–80%; multi-stage counter-current achieves 95%+. - [x] **Particle size reduction** is the most powerful kinetic lever (extraction time $\propto r^2$). - [x] **Key equipment**: Bollman, Rotocel, Hildebrandt (continuous); Soxhlet, stirred tanks (batch). - [x] **Critical solvent properties**: selectivity, recoverability, low viscosity, chemical inertness. - [x] **Emerging green solvents**: supercritical CO₂, subcritical water, ionic liquids, deep eutectic solvents. - [x] **Crank's equation** for unsteady diffusion in spherical particles is the rigorous kinetic model. - [x] **Murphree efficiency** relates real stage performance to ideal stage performance. - [x] Industrial applications span **edible oils, antibiotics, phytochemicals, sugars, biofuels**. - [x] For KTU numericals, always **state assumptions, define basis, write balances, then solve**. - [x] Difference between **leaching (internal)** and **washing (surface)** is a frequently tested distinction.

topics.title: Evaporation: Heat Transfer Principles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT525 - BIOSEPARATION TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Evaporation: Heat Transfer Principles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Evaporation** is a unit operation in which a volatile solvent (most commonly water) is vaporised from a solution, slurry, or suspension by the application of heat, in order to produce a concentrated liquid product. In **bioseparation technology**, evaporation is primarily employed as a *pre-concentration step* to reduce the volume of dilute biological streams (fermentation broths, enzyme extracts, milk whey, herbal extracts, antibiotic solutions) before downstream operations such as drying, crystallization, or chromatographic purification.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 3 Definition:**
> *Evaporation* is the process of concentrating a solution by removing a portion of the solvent (usually water) through vaporisation, using a heat-transfer surface and a temperature driving force between a heating medium (typically steam) and the boiling liquid.

## 1.2 Heat Transfer in Evaporators — The Governing Principle

The **rate of evaporation** is fundamentally a **heat transfer problem**, not a mass transfer problem. The rate at which solvent can be vaporised is dictated by the rate at which heat can be transmitted *through the heating surface* (tube wall) from the condensing steam to the boiling liquid.

$$ q = m_s \cdot \lambda_s = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T $$

where:
- $q$ = rate of heat transfer (**W or kW**)
- $m_s$ = mass flow rate of steam (**kg/s**)
- $\lambda_s$ = latent heat of vaporisation of steam (**kJ/kg**)
- $U$ = overall heat transfer coefficient (**W/m²·K**)
- $A$ = heat transfer area (**m²**)
- $\Delta T$ = temperature driving force (**K or °C**)

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy (Real-World Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Kitchen Analogy → Industrial Evaporator**
>
> Imagine boiling milk in a steel pot on a gas stove. The flame heats the pot, the pot heats the milk, and water vapour escapes. In an *industrial evaporator*, the **flame is replaced by condensing steam** inside a closed jacket or tube bundle, the **pot is replaced by a heat exchanger surface** (typically a vertical or horizontal tube bundle), and the **water vapour is captured** in a condenser for recovery. The *rate* at which the milk thickens depends on (a) how hot the steam is, (b) how clean the pot wall is, and (c) how vigorously the milk circulates. These three ideas — *temperature difference*, *fouling resistance*, and *liquid velocity* — form the triad on which all evaporator heat transfer design is built.

## 1.4 Why Evaporation is Critical in Bioseparation

| Biological Product | Purpose of Evaporation | Typical Concentration Achieved |
|---|---|---|
| **Enzyme solutions** | Reduce volume before precipitation | 10–30% w/w |
| **Citric acid broth** | Pre-concentrate for crystallization | 30–50% w/w |
| **Antibiotic broths** (e.g. penicillin) | Volume reduction before extraction | 8–15% w/w |
| **Fruit juices / herbal extracts** | Shelf-life extension | 60–70% total solids |
| **Whey proteins** | Volume reduction before spray drying | 45–55% TS |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Bioseparation streams are **heat-sensitive**. The residence time, temperature, and shear exposure must be minimised. This is why **multiple-effect evaporators** and **mechanical vapour recompression (MVR)** systems dominate this industry.

## 1.5 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded)

- **Latent heat of vaporisation of water at 100 °C** = **2257 kJ/kg**
- **Latent heat of vaporisation of water at 60 °C** = **2358 kJ/kg**
- **Typical overall heat transfer coefficient (U) in natural-circulation evaporator** = **500–1500 W/m²·K**
- **Typical U in forced-circulation evaporator** = **1500–5000 W/m²·K**
- **Standard heating steam pressure** = **0.5–4.0 bar (gauge)**
- **Vacuum operation** corresponds to **0.05–0.5 bar absolute** (used to lower boiling point of heat-sensitive biologicals)

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Modes of Heat Transfer in an Evaporator

An evaporator heat-transfer surface must overcome three thermal resistances in series. The classical **resistance-in-series model** is central to every KTU examination question on this topic.

### Mode 1 — Condensation of Steam (Outside the Tube)
Steam condenses on the *outer* surface of a horizontal or vertical tube bundle, releasing its latent heat. This side is governed by the **condensing-side film coefficient** $h_{out}$ (also written $h_c$ or $h_{steam}$).

- For *dropwise* condensation: $h_{out}$ is very high.
- For *filmwise* condensation (industrial reality): $h_{out}$ is moderate, depending on tube orientation.

### Mode 2 — Conduction Through the Tube Wall
Heat must conduct through the metal wall of the tube. The thermal conductivity of the wall ($k_w$) and its thickness ($\delta_w$) determine this resistance.

### Mode 3 — Convective Boiling on the Inside
The *inside* of the tube is in contact with the boiling liquid. The **boiling-side film coefficient** $h_{in}$ (also $h_b$) depends on:
- Liquid velocity (natural vs. forced circulation)
- Temperature difference between wall and liquid
- Physical properties of the liquid (viscosity, surface tension, thermal conductivity)

## 2.2 Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient — Derivation Logic

For steady-state heat flow, the rate of heat transfer through each layer is equal:

$$ q = h_{out} A_{out} (T_{steam} - T_{w,o}) = \frac{k_w A_{mean}}{\delta_w} (T_{w,o} - T_{w,i}) = h_{in} A_{in} (T_{w,i} - T_{liq}) $$

Defining the **overall heat transfer coefficient** $U$ based on a reference area $A$:

$$ \frac{1}{U \cdot A} = \frac{1}{h_{out} A_{out}} + \frac{\delta_w}{k_w A_{mean}} + \frac{1}{h_{in} A_{in}} $$

When the area is referenced to the *outside* tube area $A_o$ (a KTU-standard convention), and neglecting curvature effects:

$$ \frac{1}{U_o} = \frac{1}{h_{out}} + \frac{\delta_w}{k_w} \cdot \frac{A_o}{A_{mean}} + \frac{1}{h_{in}} \cdot \frac{A_o}{A_{in}} $$

For thin-walled tubes: $A_o / A_{in} \approx 1$ and $A_o / A_{mean} \approx 1$, simplifying to:

$$ \frac{1}{U} \approx \frac{1}{h_{out}} + \frac{\delta_w}{k_w} + \frac{1}{h_{in}} $$

## 2.3 Boiling Point Elevation (BPE) — The Hidden Driving Force Loss

A solution of biological solutes (salts, sugars, proteins) boils at a temperature **higher** than pure water at the same pressure. This is **Boiling Point Elevation (BPE)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why BPE matters:** The actual temperature driving force available for heat transfer is:
> $$\Delta T_{available} = T_{steam} - T_{liq}^{solution} = T_{steam} - (T_{sat}^{water} + BPE)$$
> Ignoring BPE leads to a **serious overdesign** of the heat transfer area, a common KTU mistake.

**Dühring's Rule (Empirical but Examinable):** The boiling point of a solution at a given pressure is a linear function of the boiling point of pure water at the *same pressure*:

$$ T_{solution}(P) = a + b \cdot T_{water}(P) $$

where $a$ and $b$ are fitted constants specific to the solute.

## 2.4 Factors Affecting the Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient $U$

| Factor | Effect on $U$ | Engineering Control |
|---|---|---|
| **Steam-side fouling** (inert gases, non-condensables) | Decreases $h_{out}$, decreases $U$ | Steam venting, de-aeration |
| **Liquid-side fouling** (protein deposits, scale) | Decreases $h_{in}$, decreases $U$ | Forced circulation, periodic cleaning-in-place (CIP) |
| **Tube material and thickness** | Thick wall, low $k_w$ → lower $U$ | Use thin-wall stainless steel (SS-316L) |
| **Liquid velocity (inside tubes)** | Higher velocity → higher $h_{in}$ → higher $U$ | Forced-circulation pumps |
| **Temperature difference $\Delta T$** | Very high $\Delta T$ can cause film boiling, *decreasing* $U$ | Limit $\Delta T$ to safe range |
| **Vapour binding / air binding** | Steam condensate film blankets tube → drops $U$ | Slope tubes, install vents |

## 2.5 Capacity and Economy — Key Performance Indicators

> [!NOTE]
> **Capacity** of an evaporator = mass of water evaporated per unit time (**kg/h**).
> **Economy** of an evaporator = mass of water evaporated per unit mass of steam supplied (**kg water/kg steam**).
> For a single-effect evaporator, theoretical economy ≈ **0.8–0.9** (loss due to sensible heating of feed to boiling point). For an $n$-effect evaporator, theoretical economy ≈ **0.8n**.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $q = U A \Delta T$ | Basic heat transfer rate | **W** |
| 2 | $q = m_s \lambda_s$ | Heat released by condensing steam | **W** |
| 3 | $1/U = 1/h_{out} + \delta_w/k_w + 1/h_{in}$ | Overall coefficient from resistances | **m²·K/W** |
| 4 | $\Delta T_{available} = T_{steam} - (T_{sat}^{water} + BPE)$ | Net driving force | **K** |
| 5 | $A = q / (U \cdot \Delta T)$ | Heat transfer area | **m²** |
| 6 | Capacity = $m_{evap}$ | kg water/h | **kg/h** |
| 7 | Economy = $m_{evap} / m_{steam}$ | dimensionless | **kg/kg** |
| 8 | BPE (Dühring): $T_{sol}(P_1) - T_{sol}(P_2) = b \cdot [T_w(P_1) - T_w(P_2)]$ | Boiling-point elevation rule | **K** |
| 9 | $h_{in} = 0.023 \cdot Re^{0.8} Pr^{0.4} \cdot (k/D)$ (forced convection) | Sieder–Tate type | **W/m²·K** |
| 10 | Steam economy (single effect) = $0.8$ to $0.9$ | Ratio | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the table above, $\vert \Delta T \vert$ and $\vert Re \vert$ notation has been avoided using the slash form $1/U$ to prevent markdown breakage. In handwritten KTU scripts, students may use absolute value freely.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Design of Falling-Film Evaporators** for fruit juice concentration relies on accurately predicting $U$ to size the tube length.
- **MVR (Mechanical Vapour Recompression)** systems use the formula $q = UA\Delta T$ to determine compressor power requirements.
- **Validation of CIP cycles** in pharma evaporators requires tracking $U$ over time — a drop in $U$ indicates protein fouling.
- **Energy audits** in sugar/dairy industries use Economy = $m_{evap}/m_{steam}$ to benchmark evaporator performance.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient from Individual Resistances

We consider a cylindrical tube wall of inside diameter $D_i$, outside diameter $D_o$, and thermal conductivity $k_w$. Steam at temperature $T_s$ condenses on the *outside*, releasing heat that conducts *through* the wall and convects *into* the boiling liquid at temperature $T_l$.

### Step 1 — Heat transfer on the steam side (condensation)

The convective resistance on the outside is:

$$ R_{out} = \frac{1}{h_o \cdot A_o} = \frac{1}{h_o \cdot (\pi D_o L)} $$

This represents the temperature drop per unit heat flow from the bulk steam to the outer wall surface $T_{w,o}$.

### Step 2 — Heat conduction through the tube wall

For a cylindrical wall (log-mean area approximation):

$$ R_{wall} = \frac{\ln(D_o/D_i)}{2 \pi k_w L} $$

For thin tubes where $D_o/D_i \approx 1$, this reduces to:

$$ R_{wall} \approx \frac{\delta_w}{k_w \cdot (\pi D_m L)} = \frac{(D_o - D_i)/2}{k_w \cdot A_m} $$

### Step 3 — Heat transfer on the boiling-liquid side

$$ R_{in} = \frac{1}{h_i \cdot A_i} = \frac{1}{h_i \cdot (\pi D_i L)} $$

### Step 4 — Summing resistances in series

For one-dimensional steady-state heat flow, total resistance is the sum:

$$ R_{total} = R_{out} + R_{wall} + R_{in} $$

Defining the **overall heat transfer coefficient** $U_o$ based on the outer area $A_o = \pi D_o L$:

$$ q = \frac{\Delta T_{overall}}{R_{total}} = \frac{T_s - T_l}{R_{out} + R_{wall} + R_{in}} $$

Substituting each resistance:

$$ q = \frac{T_s - T_l}{\dfrac{1}{h_o A_o} + \dfrac{\ln(D_o/D_i)}{2 \pi k_w L} + \dfrac{1}{h_i A_i}} $$

Recognising that $q = U_o A_o (T_s - T_l)$ and dividing numerator and denominator by $A_o$:

$$ \frac{1}{U_o} = \frac{1}{h_o} + \frac{A_o \ln(D_o/D_i)}{2 \pi k_w L} + \frac{A_o}{h_i A_i} $$

Since $A_o = \pi D_o L$ and $A_i = \pi D_i L$:

$$ \frac{A_o}{A_i} = \frac{D_o}{D_i} $$

Therefore:

$$ \boxed{\frac{1}{U_o} = \frac{1}{h_o} + \frac{D_o \ln(D_o/D_i)}{2 k_w} + \frac{D_o}{D_i \cdot h_i}} $$

**This is the KTU-defining equation for the overall heat transfer coefficient in an evaporator tube.** When $D_o \approx D_i \approx D$, it collapses to the simple form:

$$ \frac{1}{U} \approx \frac{1}{h_o} + \frac{\delta_w}{k_w} + \frac{1}{h_i} $$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Evaporator Heat Transfer Area from a Mass Balance

Consider a single-effect evaporator with:
- Feed: mass flow $F$ (**kg/h**), solids fraction $x_F$ (**wt%**), temperature $T_F$ (**°C**)
- Concentrated product (liquor): mass flow $L$ (**kg/h**), solids fraction $x_L$ (**wt%**), temperature $T_L$ (**°C**)
- Vapour produced: mass flow $V$ (**kg/h**), temperature $T_V$ (**°C**)
- Heating steam: mass flow $S$ (**kg/h**), saturation temperature $T_s$ (**°C**)

### Step 1 — Total mass balance

$$ F = L + V $$

### Step 2 — Solids (or solute) balance — solutes are non-volatile

$$ F \cdot x_F = L \cdot x_L $$

### Step 3 — Solving simultaneously for $V$ and $L$

From the two equations:

$$ L = F \cdot \frac{x_F}{x_L} $$

$$ V = F - L = F \left(1 - \frac{x_F}{x_L}\right) $$

### Step 4 — Overall energy balance

The heat supplied by steam = heat to raise feed to boiling + heat to evaporate water + heat to leave as vapour sensible heat + heat losses.

Simplified (assuming feed enters at boiling point, neglecting heat losses, vapour leaves as saturated):

$$ S \cdot \lambda_s = V \cdot \lambda_w $$

Therefore steam economy becomes:

$$ \frac{V}{S} = \frac{\lambda_s}{\lambda_w} $$

For $\lambda_s \approx \lambda_w$ (steam and water have similar latent heats in KTU exam ranges), $V/S \approx 1$.

### Step 5 — Heat transfer area from driving force

The driving force $\Delta T$ is computed at both ends of the evaporator:

$$ \Delta T_1 = T_s - T_l^{top}, \quad \Delta T_2 = T_s - T_l^{bottom} $$

For a natural-circulation evaporator, the temperature at the bottom (where boiling occurs) is *higher* than at the top (due to hydrostatic head). A log-mean temperature difference is used:

$$ \Delta T_{LMTD} = \frac{\Delta T_2 - \Delta T_1}{\ln(\Delta T_2 / \Delta T_1)} $$

The required area:

$$ \boxed{A = \frac{q}{U \cdot \Delta T_{LMTD}} = \frac{S \cdot \lambda_s}{U \cdot \Delta T_{LMTD}}} $$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Resistance Contribution Comparison

| Resistance Component | Typical Magnitude (m²·K/W) | % Contribution to Total | Remediation |
|---|---|---|---|
| $1/h_o$ (steam-side) | $1 \times 10^{-4}$ to $5 \times 10^{-4}$ | **5–15%** | Remove non-condensables, vent |
| $\delta_w / k_w$ (wall) | $1 \times 10^{-4}$ to $2 \times 10^{-4}$ | **3–8%** | Use thin high-$k$ wall (SS, Cu) |
| $1/h_i$ (boiling-side, *clean*) | $1 \times 10^{-4}$ to $5 \times 10^{-4}$ | **10–30%** | Forced circulation, high velocity |
| Fouling resistance (biological deposits) | $1 \times 10^{-3}$ to $1 \times 10^{-2}$ | **50–80%** | CIP, anti-fouling tubes |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The single largest resistance in a real bioseparation evaporator is the fouling layer** — proteins, salts, and denatured biological matter precipitate on the tube wall. This is why KTU exam questions routinely include a "fouling factor" $R_f$ added to the total resistance.

$$\frac{1}{U_{actual}} = \frac{1}{U_{clean}} + R_{f,steam} + R_{f,liq}$$

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Heat Flow Path in an Evaporator Tube

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Steam Inlet\nT_s, P_s] --> B1[Steam Chest\nVapour Space]
    B1 --> C1[Condensing Steam Film\nh_o resistance]
    C1 --> D1[Outer Tube Wall\nT_w,o]
    D1 --> E1[Tube Wall Metal\nk_w conduction]
    E1 --> F1[Inner Tube Wall\nT_w,i]
    F1 --> G1[Boiling Liquid Film\nh_i resistance]
    G1 --> H1[Liquid Bulk\nT_l]
    H1 --> I1[Boiling Vapour\nrises up tube]
    I1 --> J1[Vapour-Liquid Separator]
    J1 --> K1[Concentrate Outlet\nL, x_L]
    J1 --> L1[Condenser\nVapour to water]
    A2[Feed Inlet\nF, x_F, T_F] --> H1
    M1[Cooling Water] --> L1
    L1 --> N1[Non-condensable Vent]
    style A1 fill:#FFD580,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style H1 fill:#80C0FF,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style I1 fill:#C0C0C0,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style J1 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style L1 fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Resistance Network Analogy (Electrical Equivalent)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Node T_s\nSteam] --- R1[R_out = 1 by h_o]
    R1 --- A2[Node T_w,o\nOuter wall]
    A2 --- R2[R_wall = delta by k_w]
    R2 --- A3[Node T_w,i\nInner wall]
    A3 --- R3[R_in = 1 by h_i]
    R3 --- A4[Node T_l\nLiquid]
    A1 -.->|Current q| A4
    style A1 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style A4 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style R1 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style R2 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style R3 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Single-Effect Evaporator System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    F1[Feed Tank\nF, x_F] --> P1[Feed Pump]
    P1 --> HX1[Pre-heater\noptional]
    HX1 --> EV1[Evaporator Body\nVapour-Liquid separator]
    ST1[Steam Supply\nS, T_s] --> EV1
    EV1 --> L1[Concentrate Outlet\nL, x_L]
    EV1 --> V1[Vapour Line]
    V1 --> CD1[Condenser]
    CW1[Cooling Water] --> CD1
    CD1 --> RC1[Receiver\nCondensate V]
    CD1 --> VP1[Vacuum Pump\nNon-condensables]
    style EV1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style ST1 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,color:#FFF
    style CD1 fill:#87CEFA,stroke:#333,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Steam (red) enters the evaporator body and condenses on the tube bundle, releasing latent heat. The feed (blue) is sprayed over the tubes or circulated inside them, boils, and is separated. The vapour (light blue) is condensed by cooling water and removed by a vacuum pump. The thick liquor (gold) leaves at the bottom.

## 4.4 Decision Tree — Effect of Increasing $\Delta T$ on $U$

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Increase in Delta T] --> B{Delta T Range}
    B -->|Low less than 5 K| C[Nucleate boiling\nh_i rises, U rises]
    B -->|Moderate 5 to 25 K| D[Peak h_i\nU maximum]
    B -->|High greater than 25 K| E[Film boiling\nVapour blanket forms]
    B -->|Very high| F[Burnout\nU collapses]
    C --> G[U increases]
    D --> H[U peaks]
    E --> I[U decreases]
    F --> J[Heating surface fails]
    style D fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style E fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style F fill:#FF6347,stroke:#FFF,color:#FFF
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering takeaway:** Simply increasing steam pressure does *not* always increase evaporation rate. At very high $\Delta T$, the heat transfer coefficient collapses (film boiling), and the area must be increased to compensate. This is a **favourite KTU conceptual question**.

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question Paper]
**Define the term "Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient" as applied to evaporators. List any three factors that affect it.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **overall heat transfer coefficient** $U$ of an evaporator is defined as the proportionality constant between the rate of heat transfer and the product of heat transfer area and the temperature driving force, in the equation $q = U A \Delta T$. It has units of **W/m²·K** and represents the *combined* effect of all thermal resistances (condensing steam, tube wall, boiling liquid) in the heat flow path.

Three factors affecting $U$:
1. **Liquid-side velocity** (forced circulation increases $h_i$ and hence $U$).
2. **Fouling resistance** on either steam side or liquid side (biological deposits, scale).
3. **Tube wall thickness and material** (thicker wall of low $k_w$ reduces $U$).

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall (1 mark lost):** Students often write *only* $U = 1/(1/h_i + 1/h_o)$ and forget to mention the wall resistance. Mentioning **all three** resistances in series is mandatory for full marks.

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary]
**What is "Boiling Point Elevation (BPE)"? Why is it significant in the design of biological evaporators?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Boiling Point Elevation (BPE)** is the difference between the boiling temperature of a solution and the boiling temperature of pure water at the *same pressure*. BPE arises due to the lowering of vapour pressure caused by dissolved solutes (proteins, salts, sugars).

**Significance in biological evaporators:**
1. It **reduces the available temperature driving force** $\Delta T$ for heat transfer, because $\Delta T_{available} = T_{steam} - (T_{water} + BPE)$.
2. **Heat-sensitive biological products** (enzymes, antibiotics) require operation at low absolute pressure to keep the boiling temperature low — BPE shifts the optimum upward, demanding even *lower* pressures.
3. Ignoring BPE in design leads to **under-sized evaporators** (insufficient area) and potential **product thermal damage**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Many students confuse BPE with *elevation in boiling point due to hydrostatic head*. In KTU papers, BPE refers specifically to **solute-induced elevation**, which is the difference between solution BP and water BP at identical pressure.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks split into two sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. **Choice** must be provided between two full questions. Detailed step-by-step model solutions are mandatory.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Choice Option 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Paper]

A single-effect evaporator is used to concentrate a **heat-sensitive antibiotic solution** from **5% w/w to 30% w/w**. The feed enters at **30 °C** at a rate of **10,000 kg/h**. Steam is supplied at **3.0 bar (gauge)** (saturation temperature ≈ 143 °C). The evaporator operates under vacuum such that the solution boils at **70 °C** (saturation pressure of pure water at 70 °C = 0.317 bar). The overall heat transfer coefficient is **U = 1200 W/m²·K**. The latent heat of steam at 3.0 bar(g) is **λ_s = 2140 kJ/kg**, and the latent heat of vapour at 70 °C is **λ_w = 2335 kJ/kg**. Specific heat of feed ≈ **4.0 kJ/kg·K**. Assume BPE = 4 °C.

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Calculate (i) the mass flow rate of concentrated liquor, (ii) the mass flow rate of water evaporated, and (iii) the steam economy.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step (i) — Solute balance** to find $L$ (concentrate flow):

$$ F \cdot x_F = L \cdot x_L $$

$$ 10{,}000 \times 0.05 = L \times 0.30 $$

$$ L = \frac{500}{0.30} = 1666.67 \text{ kg/h} $$

**Step (ii) — Total mass balance** to find $V$ (evaporated water):

$$ V = F - L = 10{,}000 - 1666.67 = 8333.33 \text{ kg/h} $$

**Step (iii) — Steam economy calculation:**

Energy balance (feed not at boiling point, so include sensible heating):

$$ S \cdot \lambda_s = F \cdot C_p \cdot (T_b - T_F) + V \cdot \lambda_w $$

Substitute:

$$ S \times 2140 = 10{,}000 \times 4.0 \times (70 - 30) + 8333.33 \times 2335 $$

$$ S \times 2140 = 1{,}600{,}000 + 19{,}458{,}333 $$

$$ S \times 2140 = 21{,}058{,}333 \text{ kJ/h} $$

$$ S = 9839.4 \text{ kg/h} $$

**Steam Economy:**

$$ \text{Economy} = \frac{V}{S} = \frac{8333.33}{9839.4} = 0.847 \text{ kg water/kg steam} $$

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Calculate the heat transfer area required in the evaporator.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute the *true* temperature driving force** (accounting for BPE):

The solution boiling point = water BP at operating pressure + BPE = 70 + 4 = 74 °C.
But the problem statement says the solution boils at 70 °C with BPE = 4 °C — meaning the *actual* liquid temperature at the boiling surface is 70 °C, while pure water would boil at 66 °C. The driving force is then:

$$ \Delta T = T_{steam} - T_{liq} = 143 - 70 = 73 \text{ K} $$

(We use the *actual* liquid temperature as given; BPE has been absorbed into the 70 °C figure.)

**Step 2 — Compute the total heat duty $q$:**

$$ q = V \cdot \lambda_w = \frac{8333.33}{3600} \times 2335 \times 10^3 = 5.405 \times 10^6 \text{ W} $$

(Unit check: $8333.33 \text{ kg/h} \div 3600 = 2.315 \text{ kg/s}$; $2.315 \times 2335 \times 10^3 = 5.405 \times 10^6 \text{ W}$.)

**Step 3 — Compute area:**

$$ A = \frac{q}{U \cdot \Delta T} = \frac{5.405 \times 10^6}{1200 \times 73} $$

$$ A = \frac{5.405 \times 10^6}{87{,}600} = 61.7 \text{ m}^2 $$

**Adopting a design margin of 1.2 (for fouling and operational transients):**

$$ A_{design} = 1.2 \times 61.7 = 74.05 \text{ m}^2 $$

**Final Answer:** Approximately **74 m² of heat transfer area** should be specified.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall (2 marks lost):** Students often forget to (a) include the **sensible heating term** $F C_p (T_b - T_F)$ in the energy balance, (b) convert kg/h to kg/s before applying $q = U A \Delta T$ in W, and (c) include the **design margin**. Each omission costs 1–2 marks.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Choice Option 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Main Exam]

A **long-tube vertical rising-film evaporator** concentrates a fruit juice from **10% to 50% total solids**. The following resistances on the heating surface have been measured:

- $h_o$ (steam-side coefficient) = **8000 W/m²·K**
- $h_i$ (juice-side coefficient) = **2500 W/m²·K**
- Tube wall thickness $\delta_w$ = **1.5 mm**, thermal conductivity $k_w$ = **16 W/m·K** (stainless steel)
- Steam-side fouling resistance $R_{f,steam}$ = **0.0001 m²·K/W**
- Juice-side fouling resistance $R_{f,juice}$ = **0.0005 m²·K/W**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Calculate the overall heat transfer coefficient $U$ for the clean surface (no fouling) and for the operating (fouled) surface.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute the resistance of the tube wall:**

$$ R_{wall} = \frac{\delta_w}{k_w} = \frac{1.5 \times 10^{-3}}{16} = 9.375 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2\cdot\text{K/W} $$

**Step 2 — Compute the clean overall resistance** (sum of three resistances):

$$ \frac{1}{U_{clean}} = \frac{1}{h_o} + R_{wall} + \frac{1}{h_i} $$

$$ \frac{1}{U_{clean}} = \frac{1}{8000} + 9.375 \times 10^{-5} + \frac{1}{2500} $$

$$ \frac{1}{U_{clean}} = 1.25 \times 10^{-4} + 9.375 \times 10^{-5} + 4.0 \times 10^{-4} $$

$$ \frac{1}{U_{clean}} = 1.25 \times 10^{-4} + 0.9375 \times 10^{-4} + 4.0 \times 10^{-4} = 6.1875 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2\cdot\text{K/W} $$

$$ U_{clean} = \frac{1}{6.1875 \times 10^{-4}} = 1616 \text{ W/m}^2\cdot\text{K} $$

**Step 3 — Add the fouling resistances:**

$$ \frac{1}{U_{fouled}} = \frac{1}{U_{clean}} + R_{f,steam} + R_{f,juice} $$

$$ \frac{1}{U_{fouled}} = 6.1875 \times 10^{-4} + 1.0 \times 10^{-4} + 5.0 \times 10^{-4} $$

$$ \frac{1}{U_{fouled}} = 12.1875 \times 10^{-4} = 1.21875 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2\cdot\text{K/W} $$

$$ U_{fouled} = \frac{1}{1.21875 \times 10^{-3}} = 820.5 \text{ W/m}^2\cdot\text{K} $$

**Result:**

$$ \boxed{U_{clean} \approx 1616 \text{ W/m}^2\cdot\text{K} \quad ; \quad U_{fouled} \approx 820 \text{ W/m}^2\cdot\text{K}} $$

**Interpretation:** Fouling has reduced the heat transfer coefficient to about **51% of its clean value** — a 49% drop, which is *typical* in food/bio evaporators and explains why CIP is critical.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**If the evaporator must evaporate 5,000 kg/h of water with steam at 2.5 bar(g) (T_s ≈ 138 °C) and the juice boils at 80 °C, calculate the heat transfer area needed (i) on day-1 (clean) and (ii) after 30 days of operation (fouled). Discuss the practical implication.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute heat duty $q$:**

Latent heat at 80 °C ≈ 2308 kJ/kg.

$$ q = \frac{5000}{3600} \times 2308 \times 10^3 = 1.389 \times 1.0 \times 10^3 \times 2308 = 3.205 \times 10^6 \text{ W} $$

(Detailed unit check: $5000/3600 = 1.389 \text{ kg/s}$; $1.389 \times 2308 \times 10^3 = 3.205 \times 10^6 \text{ W}$.)

**Step 2 — Temperature driving force:**

$$ \Delta T = T_s - T_{liq} = 138 - 80 = 58 \text{ K} $$

**Step 3 — Area on day-1 (clean):**

$$ A_{clean} = \frac{q}{U_{clean} \cdot \Delta T} = \frac{3.205 \times 10^6}{1616 \times 58} $$

$$ A_{clean} = \frac{3.205 \times 10^6}{93{,}728} = 34.2 \text{ m}^2 $$

**Step 4 — Area after fouling:**

$$ A_{fouled} = \frac{3.205 \times 10^6}{820.5 \times 58} = \frac{3.205 \times 10^6}{47{,}589} = 67.4 \text{ m}^2 $$

**Step 5 — Practical implication:**

$$ \frac{A_{fouled} - A_{clean}}{A_{clean}} \times 100\% = \frac{67.4 - 34.2}{34.2} \times 100\% \approx 97\% $$

**Conclusion:** The area required has **nearly doubled** due to fouling. In practice, a designer must either:
1. Specify the area based on *fouled* conditions from day-1 (over-capitalisation), **or**
2. Specify a clean-surface area and accept reduced capacity after 30 days, **or**
3. Build a *cleanable* design (e.g. falling-film with daily CIP) so that the clean $U$ is sustained.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** The KTU examiner will deduct 1 full mark if the student does not explicitly state that **design area is based on fouled $U$, not clean $U$**. Many students make the error of using $U_{clean}$ for the final design, leading to under-sized equipment.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Print-Ready Summary — KTU 2024 Module 3, Evaporation Heat Transfer**

- Evaporation rate is **heat-transfer limited**, not mass-transfer limited.
- Master equation: $q = U A \Delta T = m_s \lambda_s = m_v \lambda_v$.
- Three resistances in series: $1/U = 1/h_o + \delta_w/k_w + 1/h_i$.
- **Fouling resistance** is the dominant resistance in biological evaporators — *never* ignore it in design.
- **Boiling Point Elevation (BPE)** reduces available $\Delta T$; use Dühring's rule for biological solutions.
- Higher $\Delta T$ does **not** always give higher $U$ — at very high $\Delta T$ (film boiling), $U$ collapses.
- **Capacity** = kg water/h; **Economy** = kg water/kg steam; for $n$-effect evaporators, economy scales as ≈ $0.8n$.
- Typical $U$ values: natural circulation **500–1500 W/m²·K**; forced circulation **1500–5000 W/m²·K**.
- Standard heating steam: **0.5–4.0 bar(g)**; vacuum operation: **0.05–0.5 bar(abs)** for heat-sensitive biologicals.
- Common KTU exam mistakes: (a) ignoring BPE, (b) ignoring fouling, (c) using kg/h in SI equations, (d) ignoring the design margin.
- **Industrial rule of thumb for bioseparation evaporators:** Always design for *fouled* $U$ and use stainless-steel 316L tubes with electropolished interior surface to minimise protein adhesion.
- Latent heat of steam ≈ **2140–2260 kJ/kg**; latent heat of water vapour at 60–100 °C ≈ **2300–2400 kJ/kg**.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Evaporation Equipment: Climbing-film, Falling-film, Forced-circulation, and Agitated-film Evaporators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT525 - BIOSEPARATION TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Evaporation Equipment: Climbing-film, Falling-film, Forced-circulation, and Agitated-film Evaporators

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Evaporators** are unit operations equipment used in bioseparation technology to concentrate a solution by **removing volatile solvent (usually water)** through **boiling**, leaving behind the non-volatile solute (product of interest) in a more concentrated form. The energy for vaporization is supplied through a **heat transfer surface** (steam-heated tubes or jacketed vessel).

In bioseparation processes such as the concentration of **enzymes, antibiotics, fruit juices, milk, vaccines, and fermentation broths**, evaporation must be carried out under conditions that minimize **thermal damage** (denaturation, Maillard reactions, loss of bioactivity).

The four major types of industrial evaporators covered in the KTU 2024 syllabus Module 3 are:

1. **Climbing-Film Evaporator** (also called *Long-Tube Vertical Evaporator, LTVE, or Rising-Film Evaporator*)
2. **Falling-Film Evaporator**
3. **Forced-Circulation Evaporator**
4. **Agitated-Film Evaporator** (also called *Wiped-Film or Thin-Film Evaporator*)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):**
> The classification of evaporators is based on the **mechanism by which the liquid moves over the heated surface** — natural circulation (climbing film), gravity-induced flow (falling film), pump-driven external loop (forced circulation), or mechanical agitation (agitated/wiped film). This directly impacts the **heat transfer coefficient**, **residence time**, and **suitability for heat-sensitive biological products**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine you are heating milk in a kitchen. If you heat it in a small deep pan, the milk bubbles rise from the bottom and burst at the top — this mimics a **Climbing-Film Evaporator** where liquid rises along with vapor.

If you tilt the pan and let milk flow down a hot sloped surface, you get a **Falling-Film Evaporator** — the milk flows down as a thin film, evaporating rapidly.

If you pump the milk rapidly through a heated pipe using an external pump (like a heart circulating blood), you get a **Forced-Circulation Evaporator**.

If you scrape the milk across a hot rotating blade inside a heated cylinder (like butter being churned on a hot pan), you get an **Agitated-Film Evaporator** — perfect for viscous, heat-sensitive materials like honey or polymer solutions.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Metrics

- **Boiling point elevation (BPE):** $\Delta T_b$ — typically **1–10 K** for biological solutions
- **Heat transfer coefficient:** $U$ — typically **1500–6000 W/m²·K** for film evaporators
- **Steam economy:** ratio of **kg water evaporated / kg steam consumed**
- **Typical residence time:** Agitated-film evaporators operate at **< 1 minute**; climbing-film at **20–40 seconds**; forced-circulation at **several minutes**

> [!TIP]
> **Standard Metrics in Bioseparation Evaporation:**
> - **Viscosity range:** 1 cP (juices) to > 50,000 cP (pectin, gums)
> - **Operating pressure:** Often **vacuum (0.05–0.5 bar absolute)** for thermolabile products
> - **Temperature:** Generally kept below **60 °C** for proteins/enzymes
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification Logic for Industrial Evaporators

The choice of evaporator in a bioseparation plant is governed by the following decision hierarchy (this is a **frequently asked question in KTU exams**):

1. **Heat sensitivity of the solute** → favors short residence time (Falling-film, Agitated-film)
2. **Viscosity of the feed** → favors forced circulation or agitated film
3. **Fouling / scaling tendency** → favors forced circulation
4. **Capacity requirement** → favors multi-effect climbing-film for large throughput
5. **Capital cost** → climbing-film is cheapest per unit area

## 2.2 Type 1 — Climbing-Film (Long-Tube Vertical / Rising-Film) Evaporator

### Construction
- Consists of a **steam chest (calandria)** at the bottom containing long vertical tubes, typically **3 to 10 m in length** and **25–75 mm diameter**.
- Above the tubes is a large **vapor-liquid separator** (often called the *vapor head* or *body*).
- The tubes are heated externally by steam condensing on the shell side.

### Working Principle
- Preheated feed enters the **bottom of the tubes**.
- Steam condenses on the outside of the tubes, transferring heat to the liquid.
- The liquid begins to boil, and the **vapor bubbles generated push the liquid upward** — much like a bubble pump.
- This phenomenon is called **"vapor binding"** or **"thermosiphon effect"**.
- The liquid-vapor mixture rushes upward as a **thin, fast-moving film** clinging to the tube walls.
- At the top, vapor and liquid are separated in the vapor head; concentrated liquid exits, vapor goes to a condenser or next effect.

### Why It Works (The Physics)
The driving force is the **density difference** between the liquid-rich mixture at the bottom and the vapor-rich mixture higher up. This creates a natural circulation even without a pump.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** The climbing-film evaporator is essentially a **natural circulation thermosiphon evaporator**. The term "climbing film" describes the **annular liquid film** that climbs up the tube walls propelled by vapor shear.

### Advantages
- Simple construction, low cost per m² of heat transfer area.
- High heat transfer coefficient (typically **2000–3500 W/m²·K**) due to turbulence of vapor-liquid mixture.
- Suitable for **non-viscous, non-fouling liquids** like sugar juice, milk, dilute salt solutions.
- High capacity (large throughput).

### Disadvantages
- **Long residence time (20 seconds to several minutes)** — not suitable for heat-sensitive biologicals.
- Cannot handle viscous liquids (limit ~ 0.05–0.1 Pa·s) or crystallizing solutions.
- Requires **careful level control** in the vapor head.
- Tubing must be long for adequate vapor velocity, requiring **tall buildings** (sometimes 10–12 m high).

### Bioseparation Applications
- Concentration of **sugar syrups, milk, dilute fruit juices, whey**.
- Often used as the **first effect** in a multi-effect evaporation system.

## 2.3 Type 2 — Falling-Film Evaporator

### Construction
- A bundle of **vertical tubes** (similar geometry to climbing-film) housed inside a shell.
- **Feed enters the TOP** of each tube via a **distribution device** (perforated plate, spray nozzles, or distribution cups) that ensures **uniform wetting** of the inner tube walls.
- Steam condenses on the shell side; vapor and concentrated liquid leave the bottom.

### Working Principle
- Liquid feed is distributed at the top and flows **downward as a thin film** along the inner surface of the tubes, driven by **gravity**.
- The film thickness is typically **0.1–0.3 mm**.
- Vapor generated by boiling at the wall **co-currently drags the film downward** at higher velocities, increasing turbulence.
- The liquid film and vapor leave together at the bottom and are separated in an external cyclone or vapor-liquid separator.

### Why It Works
- Co-current **gravity + vapor shear** flow ensures excellent heat transfer.
- Falling-film evaporators can operate at **very low temperature differences** ($ \Delta T = 5–10 \text{ K} $), making them ideal for **vacuum operation with heat-sensitive materials**.
- **No hydrostatic head** of liquid (unlike climbing film), so boiling temperature is uniform along the tube.

### Advantages
- **Very short residence time (5–20 seconds)** — ideal for heat-sensitive products.
- Can handle **moderately viscous liquids** (up to 0.5 Pa·s with proper design).
- Operates efficiently under **vacuum** (low boiling point).
- Easy to clean — tubes can be inspected and cleaned individually.
- Uniform temperature distribution along tube length.

### Disadvantages
- Requires **excellent feed distribution** at the top — poor distribution causes dry patches, scale formation, and tube failure.
- More expensive than climbing-film per unit area.
- Not suitable for **foaming liquids** (foam cannot be drained by gravity alone).
- Lower capacity per tube compared to climbing-film.

### Bioseparation Applications
- Concentration of **heat-sensitive products: orange juice, tomato paste, antibiotics, enzymes, vitamins, gelatin**.
- Used in **dairy industry** for milk and whey concentration.

## 2.4 Type 3 — Forced-Circulation Evaporator

### Construction
- A **pump** (usually centrifugal) circulates the liquid at high velocity (**2–6 m/s**) through:
  - The **heat exchanger** (calandria or plate heat exchanger)
  - The **vapor-liquid separator (body)**
- The heat exchanger can be horizontal or vertical.
- The liquid exits the separator as concentrated product, while vapor proceeds to condenser or next effect.
- The pump is placed **on the suction side** to prevent cavitation.

### Working Principle
- Liquid is **pumped through the heat exchanger at high velocity**, where it is heated but **does NOT boil inside the tubes** (sub-cooled operation) or boiling is suppressed by **hydrostatic pressure** of the static liquid column above the tubes.
- The superheated liquid then enters the separator, where **flash evaporation** occurs as pressure drops.
- This decouples the **heat transfer** (in tubes) from the **phase separation** (in body).

### Why It Works
- **Suppressed boiling** inside tubes prevents scaling and fouling (no localized high-temperature nucleation sites).
- High velocity provides a **high heat transfer coefficient** ($U = 2500\text{–}5000 \text{ W/m}^2\text{·K}$) and physically **scours deposits** from tube walls.
- Suitable for **viscous (up to 10 Pa·s)** and **scaling/crystallizing solutions**.

### Advantages
- Handles **viscous, fouling, scaling, and crystallizing solutions** excellently.
- High heat transfer coefficient due to forced convection.
- **Long operating cycles** between cleaning shutdowns.
- Flexibility — can handle wide range of feed characteristics.

### Disadvantages
- **High capital and operating cost** (pump consumes significant power).
- **Long residence time (several minutes to hours)** — due to large liquid holdup in circulation loop.
- Not ideal for very heat-sensitive products.
- High pressure drop across heat exchanger requires robust pump.

### Bioseparation Applications
- Concentration of **caustic solutions, fermentation broths, salt solutions, sugar syrups with crystallization, viscose, glycerine**.
- Used as **final effect** in multi-effect systems for highly viscous concentrates.

## 2.5 Type 4 — Agitated-Film (Wiped-Film / Thin-Film) Evaporator

### Construction
- A **vertical cylindrical vessel** with a **jacketed outer wall** for heating (steam or hot oil in the jacket).
- Inside is a **rotor shaft with hinged or fixed blades (wipers)** that rotate at high speed (**100–600 rpm**).
- A **condenser** is placed either internal (top) or external to the body.

### Working Principle
- Feed enters at the **top** of the vessel and is **spread into a thin film** on the heated wall by the rotating blades.
- The blades continually **wipe the film**, exposing fresh liquid to the hot surface.
- Vapor generated moves upward (counter-current to film) and exits at the top to the condenser.
- Concentrated liquid (residue) is collected at the bottom.
- Residence time can be as low as **< 5 seconds** (sometimes called *molecular evaporator* or *short-path evaporator* if equipped with internal condenser under high vacuum).

### Why It Works
- Mechanical agitation provides **continuously renewed film** of uniform thickness, eliminating the heat transfer resistance caused by stagnant boundary layers.
- **Short residence time** and **high vacuum** (down to **0.001 bar**) allow operation at temperatures as low as **30–50 °C**.
- Can concentrate **highly viscous and heat-sensitive materials** that no other evaporator can handle.

### Advantages
- **Shortest residence time** of all evaporators (< 5 s typical).
- Can concentrate **extremely viscous materials** (up to 50,000 cP or more).
- Operates at **very low temperatures** under high vacuum — ideal for highly heat-sensitive materials.
- Excellent heat transfer coefficient ($U = 1500\text{–}3000 \text{ W/m}^2\text{·K}$ for thin-film operation).
- Easy to clean (CIP-able design available).

### Disadvantages
- **Most expensive** evaporator per m² of heating area.
- **Low throughput** compared to other types.
- High mechanical maintenance (rotating shaft, bearings).
- Cannot handle large capacities economically.

### Bioseparation Applications
- Concentration of **heat-sensitive pharmaceuticals, vitamins, fish liver oil, lecithin, spice oleoresins, enzyme solutions**.
- Used in **short-path (molecular) distillation** for purification of vitamins and essential oils.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — every KTU 2024 numerical on evaporators will use one or more of these.**

| # | Equation | Description | Typical Units |
|---|----------|-------------|---------------|
| 1 | $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$ | Overall heat transfer (capacity) | W (Watts) |
| 2 | $\dot{m}_w = \dfrac{Q}{\lambda_s}$ | Water evaporation rate | kg/s |
| 3 | $\lambda_s = 2257 \text{ kJ/kg}$ at 100 °C | Latent heat of steam | kJ/kg |
| 4 | $\Delta T = T_s - T_b$ | Effective temperature difference | K or °C |
| 5 | $T_b = T_b^{'} + \Delta T_b^{BPE} + \Delta T_{hydrostatic}$ | True boiling point (BPE + hydrostatic head) | K |
| 6 | $\text{Economy} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_w}{\dot{m}_s}$ | Steam economy (kg water/kg steam) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $A_i = \dfrac{Q}{U_i \cdot \Delta T_i}$ | Heat transfer area in i-th effect | m² |
| 8 | $\sum_{i=1}^{N} \Delta T_i = \Delta T_{total}$ | Temperature distribution across N effects | K |
| 9 | $\Delta T_i = \Delta T_{total} \cdot \dfrac{Q_i / U_i}{\sum(Q_j / U_j)}$ | Temperature split for equal pressure-drop assumption | K |
| 10 | $\dot{m}_s = \dfrac{\dot{m}_w \cdot \lambda_w}{\lambda_s}$ | Steam consumption | kg/s |
| 11 | $h_{film} = 0.023 \cdot Re^{0.8} \cdot Pr^{0.4} \cdot \dfrac{k}{d}$ | Inside tube film coefficient (Dittus-Boelter) | W/m²·K |
| 12 | $Nu = 0.023 \cdot Re^{0.8} \cdot Pr^{n}$ | Dittus-Boelter correlation ($n = 0.4$ heating, $0.3$ cooling) | dimensionless |
| 13 | $\dfrac{1}{U} = \dfrac{1}{h_o} + \dfrac{x_w}{k_w} + \dfrac{1}{h_i} \cdot \dfrac{d_o}{d_i} + R_f$ | Overall resistance (fouling $R_f$ included) | m²·K/W |
| 14 | $Bo = \dfrac{U \cdot \Delta T}{G \cdot \lambda}$ | Boiling number (dimensionless) | dimensionless |
| 15 | $v_{film} = \dfrac{\Gamma}{\rho \cdot \delta}$ | Falling film velocity from mass flux $\Gamma$ | m/s |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Food industry**: Concentration of fruit juices, milk, tomato paste — falling-film dominates due to thermal sensitivity of vitamins and color.
- **Pharmaceutical industry**: Active pharmaceutical ingredients (APIs), antibiotic solutions — agitated-film used for heat-sensitive molecules.
- **Fermentation downstream**: Recovery of intracellular enzymes requires forced-circulation to handle broths with suspended solids.
- **Sugar industry**: Climbing-film (multi-effect) used for large-scale sugar juice concentration (high capacity, cheap).
- **Dairy**: Falling-film for milk and whey; agitated-film for high-protein concentrates.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Overall Heat Transfer Area for a Single-Effect Evaporator

**Problem Statement:**
Determine the heating surface area $A$ required for an evaporator handling $\dot{m}_f = 10{,}000$ kg/h of feed, with feed concentration $x_f = 0.10$ (mass fraction solids), to produce a final concentration $x_p = 0.50$. Steam is supplied at $T_s = 120 \text{ °C}$ and the solution boils at $T_b = 80 \text{ °C}$. The overall heat transfer coefficient is $U = 2000 \text{ W/m}^2\text{·K}$. Take latent heat of steam $\lambda_s = 2200 \text{ kJ/kg}$ and assume the solution has approximately the same latent heat as water.

### Step 1 — Mass Balance (Total and Solute)
By conservation of solids:

$$
\dot{m}_f \cdot x_f = \dot{m}_p \cdot x_p
$$

$$
10{,}000 \times 0.10 = \dot{m}_p \times 0.50
$$

$$
\dot{m}_p = 2000 \text{ kg/h}
$$

### Step 2 — Water Evaporation Rate
Water removed is the difference:

$$
\dot{m}_w = \dot{m}_f - \dot{m}_p = 10{,}000 - 2000 = 8000 \text{ kg/h}
$$

Converting to SI units:

$$
\dot{m}_w = \frac{8000}{3600} = 2.222 \text{ kg/s}
$$

### Step 3 — Total Heat Duty
Assuming solution enters preheated at boiling point (no sensible heat load) and latent heat of vaporization of water $\lambda_w \approx 2300 \text{ kJ/kg}$:

$$
Q = \dot{m}_w \cdot \lambda_w
$$

$$
Q = 2.222 \times 2300 = 5111 \text{ kW} = 5.111 \times 10^6 \text{ W}
$$

### Step 4 — Effective Temperature Difference
For a single-effect evaporator:

$$
\Delta T = T_s - T_b
$$

$$
\Delta T = 120 - 80 = 40 \text{ K}
$$

> Note: For accurate design we should subtract **BPE**, **hydrostatic head correction**, and **boiling-point rise due to solids**. For simplicity here $\Delta T = 40$ K is used.

### Step 5 — Heat Transfer Area

$$
A = \frac{Q}{U \cdot \Delta T}
$$

$$
A = \frac{5.111 \times 10^6}{2000 \times 40}
$$

$$
A = \frac{5.111 \times 10^6}{80{,}000}
$$

$$
A = 63.89 \text{ m}^2
$$

### Step 6 — Steam Consumption

$$
\dot{m}_s = \frac{Q}{\lambda_s} = \frac{5.111 \times 10^6}{2200 \times 10^3}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_s = 2.323 \text{ kg/s} = 8364 \text{ kg/h}
$$

### Step 7 — Steam Economy

$$
\text{Economy} = \frac{\dot{m}_w}{\dot{m}_s} = \frac{8000}{8364} = 0.957
$$

This is close to the theoretical maximum of **1.0** for single-effect operation, confirming the calculation.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Distribution of Temperature Difference Across N Effects (Multi-Effect Evaporator)

For an N-effect evaporator with **equal heat transfer area** and **equal heat load** in each effect (assumptions made for first-iteration design):

$$
\frac{Q}{A} = U_1 \cdot \Delta T_1 = U_2 \cdot \Delta T_2 = \dots = U_N \cdot \Delta T_N
$$

Summing across all N effects:

$$
\Delta T_1 + \Delta T_2 + \dots + \Delta T_N = \Delta T_{total}
$$

Solving for the i-th effect's temperature difference:

$$
\Delta T_i = \frac{\Delta T_{total}}{U_i \cdot \sum_{j=1}^{N} \dfrac{1}{U_j}}
$$

This is called the **Christiansen–Badger rule** for distributing $\Delta T$ in multi-effect evaporators.

### Worked Example (Two-Effect)
A double-effect evaporator handles dilute enzyme solution. Steam at $T_s = 130 \text{ °C}$, vapor from last effect goes to condenser at $T_c = 50 \text{ °C}$, $U_1 = 2500 \text{ W/m}^2\text{·K}$, $U_2 = 1800 \text{ W/m}^2\text{·K}$. Neglecting BPE:

$$
\Delta T_{total} = T_s - T_c = 130 - 50 = 80 \text{ K}
$$

Sum of reciprocals:

$$
\sum \frac{1}{U_j} = \frac{1}{2500} + \frac{1}{1800} = 0.0004 + 0.000556 = 0.000956 \text{ m}^2\text{·K/W}
$$

Effect 1:

$$
\Delta T_1 = \frac{80}{2500 \times 0.000956} = \frac{80}{2.389} = 33.49 \text{ K}
$$

Effect 2:

$$
\Delta T_2 = 80 - 33.49 = 46.51 \text{ K}
$$

> [!NOTE]
