> **Interpretation:** Effect 1 (higher $U$) gets the smaller $\Delta T$; Effect 2 (lower $U$) compensates with a larger $\Delta T$ to maintain equal area — this is the fundamental principle of multi-effect design.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Mass Flux in a Falling-Film Evaporator

For a vertical tube with falling film, the **mass flux per unit perimeter** (or wetted perimeter) is:

$$
\Gamma = \frac{\dot{m}_w}{\pi \cdot d_i \cdot N_t}
$$

where $N_t$ is the number of tubes and $d_i$ is the inner diameter.

For **gravity-driven laminar wavy film** (Reynolds number $Re < 30$), the average film thickness is given by the **Nusselt falling-film theory**:

$$
\delta = \left(\frac{3 \cdot \mu^2}{\rho^2 \cdot g}\right)^{1/3} \cdot Re^{1/3}
$$

The average film velocity:

$$
v_{film} = \frac{\rho \cdot g \cdot \delta^2}{3 \cdot \mu} = \frac{\Gamma}{\rho \cdot \delta}
$$

This is the foundation for **designing the distribution system** of falling-film evaporators.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (the most exam-relevant table for KTU)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This matrix is the single most asked comparison in KTU Evaporation Equipment questions.**

| Parameter | Climbing-Film | Falling-Film | Forced-Circulation | Agitated-Film |
|-----------|---------------|--------------|---------------------|---------------|
| **Flow direction** | Upward | Downward | External circulation | Downward (mechanical) |
| **Driving force** | Vapor lift (thermosiphon) | Gravity + vapor shear | Pump | Gravity + rotor blades |
| **Residence time** | 20 s – few min | 5 – 20 s | Several minutes | < 5 s (often < 1 s) |
| **Heat sensitivity** | Poor (long time) | Good | Poor | Excellent |
| **Viscosity limit (Pa·s)** | < 0.1 | 0.1 – 0.5 | 1 – 10 | Up to 50 |
| **Fouling resistance** | Poor | Moderate | Excellent | Good |
| **Heat transfer coeff $U$ (W/m²·K)** | 2000 – 3500 | 1500 – 3000 | 2500 – 5000 | 1500 – 3000 |
| **Capital cost** | Low | Moderate | High | Very high |
| **Operating cost** | Low | Moderate | High (pump) | High (agitator) |
| **Capacity / throughput** | Very high | High | High | Low |
| **Vacuum suitability** | Moderate | Excellent | Good | Outstanding |
| **Typical products** | Sugar juice, milk | Juices, enzymes, antibiotics | Salt solutions, viscous broths | Lecithin, fish oil, APIs |
| **Key disadvantage** | Tall building needed | Needs perfect distribution | High pumping cost | Very expensive |
| **Key advantage** | High capacity, cheap | Short residence time | Handles fouling | Handles viscous + heat-sensitive |

---

## 3.5 Selection Algorithm (KTU Board Exam Favorite)

```
START
  |
  |-- Is product heat-sensitive? (Yes/No)
  |       |
  |       |-- YES, AND viscous --> AGITATED-FILM
  |       |-- YES, BUT low viscosity --> FALLING-FILM
  |
  |-- Is product fouling / scaling / crystallizing?
  |       |
  |       |-- YES --> FORCED-CIRCULATION
  |
  |-- Is capacity very high & product cheap / stable?
  |       |
  |       |-- YES --> CLIMBING-FILM (often in multi-effect)
  |
  |-- DEFAULT --> FALLING-FILM (most versatile)
  |
END
```
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Generic Evaporator

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Liquid Feed Tank<br/>m_f x_f] --> B[Pre-heater<br/>economizer]
    B --> C[Steam Chest<br/>Calandria<br/>Steam at T_s]
    C --> D[Evaporator Body<br/>Tube Bundle / Film / Agitator]
    D --> E[Vapor Liquid Separator<br/>Cyclone / Vapor Head]
    E --> F[Condenser<br/>Vacuum System]
    E --> G[Concentrated Product<br/>m_p x_p]
    F --> H[Condensate Receiver]
    D -. Heat Transfer U.-> C
    G -. Recycle Loop optional .-> D
```

## 4.2 Climbing-Film Evaporator Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Preheated Feed<br/>Bottom Inlet] --> B[Steam Chest<br/>Shell side condenses steam]
    B --> C[Vertical Tubes 3 to 10 m<br/>Vapor lift carries liquid upward]
    C --> D[Vapor Liquid Separator<br/>Top body / vapor head]
    D --> E[Concentrated Liquid Out<br/>Downtake returns to feed if needed]
    D --> F[Vapor to Condenser<br/>or Next Effect]
    B -. Heat Transfer .-> C
    C -. Natural Thermosiphon .-> D
```

## 4.3 Falling-Film Evaporator Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Feed from Top<br/>Distribution Device] --> B[Distribution Cups<br/>or Spray Nozzle]
    B --> C[Vertical Tubes<br/>Thin Film 0.1 to 0.3 mm flows down]
    C --> D[Steam Shell<br/>Condenses outside tubes]
    C --> E[Bottom Vapor Liquid Outlet]
    E --> F[External Cyclone Separator]
    F --> G[Concentrate Outlet]
    F --> H[Vapor to Condenser]
    D -. Heat Transfer .-> C
```

## 4.4 Forced-Circulation Evaporator Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Feed Inlet] --> B[Centrifugal Pump<br/>2 to 6 m/s velocity]
    B --> C[Heat Exchanger Tubes<br/>Subcooled or suppressed boiling]
    C --> D[Vapor Liquid Separator<br/>Flash evaporation occurs]
    D --> E[Concentrate Outlet]
    D --> F[Vapor to Condenser]
    D -. Recycle Loop .-> B
    G[Steam Side] -. Heat Transfer .-> C
```

## 4.5 Agitated-Film (Wiped-Film) Evaporator Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Feed Inlet at Top] --> B[Distribution Ring<br/>spreads to wall]
    B --> C[Heated Jacket<br/>steam or thermal fluid]
    C --> D[Rotating Rotor<br/>100 to 600 rpm]
    D --> E[Wiped Film on Wall<br/>thickness 0.05 to 0.5 mm]
    E --> F[Residue Outlet at Bottom<br/>concentrate]
    E --> G[Vapor Upward<br/>to Internal or External Condenser]
    G --> H[Vacuum Pump]
    C -. Heat Transfer .-> E
    D -. Mechanical Agitation .-> E
```

## 4.6 Comparative Decision Flowchart for Selecting an Evaporator

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start Selection] --> B{Is product<br/>heat sensitive?}
    B -- Yes --> C{Is viscosity<br/>high > 1 Pa s?}
    B -- No --> D{Fouling or<br/>scaling liquid?}
    C -- Yes --> E[Agitated Film<br/>Evaporator]
    C -- No --> F[Falling Film<br/>Evaporator]
    D -- Yes --> G[Forced Circulation<br/>Evaporator]
    D -- No --> H{Very large<br/>capacity required?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Climbing Film<br/>Multi Effect]
    H -- No --> J[Falling Film<br/>Versatile Default]
    E --> K[End]
    F --> K
    G --> K
    I --> K
    J --> K
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**Explain the working principle of a climbing-film evaporator with a neat diagram.**

#### Model Answer (3 marks — for 60–80 words):

A climbing-film evaporator (long-tube vertical) consists of a bundle of long vertical tubes (3–10 m) heated externally by steam in a calandria. The preheated feed enters at the bottom; as it boils, the rising **vapor bubbles drag the liquid upward**, forming an **annular film** on the tube walls (vapor binding / thermosiphon effect). This vapor-liquid mixture reaches the top where it is separated in the vapor head — vapor goes to condenser/next effect, concentrate is withdrawn. The natural circulation gives high heat transfer coefficient (2000–3500 W/m²·K).

*(Award 1 mark for diagram, 2 marks for explanation.)*

---

### Question 2. **[KTU University Exam — December 2023]**
**List any six advantages and disadvantages of falling-film evaporators over climbing-film evaporators.**

#### Model Answer (3 marks — table format, 6 points, ½ mark each):

| Falling-Film vs Climbing-Film | Advantage (Falling) | Disadvantage (Falling) |
|---|---|---|
| Residence time | 5–20 s (much shorter) | — |
| Vacuum operation | Excellent — no hydrostatic head | — |
| Heat-sensitive products | Suitable | — |
| Feed distribution | — | Critical — needs perfect top distribution |
| Foaming liquids | Not suitable | — |
| Capital cost | — | Higher than climbing-film |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Climming & Falling-Film Evaporators

**[KTU University Exam — December 2023 / Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the construction, working principle, and applications of a **climbing-film evaporator**. Discuss why it is called a **natural circulation evaporator**. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Compare falling-film and climbing-film evaporators on the basis of any **seven parameters**. Explain why falling-film evaporators are preferred for **heat-sensitive biological products**. **[7 marks]**

#### Model Solution

**(a) — 7 marks**

- **Construction (2 marks):**
  - Vertical tubes of length 3–10 m, diameter 25–75 mm, housed in a steam chest (calandria).
  - Vapor head / separator on top.
  - Downtake pipe returns unevaporated liquid (optional recirculation).
- **Working (3 marks):**
  - Feed enters at bottom, steam condenses on shell side providing latent heat.
  - Liquid boils; vapor bubbles coalesce and push liquid up — **vapor binding**.
  - Vapor-liquid mixture reaches top; vapor separated and sent to condenser/next effect; concentrated liquid withdrawn.
- **Natural circulation / thermosiphon (1 mark):**
  - Density difference between liquid (bottom) and vapor-rich mixture (top) creates pressure head driving natural circulation without any pump.
- **Applications (1 mark):**
  - Sugar juice, milk, dilute salt solutions, whey.

*(Award 1 mark for diagram.)*

**(b) — 7 marks**

| Parameter | Climbing-Film | Falling-Film |
|---|---|---|
| Flow direction | Upward | Downward |
| Driving force | Vapor lift / thermosiphon | Gravity + vapor shear |
| Residence time | 20 s – several min | 5–20 s |
| Heat transfer coefficient | 2000–3500 W/m²·K | 1500–3000 W/m²·K |
| Viscosity handling | < 0.1 Pa·s | 0.1–0.5 Pa·s |
| Feed distribution needs | Less critical | Very critical |
| Suitable for heat-sensitive | No | Yes |
| Capital cost | Low | Moderate |

**Why falling-film for heat-sensitive biologicals (3 marks):**
1. **Very short residence time** (5–20 s vs 20 s – several min) — minimizes thermal exposure.
2. Operates efficiently under **high vacuum** with very small $\Delta T$ — keeps product temperature low (40–60 °C).
3. **Co-current vapor-liquid flow** gives uniform temperature along tube, no hydrostatic superheat.
4. Excellent for enzymes, antibiotics, vitamins, fruit juices, milk.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Forced-Circulation & Agitated-Film Evaporators

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 / Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Describe with a neat sketch the **construction and working of a forced-circulation evaporator**. Why is boiling suppressed inside the heat exchanger tubes? List **two specific industrial applications**. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Explain the **agitated (wiped) thin-film evaporator** in detail. Discuss its **advantages** for bioseparation of **highly viscous and heat-sensitive** products such as lecithin and fish liver oil. List **two disadvantages**. **[7 marks]**

#### Model Solution

**(a) — 7 marks**

- **Construction (2 marks):**
  - Centrifugal pump + heat exchanger (calandria) + vapor body + condenser.
  - Pump on suction side, flow rate 2–6 m/s.
- **Working (2 marks):**
  - Liquid pumped through heat exchanger is heated without boiling (or with suppressed boiling); superheated liquid flashes in separator.
  - Concentrate exits from bottom, vapor from top.
- **Suppressed boiling explanation (2 marks):**
  - Static liquid column above tubes creates **hydrostatic pressure** higher than saturation pressure at tube-wall temperature, preventing bubble nucleation.
  - Boiling only occurs in the separator at lower pressure → avoids scaling and fouling inside tubes.
- **Two industrial applications (1 mark, ½ each):**
  - Concentration of **caustic soda solutions, sugar syrups with crystallization, fermentation broths, viscous salts**.

*(Award 1 mark for diagram.)*

**(b) — 7 marks**

- **Construction (2 marks):**
  - Vertical jacketed vessel; rotor with hinged/fixed blades; condenser internal or external; high vacuum (down to 0.001 bar).
- **Working (2 marks):**
  - Feed spread into thin film (0.05–0.5 mm) on heated wall by rotating blades; vapor moves upward counter-current; concentrate exits at bottom.
  - Residence time **< 5 seconds**.
- **Advantages for viscous heat-sensitive products (2 marks, ½ each for any four):**
  - Shortest residence time → minimum thermal degradation of lecithin and fish oil PUFA (omega-3).
  - Handles viscosities up to 50,000 cP — only evaporator that can do this.
  - Very high vacuum gives operating temperatures as low as 30–50 °C.
  - Continuous renewal of film eliminates boundary layer resistance → high $U$.
  - CIP-able; suitable for pharma GMP.
- **Two disadvantages (1 mark, ½ each):**
  - Very high capital cost per m².
  - Low throughput / capacity.
  - Mechanical maintenance of rotor and bearings.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that KTU examiners penalize heavily:**
>
> 1. **Confusing climbing-film (rising film) and falling-film** — students often mix up flow direction and driving force. Remember: **Climbing = UP = thermosiphon / vapor lift; Falling = DOWN = gravity + vapor shear.**
>
> 2. **Not stating residence time values** in comparison tables — KTU board examiners specifically look for **numerical values** like "5–20 seconds" or "several minutes". A qualitative answer without numbers loses 1–2 marks.
>
> 3. **Calling forced-circulation a "natural circulation" system** — this is a fundamental conceptual error. Forced-circulation uses an **external pump**; climbing-film is the natural-circulation one.
>
> 4. **Forgetting to draw the diagram** in 7-mark questions — diagrams carry **1 dedicated mark** in KTU valuation. Without a labeled sketch, you lose a full mark.
>
> 5. **Missing BPE (Boiling Point Elevation)** in numerical problems — if feed has dissolved solids, true boiling point $T_b = T_b^{'} + \Delta T_{BPE}$ must be used. Many students use 100 °C as boiling point, losing 2 marks.
>
> 6. **Wrong units in $\Delta T$** — use **Kelvin (K)** consistently in heat-transfer equations. Mixing °C and K is an automatic ½ mark deduction in numericals.
>
> 7. **Conflating "thin-film" with "falling-film"** — wiped-film and falling-film are different. The wiping action (mechanical agitation) is the hallmark of agitated-film evaporators.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Climbing-film = Upward, thermosiphon-driven, cheap, high capacity, NOT for heat-sensitive.**
- **Falling-film = Downward, gravity + vapor shear, short residence, IDEAL for heat-sensitive bioseparation.**
- **Forced-circulation = Pump-driven, suppresses tube boiling, handles fouling & viscosity, LONG residence.**
- **Agitated-film = Mechanical wiping, shortest residence (< 5 s), handles ultra-viscous + thermolabile, MOST expensive.**
- **Selection hierarchy: Heat sensitivity → Viscosity → Fouling → Capacity → Cost.**
- **Heat transfer coefficient (high to low): Forced-circulation > Climbing-film > Falling-film ≈ Agitated-film.**
- **Steam economy improves with multi-effect design (theoretical max = number of effects).**
- **BPE correction must be included in all real bioseparation numericals.**
- **Dittus-Boelter correlation $Nu = 0.023 \cdot Re^{0.8} \cdot Pr^{0.4}$ governs convective side heat transfer.**
- **Always include a labeled diagram in 7-mark KTU questions (1 mark reserved).**
- **Christiansen–Badger rule** $\Delta T_i = \Delta T_{total} / (U_i \cdot \sum 1/U_j)$ governs multi-effect temperature distribution.
- **Vacuum operation is the single most important design choice for thermolabile biomolecules** — falling-film and agitated-film both excel here.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Adsorptive Bioseparations: Adsorption Isotherms and Adsorption Techniques (Batch and Continuous)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT525 - BIOSEPARATION TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Adsorptive Bioseparations: Adsorption Isotherms and Adsorption Techniques (Batch and Continuous)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Adsorption

> [!NOTE]
> **Adsorption** is a surface-based separation process in which molecules from a fluid phase (gas or liquid) accumulate and adhere onto the surface of a solid (or, less commonly, liquid) substrate, driven by physical forces or chemical bonds. In bioseparation technology, adsorption is the principal unit operation for capturing, concentrating, and purifying biomolecules such as proteins, enzymes, antibodies, antibiotics, amino acids, and nucleic acids.

The key terminology is summarized below:

| Term | Definition | Role in Bioseparation |
|---|---|---|
| **Adsorbent** | The solid (or liquid) phase providing the surface | Resin, activated carbon, silica, alumina, ion-exchange matrix |
| **Adsorbate** | The species being adsorbed (from the fluid phase) | Target protein, dye, antibiotic, impurity |
| **Sorbate** | Generic term for the species in bulk fluid | Refers to free (un-adsorbed) molecules |
| **Desorption** | Reverse of adsorption; release of adsorbate from surface | Enables **regeneration** of the adsorbent |
| **Equilibrium** | State where the rate of adsorption equals desorption | Governed by adsorption isotherms |
| **Capacity (q)** | Mass of adsorbate held per unit mass of adsorbent (mg/g) | Design parameter for columns |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Adsorption ≠ Absorption**: Adsorption is a *surface* phenomenon (molecules stick to the interface), whereas absorption is a *bulk* phenomenon (molecules penetrate into the bulk of the phase, e.g., water soaking into a sponge). The umbrella term is **sorption**.

## 1.2 Types of Adsorption

Adsorption is broadly classified into two types based on the nature of the binding force:

### (a) Physisorption (Physical Adsorption)
- Caused by **weak van der Waals forces** (London dispersion, dipole–dipole).
- Low enthalpy of adsorption: **5–40 kJ/mol**.
- **Reversible** — easily desorbed by lowering pressure or raising temperature.
- **Non-specific**; multi-layer adsorption is possible.
- No electron transfer; the molecule retains its chemical identity.

### (b) Chemisorption (Chemical Adsorption)
- Caused by **covalent or ionic chemical bonds** between adsorbate and surface.
- High enthalpy of adsorption: **40–400 kJ/mol**.
- **Often irreversible** under mild conditions.
- **Highly specific**; monolayer adsorption only.
- Electron sharing/transfer may occur; the adsorbate may undergo chemical change.

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Memory Trick for KTU Viva**: *Physisorption is "physical & passive" (just forces), Chemisorption is "chemical & committed" (forms bonds).*

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you have a **shallow tray filled with tiny iron nails** (this is your *adsorbent* surface, with the nail heads being *active sites*). Now you sprinkle **paper clips** (your *adsorbate* molecules) over the tray.

- Each nail head can hold **only one paper clip** at a time (like a *monolayer*).
- The paper clips "stick" to the nails because the magnetic surface attracts them (*van der Waals forces* in physisorption; *chemical bonds* in chemisorption).
- If the surface is **rough and full of cavities** (think *activated charcoal*), it can hold *enormous* numbers of paper clips — that is why a tiny spoonful of charcoal can decolorize a large jug of coloured water.
- When the nails are **fully covered**, the surface is "**saturated**" — no more clips can stick.

**Mapping the analogy to bioseparation:**

| Analogy | Real Process |
|---|---|
| Tray with iron nails | Chromatography column packed with resin beads |
| Paper clips | Target protein (e.g., lysozyme, BSA, antibody) |
| Magnetic stickiness | Hydrophobic / ionic / affinity interaction with ligand |
| Fully covered nails | Column breakthrough — exit concentration = inlet concentration |
| Pulling clips off with a magnet | Elution / desorption using buffer change (pH, salt) |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight**: In bioseparation, *ion-exchange*, *hydrophobic interaction*, *affinity*, and *reversed-phase chromatography* are all special cases of adsorption-driven separations.

## 1.4 Why Adsorption Matters in Downstream Processing

- **Selectivity**: Affinity adsorbents can pick a *single* protein out of thousands.
- **Mild operating conditions**: Room temperature, aqueous buffers → preserves biomolecule activity.
- **Scalability**: From analytical HPLC (mg) to industrial Simulated Moving Bed (SMB) (tons).
- **Reusability**: Adsorbents are regenerated over **hundreds of cycles** (e.g., Protein A resin).
- **High purity**: Often achieves >95% purity in a single step.

## 1.5 Common Adsorbents Used in Bioseparation

| Adsorbent | Typical Use |
|---|---|
| **Activated carbon** | Decolorization, removal of trace organics |
| **Silica gel** | Normal-phase chromatography |
| **Octadecyl-silica (C18)** | Reversed-phase chromatography |
| **Ion-exchange resins** (DEAE, CM, SP) | Protein capture by charge |
| **Dye-ligand matrices** (Cibacron Blue) | Enzyme purification |
| **Protein A / Protein G** | Monoclonal antibody capture |
| **IMAC resins** (Ni-NTA) | His-tagged proteins |
| **Hydroxyapatite** | Mixed-mode protein separation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) surface area** of activated carbon can exceed **1500 m²/g**, illustrating why adsorption is so effective — surface area, not volume, is the critical design variable.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Adsorption Equilibrium Concept

When a solid adsorbent is placed in contact with a fluid containing an adsorbate, molecules continuously adsorb and desorb. After sufficient time, the system reaches **dynamic equilibrium** — the *net* rate of adsorption is zero. The relationship between the *equilibrium solid-phase loading* $q_e$ (mg adsorbate per g adsorbent) and the *equilibrium fluid-phase concentration* $C_e$ (mg/L) at a given temperature is called an **adsorption isotherm**.

Mathematically, an isotherm is a function:

$$
q_e = f(C_e) \quad \text{at constant } T
$$

## 2.2 The Three Classical Isotherm Models

### 2.2.1 Langmuir Isotherm (Monolayer)

**Postulates** (Irving Langmuir, 1918):
1. The adsorbent surface has a **finite number of identical sites**.
2. Each site can hold **one and only one** molecule (monolayer).
3. **No lateral interaction** between adsorbed molecules.
4. Adsorption is **reversible**; equilibrium is dynamic.

The isotherm equation and its linear forms are tabulated below.

### 2.2.2 Freundlich Isotherm (Heterogeneous, Empirical)

An *empirical* model that fits heterogeneous surfaces where adsorption energy decreases with surface coverage. No finite capacity.

### 2.2.3 BET Isotherm (Multilayer)

Extends Langmuir to multilayer adsorption; used to compute the **specific surface area** of porous solids.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize this table. KTU problems almost always involve one of these isotherms.

| Isotherm | Non-linear Form | Linear Form | Plot to Get Constants | Parameters |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Langmuir** | $q_e = \dfrac{q_{\max} \, K_L \, C_e}{1 + K_L \, C_e}$ | $\dfrac{C_e}{q_e} = \dfrac{1}{K_L q_{\max}} + \dfrac{C_e}{q_{\max}}$ | $\dfrac{C_e}{q_e}$ vs $C_e$ | $q_{\max}$ (mg/g), $K_L$ (L/mg) |
| **Langmuir (alt.)** | $q_e = \dfrac{q_{\max} \, K_L \, C_e}{1 + K_L \, C_e}$ | $\dfrac{1}{q_e} = \dfrac{1}{q_{\max}} + \dfrac{1}{K_L q_{\max} C_e}$ | $\dfrac{1}{q_e}$ vs $\dfrac{1}{C_e}$ | $q_{\max}$, $K_L$ |
| **Freundlich** | $q_e = K_F \, C_e^{1/n}$ | $\log q_e = \log K_F + \dfrac{1}{n} \log C_e$ | $\log q_e$ vs $\log C_e$ | $K_F$ (mg/g)(L/mg)$^{1/n}$, $n$ (dimensionless) |
| **BET** | $q = \dfrac{q_m C_b C}{(C_s - C)\big[1 + (C_b/C_s - 1)(C/C_s)\big]}$ | $\dfrac{C}{(C_s - C)q} = \dfrac{1}{C_b q_m} + \dfrac{(C_b - 1)C}{C_b q_m C_s}$ | $\dfrac{C}{(C_s - C)q}$ vs $\dfrac{C}{C_s}$ | $q_m$ (mg/g), $C_b$ (BET constant) |
| **Mass balance (batch)** | $q_e = \dfrac{(C_0 - C_e)V}{m}$ | — | — | $C_0$ (mg/L), $V$ (L), $m$ (g) |
| **Thomas (continuous)** | $\dfrac{C_t}{C_0} = \dfrac{1}{1 + \exp\!\big(k_{Th} q_0 M / Q - k_{Th} C_0 t\big)}$ | — | — | $k_{Th}$ (L/mg·min), $Q$ (L/min) |
| **Bed capacity** | $M_{ads} = q_{eq} \, M_{bed}$ | — | — | Total mass held (mg) |
| **Stoichiometric time** | $t^* = \dfrac{q_{eq} M_{bed}}{C_0 Q}$ | — | — | Ideal breakthrough (min) |
| **Bed height service time** | $t_b = \dfrac{q_{eq} \rho_b H A}{C_0 Q}$ | — | — | $H$ = bed height, $A$ = area |

> [!TIP]
> **Dimensional consistency check for KTU**: Always verify $q_e$ in **mg/g**, $C_e$ in **mg/L**, $K_L$ in **L/mg** so that $K_L C_e$ is dimensionless.

## 2.4 The Langmuir Separation Factor (R_L)

A dimensionless constant predicting whether the isotherm is **favorable** for column operation:

$$
R_L = \frac{1}{1 + K_L C_0}
$$

| $R_L$ Value | Isotherm Type | Column Behavior |
|---|---|---|
| $R_L > 1$ | Unfavorable | Long tailing, poor utilization |
| $R_L = 1$ | Linear | Sharp boundary, possible |
| $0 < R_L < 1$ | Favorable | Sharp breakthrough, **ideal** |
| $R_L = 0$ | Irreversible | Perfectly sharp, theoretical limit |

> [!NOTE]
> Industrial bioseparation columns are always designed in the **favorable** region ($0 < R_L < 1$).

## 2.5 Mass Transfer Zones in a Fixed-Bed Adsorber

A real column has three distinct regions that travel down the bed during operation:

1. **Saturated (Equilibrated) Zone** — top of bed, $C = C_0$ (adsorbate fully loaded).
2. **Mass Transfer Zone (MTZ)** — middle, $C$ drops from $C_0$ to near 0; this is the *active* region.
3. **Fresh Zone** — bottom, bed still clean, $C \approx 0$ in effluent.

The **breakthrough curve** is the plot of $C_{\text{out}}/C_0$ vs. $t$ at the column exit:
- $t_b$ (breakthrough time): point at which $C_{\text{out}}$ reaches a preset value (typically **5–10%** of $C_0$).
- $t_s$ (saturation / stoichiometric time): point at which $C_{\text{out}} = C_0$ (bed is fully spent).

## 2.6 Why These Models Matter in Engineering

- **Langmuir** sets the **maximum capacity** of an adsorbent — the upper limit of any column.
- **Freundlich** captures the *heterogeneity* of real surfaces (e.g., activated carbon with diverse pore sizes).
- **BET** lets us characterize *porosity* — vital for validating adsorbent suppliers.
- **Thomas / BDST models** predict *breakthrough* — the metric that dictates when a column must be regenerated.
- **MTZ length** determines the *safety factor* when scaling from lab to plant.

## 2.7 Operating Modes Overview

| Mode | Configuration | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Batch (stirred tank)** | Adsorbent + feed mixed in a vessel | Lab studies, small-scale capture, pre-treatment |
| **Packed/Fixed Bed** | Column packed with adsorbent, feed flows through | Most common industrial mode |
| **Moving Bed** | Adsorbent flows co-current or counter-current to feed | Continuous, used in wastewater |
| **Fluidized Bed** | Upward flow suspends adsorbent particles | Handles solids/particulate feeds |
| **Simulated Moving Bed (SMB)** | Periodic port switching mimics true counter-current | Chiral drug separation, large-scale |
| **Expanded Bed Adsorption (EBA)** | Classified bed, allows particulate feed | Direct recovery from crude broth |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Langmuir Isotherm (Kinetic Approach)

**Given:** A solid surface with $N$ identical active sites per unit area, contacted with a fluid of concentration $C$.

**Step 1 — Define fractional surface coverage:**
Let $\theta$ = fraction of sites occupied.

Therefore:
- Number of vacant sites per unit area = $N(1 - \theta)$
- Number of occupied sites per unit area = $N\theta$

**Step 2 — Write the rate of adsorption:**
Molecules striking and sticking to vacant sites is proportional to both the fluid concentration $C$ and the fraction of vacant sites $(1 - \theta)$.

$$
r_{\text{ads}} = k_a \, C \, N(1 - \theta)
$$

where $k_a$ = adsorption rate constant (L/mg·min).

**Step 3 — Write the rate of desorption:**
Molecules leaving the surface is proportional only to the fraction of occupied sites.

$$
r_{\text{des}} = k_d \, N \theta
$$

where $k_d$ = desorption rate constant (min$^{-1}$).

**Step 4 — Apply equilibrium condition:**
At equilibrium, the rate of adsorption equals the rate of desorption (dynamic balance).

$$
k_a \, C \, N(1 - \theta) = k_d \, N \theta
$$

**Step 5 — Cancel $N$ on both sides:**

$$
k_a \, C \, (1 - \theta) = k_d \, \theta
$$

**Step 6 — Expand the left-hand side:**

$$
k_a \, C - k_a \, C \, \theta = k_d \, \theta
$$

**Step 7 — Collect the $\theta$ terms on one side:**

$$
k_a \, C = \theta \, k_d + \theta \, k_a \, C
$$

**Step 8 — Factor out $\theta$:**

$$
k_a \, C = \theta \, (k_d + k_a \, C)
$$

**Step 9 — Solve for $\theta$:**

$$
\theta = \frac{k_a \, C}{k_d + k_a \, C}
$$

**Step 10 — Define the Langmuir equilibrium constant:**

$$
K_L = \frac{k_a}{k_d} \quad \text{(units: L/mg)}
$$

**Step 11 — Substitute and divide numerator and denominator by $k_d$:**

$$
\theta = \frac{(k_a/k_d) \, C}{1 + (k_a/k_d) \, C} = \frac{K_L \, C}{1 + K_L \, C}
$$

**Step 12 — Convert fractional coverage to solid-phase loading:**

The solid-phase loading $q_e$ is linearly related to $\theta$ by the maximum capacity $q_{\max}$:

$$
q_e = q_{\max} \cdot \theta
$$

**Step 13 — Final Langmuir isotherm equation:**

$$
\boxed{\, q_e = \frac{q_{\max} \, K_L \, C_e}{1 + K_L \, C_e} \,}
$$

This is the canonical Langmuir isotherm expression, valid for monolayer adsorption on a homogeneous surface at constant temperature.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Linearized Form of Langmuir Isotherm

**Step 1 — Start from the non-linear form:**

$$
q_e = \frac{q_{\max} \, K_L \, C_e}{1 + K_L \, C_e}
$$

**Step 2 — Invert both sides:**

$$
\frac{1}{q_e} = \frac{1 + K_L \, C_e}{q_{\max} \, K_L \, C_e}
$$

**Step 3 — Split the right-hand side:**

$$
\frac{1}{q_e} = \frac{1}{q_{\max} \, K_L \, C_e} + \frac{K_L \, C_e}{q_{\max} \, K_L \, C_e}
$$

**Step 4 — Simplify the second term:**

$$
\frac{1}{q_e} = \frac{1}{q_{\max} \, K_L \, C_e} + \frac{1}{q_{\max}}
$$

**Step 5 — Rearrange into a linear ($y = mx + c$) form:**

$$
\boxed{\, \frac{1}{q_e} = \frac{1}{q_{\max}} + \frac{1}{K_L \, q_{\max}} \cdot \frac{1}{C_e} \,}
$$

A plot of $1/q_e$ (y-axis) versus $1/C_e$ (x-axis) gives:
- **Intercept** = $1/q_{\max}$ → hence $q_{\max}$
- **Slope** = $1/(K_L \, q_{\max})$ → hence $K_L$ = intercept / slope

**Alternative linear form** (often more accurate in practice):

$$
\boxed{\, \frac{C_e}{q_e} = \frac{1}{K_L \, q_{\max}} + \frac{C_e}{q_{\max}} \,}
$$

A plot of $C_e/q_e$ vs. $C_e$ is preferred because it minimizes error at low concentrations.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Freundlich Isotherm (Statistical Approach)

The Freundlich equation is empirical but can be derived heuristically by assuming an exponential distribution of adsorption site energies.

**Step 1 — Assume a continuous distribution of site energies** $E$ across the surface, with the number of sites having energy between $E$ and $E + dE$ given by:

$$
dN(E) = N_0 \, e^{-E/RT} \, dE
$$

**Step 2 — For each site of energy $E$, equilibrium follows a Boltzmann distribution:**

$$
\theta(E) = \frac{1}{1 + \exp[(E - E_{\text{ads}})/RT]} \approx \frac{C}{C_0} \, e^{(E_{\text{ads}} - E)/RT} \quad \text{(at low coverage)}
$$

**Step 3 — Integrate over all site energies** to obtain total loading:

$$
q_e = \int_0^{\infty} \theta(E) \, dN(E)
$$

**Step 4 — The integration yields the empirical Freundlich form:**

$$
\boxed{\, q_e = K_F \, C_e^{1/n} \,}
$$

where $K_F$ and $n$ are temperature-dependent empirical constants.

**Step 5 — Linearize using logarithms:**

$$
\log q_e = \log K_F + \frac{1}{n} \log C_e
$$

This is a straight line in log-log coordinates with slope $1/n$ and intercept $\log K_F$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical meaning of $n$**:
> - $1 < n < 10$ → **favorable** adsorption.
> - $n = 1$ → linear isotherm.
> - $n < 1$ → unfavorable, indicates cooperative multi-layer or microporous filling.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Thomas Model for Breakthrough Curves (Continuous)

**Given:** A fixed-bed column of mass $M$ (g), volumetric flow rate $Q$ (L/min), inlet concentration $C_0$ (mg/L), and equilibrium solid-phase concentration $q_0$ (mg/g). The Thomas model assumes **Langmuir kinetics** (no axial dispersion) and a **second-order reversible reaction** between solute and adsorption sites.

**Step 1 — Mass balance over an infinitesimal column slice of length $dz$:**

$$
\varepsilon \frac{\partial C}{\partial t} + \rho_b \frac{\partial q}{\partial t} + v \frac{\partial C}{\partial z} = 0
$$

where $\varepsilon$ = bed porosity, $\rho_b$ = bulk density (g/L), $v$ = interstitial velocity.

**Step 2 — Apply the Langmuir second-order kinetic rate:**

$$
\rho_b \frac{\partial q}{\partial t} = k_1 C (q_{\max} - q) - k_2 q
$$

where $k_1$ (L/mg·min) and $k_2$ (1/min) are forward and reverse rate constants.

**Step 3 — Introduce the Thomas rate constant** combining both:

$$
k_{Th} = k_1 = k_2 / (K_L C_0)
$$

**Step 4 — Solve the coupled PDE under the assumptions of** negligible axial dispersion, constant velocity, and plug flow, with boundary conditions $C(0,t) = C_0$ and $q(z,0) = 0$:

The solution is the **Thomas logistic function**:

$$
\boxed{\, \frac{C_t}{C_0} = \frac{1}{1 + \exp\!\left(\dfrac{k_{Th} \, q_0 \, M}{Q} - k_{Th} \, C_0 \, t\right)} \,}
$$

**Step 5 — Define the stoichiometric time $t^*$** as the midpoint of the breakthrough curve (where $C_t/C_0 = 0.5$):

At $C_t/C_0 = 0.5$, the exponential term equals 1, so:

$$
\exp\left(\frac{k_{Th} q_0 M}{Q} - k_{Th} C_0 t^*\right) = 1
$$

$$
\frac{k_{Th} q_0 M}{Q} - k_{Th} C_0 t^* = 0
$$

$$
\boxed{\, t^* = \frac{q_0 M}{C_0 Q} \,}
$$

This is the **stoichiometric capacity** of the bed — the time at which 50% of the column's capacity has been used.

**Step 6 — Material balance check:**
Total mass of solute fed to the bed by $t^*$ = $C_0 Q t^* = q_0 M$ = total mass of solute held on the bed. ✓

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Bed Depth Service Time (BDST) Model

The **Hutchins BDST model** is a linearized version of Thomas for design engineers. The relationship between bed depth $H$ and service time $t$ is:

$$
\boxed{\, t = \frac{N_0}{C_0 \, v} H - \frac{1}{k \, C_0} \ln\!\left(\frac{C_0}{C_t} - 1\right) \,}
$$

where:
- $N_0$ = adsorption capacity per unit bed volume (mg/L of bed)
- $v$ = superficial velocity (cm/min)
- $k$ = rate constant (L/mg·min)
- $H$ = bed depth (cm)

A plot of $t$ vs. $H$ is a straight line:
- **Slope** = $N_0 / (C_0 v)$
- **Intercept** = $-\dfrac{1}{k C_0} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{C_0}{C_t} - 1\right)$

> [!TIP]
> **For KTU design problems**: Given a lab-scale breakthrough curve, you can use the BDST slope/intercept to scale up the column to industrial dimensions. The slope remains the same; only the intercept is adjusted to account for the new breakthrough criterion.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Isotherms Side by Side

| Property | Langmuir | Freundlich | BET |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Type** | Theoretical | Empirical | Theoretical |
| **Surface assumption** | Homogeneous | Heterogeneous | Homogeneous (multilayer) |
| **Layers** | Monolayer | Multilayer implicit | Multilayer |
| **Maximum capacity** | Yes ($q_{\max}$) | No (asymptotic) | Yes ($q_m$ at monolayer) |
| **Number of parameters** | 2 | 2 | 2 |
| **Best fit region** | Low to medium $C_e$ | Wide $C_e$ range | Up to $C \approx C_s$ |
| **Used for** | Capacity design | Quick screening | Surface area (BET method) |
| **Limitation** | Fails at high $C_e$ | No theoretical basis at high $C_e$ | Complex; assumes infinite layers |
| **KTU favorite?** | **Yes (most common)** | Yes (for activated carbon) | Sometimes (for surface area) |

## 3.7 Worked Numerical Example — Batch Adsorption (Langmuir Fit)

A **batch adsorption study** is carried out to remove a coloured dye (Methylene Blue) from textile effluent using activated carbon.

**Data:**
- $m = 0.5$ g (mass of carbon)
- $V = 0.25$ L (volume of dye solution)
- $C_0 = 200$ mg/L (initial concentration)
- $C_e = 50$ mg/L (equilibrium concentration)

**Step 1 — Calculate equilibrium loading $q_e$ using mass balance:**

$$
q_e = \frac{(C_0 - C_e) V}{m} = \frac{(200 - 50) \times 0.25}{0.5}
$$

$$
q_e = \frac{150 \times 0.25}{0.5} = \frac{37.5}{0.5} = 75 \;\text{mg/g}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate percentage removal:**

$$
\% \text{ Removal} = \frac{C_0 - C_e}{C_0} \times 100 = \frac{200 - 50}{200} \times 100 = 75\%
$$

**Step 3 — If $q_{\max} = 250$ mg/g and $K_L = 0.05$ L/mg, predict $q_e$ for $C_e = 80$ mg/L:**

$$
q_e = \frac{q_{\max} K_L C_e}{1 + K_L C_e} = \frac{250 \times 0.05 \times 80}{1 + 0.05 \times 80}
$$

$$
q_e = \frac{1000}{1 + 4} = \frac{1000}{5} = 200 \;\text{mg/g}
$$

The result is consistent with a high-capacity carbon at moderate equilibrium concentration.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram A — Conceptual Architecture of Adsorptive Bioseparation

This flowchart maps the *decision flow* a chemical engineer follows when designing an adsorption-based bioseparation step. It is a *block-level functional architecture flow*, not a physical drawing, and is rendered using Mermaid.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Crude Feedstock broth cell lysate or fermentation broth] --> B[Clarification centrifugation microfiltration]
    B --> C{Is target molecule known with a specific affinity ligand}
    C -- Yes --> D[Affinity Adsorption Protein A IMAC dye ligand]
    C -- No --> E[Screen Adsorbents Ion exchange hydrophobic reversed phase]
    E --> F[Batch Equilibrium Study at lab scale]
    D --> F
    F --> G[Fit Isotherm Langmuir or Freundlich]
    G --> H[Compute q max and K L]
    H --> I[Select Operating Mode Batch Packed Bed or SMB]
    I --> J[Design Column Bed Height Diameter Flow Rate]
    J --> K[Run Pilot Scale Breakthrough Curve]
    K --> L{Breakthrough curve acceptable sharp and symmetric}
    L -- Yes --> M[Scale Up to Production Column]
    L -- No --> N[Revisit Mass Transfer Zone or Adjust Flow]
    M --> O[Elute Regenerate and Recycle Adsorbent]
    O --> P[Purified Target Biomolecule]
```

**How to read this diagram**:
- **Top–down flow**: from crude broth to purified product.
- **Decision diamonds** (`{ }`): *is the target known?* and *is the breakthrough curve acceptable?*
- **Recycle arrow**: regeneration loops back to a *clean adsorbent* state — this is the *economic* heart of adsorption technology.

---

## 4.2 Diagram B — Batch vs. Continuous (Fixed-Bed) Adsorption

The block diagram below contrasts the **batch stirred-tank** and **continuous packed-bed** modes — the two configurations a KTU student must know how to differentiate.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BatchMode[Batch Adsorption in Stirred Tank]
        B1[Feed Solution containing target] --> B2[Add Measured Mass of Adsorbent Particles]
        B2 --> B3[Stir at Controlled RPM and T for Time t]
        B3 --> B4{Sampling at Intervals to track C of t}
        B4 --> B5[Filter to Separate Spent Adsorbent]
        B5 --> B6[Treated Solution low in target]
        B5 --> B7[Spent Adsorbent sent to Desorption]
    end

    subgraph ContinuousMode[Continuous Adsorption in Packed Bed]
        C1[Feed Inlet at C0 continuously pumped] --> C2[Top of Packed Column]
        C2 --> C3[Column with Three Zones Top Saturated MTZ Bottom Fresh]
        C3 --> C4[Effluent Outlet at C of t monitored]
        C4 --> C5[Plot Breakthrough Curve C of t versus C0]
    end

    B7 -.->|regeneration| B2
    C5 --> C6{Is C of t greater than threshold}
    C6 -- Yes --> C7[Stop Feed Switch to Elution Buffer]
    C6 -- No --> C8[Continue Adsorption Step]
    C7 --> C9[Regenerate Column with Strip Buffer]
    C9 --> C10[Re-equilibrate and Reuse]
```

**Key takeaway**:
- **Batch** is *time-wise discrete*; we mix, wait, and filter.
- **Continuous** is *time-wise continuous*; the breakthrough curve dictates *when* to stop, regenerate, and re-equilibrate.

---

## 4.3 Diagram C — Anatomy of a Fixed-Bed Column During Operation

This Mermaid block emulates the longitudinal cross-section of a packed-bed adsorber as it operates. The *mass transfer zone* (MTZ) is the moving reaction front.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph ColumnBody[Fixed Bed Column Cross Section]
        direction TB
        Z1[Zone 1 Saturated Section at top C equals C0]
        Z2[Zone 2 Mass Transfer Zone MTZ where C drops from C0 to 0]
        Z3[Zone 3 Fresh Section at bottom C approximately 0]
    end

    FeedIn[Feed In at C0] --> Z1
    Z1 --> Z2
    Z2 --> Z3
    Z3 --> EffluentOut[Effluent Out at C of t]

    Z1 -.->|moves downward with time| Z2
    Z2 -.->|moves downward with time| Z3

    Z3 -.->|initially clean| CcurveStart
    Z2 -.->|active sorption region| Midpoint
    Z1 -.->|fully spent| Saturation
```

**Operational insight**:
- As the feed continues, the **saturated zone grows**, the **MTZ slides downward**, and the **fresh zone shrinks**.
- When the MTZ touches the column exit, **breakthrough** occurs.
- The column is regenerated *just before* breakthrough, ensuring the *exit fluid* is always within spec.

---

## 4.4 Diagram D — Breakthrough Curve Anatomy (Plot Replica)

The figure below reproduces the **S-shaped breakthrough curve** with the engineering markers a KTU examiner will look for.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TimeAxis[Time on x axis]
        T0[t equals 0 column starts clean]
        Tb[t equals t_b breakthrough point C equals 0 point 1 C0]
        Tstar[t equals t star stoichiometric time C equals 0 point 5 C0]
        Ts[t equals t_s saturation point C equals C0]
        Tend[t equals t end regeneration begins]
    end

    subgraph ConcAxis[Normalized Concentration C by C0 on y axis]
        C0Line[C by C0 equals 1]
        CBreak[C by C0 equals 0 point 1 threshold]
        CMid[C by C0 equals 0 point 5]
    end

    T0 --> S1[Origin point C by C0 is 0]
    Tb --> S2[Breakthrough Threshold marked]
    Tstar --> S3[Midpoint of S curve]
    Ts --> S4[Saturation Top of S curve]
    Tend --> S5[Column Switched to Regeneration]
```

**Engineering use**:
- **$t_b$** is the *operational limit* — column must be regenerated by this time.
- **$t^*$** is the *design midpoint* — used in Thomas model calculations.
- **$t_s$** is the *ultimate capacity* — rarely used in production, but it sets the upper bound on column life.
- The *area above the curve* (from $t = 0$ to $t^*$) represents the **utilized capacity**; the *area below* is the **wasted capacity**.

---

## 4.5 Diagram E — Simulated Moving Bed (SMB) Topology

SMB is the *advanced continuous mode* used in chiral drug and sugar separations; it mimics true counter-current movement by *periodically switching* inlet/outlet ports.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SMBRing[SMB with 4 to 12 Columns in Ring]
        S1[Column 1 Desorbent] --> S2[Column 2 Desorb Extract]
        S2 --> S3[Column 3 Stripping]
        S3 --> S4[Column 4 Adsorb Raffinate]
        S4 --> S1
    end

    InDesorbent[Desorbent In] --> S1
    S2 --> OutExtract[Extract Out high affinity]
    S3 --> InFeed[Feed In]
    S4 --> OutRaffinate[Raffinate Out low affinity]

    Switch[Rotary Valve Switches every t_shift seconds] -.->|port shift| SMBRing
```

**Why SMB matters**:
- Two product streams (**extract** and **raffinate**) are produced *simultaneously* and *continuously*.
- Desorbent consumption is *much lower* than batch chromatography.
- Used industrially for **chiral separations** (e.g., enantiomeric drug intermediates) and **fructose/glucose** separation.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Differentiate Physisorption and Chemisorption. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

| Property | Physisorption | Chemisorption |
|---|---|---|
| **Force involved** | Van der Waals | Covalent / ionic bonds |
| **Enthalpy** | 5 – 40 kJ/mol | 40 – 400 kJ/mol |
| **Specificity** | Non-specific | Highly specific |
| **Layers** | Multi-layer possible | Strictly mono-layer |
| **Reversibility** | Easily reversible | Often irreversible |
| **Activation energy** | Very low | Significant |
| **Effect of temperature** | Decreases with T | May increase then decrease |
| **Example** | N₂ on charcoal at low T | O₂ on tungsten; enzymes on substrate |

> [!NOTE]
> **Model answer hint**: Use a *comparison table* with at least 5 differentiating points to secure full marks.

---

### Question 2: State and Explain the Assumptions of the Langmuir Adsorption Isotherm. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

The Langmuir isotherm is based on the following postulates:

1. **Fixed number of sites**: The solid surface has a *finite*, *uniform* number of adsorption sites per unit area, $N$.
2. **Monolayer coverage**: Each site can hold *one and only one* adsorbate molecule; no stacking.
3. **Equivalent sites**: All sites have *identical* adsorption energy (homogeneous surface).
4. **No lateral interaction**: Adsorbed molecules do *not* interact with each other laterally.
5. **Dynamic equilibrium**: Adsorption and desorption occur simultaneously; at equilibrium the two rates are equal.

The resulting isotherm is $q_e = \dfrac{q_{\max} K_L C_e}{1 + K_L C_e}$, with $K_L = k_a/k_d$ representing the affinity between adsorbate and surface.

---

### Question 3: Define "Breakthrough Curve" and Mark the Important Points on It. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**

**Model Answer:**

A **breakthrough curve** is a plot of the *effluent concentration* $C_{\text{out}}$ (or $C_t/C_0$) versus *time* $t$ for a fixed-bed adsorber operating at constant inlet concentration $C_0$.

Key markers on the S-shaped curve:
- **Breakthrough time ($t_b$)**: Time when $C_{\text{out}}$ reaches a *preset threshold* (usually 5–10% of $C_0$). This is the *operational endpoint* — beyond this, the column must be regenerated.
- **Stoichiometric time ($t^*$)**: Time when $C_{\text{out}} = 0.5 \, C_0$. Used in the **Thomas model** for design.
- **Saturation time ($t_s$)**: Time when $C_{\text{out}} \approx C_0$ — the bed is *fully spent*.

The *area above* the curve represents **adsorbed mass**; the *area below* represents **mass lost** to the effluent.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning**:
> 1. **Do not skip units** in the formula sheet — losing units = losing 1–2 marks per question.
> 2. In isotherm derivations, students frequently omit the *equilibrium condition* step (rate ads = rate des). This is worth 2 marks.
> 3. Always state the **assumptions** of an isotherm *before* deriving it; a derivation without assumptions is *incomplete*.
> 4. In numerical problems, **substitute values with units in the same line** as the formula — examiners value this.
> 5. In continuous-column questions, students often forget to mention the **mass transfer zone (MTZ)** — this is a 2-mark item.

---

### Question A: Derivation + Continuous Column Design (14 Marks)

#### (a) Derive the Langmuir Adsorption Isotherm from Kinetic Considerations. State All Assumptions. **[7 Marks]** **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**Model Solution:**

**Assumptions** (write these first; 2 marks):
1. The surface has a fixed, finite number $N$ of identical active sites.
2. Each site holds one molecule only — monolayer coverage.
3. The energy of adsorption is the same for all sites.
4. No interaction between adsorbed molecules.
5. Dynamic equilibrium is established.

**Derivation** (5 marks, broken down):

Let $\theta$ = fraction of surface covered. Then:
- Number of *vacant* sites = $N(1 - \theta)$.
- Number of *occupied* sites = $N \theta$.

Rate of adsorption is proportional to $C$ and to vacant sites:

$$
r_{\text{ads}} = k_a \, C \, N (1 - \theta)
$$

Rate of desorption is proportional to occupied sites:

$$
r_{\text{des}} = k_d \, N \theta
$$

At equilibrium, $r_{\text{ads}} = r_{\text{des}}$:

$$
k_a \, C \, N(1 - \theta) = k_d \, N \theta
$$

Cancel $N$ and define $K_L = k_a / k_d$:

$$
\theta = \frac{K_L C}{1 + K_L C}
$$

Since $q_e = q_{\max} \theta$:

$$
\boxed{\, q_e = \frac{q_{\max} K_L C_e}{1 + K_L C_e} \,}
$$

---

#### (b) Numerical: Continuous Fixed-Bed Column with Thomas Model. **[7 Marks]** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Problem:** A packed-bed adsorber is used to remove a protein from a fermentation broth. The operating data are:

- Inlet concentration: $C_0 = 100$ mg/L
- Volumetric flow rate: $Q = 5$ L/min
- Mass of adsorbent: $M = 200$ g
- Equilibrium solid-phase concentration: $q_0 = 8$ mg/g
- Thomas rate constant: $k_{Th} = 0.0005$ L/mg·min

**Find:**
1. The stoichiometric breakthrough time $t^*$.
2. The effluent concentration ratio $C_t/C_0$ at $t = 60$ min.
3. Comment on whether the bed is *under-utilized* or *fully utilized* at this moment.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute stoichiometric time $t^*$:**

$$
t^* = \frac{q_0 \, M}{C_0 \, Q} = \frac{8 \times 200}{100 \times 5} = \frac{1600}{500} = 3.2 \;\text{min}
$$

> **Note**: This very small $t^*$ suggests a *very low capacity*; in practice one would increase $M$ or pre-concentrate the broth.

**Step 2 — Compute $C_t/C_0$ at $t = 60$ min:**

$$
\frac{C_t}{C_0} = \frac{1}{1 + \exp\!\left(\dfrac{k_{Th} \, q_0 \, M}{Q} - k_{Th} \, C_0 \, t\right)}
$$

**Calculate the two terms in the exponent:**

$$
\frac{k_{Th} \, q_0 \, M}{Q} = \frac{0.0005 \times 8 \times 200}{5} = \frac{0.8}{5} = 0.16
$$

$$
k_{Th} \, C_0 \, t = 0.0005 \times 100 \times 60 = 3.0
$$

**Exponent value:**

$$
0.16 - 3.0 = -2.84
$$

**Compute the exponential:**

$$
\exp(-2.84) = e^{-2.84} \approx 0.0584
$$

**Final ratio:**

$$
\frac{C_t}{C_0} = \frac{1}{1 + 0.0584} = \frac{1}{1.0584} \approx 0.945
$$

**Step 3 — Comment on utilization:**

Since $C_t/C_0 \approx 0.945$ at $t = 60$ min, the *effluent concentration* is **94.5% of the inlet** — the column is essentially *fully spent* (saturation regime). The bed has been *over-utilized*; in an industrial setting, regeneration should have been initiated long before $t = 60$ min. For practical operation, the breakthrough threshold is typically $C_t/C_0 = 0.10$, which corresponds to a much earlier time.

---

### Question B: Alternative Choice — Freundlich + Batch Numerical (14 Marks)

#### (a) Explain the Freundlich Adsorption Isotherm. Write Its Linearized Form. Compare with Langmuir. **[7 Marks]** **[KTU University Exam — May 2023]**

**Model Solution:**

**Empirical origin** (1 mark):
The Freundlich isotherm is an *empirical* relation proposed by Herbert Freundlich in 1909 to describe adsorption on *heterogeneous* surfaces where the *adsorption energy decreases* as surface coverage increases (exponential energy distribution).

**Equation** (1 mark):

$$
q_e = K_F \, C_e^{1/n}
$$

where $K_F$ (mg/g)(L/mg)$^{1/n}$ is the Freundlich constant indicating *capacity*, and $n$ is a dimensionless exponent indicating *intensity* or *favorability*.

**Linear form** (1 mark):

$$
\log q_e = \log K_F + \frac{1}{n} \log C_e
$$

A plot of $\log q_e$ vs. $\log C_e$ yields slope $1/n$ and intercept $\log K_F$.

**Comparison with Langmuir** (3 marks):

| Property | Langmuir | Freundlich |
|---|---|---|
| Nature | Theoretical | Empirical |
| Surface | Homogeneous | Heterogeneous |
| Layers | Monolayer | Multilayer implicit |
| Maximum capacity | Predictable ($q_{\max}$) | Not predicted; $q_e \to \infty$ as $C_e \to \infty$ |
| Pressure dependence | Saturates at high $C$ | Power-law (no saturation) |
| Best use | Ion exchange, monolayer adsorption | Activated carbon, dye removal, natural surfaces |

**Significance of $n$** (1 mark):
- $1/n = 0$ → irreversible.
- $0 < 1/n < 1$ → favorable (typical range: 0.1 to 0.8).
- $1/n = 1$ → linear.
- $1/n > 1$ → unfavorable (cooperative binding).

---

#### (b) Numerical: Batch Adsorption with Mass Balance. **[7 Marks]** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**

**Problem:** A batch adsorption experiment is conducted to remove a coloured dye from 1 L of wastewater using 2 g of activated carbon. The initial dye concentration is 250 mg/L. After 4 hours of stirring at 30 °C, the equilibrium concentration is 50 mg/L. The adsorption isotherm at 30 °C is described by the Freundlich equation with $K_F = 30$ (mg/g)(L/mg)$^{1/n}$ and $n = 2.5$.

**Find:**
1. The experimental value of $q_e$ (mg/g).
2. The percentage removal of the dye.
3. The Freundlich-predicted $q_e$ at the experimental $C_e$ and the deviation from the experimental value.
4. The required adsorbent mass to achieve a *target final concentration* of 10 mg/L, assuming the same isotherm applies.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Experimental $q_e$:**

$$
q_{e,\text{exp}} = \frac{(C_0 - C_e) V}{m} = \frac{(250 - 50) \times 1}{2} = \frac{200}{2} = 100 \;\text{mg/g}
$$

**Step 2 — Percentage removal:**

$$
\% \text{ Removal} = \frac{C_0 - C_e}{C_0} \times 100 = \frac{200}{250} \times 100 = 80\%
$$

**Step 3 — Freundlich-predicted $q_e$ at $C_e = 50$ mg/L:**

$$
q_{e,\text{pred}} = K_F \, C_e^{1/n} = 30 \times (50)^{1/2.5}
$$

**Compute the exponent:**

$$
\frac{1}{n} = \frac{1}{2.5} = 0.4
$$

$$
50^{0.4} = e^{0.4 \ln 50} = e^{0.4 \times 3.912} = e^{1.565} \approx 4.78
$$

**Substitute:**

$$
q_{e,\text{pred}} = 30 \times 4.78 \approx 143.4 \;\text{mg/g}
$$

**Deviation:**

$$
\% \text{ Deviation} = \frac{143.4 - 100}{100} \times 100 = 43.4\%
$$

The Freundlich model overpredicts the loading — a sign that the *high-concentration* region deviates from the power-law behavior; the Langmuir model may fit better here.

**Step 4 — Required mass for $C_{\text{target}} = 10$ mg/L:**

The amount of dye to be removed: $(C_0 - C_t) V = (250 - 10) \times 1 = 240$ mg.

The Freundlich-predicted loading at $C_t = 10$ mg/L:

$$
q_{e,\text{target}} = 30 \times (10)^{0.4} = 30 \times 2.512 \approx 75.4 \;\text{mg/g}
$$

(Note: $10^{0.4} = e^{0.4 \times 2.3026} = e^{0.921} \approx 2.512$.)

The required adsorbent mass:

$$
m_{\text{req}} = \frac{(C_0 - C_t) V}{q_{e,\text{target}}} = \frac{240}{75.4} \approx 3.18 \;\text{g}
$$

To reduce the dye from 250 mg/L to 10 mg/L in 1 L, we need approximately **3.2 g of activated carbon**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must-Know for KTU 2024 Exam**

### Definitions
- **Adsorption** is a *surface* phenomenon; **absorption** is *bulk*. Don't confuse the two in 1-mark questions.
- **Adsorbate** = species being adsorbed; **adsorbent** = solid surface; **desorption** = release.

### Types
- **Physisorption**: weak forces, low ΔH, reversible, multilayer possible.
- **Chemisorption**: chemical bond, high ΔH, often irreversible, monolayer only.

### Isotherms
- **Langmuir**: $q_e = \dfrac{q_{\max} K_L C_e}{1 + K_L C_e}$ → linearize as $\dfrac{C_e}{q_e} = \dfrac{1}{K_L q_{\max}} + \dfrac{C_e}{q_{\max}}$.
- **Freundlich**: $q_e = K_F C_e^{1/n}$ → linearize in log-log.
- **BET**: multilayer; used for surface area characterization.
- **Separation factor** $R_L = \dfrac{1}{1 + K_L C_0}$ → *favorable* if $0 < R_L < 1$.

### Operating Modes
- **Batch (stirred tank)**: easy, small scale; calculate $q_e$ from mass balance.
- **Packed/Fixed bed**: most common industrial; use Thomas or BDST models.
- **Moving/Fluidized bed**: handles solids, continuous.
- **SMB**: continuous, two-product, used in chiral separations.

### Breakthrough Curve
- Plot $C_{\text{out}}/C_0$ vs. $t$; identify $t_b$, $t^*$, $t_s$.
- The **MTZ** is the *active sorption region*; minimize its length to maximize bed utilization.
- Thomas model: $\dfrac{C_t}{C_0} = \dfrac{1}{1 + \exp\!\left(\dfrac{k_{Th} q_0 M}{Q} - k_{Th} C_0 t\right)}$.

### Engineering Quantities
- $q_e = \dfrac{(C_0 - C_e) V}{m}$ (batch mass balance).
- $t^* = \dfrac{q_0 M}{C_0 Q}$ (stoichiometric time, fixed bed).
- $R_L = \dfrac{1}{1 + K_L C_0}$ (favorability).
- $S_{\text{BET}}$ from BET plot (m²/g) — used to compare adsorbents.

### Typical Numerical Values (for sanity-check)
- $q_{\max}$ for activated carbon: **200–1500 mg/g**.
- $K_L$ for organic dyes on carbon: **0.01–0.1 L/mg**.
- Surface area of activated carbon: **500–1500 m²/g**.
- Industrial breakthrough threshold: $C/C_0 = 0.05$–$0.10$.

### Common Pitfalls
- Forgetting to convert mL to L or mg to g — *always* show units in every calculation step.
- Mixing up $q_{\max}$ (capacity) with $K_L$ (affinity) when reading slopes/intercepts.
- Using **Freundlich** at very low concentrations where $C_e^{1/n}$ behavior is unreliable.
- Reporting $t^*$ without specifying whether it is *breakthrough* ($t_b$) or *stoichiometric* ($t^*$).

### Industrial Applications
- **Protein A chromatography** — mAb capture.
- **IMAC** — His-tagged proteins.
- **Activated carbon** — decolorization, antibiotic removal.
- **Ion exchange** — water softening, protein polishing.
- **SMB** — chiral drugs, sugar isomer separation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Chromatographic Separations: Classification of Chromatographic Techniques
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT525 - BIOSEPARATION TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Chromatographic Separations: Classification of Chromatographic Techniques

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition
**Chromatography** is a versatile physical method of separation in which the components of a mixture are separated based on their differential distribution between two immiscible phases: a **stationary phase** (which remains fixed within the system) and a **mobile phase** (which percolates through or over the stationary phase carrying the analyte). The term is derived from the Greek words *chroma* (color) and *graphein* (to write), coined by the Russian-Italian botanist **Mikhail Tswett** in 1906, who separated plant pigments (chlorophylls, xanthophylls, and carotenes) using a calcium carbonate column.

In **bioseparation technology**, chromatographic techniques form the workhorse of downstream processing, enabling purification of proteins, enzymes, antibodies, nucleic acids, viruses, and small-molecule pharmaceuticals to the required purity, recovery, and activity levels demanded by regulatory and commercial specifications.

### 1.2 The Two Phases — Conceptual Roles
| Phase | Physical State Options | Functional Role |
|---|---|---|
| **Stationary Phase** | Solid (packed bed) or Liquid (coated/immobilized) | Retards analytes via selective intermolecular interactions |
| **Mobile Phase** | Gas, Liquid, or Supercritical Fluid | Transports analytes through the system |

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Marathon Race on a Sand Track"
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Sand–Track Analogy for Chromatography**
>
> Imagine a group of runners competing on a track filled with sand. Each runner must pass through the sand from start to finish. Runners who are **heavier** or who **interact more strongly with the sand** will sink and be slowed down. Runners who are **lighter** or **less interactive** will skim across quickly.
>
> In chromatography:
> - The **track** = stationary phase
> - The **wind/runner's effort** = mobile phase
> - The **runners** = analyte molecules
> - The **finish line position** = retention time
>
> Molecules that interact strongly with the stationary phase emerge later; weak interactors emerge early. This is the essence of chromatographic resolution.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight**
> Students are expected to classify chromatographic techniques based on **multiple criteria** (mobile phase state, bed geometry, separation mechanism, and operational mode). Memorize a single unified classification matrix — not just one criterion.

### 1.4 Why Chromatography is Indispensable in Bioseparations
- **High resolution** — separates molecules with subtle structural differences (e.g., isomers, glycosylated vs. non-glycosylated proteins).
- **Scalability** — analytical (micrograms) to industrial (kilograms) scale.
- **Mild operating conditions** — preserves biological activity of sensitive biomolecules.
- **Versatility** — multiple modes allow orthogonal purification strategies.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Multi-Criteria Classification of Chromatographic Techniques

#### A. Classification Based on the Physical State of the Mobile Phase

| Sub-Technique | Mobile Phase | Stationary Phase | Typical Applications |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Gas Chromatography (GC)** | Inert carrier gas (He, N₂, H₂) | Non-volatile liquid or solid | Volatile organics, fatty acids, gases |
| **Liquid Chromatography (LC)** | Aqueous or organic solvent | Solid or bonded silica/polymer | Proteins, peptides, pharmaceuticals |
| **Supercritical Fluid Chromatography (SFC)** | CO₂ above critical point (T > 31 °C, P > 73 bar) | Bonded silica | Chiral separations, lipids, natural products |

> [!NOTE]
> **SFC bridges GC and LC** — it combines the high diffusivity of gases with the solvating power of liquids, enabling fast, efficient separations with low solvent consumption.

#### B. Classification Based on the Geometry of the Chromatographic Bed

| Bed Shape | Sub-Forms | Characteristic Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Planar Chromatography** | Paper Chromatography (PC), Thin-Layer Chromatography (TLC) | Quick qualitative analysis, identity checks |
| **Column Chromatography** | Packed Column, Open Tubular (Capillary) Column | Quantitative, preparative, and process-scale purification |

#### C. Classification Based on the Underlying Separation Mechanism

This is the **most important classification** for bioseparation engineers because it dictates which biomolecule properties are exploited for selectivity.

##### 1. **Adsorption Chromatography**
- **Principle:** Differential adsorption onto a solid surface (e.g., silica, alumina).
- **Interaction:** van der Waals, dipole, hydrogen bonding.
- **Biosequence Example:** Hydrophobic interaction chromatography (**HIC**) — a specialized adsorption variant using salt gradients.

##### 2. **Partition Chromatography**
- **Principle:** Differential solubility of analyte between two liquid phases.
- **Sub-types:** Normal-phase (polar stationary, non-polar mobile) and **Reversed-Phase (RP)** (non-polar stationary, polar mobile).
- **Biosequence Example:** Reversed-Phase HPLC (RP-HPLC) for peptides, oligonucleotides, and small-molecule drugs.

##### 3. **Ion-Exchange Chromatography (IEX)**
- **Principle:** Differential Coulombic attraction between charged analytes and oppositely charged resin functional groups.
- **Sub-types:** Cation exchange (CM, SP) and Anion exchange (DEAE, Q).
- **Biosequence Example:** Capture step in monoclonal antibody (mAb) purification using Protein A → IEX polishing.

##### 4. **Size-Exclusion Chromatography (SEC) / Gel Filtration / Gel Permeation**
- **Principle:** Separation by molecular size via differential penetration into porous beads.
- **Critical Property:** No chemical interaction — purely entropic.
- **Biosequence Example:** Buffer exchange, aggregate removal (monomer–dimer separation), polishing of mAbs and enzymes.

##### 5. **Affinity Chromatography**
- **Principle:** Highly specific, lock-and-key biological interaction (e.g., enzyme–substrate, antibody–antigen, lectin–sugar).
- **Biosequence Example:** **Protein A / Protein G** affinity for IgG purification — gold standard for mAb capture.

##### 6. **Chiral Chromatography**
- **Principle:** Differential interaction with a chiral stationary phase (CSP) to separate enantiomers.
- **Biosequence Example:** Separation of (R)- and (S)-ibuprofen; purification of chiral APIs.

#### D. Classification Based on Operational / Elution Mode

| Mode | Description | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Isocratic Elution** | Constant mobile-phase composition | Simple pumps, suited for simple mixtures |
| **Gradient Elution** | Time-programmed change in mobile-phase strength | Better resolution for complex bioseparations |
| **Step Elution** | Discrete changes in mobile-phase composition | Common in process-scale IEX and HIC |
| **Isothermal / Temperature-Programmed** | Temperature varied to alter retention | Used in GC |

### 2.2 The Core Chromatographic Performance Equations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **master formula sheet** for chromatography questions that appear in KTU 2024 End-Semester Examinations. All derivations in Section 3 follow from these definitions.

| Parameter | Symbol | Formula | Units | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Retention Time | $t_R$ | Time from injection to peak maximum | s or min | How long the analyte is held |
| Dead Time | $t_M$ | Time for unretained species to elute | s or min | Time spent in mobile phase only |
| Adjusted Retention Time | $t_R'$ | $t_R - t_M$ | s or min | Time spent interacting with stationary phase |
| Retention (Capacity) Factor | $k$ | $\dfrac{t_R - t_M}{t_M} = \dfrac{t_R'}{t_M}$ | dimensionless | Strength of interaction |
| Selectivity Factor | $\alpha$ | $\dfrac{k_2}{k_1}$ (for adjacent peaks 1, 2) | dimensionless > 1 | Separation selectivity |
| Number of Theoretical Plates | $N$ | $16 \left(\dfrac{t_R}{w}\right)^2 = 5.54 \left(\dfrac{t_R}{w_{1/2}}\right)^2$ | dimensionless | Column efficiency |
| Plate Height | $H$ | $\dfrac{L}{N}$ | mm or μm | Efficiency per unit length |
| Resolution | $R_s$ | $\dfrac{2(t_{R2} - t_{R1})}{w_1 + w_2}$ | dimensionless | Quality of separation |
| **Purnell Resolution Equation** | $R_s$ | $\dfrac{\sqrt{N}}{4} \cdot \dfrac{\alpha - 1}{\alpha} \cdot \dfrac{k_2}{1 + k_2}$ | dimensionless | Master design equation |
| Distribution Coefficient | $K_d$ | $\dfrac{C_s}{C_m}$ | dimensionless | Equilibrium partition ratio |
| Linear Velocity | $u$ | $\dfrac{L}{t_M}$ | cm/s | Mobile phase velocity |
| Sample Volume Capacity | $V_{max}$ | Function of column geometry and $K_d$ | mL or L | Maximum loadable sample |

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL MARKDOWN RULE:** All vertical bars (`|`) have been replaced with `\vert` or `\mid` to prevent table-format corruption. Students should use the same formatting in their own KTU answer sheets when typing equations.

### 2.3 Engineering Utility — The "Purnell Triangle" of Resolution
The **Purnell Resolution Equation** is the single most important equation in chromatography because it allows engineers to **design** a separation by tuning three independent variables:

$$
R_s = \underbrace{\dfrac{\sqrt{N}}{4}}_{\text{Efficiency term}} \cdot \underbrace{\dfrac{\alpha - 1}{\alpha}}_{\text{Selectivity term}} \cdot \underbrace{\dfrac{k_2}{1 + k_2}}_{\text{Retention term}}
$$

- **Efficiency term** $\to$ Improve by increasing $N$ (longer column, smaller particles, lower H).
- **Selectivity term** $\to$ Improve by changing stationary phase, temperature, or mobile phase chemistry. This is the **most powerful knob** — a small change in $\alpha$ gives a large change in $R_s$.
- **Retention term** $\to$ Improve by tuning $k$ to an optimum range of **2 < k < 10**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world application:** In pharmaceutical downstream processing, a $R_s \geq 1.5$ is the industry-accepted benchmark for **baseline separation** (peaks return to baseline between maxima).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — From First Principles: The Retention Factor $k$

#### Step 1: Define the Distribution Coefficient
At equilibrium, an analyte $A$ distributes between the stationary phase (concentration $C_s$) and the mobile phase (concentration $C_m$) according to:

$$
A_m \rightleftharpoons A_s
$$

The equilibrium distribution coefficient is:

$$
K_d = \frac{C_s}{C_m}
$$

**Logic:** A high $K_d$ means the analyte prefers the stationary phase and will be strongly retained.

#### Step 2: Relate the Mass of Analyte in Each Phase
For a given mass $m$ of analyte distributed at equilibrium:

$$
m_s = C_s \cdot V_s, \quad m_m = C_m \cdot V_m
$$

The total mass is $m_{total} = m_s + m_m = C_s V_s + C_m V_m$.

#### Step 3: Express the Fraction in Each Phase
The fraction in the stationary phase:

$$
f_s = \frac{m_s}{m_{total}} = \frac{C_s V_s}{C_s V_s + C_m V_m}
$$

Dividing numerator and denominator by $C_m V_m$:

$$
f_s = \frac{(C_s/C_m)(V_s/V_m)}{1 + (C_s/C_m)(V_s/V_m)} = \frac{K_d \cdot (V_s/V_m)}{1 + K_d \cdot (V_s/V_m)}
$$

#### Step 4: Connect to Retention Time
The retention time $t_R$ is the time the analyte spends in the column. It is the **weighted average** of time in stationary phase (immobile) and time in mobile phase (mobile at velocity $u$):

$$
t_R = f_m \cdot t_M + f_s \cdot t_M \cdot (\text{ratio of velocities for stationary residence})
$$

In a packed column, the analyte spends $(1 + K_d \cdot V_s/V_m)$ times the dead time. The final result is the **fundamental retention equation**:

$$
t_R = t_M \cdot (1 + K_d \cdot \frac{V_s}{V_m})
$$

#### Step 5: Define the Retention Factor $k$
Defining $k = K_d \cdot \dfrac{V_s}{V_m}$:

$$
t_R = t_M \cdot (1 + k)
$$

$$
\boxed{\,k = \frac{t_R - t_M}{t_M} = \frac{t_R'}{t_M}\,}
$$

**Engineering Insight:** $k$ is **dimensionless** and represents the ratio of time spent in the stationary phase to the time spent in the mobile phase. It is the most useful single descriptor of retention strength.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Plate Theory and the Van Deemter Equation

The chromatographic column is modeled as a series of $N$ discrete "theoretical plates," within each of which equilibrium is reached instantaneously.

#### Step 1: Define Plate Height
A column of length $L$ divided into $N$ equilibrium stages has plate height:

$$
H = \frac{L}{N}
$$

**Logic:** Smaller $H$ → more plates per unit length → higher efficiency → sharper peaks.

#### Step 2: Identify the Three Contributions to Band Broadening
Band broadening arises from three physical phenomena, each contributing additively to $H$:

$$
H = H_u + H_l + H_m
$$

where:
- $H_u$ = **Eddy diffusion** (multiple flow paths in a packed bed)
- $H_l$ = **Longitudinal diffusion** (molecular diffusion along the flow axis)
- $H_m$ = **Mass transfer resistance** (finite rate of equilibration between phases)

#### Step 3: Express Each Term Mathematically
**Eddy diffusion** depends on packing geometry (constant $\lambda$) and particle diameter $d_p$:

$$
H_u = 2 \lambda d_p
$$

**Longitudinal diffusion** depends on the diffusion coefficient $D_m$ in the mobile phase and the linear velocity $u$:

$$
H_l = \frac{2 \gamma D_m}{u}
$$

**Mass transfer resistance** in both stationary and mobile phases:

$$
H_m = \frac{f(k) \cdot d_p^2}{D_m} \cdot u
$$

where $f(k) = \dfrac{k}{(1+k)^2}$ is a function of the retention factor.

#### Step 4: Combine into the Van Deemter Equation
$$
\boxed{\,H = A + \frac{B}{u} + C \cdot u\,}
$$

where:
- $A = 2 \lambda d_p$ (eddy diffusion)
- $B = 2 \gamma D_m$ (longitudinal diffusion)
- $C = \dfrac{f(k) d_p^2}{D_m}$ (mass transfer)

#### Step 5: Find the Optimum Velocity
Differentiate $H$ with respect to $u$ and set to zero:

$$
\frac{dH}{du} = -\frac{B}{u^2} + C = 0
$$

$$
\boxed{\,u_{opt} = \sqrt{\frac{B}{C}}\,}, \quad H_{min} = A + 2\sqrt{BC}
$$

**Engineering Insight:** At the optimum velocity, plate height is minimized and column efficiency is maximized. Operating at $u_{opt}$ gives the **sharpest peaks** and the **best resolution per unit length**.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — The Purnell Resolution Equation (from Peak Statistics)

#### Step 1: Define Resolution from Peak Widths
Two adjacent Gaussian peaks with maxima at $t_{R1}$ and $t_{R2}$ and baseline widths $w_1$ and $w_2$ have resolution:

$$
R_s = \frac{t_{R2} - t_{R1}}{0.5 (w_1 + w_2)}
$$

#### Step 2: Express Peak Width in Terms of Plates
For a Gaussian peak, the baseline width is $w = 4\sigma$, and the relationship between standard deviation $\sigma$ and plate number is:

$$
N = \left( \frac{t_R}{\sigma} \right)^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \sigma = \frac{t_R}{\sqrt{N}}, \quad w = \frac{4 t_R}{\sqrt{N}}
$$

Substituting:

$$
R_s = \frac{t_{R2} - t_{R1}}{2 \left( \dfrac{t_{R1}}{\sqrt{N_1}} + \dfrac{t_{R2}}{\sqrt{N_2}} \right)}
$$

#### Step 3: Assume $N_1 \approx N_2 = N$ (similar column interactions)
For closely-eluting peaks:

$$
R_s = \frac{(t_{R2} - t_{R1}) \sqrt{N}}{2 (t_{R1} + t_{R2})}
$$

#### Step 4: Convert to Selectivity and Retention Factors
Using $t_R = t_M(1+k)$ and the definitions $\alpha = k_2/k_1$:

$$
t_{R2} - t_{R1} = t_M(k_2 - k_1) = t_M k_1(\alpha - 1)
$$

$$
t_{R1} + t_{R2} = t_M(2 + k_1 + k_2) \approx 2 t_M(1 + k_2) \quad \text{(for } k_2 \gg 1\text{)}
$$

Substituting and simplifying:

$$
\boxed{\,R_s = \frac{\sqrt{N}}{4} \cdot \frac{\alpha - 1}{\alpha} \cdot \frac{k_2}{1 + k_2}\,}
$$

**Engineering Insight:** To **double** the resolution, you can either **quadruple** the column length (4× $N$) or change the chemistry to **increase $\alpha$ from 1.05 to 1.10** (4× in selectivity). The latter is far cheaper.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing a Chromatographic Mode in Bioprocessing

| Target Property to Exploit | Recommended Mode | Typical Step Position | Strengths | Limitations |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Charge** | Ion-Exchange (IEX) | Capture / Intermediate | High capacity, scalable | Needs salt removal pre/post |
| **Hydrophobicity** | HIC / RPC | Intermediate / Polishing | Good for variants | Risk of denaturation (RPC) |
| **Size** | SEC (Gel Filtration) | Polishing | Gentle, no binding | Low capacity, dilution |
| **Specific Affinity** | Affinity (Protein A/G) | Capture | Exceptional selectivity | Expensive ligands |
| **Biological Activity** | Bio-affinity | Polishing | Single-step purity | Limited to known ligands |
| **Chirality** | Chiral Chromatography | Polishing | Enantiomer resolution | Expires chiral columns |
| **Polarity** | Normal / Reversed-Phase | Analytical / Polishing | High efficiency | RPC denatures proteins |

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Practice (mAb platform):** Harvest → Protein A capture → Low-pH viral inactivation → CEX (intermediate) → AEX (polishing) → UF/DF. Each orthogonal mode exploits a **different molecular property**, giving multiplicative selectivity gains.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Master Classification Tree of Chromatographic Techniques

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["CHROMATOGRAPHIC TECHNIQUES"] --> B["BY MOBILE PHASE STATE"]
    A --> C["BY BED GEOMETRY"]
    A --> D["BY SEPARATION MECHANISM"]
    A --> E["BY ELUTION MODE"]

    B --> B1["GAS CHROMATOGRAPHY GC"]
    B --> B2["LIQUID CHROMATOGRAPHY LC"]
    B --> B3["SUPERCRITICAL FLUID CHROMATOGRAPHY SFC"]

    C --> C1["PLANAR PC TLC"]
    C --> C2["COLUMN PACKED OPEN TUBULAR"]

    D --> D1["ADSORPTION"]
    D --> D2["PARTITION NP RP"]
    D --> D3["ION EXCHANGE CEX AEX"]
    D --> D4["SIZE EXCLUSION SEC GPC GFC"]
    D --> D5["AFFINITY PROTEIN A LECTIN"]
    D --> D6["CHIRAL"]

    E --> E1["ISOCRATIC CONSTANT"]
    E --> E2["GRADIENT PROGRAMMED"]
    E --> E3["STEP DISCRETE"]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Functional Architecture of a Liquid Chromatography System

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph MOBILE_PHASE_SUPPLY ["MOBILE PHASE RESERVOIR AND DEGASSER"]
        R1["Solvent Reservoir A"]
        R2["Solvent Reservoir B"]
        DG["Online Degasser"]
    end

    subgraph DELIVERY ["PUMPING AND INJECTION UNIT"]
        PU["High Pressure Pump"]
        MX["Mixing Chamber"]
        INJ["Sample Injector"]
    end

    subgraph SEPARATION ["CHROMATOGRAPHIC COLUMN"]
        COL["Packed Column Stationary Phase"]
    end

    subgraph DETECTION ["DETECTION AND DATA ACQUISITION"]
        DET["Detector UV Vis RI MS"]
        DAQ["Data Recorder and Integrator"]
    end

    R1 --> DG
    R2 --> DG
    DG --> PU
    PU --> MX
    MX --> INJ
    INJ --> COL
    COL --> DET
    DET --> DAQ
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Resolution Optimization Strategy (Purnell Triangle)

```mermaid
graph TD
    RS["REQUIRED RESOLUTION RS GREATER THAN 1.5"] --> CHK1["IS K IN RANGE 2 TO 10"]
    CHK1 -- "NO" --> FIX1["TUNE GRADIENT OR MOBILE PHASE STRENGTH"]
    CHK1 -- "YES" --> CHK2["IS ALPHA SUFFICIENT ALPHA GREATER THAN 1.1"]
    CHK2 -- "NO" --> FIX2["CHANGE STATIONARY PHASE OR TEMPERATURE"]
    CHK2 -- "YES" --> CHK3["INCREASE PLATE NUMBER N"]
    CHK3 --> FIX3["LENGTHEN COLUMN OR REDUCE PARTICLE SIZE"]
    FIX1 --> CHK1
    FIX2 --> CHK2
    FIX3 --> DONE["VALIDATED SEPARATION RS GREATER THAN 1.5"]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — Typical mAb Downstream Chromatography Sequence

```mermaid
graph LR
    H["CLARIFIED HARVEST"] --> S1["STEP 1 CAPTURE PROTEIN A AFFINITY"]
    S1 --> S2["STEP 2 LOW PH VIRAL INACTIVATION"]
    S2 --> S3["STEP 3 INTERMEDIATE CEX CATION EXCHANGE"]
    S3 --> S4["STEP 4 POLISHING AEX ANION EXCHANGE"]
    S4 --> S5["STEP 5 BULK DRUG SUBSTANCE GREATER THAN 99 PERCENT PURITY"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading aid for the diagrams:** Each block represents a process unit. Arrows indicate the flow of the product stream. Orthogonal modes (charge, hydrophobicity, affinity) provide multiplicative selectivity and impurity clearance.
