<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**  
Classify chromatographic techniques based on the physical state of the mobile phase. Give one example of an analyte separated by each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Chromatographic techniques are classified based on the mobile phase state into three main types:

1. **Gas Chromatography (GC):** A gaseous mobile phase (typically helium, nitrogen, or hydrogen) carries volatile analytes through a column containing a liquid or solid stationary phase. *Example:* Separation of fatty acid methyl esters (FAMEs) in edible oil analysis.

2. **Liquid Chromatography (LC):** A liquid mobile phase (water, organic solvents, or buffer mixtures) carries analytes through a solid or bonded stationary phase. *Example:* Reversed-phase HPLC separation of insulin variants.

3. **Supercritical Fluid Chromatography (SFC):** A supercritical fluid (commonly CO₂ above 31 °C and 73 bar) acts as the mobile phase. *Example:* Chiral separation of pharmaceutical enantiomers such as (R)- and (S)-warfarin.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (3-mark loss):** Students often omit the **third category (SFC)** and write only GC and LC. Always list **all three** for full marks in KTU 2024 scheme.

---

#### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**  
Define the **retention factor (k)** and state the optimal range of $k$ for a good chromatographic separation. Justify why values outside this range are undesirable.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The retention factor (also called the capacity factor) is defined as the ratio of the adjusted retention time to the dead time:

$$
k = \frac{t_R - t_M}{t_M}
$$

It is a **dimensionless quantity** that quantifies the strength of interaction of an analyte with the stationary phase relative to the mobile phase.

**Optimal Range:** $2 \leq k \leq 10$ (with $1 \leq k \leq 20$ being acceptable).

**Justification:**
- **If $k < 1$:** The analyte elutes too quickly; peaks overlap with the void-volume solvent front, and resolution is poor.
- **If $k > 20$:** The analyte is excessively retained; analysis time is long, peaks broaden due to longitudinal diffusion, and detection sensitivity decreases.
- The optimal range balances **resolution, run time, and detectability**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (1-mark loss):** Students write the formula but forget to mention that $k$ is **dimensionless**. Always state the units explicitly.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question has sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. The internal choice is between two complete alternative questions (Q11 OR Q12, etc.).

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Chromatographic Theory and Classification

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a)** [7 marks] Classify chromatographic techniques based on the **separation mechanism**. For each mechanism, state the molecular property exploited, give one stationary phase example, and name one bioproduct purified by that method.

**(b)** [7 marks] With the help of a **well-labeled diagram**, explain the **van Deemter equation**. Show mathematically how the **optimum linear velocity** is obtained and discuss its significance in column design.

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Classification by Separation Mechanism — Tabular Form (7 marks)**

| Mechanism | Property Exploited | Stationary Phase Example | Bioproduct Purified |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Adsorption** | Polarity / surface affinity | Silica gel, alumina | Vitamin A, lipid pigments |
| **Partition (Normal-Phase)** | Polarity differences | Polar bonded silica (CN, NH₂) | Tocopherols |
| **Partition (Reversed-Phase)** | Hydrophobicity | C18, C8 bonded silica | Insulin, peptides, oligonucleotides |
| **Ion-Exchange (CEX)** | Net positive charge | Carboxymethyl (CM), SP-Sepharose | Monoclonal antibodies, lysozyme |
| **Ion-Exchange (AEX)** | Net negative charge | DEAE-cellulose, Q-Sepharose | BSA, β-lactoglobulin, endotoxin removal |
| **Size-Exclusion (SEC)** | Molecular size / hydrodynamic radius | Sephadex, Superdex, Sephacryl | Aggregate removal, buffer exchange |
| **Affinity** | Biospecific recognition | Protein A / G agarose, Ni-NTA, lectin | mAb capture, His-tagged proteins |
| **Chiral** | Enantioselectivity | Chiralpak, Chiralcel columns | Chiral APIs, (R)/(S)-drugs |

**Distribution of marks (7):** 1 mark for naming the six major mechanisms + 0.5 mark each for property, stationary phase, and example × 6 = 6 + 0.5 wrap-up = 7 marks.

---

**(b) The Van Deemter Equation (7 marks)**

The van Deemter equation describes how the **height equivalent to a theoretical plate (H)** varies with the linear velocity $u$ of the mobile phase. It is written as:

$$
H = A + \frac{B}{u} + C \cdot u
$$

where:
- $A = 2 \lambda d_p$ is the **eddy diffusion** term (depends on particle diameter $d_p$ and packing irregularity $\lambda$).
- $B = 2 \gamma D_m$ is the **longitudinal (molecular) diffusion** term (depends on the diffusion coefficient of the analyte in the mobile phase).
- $C = \dfrac{f(k) d_p^2}{D_m}$ is the **mass transfer resistance** term (function of $k$ and $d_p$).

**Derivation of Optimum Velocity:**

Differentiate $H$ with respect to $u$:

$$
\frac{dH}{du} = -\frac{B}{u^2} + C
$$

Setting $\dfrac{dH}{du} = 0$ for the minimum:

$$
\frac{B}{u_{opt}^2} = C \quad \Rightarrow \quad u_{opt} = \sqrt{\frac{B}{C}}
$$

The minimum plate height is:

$$
H_{min} = A + 2\sqrt{BC}
$$

**Significance in Column Design:**
- Operating at $u_{opt}$ yields the **smallest plate height** and the **highest number of theoretical plates** $N$ for a given column length.
- Selecting a smaller particle size $d_p$ reduces both $A$ and $C$ (since both depend on $d_p$), shifting the entire $H$–$u$ curve downward and to the right — the basis of **UHPLC** (Ultra-High-Performance LC).
- Operating at velocities above $u_{opt}$ is sometimes done in preparative chromatography to save analysis time at the cost of modest efficiency loss.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (2-mark loss):** Students frequently write the equation but skip the **differential step** $dH/du = 0$ that gives $u_{opt}$. Always show the optimization calculus explicitly. KTU examiners deduct marks for missing intermediate algebra.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Resolution Engineering

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

**(a)** [7 marks] Define **resolution ($R_s$)** in chromatography. Derive the **Purnell resolution equation** from first principles, clearly identifying the **efficiency**, **selectivity**, and **retention** terms.

**(b)** [7 marks] A protein A affinity column is operated with $N = 5000$ theoretical plates. The retention factors of two monoclonal antibody variants are $k_1 = 3.0$ and $k_2 = 3.6$. Calculate the resolution and suggest **two specific strategies** to improve the resolution to $R_s \geq 1.5$ (baseline separation).

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Definition and Derivation of the Purnell Equation (7 marks)**

**Definition:** Resolution is the degree of separation between two adjacent chromatographic peaks, defined as the ratio of the distance between the peak maxima to the **average** peak baseline width:

$$
R_s = \frac{t_{R2} - t_{R1}}{0.5 (w_1 + w_2)}
$$

**Derivation (using algebra shown in Section 3.3 of these notes):**

For Gaussian peaks, $w = 4\sigma$ and $\sigma = t_R / \sqrt{N}$. Assuming $N_1 \approx N_2 = N$:

$$
R_s = \frac{(t_{R2} - t_{R1}) \sqrt{N}}{2 (t_{R1} + t_{R2})}
$$

Substituting $t_R = t_M (1 + k)$ and $\alpha = k_2 / k_1$:

$$
\boxed{\,R_s = \frac{\sqrt{N}}{4} \cdot \frac{\alpha - 1}{\alpha} \cdot \frac{k_2}{1 + k_2}\,}
$$

**Three-Term Identification:**
- **Efficiency term:** $\dfrac{\sqrt{N}}{4}$ — depends on column length, particle size, and flow rate.
- **Selectivity term:** $\dfrac{\alpha - 1}{\alpha}$ — depends on chemistry of the stationary and mobile phases.
- **Retention term:** $\dfrac{k_2}{1 + k_2}$ — depends on retention strength (gradient slope or mobile-phase composition).

**Mark Distribution (7):** 1 mark definition + 4 marks derivation steps + 1 mark term identification + 1 mark discussion of each term.

---

**(b) Numerical Calculation and Improvement Strategies (7 marks)**

**Given:**
- $N = 5000$ → $\sqrt{N} = \sqrt{5000} \approx 70.71$
- $k_1 = 3.0$, $k_2 = 3.6$
- $\alpha = k_2 / k_1 = 3.6 / 3.0 = 1.2$

**Step 1: Calculate each term of the Purnell equation.**

Efficiency term:

$$
\frac{\sqrt{N}}{4} = \frac{70.71}{4} \approx 17.68
$$

Selectivity term:

$$
\frac{\alpha - 1}{\alpha} = \frac{1.2 - 1}{1.2} = \frac{0.2}{1.2} = 0.1667
$$

Retention term:

$$
\frac{k_2}{1 + k_2} = \frac{3.6}{1 + 3.6} = \frac{3.6}{4.6} \approx 0.7826
$$

**Step 2: Compute the resolution.**

$$
R_s = 17.68 \times 0.1667 \times 0.7826
$$

$$
R_s \approx 2.306
$$

**Result:** $R_s \approx 2.31$, which is already **above the baseline-separation threshold of 1.5**. Therefore, the existing separation is acceptable.

**Step 3: Hypothetical improvement (if $R_s < 1.5$ were observed).**

Since $R_s \propto \sqrt{N} \cdot \dfrac{\alpha - 1}{\alpha} \cdot \dfrac{k_2}{1 + k_2}$, two specific strategies are:

1. **Increase plate number $N$:** Lengthen the column or switch to sub-2-µm particles (UHPLC). To **double** $R_s$, $N$ must increase by a factor of **4** (since $R_s \propto \sqrt{N}$).

2. **Increase selectivity $\alpha$:** Change the mobile-phase pH, temperature, or organic modifier. Even a small change from $\alpha = 1.2$ to $\alpha = 1.4$ (a 17% change) approximately **doubles** $R_s$ because the selectivity term is the most sensitive.

**Mark Distribution (7):** 2 marks for substitution + 2 marks for arithmetic + 1 mark for final $R_s$ + 2 marks for improvement strategies.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (1-mark loss):** Students often forget to take the **square root** of $N$ in the efficiency term. Writing $N$ directly is a common error. Always write $\sqrt{N}$ in the formula and the calculation.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Chromatographic Separations & Classification**

- [ ] **Chromatography** = separation by differential distribution between a **stationary phase** and a **mobile phase**. Coined by **Mikhail Tswett** in 1906 (Greek: *chroma* = color, *graphein* = to write).
- [ ] **Three mobile-phase states** → GC, LC, SFC. SFC uses supercritical CO₂ and bridges GC and LC.
- [ ] **Two bed geometries** → Planar (PC, TLC) and Column (packed or open-tubular).
- [ ] **Six major mechanisms** → Adsorption, Partition (NP/RP), Ion-Exchange (CEX/AEX), Size-Exclusion, Affinity, Chiral.
- [ ] **Elution modes** → Isocratic, Gradient, Step. Bioprocess chromatography is mostly gradient or step.
- [ ] **Retention factor:** $k = (t_R - t_M)/t_M$. Optimal range: **2 ≤ k ≤ 10**.
- [ ] **Selectivity factor:** $\alpha = k_2/k_1 \geq 1$. The **most powerful resolution knob**.
- [ ] **Efficiency:** $N = 16(t_R/w)^2 = 5.54(t_R/w_{1/2})^2$. Plate height $H = L/N$.
- [ ] **Resolution:** $R_s = 2(t_{R2} - t_{R1})/(w_1 + w_2) \geq 1.5$ for **baseline separation**.
- [ ] **Purnell equation:** $R_s = \dfrac{\sqrt{N}}{4} \cdot \dfrac{\alpha - 1}{\alpha} \cdot \dfrac{k_2}{1 + k_2}$.
- [ ] **Van Deemter equation:** $H = A + B/u + C \cdot u$. Optimum velocity $u_{opt} = \sqrt{B/C}$.
- [ ] **mAb platform sequence** (industry standard): Harvest → Protein A → Low-pH → CEX → AEX → UF/DF.
- [ ] **Orthogonal purification** = using sequential chromatographic modes that exploit **different molecular properties** → multiplicative selectivity.
- [ ] **In KTU 2024 answer sheets**, always **show intermediate steps**, **state units**, and **define symbols** before using them.

> **End of Module 3 Notes — Chromatographic Separations: Classification of Chromatographic Techniques**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: General Description of Column Chromatography, Practice of Chromatography, and Process Considerations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT525 - BIOSEPARATION TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** General Description of Column Chromatography, Practice of Chromatography, and Process Considerations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

**Column Chromatography** is a chromatographic separation technique in which the **stationary phase** is packed (or coated on the inner wall) inside a cylindrical tube called the **chromatographic column**, and the **mobile phase** is continuously forced through it, either by gravity, peristaltic pumping, or high-pressure piston pumping. The sample mixture is introduced at the column inlet, and individual components migrate through the bed at different velocities dictated by their relative affinity for the two phases, ultimately eluting as discrete bands at the column outlet where a suitable detector monitors the effluent.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> In KTU's *Bioseparation Technology* (PEBBT525), the term "Column Chromatography" is the umbrella module covering all **packed-bed chromatographic modalities** used at preparative and process scale — Gel Filtration, Ion Exchange, Affinity, Reversed Phase, and Hydrophobic Interaction Chromatography. The mathematical treatment (plate theory, van Deemter, resolution) applies universally across all these sub-modes.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **long hallway lined with magnetic lockers** (the stationary phase). A crowd of mixed people (the sample) enters one end. Some people have strong magnetic keys (high affinity to stationary phase) — they keep getting pulled aside and stop at nearly every locker. Others have weak plastic keys (low affinity) — they race straight through the hallway with little interruption. **The first person to exit the far end is the one with the weakest interaction**; the last to exit has the strongest interaction. The time each person takes to traverse the hallway is unique, just as the **retention time (t_R)** is unique for each analyte. The "hallway length" is the column length **L**, the "lockers" are the stationary phase particles, and the "flow of air carrying people" is the mobile phase linear velocity **u**.

> [!TIP]
> **Plain-English Summary:** A chromatographic column is essentially a *race track* for molecules. Compounds that "hug the track" (stick to the stationary phase) finish last; compounds that ignore the track (stay in the mobile phase) finish first. The race is governed by physical chemistry, not random chance — and that physics is the heart of this module.

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics (Must Memorise)

- **Linear velocity (u):** typically **1–600 cm/hr** depending on modality (low for gel filtration, high for reversed phase).
- **Column length (L):** analytical = 5–25 cm; preparative/process = 10–100 cm.
- **Particle diameter (d_p):** analytical = 3–10 µm; preparative = 15–50 µm; process = 50–200 µm.
- **Back-pressure (ΔP):** can range from <1 bar (low-pressure) to >1000 bar (UHPLC).
- **Plate number (N):** 1,000 to >100,000 for high-efficiency analytical columns.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CO Mapping (KTU 2024):** This topic is mapped to **CO3 — "Apply the principles of chromatographic and membrane-based separation processes for the recovery and purification of bioproducts."** It carries an average weightage of **12–15 marks** in the End Semester Examination.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Physical Anatomy of a Column Chromatography System

A complete chromatographic workstation consists of the following sub-systems, each of which contributes to the *band-broadening budget*:

1. **Mobile phase reservoir & degasser** — supplies bubble-free solvent.
2. **Pump** — delivers pulse-free, constant flow rate **F** (mL/min).
3. **Injector / Sample loop** — introduces the sample as a sharp plug.
4. **Column** — the heart of the system, containing the stationary phase.
5. **Detector** — UV-Vis, RI, conductivity, or mass spectrometer.
6. **Data acquisition / Fraction collector** — records the chromatogram and collects eluting fractions.

### 2.2 Theoretical Plate Theory (Martin & Synge, 1941)

The column is conceptually divided into **N discrete, hypothetical equilibrium stages** called **theoretical plates**. Each plate is treated as a tiny vessel where the solute equilibrates instantaneously between the mobile and stationary phases before moving to the next plate.

- **Number of theoretical plates (N):** a dimensionless measure of column efficiency.
- **Height equivalent to a theoretical plate (HETP or H):** the length of column occupied by one plate.

$$ H = \frac{L}{N} $$

where:
- $H$ = HETP (cm or mm)
- $L$ = column length (cm or mm)
- $N$ = number of theoretical plates (dimensionless)

**Two methods to calculate N from a chromatographic peak (Gaussian shape assumed):**

$$ N = 16 \cdot \left(\frac{t_R}{w}\right)^2 $$

$$ N = 5.54 \cdot \left(\frac{t_R}{w_{1/2}}\right)^2 $$

where:
- $t_R$ = retention time of the peak apex
- $w$ = baseline peak width
- $w_{1/2}$ = peak width at half-height

> [!NOTE]
> The factor **16** comes from the property that for a Gaussian peak, the baseline width equals **4σ** (where σ is the standard deviation), since 99.7 % of the peak lies within ±3σ. The factor **5.54** comes from the relationship $w_{1/2} = 2.354 \sigma$, so $(t_R / w_{1/2})^2 = (t_R / (2.354\sigma))^2 = 1/(2.354)^2 \cdot (t_R/\sigma)^2 = 0.1805 \cdot N/16$, yielding $N = 5.54(t_R/w_{1/2})^2$.

### 2.3 The van Deemter Equation (1956)

A landmark kinetic model describing the relationship between plate height **H** and mobile phase linear velocity **u**:

$$ H = A + \frac{B}{u} + C \cdot u $$

where:
- $A$ = **Eddy diffusion** term (multiple flow paths around particles)
- $B/u$ = **Longitudinal (molecular) diffusion** term
- $C \cdot u$ = **Mass transfer resistance** term (both stationary and mobile phase)
- $u$ = linear velocity of mobile phase (cm/s)

| Term | Physical Origin | Mathematical Approximation |
|------|----------------|---------------------------|
| $A = 2 \lambda d_p$ | Flow-paths around packed particles of diameter $d_p$ | $\lambda$ is a packing constant (≈ 0.5–1) |
| $B = 2 \gamma D_m$ | Longitudinal Brownian diffusion | $D_m$ is solute diffusivity in mobile phase, $\gamma$ is an obstruction factor |
| $C = C_s + C_m$ | Combined resistance to mass transfer in stationary ($C_s$) and mobile ($C_m$) phases | $C_s \propto d_f^2 / D_s$, $C_m \propto d_p^2 / D_m$ |

### 2.4 Resolution Equation (Purnell, 1960)

The **master equation of chromatography** — quantifies how well two adjacent peaks are separated:

$$ R_s = \frac{\sqrt{N}}{4} \cdot \frac{(\alpha - 1)}{\alpha} \cdot \frac{k'}{1 + k'} $$

In an alternative, more intuitive form:

$$ R_s = \frac{t_{R2} - t_{R1}}{0.5 \cdot (w_1 + w_2)} $$

where:
- $\alpha$ = selectivity factor = $k'_2 / k'_1$ (must be > 1 for separation)
- $k'$ = retention factor = $(t_R - t_0) / t_0$
- $t_0$ = column dead time (time for unretained solute to pass through)
- $t_{R1}, t_{R2}$ = retention times of peaks 1 and 2
- $w_1, w_2$ = baseline widths of peaks 1 and 2

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Resolution Master Rule:** $R_s = 1.0$ gives **94 % baseline separation** (touching peaks); $R_s = 1.5$ gives **99.7 % baseline separation** (industry "baseline" criterion). A $1.4\times$ increase in $L$ doubles $N$, which **increases $R_s$ by $\sqrt{2} \approx 1.41$**.

### 2.5 Pressure Drop Across a Packed Column (Kozeny–Carman)

The back-pressure must be overcome by the pump. For a packed bed:

$$ \Delta P = \frac{150 \cdot \mu \cdot u \cdot L \cdot (1-\varepsilon)^2}{d_p^2 \cdot \varepsilon^3} $$

where:
- $\mu$ = mobile phase viscosity (Pa·s)
- $u$ = superficial linear velocity (m/s)
- $L$ = column length (m)
- $\varepsilon$ = bed porosity (interparticle void fraction, typically 0.3–0.4)
- $d_p$ = particle diameter (m)

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Application / Key Take-away |
|---|---------|----------------------------|
| 1 | $H = L / N$ | Smaller H = higher efficiency |
| 2 | $N = 16 \cdot (t_R / w)^2$ | Efficiency from baseline width |
| 3 | $N = 5.54 \cdot (t_R / w_{1/2})^2$ | Efficiency from half-height width |
| 4 | $k' = (t_R - t_0) / t_0$ | Retention factor (dimensionless) |
| 5 | $\alpha = k'_2 / k'_1$ | Selectivity (always $\geq 1$) |
| 6 | $R_s = \sqrt{N}/4 \cdot (\alpha-1)/\alpha \cdot k'/(1+k')$ | Resolution master equation |
| 7 | $R_s = (t_{R2}-t_{R1}) / [0.5(w_1+w_2)]$ | Resolution from chromatogram |
| 8 | $H = A + B/u + C \cdot u$ | van Deemter — optimal velocity $u_{opt} = \sqrt{B/C}$ |
| 9 | $\Delta P \propto \mu \cdot u \cdot L / d_p^2$ | Kozeny-Carman pressure drop |
| 10 | $u_{opt} = \sqrt{B / C}$ | Velocity that minimises H |
| 11 | $H_{min} = A + 2\sqrt{B \cdot C}$ | Minimum plate height at $u_{opt}$ |

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** The Purnell resolution equation guides the design of every preparative chromatographic step in industry — Pfizer, Biocon, and Reliance Life Sciences routinely compute $L$, $N$, $\alpha$, and $k'$ to scale-up antibody purification. Halving $d_p$ from 50 µm to 25 µm quadruples $N$ but **16×** the back-pressure, forcing a process engineer to balance *resolution vs pump cost*.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of HETP from a Gaussian Peak (Plate Model)

**Starting assumption:** A solute peak exiting the column approximates a Gaussian distribution with standard deviation σ and mean (apex) at retention time $t_R$.

**Step 1 — Variance is additive.**
In a column of length $L$ divided into $N$ plates, the total variance of the peak is the sum of variances contributed by each plate. If each plate contributes the same variance $\sigma_{plate}^2$:

$$ \sigma_{total}^2 = N \cdot \sigma_{plate}^2 $$

**Step 2 — Definition of HETP.**
HETP, by definition, is the column length per plate:

$$ H = \frac{L}{N} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad N = \frac{L}{H} $$

**Step 3 — Relate peak width to variance.**
For a Gaussian peak, the baseline width $w$ corresponds to $\pm 2\sigma$ (4σ total), since 95.4 % of the distribution lies within ±2σ:

$$ w = 4 \sigma \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \sigma = \frac{w}{4} $$

**Step 4 — Substitute into the plate-count formula.**
Combining the variance expression and the HETP definition:

$$ N = \frac{L}{H} = \frac{t_R^2}{\sigma_{total}^2} $$

This is a fundamental result of the plate model — the variance of the elution time equals $t_R^2 / N$.

**Step 5 — Express N in terms of measurable quantities.**
Using $\sigma = w/4$:

$$ N = \left(\frac{t_R}{\sigma}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{t_R}{w/4}\right)^2 = 16 \left(\frac{t_R}{w}\right)^2 $$

**Step 6 — Express H in terms of measurable quantities.**
From $H = L / N$:

$$ H = \frac{L}{16 \cdot (t_R / w)^2} = \frac{L \cdot w^2}{16 \cdot t_R^2} $$

**Final Result:**

$$ \boxed{\, H = \frac{L \cdot w^2}{16 \cdot t_R^2} \,} \quad ; \quad \boxed{\, N = 16 \left(\frac{t_R}{w}\right)^2 \,} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A **sharper peak** (small $w$) gives a **larger N** and **smaller H**, i.e., a more efficient column. Modern UHPLC columns achieve $H \approx 5 \,\mu m$ at $u_{opt}$ — meaning each theoretical plate is only 5 micrometres long.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Optimal Linear Velocity from the van Deemter Equation

**Step 1 — Start with the van Deemter equation.**

$$ H(u) = A + \frac{B}{u} + C \cdot u $$

**Step 2 — To find the minimum H, differentiate with respect to u and set to zero.**

$$ \frac{dH}{du} = -\frac{B}{u^2} + C = 0 $$

**Step 3 — Solve for u.**

$$ C = \frac{B}{u^2} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad u^2 = \frac{B}{C} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad u_{opt} = \sqrt{\frac{B}{C}} $$

**Step 4 — Substitute $u_{opt}$ back into van Deemter to obtain $H_{min}$.**

$$ H_{min} = A + \frac{B}{\sqrt{B/C}} + C \cdot \sqrt{B/C} = A + \frac{B\sqrt{C}}{\sqrt{B}} + C \cdot \frac{\sqrt{B}}{\sqrt{C}} $$

$$ H_{min} = A + \sqrt{B \cdot C} + \sqrt{B \cdot C} = A + 2\sqrt{B \cdot C} $$

**Final Result:**

$$ \boxed{\, u_{opt} = \sqrt{\frac{B}{C}} \,} \quad ; \quad \boxed{\, H_{min} = A + 2\sqrt{B \cdot C} \,} $$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering insight:** Operating a preparative column above $u_{opt}$ sacrifices efficiency for throughput — a common compromise in industry. Operating below $u_{opt}$ wastes time and dilutes the sample (peak broadening from longitudinal diffusion dominates).

---

### 3.3 Derivation of the Resolution Equation (Purnell Form)

**Step 1 — Start from the plate definition of N.**

$$ R_s = \frac{t_{R2} - t_{R1}}{0.5(w_1 + w_2)} $$

**Step 2 — Approximate peak widths using $w = 4\sigma$ and the relationship $\sigma = t_R / \sqrt{N}$ (from the plate model).**

$$ w_1 = \frac{4 t_{R1}}{\sqrt{N}} \quad ; \quad w_2 = \frac{4 t_{R2}}{\sqrt{N}} $$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the resolution equation.**

$$ R_s = \frac{t_{R2} - t_{R1}}{0.5 \cdot \frac{4}{\sqrt{N}} \cdot (t_{R1} + t_{R2})} = \frac{\sqrt{N}}{2} \cdot \frac{t_{R2} - t_{R1}}{t_{R1} + t_{R2}} $$

**Step 4 — Introduce retention factor $k' = (t_R - t_0) / t_0$, hence $t_R = t_0(1 + k')$.**

$$ R_s = \frac{\sqrt{N}}{2} \cdot \frac{t_0(1 + k'_2) - t_0(1 + k'_1)}{t_0(1 + k'_2) + t_0(1 + k'_1)} = \frac{\sqrt{N}}{2} \cdot \frac{k'_2 - k'_1}{2 + k'_1 + k'_2} $$

**Step 5 — Approximate $k'_2 + k'_1 \approx 2k'$ (valid when $k'_1 \approx k'_2 = k'$).**

$$ R_s \approx \frac{\sqrt{N}}{2} \cdot \frac{k'_2 - k'_1}{2(1 + k')} = \frac{\sqrt{N}}{4} \cdot \frac{k'_2 - k'_1}{1 + k'} $$

**Step 6 — Introduce selectivity $\alpha = k'_2 / k'_1$, i.e., $k'_2 = \alpha k'_1$, so $k'_2 - k'_1 = k'_1(\alpha - 1)$.**

$$ R_s = \frac{\sqrt{N}}{4} \cdot \frac{k'_1(\alpha - 1)}{1 + k'_1} \cdot \frac{1+k'}{1+k'} \approx \frac{\sqrt{N}}{4} \cdot \frac{(\alpha - 1)}{\alpha} \cdot \frac{k'}{1+k'} $$

where the final approximation uses $k' = (k'_1 + k'_2)/2$ and $1 + k' \approx 1 + k'_1$ for closely eluting peaks.

**Final Result (Purnell Equation):**

$$ \boxed{\, R_s = \frac{\sqrt{N}}{4} \cdot \frac{\alpha - 1}{\alpha} \cdot \frac{k'}{1 + k'} \,} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Resolution Driver Hierarchy:**
> 1. **Efficiency (N)** — squared-root effect. 4× more plates → 2× better resolution.
> 2. **Selectivity (α)** — exponential effect. The most powerful knob; change the stationary/mobile phase chemistry.
> 3. **Retention (k')** — weak effect, but crucial. Operate in the range $2 \leq k' \leq 10$ for maximum benefit.

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style Calculation)

> **Problem:** A packed column is 25 cm long. A solute elutes with $t_R = 9.0$ min and the baseline width $w = 1.0$ min. Calculate (a) the number of theoretical plates N, (b) the HETP, and (c) the resolution if a second peak elutes at $t_{R2} = 11.0$ min with $w_2 = 1.2$ min.

**Solution (a):** Using the half-height approximation since $w$ is given at baseline:

$$ N = 16 \cdot \left(\frac{9.0}{1.0}\right)^2 = 16 \cdot 81 = 1296 \text{ plates} $$

**Solution (b):**

$$ H = \frac{L}{N} = \frac{25 \text{ cm}}{1296} = 0.0193 \text{ cm} = 193 \,\mu m $$

**Solution (c):**

$$ R_s = \frac{11.0 - 9.0}{0.5 \cdot (1.0 + 1.2)} = \frac{2.0}{1.1} = 1.818 $$

**Interpretation:** $R_s = 1.818 > 1.5$ → **baseline separation** achieved. ✓

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram of a Column Chromatography System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R[Mobile Phase Reservoir] --> D[Degasser]
    D --> P[Pump]
    P --> I[Sample Injector]
    I --> COL[Chromatographic Column]
    COL --> DET[Detector]
    DET --> DAQ[Data Acquisition System]
    DAQ --> FC[Fraction Collector]
    COL -.-> SP[Stationary Phase Particles]
    COL -.-> MP[Mobile Phase Flow Path]
    SP --- |"Mass Transfer"| MP
    DAQ --> CHR[Chromatogram Output]
    FC --> WASTE[Waste / Product Vessel]
```

### 4.2 Internal Architecture of a Packed Column (Sample Band Migration)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Column Inlet] --> A1[Zone 1: Sample Injection]
    A1 --> A2[Zone 2: Initial Band Broadening]
    A2 --> A3[Zone 3: Differential Migration Begins]
    A3 --> A4[Zone 4: Partial Separation]
    A4 --> A5[Zone 5: Resolved Bands]
    A5 --> A6[Column Outlet: Peak 1 First]
    A5 --> A7[Column Outlet: Peak 2 Second]
    A5 --> A8[Column Outlet: Peak 3 Last]
    B1[Stationary Phase Beads d_p] -.-> A2
    B2[Mobile Phase Linear Velocity u] -.-> A3
    B3[Mass Transfer Coefficient] -.-> A4
    B4[Selectivity Factor alpha] -.-> A5
```

### 4.3 Process Considerations Decision Tree (Practical Practice)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START([Start: Sample Loaded]) --> Q1{Sample Volume?}
    Q1 -->|<2% Bed Volume| PATH1[Overload Mode: High Resolution]
    Q1 -->|2 to 5% Bed Volume| PATH2[Standard Analytical Mode]
    Q1 -->|>5% Bed Volume| PATH3[Preparative Mode: Volume Overload]
    PATH1 --> Q2{Flow Rate Set?}
    PATH2 --> Q2
    PATH3 --> Q2
    Q2 -->|Yes at u_opt| OK[Optimal Resolution Achieved]
    Q2 -->|No: Too High| WARN1[Warning: Loss of Efficiency]
    Q2 -->|No: Too Low| WARN2[Warning: Band Broadening from B Term]
    OK --> REGEN[Step 4: Column Regeneration]
    WARN1 --> REGEN
    WARN2 --> REGEN
    REGEN --> CIP[Clean-In-Place Protocol]
    CIP --> REUSE[Column Ready for Next Cycle]
```

### 4.4 Band Broadening Contributors (Mapped to van Deemter)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    BB[Total Band Broadening H] --> A_TERM[Eddy Diffusion A term]
    BB --> B_TERM[Longitudinal Diffusion B over u]
    BB --> C_TERM[Mass Transfer Resistance C times u]
    A_TERM --> A1[Particle Size d_p]
    A_TERM --> A2[Packing Quality lambda]
    B_TERM --> B1[Solute Diffusivity D_m]
    B_TERM --> B2[Time in Column t]
    C_TERM --> C1[Film Mass Transfer C_m]
    C_TERM --> C2[Stationary Phase Diffusion C_s]
```

Examiners are explicitly trained to award partial marks for *correctly identifying* process blocks and their interconnections.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define the term "theoretical plate" and "HETP" with respect to column chromatography. Why is a smaller HETP desirable?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **theoretical plate** is a hypothetical equilibrium stage within a chromatographic column in which the solute is assumed to instantaneously partition between the mobile and stationary phases before being transferred to the next stage. The **Height Equivalent to a Theoretical Plate (HETP or H)** is the column length occupied by one such plate and is calculated as $H = L/N$, where $L$ is the column length and $N$ is the total number of theoretical plates.

A **smaller HETP** is desirable because it indicates **higher column efficiency** — each unit length of column accomplishes more separation work. A column with $H = 10 \,\mu m$ generates 100,000 plates per metre, vastly outperforming one with $H = 100 \,\mu m$ that yields only 10,000 plates per metre. (3 marks)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*List the three terms of the van Deemter equation and state the physical phenomenon each represents.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The van Deemter equation is $H = A + B/u + C \cdot u$:

1. **A term (Eddy diffusion):** Represents the multiple flow-paths that solute molecules take around packed particles, causing band broadening. Depends on particle size $d_p$ and packing homogeneity.
2. **B/u term (Longitudinal diffusion):** Represents the natural Brownian diffusion of solute molecules along the flow axis, broadening the band. Dominant at low velocities.
3. **C·u term (Mass transfer resistance):** Represents the finite time required for solutes to equilibrate between mobile and stationary phases. Dominant at high velocities.

(3 marks)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice Note (KTU 2024):** Question A OR Question B — answer ANY ONE.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3, PEBBT525]**

(a) Derive the Purnell resolution equation for column chromatography, starting from the basic definition of resolution. **(7 marks)**

(b) In a chromatographic run, two solutes A and B elute at $t_{RA} = 6.0$ min and $t_{RB} = 8.5$ min with baseline widths $w_A = 0.6$ min and $w_B = 0.8$ min respectively. The column dead time is $t_0 = 1.0$ min, and the column is 15 cm long. Calculate:
- (i) the retention factors $k'_A$ and $k'_B$
- (ii) the selectivity factor $\alpha$
- (iii) the number of theoretical plates $N$ (for solute B)
- (iv) the resolution $R_s$ **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) — Derivation of Purnell Resolution Equation (7 marks):**

**Step 1:** Begin with the fundamental definition of resolution:

$$ R_s = \frac{t_{R2} - t_{R1}}{0.5(w_1 + w_2)} \quad \text{...(1 mark)} $$

**Step 2:** Apply Gaussian peak theory: each peak width relates to its standard deviation as $w_i = 4\sigma_i$, and from the plate model $\sigma_i = t_{Ri}/\sqrt{N}$:

$$ w_1 = \frac{4 t_{R1}}{\sqrt{N}}, \quad w_2 = \frac{4 t_{R2}}{\sqrt{N}} \quad \text{...(1 mark)} $$

**Step 3:** Substitute into (1):

$$ R_s = \frac{t_{R2} - t_{R1}}{0.5 \cdot \frac{4}{\sqrt{N}} (t_{R1} + t_{R2})} = \frac{\sqrt{N}}{2} \cdot \frac{t_{R2} - t_{R1}}{t_{R1} + t_{R2}} \quad \text{...(1 mark)} $$

**Step 4:** Introduce retention factors using $t_R = t_0(1 + k')$:

$$ R_s = \frac{\sqrt{N}}{2} \cdot \frac{(1 + k'_2) - (1 + k'_1)}{(1 + k'_2) + (1 + k'_1)} = \frac{\sqrt{N}}{2} \cdot \frac{k'_2 - k'_1}{2 + k'_1 + k'_2} \quad \text{...(1 mark)} $$

**Step 5:** Apply the close-elution approximation $k'_1 + k'_2 \approx 2k'$ and $k'_1 \approx k'$:

$$ R_s \approx \frac{\sqrt{N}}{2} \cdot \frac{k'_2 - k'_1}{2(1 + k')} = \frac{\sqrt{N}}{4} \cdot \frac{k'_2 - k'_1}{1 + k'} \quad \text{...(1 mark)} $$

**Step 6:** Introduce $\alpha = k'_2/k'_1$, so $k'_2 - k'_1 = k'_1(\alpha - 1)$:

$$ \boxed{\, R_s = \frac{\sqrt{N}}{4} \cdot \frac{\alpha - 1}{\alpha} \cdot \frac{k'}{1 + k'} \,} \quad \text{...(2 marks)} $$

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Calculations (7 marks):**

**(i) Retention factors** (1 mark):

$$ k'_A = \frac{t_{RA} - t_0}{t_0} = \frac{6.0 - 1.0}{1.0} = 5.0 $$

$$ k'_B = \frac{t_{RB} - t_0}{t_0} = \frac{8.5 - 1.0}{1.0} = 7.5 $$

**(ii) Selectivity factor** (1 mark):

$$ \alpha = \frac{k'_B}{k'_A} = \frac{7.5}{5.0} = 1.5 $$

**(iii) Number of theoretical plates (for solute B)** (2 marks):

$$ N = 16 \cdot \left(\frac{t_{RB}}{w_B}\right)^2 = 16 \cdot \left(\frac{8.5}{0.8}\right)^2 = 16 \cdot (10.625)^2 = 16 \cdot 112.89 = 1806 \text{ plates} $$

**(iv) Resolution** (3 marks):

$$ R_s = \frac{t_{RB} - t_{RA}}{0.5(w_A + w_B)} = \frac{8.5 - 6.0}{0.5(0.6 + 0.8)} = \frac{2.5}{0.7} = 3.57 $$

**Verification using Purnell equation:**

$$ R_s = \frac{\sqrt{1806}}{4} \cdot \frac{1.5 - 1}{1.5} \cdot \frac{6.25}{1 + 6.25} = \frac{42.50}{4} \cdot 0.333 \cdot 0.862 = 10.625 \cdot 0.333 \cdot 0.862 = 3.05 $$

*Note: The small discrepancy arises from the close-elution approximation; the direct calculation $R_s = 3.57$ is the more accurate value.*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> - **Common mistake #1:** Students frequently forget to square the term $(t_R/w)$ when calculating N. **Always** write the formula before substituting numerical values.
> - **Common mistake #2:** Using $t_R$ instead of $(t_R - t_0)$ when computing $k'$. This gives negative $k'$ values and **zero marks** for the part.
> - **Common mistake #3:** Confusing $R_s$ from direct formula vs Purnell equation. In KTU valuation, the direct formula method is preferred when peak widths are given; Purnell is preferred for theoretical/design questions.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3, PEBBT525]**

(a) Explain the van Deemter equation in detail. With the help of a labelled plot, describe how the plate height H varies with linear velocity u. Discuss the significance of the optimal linear velocity. **(7 marks)**

(b) During a van Deemter study on a 10 cm column, the following data were obtained for a solute at different linear velocities:

| u (cm/s) | 0.05 | 0.10 | 0.20 | 0.40 | 0.80 |
|----------|------|------|------|------|------|
| H (mm)   | 0.18 | 0.13 | 0.11 | 0.12 | 0.17 |

Plot the van Deemter curve and determine the values of A, B, and C. Also calculate the minimum plate height $H_{min}$ and the optimal linear velocity $u_{opt}$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) — Van Deemter Equation (7 marks):**

**Statement of the equation (1 mark):**

$$ H = A + \frac{B}{u} + C \cdot u $$

**Description of each term (3 marks):**

- **A (Eddy diffusion):** Independent of velocity. Caused by solute molecules taking different paths around the irregularly packed particles. $A = 2\lambda d_p$ where $\lambda$ is a packing constant.
- **B/u (Longitudinal diffusion):** Inversely proportional to velocity. Caused by Brownian motion of solute molecules along the column axis, dispersing the band. $B = 2\gamma D_m$.
- **C·u (Mass transfer resistance):** Linearly proportional to velocity. Caused by finite rate of equilibration between mobile and stationary phases. $C = C_s + C_m$.

**Plot description (2 marks):** A typical van Deemter plot is a **hyperbola-like curve** with a minimum. At low $u$, the $B/u$ term dominates and $H$ is large. At high $u$, the $C \cdot u$ term dominates. The minimum occurs at $u_{opt} = \sqrt{B/C}$ where $H_{min} = A + 2\sqrt{BC}$.

**Significance of $u_{opt}$ (1 mark):** Operating at $u_{opt}$ provides maximum efficiency. In practice, most analytical columns are run near $u_{opt}$; preparative columns may operate at higher $u$ to save time at the cost of resolution.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Determination of A, B, C (7 marks):**

**Method:** Multiply the van Deemter equation by $u$ to obtain a linear form:

$$ H \cdot u = A \cdot u + B + C \cdot u^2 $$

Rearranging:

$$ \frac{H \cdot u - C \cdot u^2 - B}{u} \quad \text{(iterative fitting approach)} $$

A more straightforward approach is **non-linear regression**, but for KTU the recommended method is the **method of selected points** at $u_{opt}$ and $u \to \infty$.

**At $u_{opt} = \sqrt{B/C}$ (minimum point):** From the data, the minimum $H$ occurs near $u = 0.20$ cm/s with $H \approx 0.11$ mm. Hence $u_{opt} \approx 0.20$ cm/s and $H_{min} \approx 0.11$ mm.

**At very high u (A-term dominance):** Extrapolating, the curve flattens at $H \to A$ as $u \to \infty$. From the data at $u = 0.80$ cm/s, $H = 0.17$ mm. Since the curve is still rising here, $A$ is slightly less than 0.17 mm. A reasonable estimate: $A \approx 0.05$ mm.

**At very low u (B/u dominance):** As $u \to 0$, $H \to \infty$. From the data at $u = 0.05$ cm/s, $H = 0.18$ mm. Using $H \approx B/u$ at low $u$:

$$ B \approx H \cdot u = 0.18 \text{ mm} \times 0.05 \text{ cm/s} = 0.009 \text{ mm} \cdot \text{cm/s} $$

**C calculation:** From $u_{opt} = \sqrt{B/C}$:

$$ C = \frac{B}{u_{opt}^2} = \frac{0.009}{(0.20)^2} = \frac{0.009}{0.04} = 0.225 \text{ mm} \cdot \text{s/cm} $$

**Final values (3 marks):**
- $A \approx 0.05$ mm
- $B \approx 0.009$ mm·cm/s
- $C \approx 0.225$ mm·s/cm
- $H_{min} = A + 2\sqrt{BC} = 0.05 + 2\sqrt{0.009 \times 0.225} = 0.05 + 2 \times 0.045 = 0.14$ mm
- $u_{opt} = \sqrt{B/C} = 0.20$ cm/s ✓ (consistent with observation)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning — Question B:**
> - **Common mistake #1:** Treating the van Deemter plot as a parabola. It is **not** parabolic — it is a **hyperbola-like curve** with an asymptote at $H = A$ for $u \to \infty$ and $H \to \infty$ for $u \to 0$. Drawing a "U-shape" loses 1 mark.
> - **Common mistake #2:** Reporting $A$, $B$, $C$ without units. **Always** include units in KTU answer sheets.
> - **Common mistake #3:** Forgetting to verify that calculated $u_{opt}$ matches the observed minimum. Examiners award 1 mark for self-consistency.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Column chromatography** = stationary phase packed in a tube; mobile phase flows continuously.
- [x] **Plate theory (Martin & Synge, 1941)** treats the column as a series of $N$ equilibrium stages.
- [x] **HETP:** $H = L / N$ — smaller is better.
- [x] **Two N-formulas:** $N = 16(t_R/w)^2$ (baseline) and $N = 5.54(t_R/w_{1/2})^2$ (half-height).
- [x] **Van Deemter:** $H = A + B/u + C \cdot u$ — the kinetic master equation.
- [x] **A** = eddy diffusion (depends on $d_p$ and packing).
- [x] **B/u** = longitudinal diffusion (dominates at low $u$).
- [x] **C·u** = mass transfer resistance (dominates at high $u$).
- [x] **$u_{opt} = \sqrt{B/C}$** — point of maximum column efficiency.
- [x] **$H_{min} = A + 2\sqrt{BC}$** — minimum achievable plate height.
- [x] **Purnell resolution:** $R_s = (\sqrt{N}/4) \cdot [(\alpha-1)/\alpha] \cdot [k'/(1+k')]$.
- [x] **Resolution driver hierarchy:** $N$ (square-root effect) > $\alpha$ (exponential) > $k'$ (weak).
- [x] **$R_s = 1.5$** is the industrial baseline-separation criterion.
- [x] **Doubling L** → $N$ doubles → $R_s$ increases by $\sqrt{2} \approx 1.41$.
- [x] **Pressure drop** $\Delta P \propto \mu u L / d_p^2$ (Kozeny-Carman) — halving $d_p$ **16×** the pressure.
- [x] **Optimal $k'$ range:** $2 \leq k' \leq 10$ for efficient separation.
- [x] **Dead time $t_0$** = retention time of an unretained tracer (e.g., acetone in RP, blue dextran in gel filtration).
- [x] **Process considerations:** sample loading ≤ 5 % bed volume (analytical), ≤ 30 % (preparative volume overload); flow rate at or slightly above $u_{opt}$; regeneration with strong eluent after each cycle; CIP (Clean-In-Place) with NaOH for ion exchange, ethanol for RP.
- [x] **Scale-up rule:** Maintain constant $L/d_p$ ratio and constant $u$ to preserve resolution.
- [x] **Industry-standard resin particle sizes:** 15–25 µm (analytical HPLC), 30–50 µm (preparative), 50–200 µm (process).
- [x] **Column packing methods:** slurry packing (high pressure), dry packing (gravity), with $L/d_p > 500$ for efficient beds.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Exam Tip:** The three equations examiners love to test are: **(1)** the plate-count formula, **(2)** the van Deemter equation with $u_{opt}$ derivation, and **(3)** the Purnell resolution equation. Memorise these *symbol-by-symbol* — even one misplaced bracket or wrong subscript can cost a full mark in KTU's strict valuation scheme.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Crystallization Principles: Nucleation and Crystal Growth
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT525 - BIOSEPARATION TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Crystallization Principles: Nucleation and Crystal Growth

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4 — CRYSTALLIZATION PRINCIPLES: NUCLEATION AND CRYSTAL GROWTH

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Crystallization (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):**
> Crystallization is a **unit operation of bioseparation** in which solid crystals are formed from a homogeneous liquid phase (solution or melt) by deliberately creating **supersaturation**, followed by the sequential events of **nucleation** (birth of new crystals) and **crystal growth** (size enlargement of existing crystals). It is one of the oldest, most energy-efficient, and most widely used **purification + solid-form shaping** techniques in the bioprocess, pharmaceutical, food, and fine-chemicals industries.

Within the **KTU PEBBT525 (Bioseparation Technology) 2024 scheme**, crystallization occupies a unique position because it can simultaneously achieve:
1. **High purification** (exceeding 99.9 % in many protein and amino-acid systems),
2. **Definitive solid form** (the crystal itself is the final product),
3. **Gentle operating temperatures** (low thermal damage to thermo-labile biomolecules).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Honey Jar" Picture

Imagine pouring a very sweet, hot tea into a glass jar. While the tea is hot, the sugar stays dissolved — the system is **undersaturated** or just **saturated**. As the tea cools, the solution can no longer hold all the sugar, so the system becomes **supersaturated** (the driving force). Now, two things can happen:

* If a tiny dust particle or air bubble is already present, sugar molecules preferentially "glue" onto it and a sugar crystal **grows**.
* If the solution is perfectly clean, the system must first spend energy to form a *new* tiny sugar cluster — a **nucleus** — before any growth can occur.

This is exactly what happens in an industrial crystallizer: **supersaturation supplies the energy**, **nucleation makes the seeds**, and **crystal growth enlarges the seeds into usable particles**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Engineering Insight:** The *same* supersaturation drives *both* nucleation and growth. The art of crystallization engineering is to **manipulate this balance** to obtain the desired Crystal Size Distribution (CSD).

### 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics Used Throughout This Module

| Quantity | Symbol | Typical Magnitude | Units |
|----------|--------|-------------------|-------|
| Supersaturation ratio | $S$ | 1.01 – 1.50 | dimensionless |
| Surface free energy | $\gamma$ | 0.01 – 0.10 | **J m⁻²** |
| Molar volume of solute | $V_m$ | 1 × 10⁻⁵ – 5 × 10⁻⁴ | **m³ mol⁻¹** |
| Critical nucleus radius | $r_c$ | 1 – 1000 | **nm** |
| Universal gas constant | $R$ | **8.314** | **J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹** |
| Boltzmann constant | $k$ | **1.381 × 10⁻²³** | **J K⁻¹** |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Master Variable — Supersaturation

Crystallization cannot occur in a saturated or undersaturated solution. The **thermodynamic prerequisite** is **supersaturation**, defined in **three equivalent ways**:

$$S \;=\; \frac{C}{C^*}$$

$$\sigma \;=\; S - 1 \;=\; \frac{C - C^*}{C^*}$$

$$\Delta C \;=\; C - C^*$$

where
* $C$ = current solution concentration (kg solute / kg solvent),
* $C^*$ = equilibrium saturation concentration at the operating temperature.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Terminology:** The 2024 syllabus uses $\sigma$ (relative supersaturation) most often in growth-rate expressions, $S$ in nucleation-rate expressions, and $\Delta C$ in mass-transfer derivations.

### 2.2 The Miers Supersolubility Diagram (Master Map of Crystallization)

On a *concentration vs. temperature* plot three curves exist:

1. **Saturation (Solubility) curve** — thermodynamic equilibrium between dissolved and crystalline solid.
2. **Supersolubility curve** — locus beyond which spontaneous nucleation occurs. It lies *above* the solubility curve.
3. The **meta-stable zone** is the region *between* the two curves — here growth occurs freely but spontaneous nucleation does not.

The **Width of the Meta-stable Zone (MSZW)** is a critical crystallizer-design parameter: a large MSZW allows good control of crystal size, a narrow MSZW does not.

### 2.3 Nucleation — Birth of the Crystal

Nucleation is classified in **two families**:

| Family | Sub-type | Driver | KTU Relevance |
|--------|----------|--------|---------------|
| **Primary Nucleation** | Homogeneous | Spontaneous, in absence of foreign particles | Studied theoretically |
| | Heterogeneous | Catalysed by foreign surfaces/dust | Most industrial systems |
| **Secondary Nucleation** | Attrition, breakage, contact | Existing crystals collide with impeller/walls | Dominates seeded industrial crystallizers |

**Primary homogeneous nucleation rate** (classical CNT — Classical Nucleation Theory):

$$J \;=\; A \exp\!\left(-\frac{\Delta G_c}{kT}\right) \;=\; A \exp\!\left(-\frac{16 \pi \gamma^{3} V_m^{2}}{3 k^{3} T^{3} (\ln S)^{2}}\right)$$

The **exponential sensitivity** to $(\ln S)^2$ is the reason small changes in supersaturation cause huge changes in nucleation rate — the central reason industrial crystallizer control is difficult.

**Heterogeneous nucleation** is described by a contact-angle-dependent factor $\phi$:

$$\Delta G_{c,\text{het}} \;=\; \phi \cdot \Delta G_{c,\text{hom}}, \quad \phi \;=\; \frac{2 - 3\cos\theta + \cos^{3}\theta}{4}, \quad 0 \le \phi \le 1$$

where $\theta$ is the contact angle between nucleus and foreign surface.

**Secondary nucleation** is empirical (power-law form used in KTU problems):

$$B^{0} \;=\; k_{N}\, M_T^{\,j}\, N^{k}\, \Delta C^{n}$$

where $M_T$ is magma (suspension) density, $N$ is impeller speed, $\Delta C$ is supersaturation.

### 2.4 Crystal Growth — Size Enlargement

A two-step **diffusion–integration** mechanism is universally taught:

| Step | Physical Event | Resistance |
|------|----------------|------------|
| 1 | Bulk diffusion of solute through the boundary layer to the crystal face | $1/k_d$ |
| 2 | Surface integration (reaction) of the solute into the crystal lattice | $1/k_r$ |
| **Overall** | Series combination | $\dfrac{1}{K_G} = \dfrac{1}{k_d} + \dfrac{1}{k_r}$ |

**McCabe's $\Delta L$ Law (KTU-favourite, frequently asked):**
*All geometrically similar crystals of a given material suspended in the same solution grow at the same linear rate, independent of their size $L$.*

$$G \;=\; \frac{dL}{dt} \;=\; \text{constant (independent of }L\text{)}$$

This single statement is the foundation of the entire population-balance treatment of crystallizers and is the reason CSD can be mathematically predicted.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Formula Table — Memorise this block; it covers ~70 % of the numerical problems asked in Module 4.**

| # | Equation | LaTeX Form | Key Variables | KTU Use |
|---|----------|------------|---------------|---------|
| 1 | Supersaturation ratio | $S = C / C^{*}$ | $S, C, C^{*}$ | Always |
| 2 | Relative supersaturation | $\sigma = S - 1$ | $\sigma$ | Growth law |
| 3 | Critical nucleus radius | $r_c = 2 \gamma V_m / (R T \ln S)$ | $r_c, \gamma, V_m, S$ | Theory derivations |
| 4 | Critical free-energy barrier | $\Delta G_c = 16 \pi \gamma^{3} V_m^{2} / [3 (RT)^{2} (\ln S)^{2}]$ | $\Delta G_c$ | Nucleation rate |
| 5 | Primary nucleation rate | $J = A \exp[-\Delta G_c / (kT)]$ | $J$ | Derivation / numerical |
| 6 | Heterogeneous factor | $\phi = (2 - 3\cos\theta + \cos^{3}\theta)/4$ | $\phi, \theta$ | Theory |
| 7 | Overall growth rate | $G = K_G\,(C - C^{*})$ | $G, K_G$ | CSD problems |
| 8 | Combined resistance | $1/K_G = 1/k_d + 1/k_r$ | $k_d, k_r$ | Mass-transfer analysis |
| 9 | McCabe $\Delta L$ law | $G = dL/dt = \text{const}$ | — | CSD |
| 10 | Gibbs–Thomson (Kelvin) eqn | $\ln S = 2 \gamma V_m / (r R T)$ | $r$ | Solubility shift at small $r$ |
| 11 | MSMPR population density | $n(L) = n^{0} \exp(-L / (G \tau))$ | $n^{0}, G, \tau$ | Crystallizer design |
| 12 | Dominant crystal size (MSMPR) | $L_D = 3 G \tau$ | $L_D$ | Design question |
| 13 | Yield of crystallization | $Y = \dfrac{C_1 - C_2 (1 - V_2/V_1)}{C_1 - C_2 (1 - V_2/V_1) \cdot \text{stuff}}$ simplified as $Y \approx (C_1 - C_2) V_1$ | — | Mass balance |
| 14 | Crystal yield (hydrate form) | $Y = \dfrac{V_1 C_1 - V_2 C_2 - W (R - 1) / M_s}{1 - (R - 1) W / M_s}$ | $W$ = water removed | Full balance |

> **Anti-corruption reminder:** Within the table cells, $\vert x \vert$ has been written as `\vert x\vert` to avoid breaking markdown pipe-parsing.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

* **Pharmaceutical industry** — production of insulin, monoclonal antibodies, antibiotics (e.g., penicillin-G, erythromycin) as defined solid crystals.
* **Food industry** — sugar refining, salt harvesting, lactose crystallisation from whey.
* **Bioprocessing** — recovery of citric acid, glutamic acid, lysine, xanthan gum.
* **Crystallizer design** — MSMPR, draft-tube baffle (DTB), forced-circulation, Oslo (fluidized-bed) units, all based on the nucleation-growth theory above.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The derivations below are **exam-board complete**. Every algebraic step is shown; no logical jumps. Work through them with pen and paper once and they are permanently owned.

### 3.1 Derivation of the Critical Nucleus Radius $r_c$ (Classical Nucleation Theory)

**Start — physical statement:**
A spherical embryo of radius $r$ is born in a supersaturated solution. Two competing energy terms act on it:

* A **surface term** (energy *cost* of creating new interface) — proportional to $r^{2}$.
* A **volume term** (energy *gain* of forming bulk solid from supersaturated solution) — proportional to $r^{3}$.

Total Gibbs free energy change of forming the embryo:

$$\Delta G \;=\; \Delta G_{\text{surface}} \;+\; \Delta G_{\text{volume}}$$

Step 1 — write each term.
Surface area of a sphere is $4 \pi r^{2}$, so:

$$\Delta G_{\text{surface}} \;=\; 4 \pi r^{2} \gamma$$

where $\gamma$ is the surface (interfacial) free energy per unit area, **J m⁻²**.
Volume of a sphere is $(4/3) \pi r^{3}$, so:

$$\Delta G_{\text{volume}} \;=\; \frac{4}{3} \pi r^{3} \cdot \Delta g_v$$

where $\Delta g_v$ is the free-energy change per unit volume of new solid (J m⁻³).

Step 2 — express $\Delta g_v$ in terms of supersaturation.
For a solute going from a chemical potential $\mu_{\text{soln}}$ to $\mu_{\text{solid}}$, with $\mu_{\text{soln}} = \mu^{0} + RT \ln a$:

$$\Delta g_v \;=\; -\,\frac{R T}{V_m} \ln\!\left(\frac{a}{a^{*}}\right) \;=\; -\,\frac{R T}{V_m} \ln S$$

The minus sign is crucial: in a *supersaturated* solution, $\ln S > 0$, so $\Delta g_v < 0$ (volume term is favourable).

Step 3 — substitute back.

$$\Delta G \;=\; 4 \pi r^{2} \gamma \;-\; \frac{4}{3} \pi r^{3} \frac{R T}{V_m} \ln S$$

Step 4 — find the extremum by differentiating with respect to $r$ and setting the derivative to zero (this locates the critical size where an embryo is in unstable equilibrium with the solution).

$$\frac{d(\Delta G)}{dr} \;=\; 8 \pi r \gamma \;-\; 4 \pi r^{2} \frac{R T}{V_m} \ln S \;=\; 0$$

Step 5 — factor and solve.

$$4 \pi r \left( 2 \gamma \;-\; r \frac{R T}{V_m} \ln S \right) \;=\; 0$$

The non-trivial root gives the **critical radius**:

$$\boxed{\,r_c \;=\; \frac{2 \gamma V_m}{R T \ln S}\,}$$

Step 6 — interpret.
* For $r < r_c$: embryo is *sub-critical*; the surface term dominates; the embryo dissolves.
* For $r > r_c$: embryo is *super-critical*; the volume term dominates; the embryo grows spontaneously.
* This explains the **meta-stable zone** of the Miers diagram: while $S$ exists, embryos $r < r_c$ cannot pass the barrier.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Critical Free-Energy Barrier $\Delta G_c$

Step 1 — substitute $r_c$ back into the master expression for $\Delta G$.

$$\Delta G_c \;=\; 4 \pi r_c^{2} \gamma \;-\; \frac{4}{3} \pi r_c^{3} \frac{R T}{V_m} \ln S$$

Step 2 — recognise that at $r = r_c$ the two terms are related.
From the optimality condition:
$$2 \gamma \;=\; r_c \frac{R T}{V_m} \ln S \;\Rightarrow\; \frac{4}{3} \pi r_c^{3} \frac{R T}{V_m} \ln S \;=\; \frac{2}{3} \cdot 4 \pi r_c^{2} \gamma$$

Step 3 — substitute.

$$\Delta G_c \;=\; 4 \pi r_c^{2} \gamma \;-\; \frac{2}{3} \cdot 4 \pi r_c^{2} \gamma \;=\; \frac{1}{3} \cdot 4 \pi r_c^{2} \gamma$$

So the barrier is **one-third of the surface energy** of the critical cluster. This identity is universal and often tested.

Step 4 — substitute $r_c = 2 \gamma V_m / (R T \ln S)$.

$$\Delta G_c \;=\; \frac{1}{3} \cdot 4 \pi \left(\frac{2 \gamma V_m}{R T \ln S}\right)^{\!2} \gamma$$

$$\boxed{\,\Delta G_c \;=\; \frac{16 \pi \gamma^{3} V_m^{2}}{3 (R T)^{2} (\ln S)^{2}}\,}$$

**Engineering implication:** because $\Delta G_c \propto 1 / (\ln S)^{2}$, a 10 % rise in $S$ can drop the barrier by a factor of 3 – 5, causing the nucleation rate to *explode*.

### 3.3 Derivation of the Combined Mass-Transfer + Surface-Reaction Growth Model

Step 1 — flux by bulk diffusion (Fick's first law through a boundary layer of thickness $\delta$):

$$\dot{m}_d \;=\; k_d\,(C_b - C_i)$$

Step 2 — flux by surface integration (first order in the surface concentration):

$$\dot{m}_r \;=\; k_r\,(C_i - C^{*})$$

Step 3 — at steady state $\dot{m}_d = \dot{m}_r = \dot{m}$. Equate and solve for $C_i$:

$$k_d (C_b - C_i) \;=\; k_r (C_i - C^{*})$$

$$k_d C_b - k_d C_i \;=\; k_r C_i - k_r C^{*}$$

$$C_i (k_d + k_r) \;=\; k_d C_b + k_r C^{*}$$

$$C_i \;=\; \frac{k_d C_b + k_r C^{*}}{k_d + k_r}$$

Step 4 — back-substitute to find the overall flux:

$$\dot{m} \;=\; k_r (C_i - C^{*}) \;=\; k_r \!\left(\frac{k_d C_b + k_r C^{*}}{k_d + k_r} - C^{*}\right)$$

$$= k_r \cdot \frac{k_d C_b + k_r C^{*} - (k_d + k_r) C^{*}}{k_d + k_r} \;=\; k_r \cdot \frac{k_d (C_b - C^{*})}{k_d + k_r}$$

Step 5 — write in the standard form.

$$\boxed{\,\dot{m} \;=\; \frac{k_d k_r}{k_d + k_r} (C_b - C^{*}) \;=\; K_G (C_b - C^{*})\,}$$

with the **overall growth coefficient** satisfying:

$$\frac{1}{K_G} \;=\; \frac{1}{k_d} + \frac{1}{k_r}$$

Step 6 — interpret.
* If $k_d \ll k_r$ (diffusion-controlled, large crystals, viscous solution): $K_G \approx k_d$, growth is mass-transfer limited.
* If $k_r \ll k_d$ (surface-reaction-controlled, very small crystals, low supersaturation): $K_G \approx k_r$, growth is integration-limited.

### 3.4 Derivation of the MSMPR Population Density Function

The **Mixed Suspension Mixed Product Removal** crystallizer is steady, well-mixed, with mean residence time $\tau = V/\dot{V}$. The 1-D population balance under McCabe's $\Delta L$ law:

$$\frac{\partial n}{\partial t} + G \frac{\partial n}{\partial L} + \frac{n}{\tau} \;=\; 0$$

Step 1 — at steady state $\partial n / \partial t = 0$:

$$G \frac{dn}{dL} + \frac{n}{\tau} \;=\; 0$$

Step 2 — separate variables.

$$\frac{dn}{n} \;=\; -\,\frac{dL}{G \tau}$$

Step 3 — integrate from $0$ to $L$, with $n(0) = n^{0}$:

$$\int_{n^{0}}^{n} \frac{dn'}{n'} \;=\; -\frac{1}{G\tau}\!\int_{0}^{L} dL'$$

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{n}{n^{0}}\right) \;=\; -\frac{L}{G\tau}$$

Step 4 — exponentiate.

$$\boxed{\,n(L) \;=\; n^{0} \exp\!\left(-\frac{L}{G \tau}\right)\,}$$

Step 5 — the dominant size (mode of the number-density distribution) is where $dN/dL$ peaks. For an exponential $n(L)$ the dominant size is:

$$L_D \;=\; 3\, G\, \tau$$

This is the KTU-favourite *single-line result* for design.

### 3.5 Yield of Crystallization (Mass-Balanced)

Let $V_1, C_1$ be the initial solution volume and concentration, $V_2, C_2$ the final ones, $W$ the mass of water evaporated, $R$ the moles of water of hydration per mole of solute, $M_s$ the molar mass of the hydrated crystal.

The solute balance (solute leaving solution = solute entering crystals) gives the **final yield** formula:

$$\boxed{\,Y \;=\; \frac{V_1 C_1 - V_2 C_2 - \dfrac{W (R - 1)}{M_s} \cdot \text{(scaled)}}{1 \;-\; \dfrac{W (R - 1) M_s}{M_w}} \;\approx\; \frac{V_1 C_1 - V_2 C_2}{1 - (R-1)W/M_s^{\text{stuff}}}\,}$$

For the **non-hydrate** (anhydrate) case $R = 0$ and the simple engineering form applies:

$$Y \;\approx\; V_1 C_1 - V_2 C_2$$

> **Note to students:** Examiners usually simplify to the anhydrate case. Memorise both, but in 14-mark problems always *write the full balance* before substituting.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Miers Supersolubility Diagram — Engineering Map of Crystallization

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Solution State] --> B{Concentration vs Temperature}
    B -- Below saturation curve --> C[Undersaturated Zone: Crystal dissolves]
    B -- Exactly on saturation curve --> D[Saturated: Equilibrium - No net change]
    B -- Between curves --> E[Meta-stable Zone: Growth only, no spontaneous nucleation]
    B -- Above supersolubility curve --> F[Labile Zone: Spontaneous nucleation dominates]

    E --> E1[Ideal for seeded industrial crystallization]
    E --> E2[McCabe Delta-L law valid here]
    F --> F1[Uncontrolled nuclei - fines, poor CSD]
    F --> F2[High J, low G - many small crystals]

    style A fill:#E0F2FE,stroke:#0369A1,stroke-width:2px
    style C fill:#DCFCE7,stroke:#166534
    style D fill:#FEF9C3,stroke:#854D0E
    style E fill:#FFE4E6,stroke:#9F1239,stroke-width:3px
    style F fill:#FECACA,stroke:#7F1D1D,stroke-width:3px
    style E1 fill:#FFFFFF,stroke:#9F1239
    style E2 fill:#FFFFFF,stroke:#9F1239
    style F1 fill:#FFFFFF,stroke:#7F1D1D
    style F2 fill:#FFFFFF,stroke:#7F1D1D
```

### 4.2 Nucleation Mechanism Classification

```mermaid
graph TD
    N[Nucleation] --> P[Primary Nucleation]
    N --> S[Secondary Nucleation]

    P --> PH[Homogeneous]
    P --> PHT[Heterogeneous]

    PH --> PH1[In absence of foreign surfaces]
    PH --> PH2[High energy barrier - rare in industry]
    PH --> PH3[Classical CNT applies]

    PHT --> PHT1[Foreign particle lowers barrier]
    PHT --> PHT2[Shape factor phi modifies Delta Gc]
    PHT --> PHT3[Most industrial cases]

    S --> S1[Attrition - crystals break on impeller]
    S --> S2[Contact nucleation - crystal-wall impact]
    S --> S3[Shear-induced - fluid shear peels fragments]
    S --> S4[Dominates in seeded crystallizers]

    style N fill:#DBEAFE,stroke:#1E3A8A,stroke-width:2px
    style P fill:#FEF3C7,stroke:#854D0E
    style S fill:#FCE7F3,stroke:#9D174D
    style PH fill:#FFFFFF,stroke:#854D0E
    style PHT fill:#FFFFFF,stroke:#854D0E
    style S1 fill:#FFFFFF,stroke:#9D174D
    style S2 fill:#FFFFFF,stroke:#9D174D
    style S3 fill:#FFFFFF,stroke:#9D174D
    style S4 fill:#FFFFFF,stroke:#9D174D
    style PH1 fill:#F9FAFB
    style PH2 fill:#F9FAFB
    style PH3 fill:#F9FAFB
    style PHT1 fill:#F9FAFB
    style PHT2 fill:#F9FAFB
    style PHT3 fill:#F9FAFB
```

### 4.3 The Two-Step Crystal Growth Pathway (Diffusion + Surface Integration)

```mermaid
graph LR
    SOL[Bulk Supersaturated Solution] -- Convection --> BL[Hydrodynamic Boundary Layer]
    BL -- Molecular Diffusion, kd --> SF[Crystal Surface, conc Ci]
    SF -- Surface Integration, kr --> LAT[Crystal Lattice, C-star]
    LAT -- Incorporation --> CRYST[Growing Crystal Face]

    SOL -.-> SOL1[Cb bulk]
    BL -.-> BL1[delta - boundary layer thickness]
    SF -.-> SF1[Ci - intermediate concentration]
    LAT -.-> LAT1[C-star - equilibrium]

    style SOL fill:#DBEAFE,stroke:#1E3A8A
    style BL fill:#FEF9C3,stroke:#854D0E
    style SF fill:#FDE68A,stroke:#854D0E
    style LAT fill:#BBF7D0,stroke:#166534
    style CRYST fill:#A7F3D0,stroke:#166534,stroke-width:3px
    style SOL1 fill:#FFFFFF
    style BL1 fill:#FFFFFF
    style SF1 fill:#FFFFFF
    style LAT1 fill:#FFFFFF
```

### 4.4 MSMPR Crystallizer Functional Block Flow (Population Balance View)

```mermaid
graph TD
    IN[Feed Solution: V_dot, Cin, T_in] --> MIX[Perfectly Mixed Vessel Volume V]
    MIX -- Nucleation J --> NUC[New Crystals born n0]
    NUC -- Growth G --> GR[Crystals of size 0 to L_max]
    GR -- Suspension --> OUT[Magma Out: V_dot, C, nL]
    MIX -- Residence time tau --> TBL[tau = V slash V_dot]
    TBL --> POP[Population Density nL = n0 exp -L over G tau]
    POP --> DM[Dominant Size LD = 3 G tau]

    style IN fill:#E0F2FE,stroke:#0369A1
    style MIX fill:#FEF3C7,stroke:#854D0E,stroke-width:2px
    style NUC fill:#FECACA,stroke:#7F1D1D
    style GR fill:#BBF7D0,stroke:#166534
    style OUT fill:#E0E7FF,stroke:#3730A3
    style TBL fill:#FFFFFF
    style POP fill:#FFFFFF
    style DM fill:#FDE68A,stroke:#854D0E,stroke-width:3px
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> The question bank below mirrors the **KTU 2024 Scheme End-Semester Evaluation (ESE) pattern**: short 3-mark items in Part A and a 14-mark internal-choice question in Part B with two 7-mark sub-parts. Each sub-part carries an exhaustive model answer.

---

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]`
**Define the "meta-stable zone" of supersaturation. Why is it important in industrial crystallization?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The **meta-stable zone** is the region of the supersaturation diagram that lies **between the saturation (solubility) curve and the supersolubility curve**.
> Within this region, the solution is supersaturated but **spontaneous nucleation does not occur**; if seed crystals are added, they will grow steadily without the formation of new nuclei.
> Industrial importance:
> 1. It defines the **safe operating window** for seeded crystallizers, allowing controlled crystal growth and uniform CSD.
> 2. It governs the **mode of cooling / evaporation / anti-solvent addition** so that the system does not cross into the labile zone and produce excessive fines.
> 3. The **width of the meta-stable zone (MSZW)** is a measurable design parameter used to select cooling profiles and agitator speeds.

#### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]`
**State and explain McCabe's $\Delta L$ law. Mention one situation where it does not hold.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **McCabe's $\Delta L$ Law:** *All geometrically similar crystals of the same material, suspended in the same solution and growing under the same hydrodynamic conditions, grow at the same linear rate, independent of their size.*
> Mathematically: $G = dL/dt = \text{constant (independent of }L\text{)}$.
> **Why it holds:** The growth is limited by **surface integration**, where the rate per unit face area is constant for a given supersaturation; therefore the rate of advance of a face is the same whether the crystal is 10 µm or 1 mm.
> **Where it fails:** When growth is strongly **diffusion-controlled** in a viscous or unstirred medium — large crystals experience a thicker effective boundary layer and grow more slowly. Also fails when crystals of very different sizes have markedly different surface defect densities (size-dependent growth).

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Pitfall Callout)**
> • **Do NOT confuse** supersaturation $S$ with relative supersaturation $\sigma$ — they appear in different equations.
> • **Always state units** with the final answer in numerical problems (commonly $J$ in nuclei m⁻³ s⁻¹, $G$ in m s⁻¹, $L$ in m or µm).
> • **Always show the substitution step** into McCabe/derivation expressions — KTU examiners award the *substitution mark* separately from the *final answer mark*.
> • **Do not skip the meta-stable-zone definition** in 14-mark questions on supersaturation; it is the bridge between theory and practice.
> • **Heterogeneous vs homogeneous** nucleation must be clearly distinguished — the contact-angle factor $\phi$ is the discriminator.

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Jul 2024, Model]`

**(a)** *Derive an expression for the critical radius $r_c$ of a spherical nucleus formed in a supersaturated solution. Discuss how $r_c$ depends on supersaturation and temperature. State its engineering significance.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *A cooling crystallizer is used to crystallise **sodium acetate trihydrate (NaC₂H₃O₂·3H₂O, $M_s = 136$ g mol⁻¹)** from an aqueous solution. Initially 1000 L of solution contains 35 % (w/w) sodium acetate. The solution is cooled from 60 °C to 20 °C. Solubility of sodium acetate at 20 °C is 0.46 g per g of water. Density of the initial solution is 1.15 g cm⁻³. Calculate the **mass of crystals** obtained, assuming the crystals form as the **tri-hydrate** ($R = 3$ moles of water of hydration per mole of solute, $M_w = 18$ g mol⁻¹). Assume water lost by evaporation is negligible.* **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution — Question A

**Part (a) — Derivation of $r_c$ (7 marks)**

The standard derivation as in Section 3.1 is expected. Awarding map:

| Step | Marks |
|------|-------|
| Statement of total free energy $\Delta G = 4\pi r^{2}\gamma + (4/3)\pi r^{3}\Delta g_v$ | 1 |
| Expression $\Delta g_v = -RT \ln S / V_m$ with sign explanation | 1 |
| $d\Delta G / dr = 0$ differentiation | 1 |
| Solving for $r_c = 2 \gamma V_m / (RT \ln S)$ | 1 |
| Discussion — inverse dependence on $\ln S$ and on $T$ | 1 |
| Discussion — engineering significance (meta-stable zone, seeding strategy) | 1 |
| Final boxed answer & correct units (m) | 1 |

> $r_c = \dfrac{2 \gamma V_m}{R T \ln S}$ — the critical radius *decreases* as supersaturation or temperature rises, hence sub-critical embryos are more easily pushed past the barrier at high $S$ or high $T$. Engineering significance: by controlling $S$ and $T$ one can deliberately choose to be in the meta-stable zone (growth-dominated) or the labile zone (nucleation-dominated) — the very basis of seeded-crystallizer design.

**Part (b) — Mass balance (7 marks)**

Step 1 — initial mass of solution:

$$m_1 \;=\; V_1 \rho_1 \;=\; 1000~\text{L} \times 1.15~\text{kg L}^{-1} \;=\; 1150~\text{kg}$$

Step 2 — composition of initial solution (35 % w/w NaOAc):

$$m_{\text{NaOAc},1} \;=\; 0.35 \times 1150 \;=\; 402.5~\text{kg}$$

$$m_{\text{water},1} \;=\; 0.65 \times 1150 \;=\; 747.5~\text{kg}$$

Step 3 — solubility at 20 °C is 0.46 g NaOAc per g water, so the *final* solution (mother liquor) contains:

$$m_{\text{NaOAc},2} \;=\; 0.46 \times m_{\text{water},2}$$

Step 4 — water balance. Because evaporation is negligible, all water is in mother liquor + hydrate.

Each mole of NaOAc crystallised carries $R = 3$ moles of water. Per kg of NaOAc the associated water mass is:

$$m_{\text{water, hydrate}} \;=\; m_{\text{NaOAc, cryst}} \times \frac{R M_w}{M_s} \;=\; m_{\text{NaOAc, cryst}} \times \frac{3 \times 18}{136} \;=\; 0.3971\, m_{\text{NaOAc, cryst}}$$

Let $Y$ = mass of NaOAc crystallised (kg). Then:

$$m_{\text{water, mother liquor}} \;=\; 747.5 - 0.3971\, Y$$

Step 5 — solute balance.

$$402.5 - Y \;=\; 0.46 \times (747.5 - 0.3971\, Y)$$

$$402.5 - Y \;=\; 343.85 - 0.1827\, Y$$

$$402.5 - 343.85 \;=\; Y - 0.1827\, Y \;=\; 0.8173\, Y$$

$$Y \;=\; \frac{58.65}{0.8173} \;=\; 71.76~\text{kg NaOAc crystallised}$$

Step 6 — convert to mass of tri-hydrate crystals:

$$m_{\text{crystals}} \;=\; Y \times \frac{M_s + R M_w}{M_s} \;=\; 71.76 \times \frac{136 + 3 \times 18}{136} \;=\; 71.76 \times \frac{190}{136} \;=\; 100.27~\text{kg}$$

> **Final Answer:** Mass of sodium acetate tri-hydrate crystals ≈ **100.3 kg** (7 marks).

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]`

**(a)** *Explain the **two-step crystal growth** mechanism. Derive the overall growth coefficient $K_G$ in terms of the diffusion coefficient $k_d$ and the surface-reaction coefficient $k_r$. State the limiting forms under diffusion-control and surface-reaction-control.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *A continuous **MSMPR crystallizer** operates at steady state with mean residence time $\tau = 2$ h. The linear growth rate is $G = 5 \times 10^{-8}$ m s⁻¹. The population density at $L = 0$ is $n^{0} = 10^{15}$ m⁻⁴. Determine:
*(i) the population density at $L = 200$ µm,*
*(ii) the dominant crystal size,*
*(iii) the number of crystals per unit volume in the size range 0 – 200 µm.* **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution — Question B

**Part (a) — Two-step growth + combined coefficient (7 marks)**

| Step | Marks |
|------|-------|
| Statement of the two steps: bulk diffusion + surface integration | 1 |
| Fick's law: $\dot m_d = k_d (C_b - C_i)$ | 1 |
| Surface reaction: $\dot m_r = k_r (C_i - C^{*})$ | 1 |
| Steady-state equality and elimination of $C_i$ | 2 |
| Final $1/K_G = 1/k_d + 1/k_r$ | 1 |
| Limiting forms: $K_G \to k_d$ when $k_d \ll k_r$ (diffusion-control); $K_G \to k_r$ when $k_r \ll k_d$ (surface-control) | 1 |

> **Result:** $K_G = k_d k_r / (k_d + k_r)$ (overall growth coefficient).

**Part (b) — MSMPR numerical (7 marks)**

Convert units first: $\tau = 2~\text{h} = 7200~\text{s}$, so $G\tau = 5 \times 10^{-8} \times 7200 = 3.6 \times 10^{-4}~\text{m} = 360~\mu\text{m}$.

(i) Population density at $L = 200~\mu\text{m} = 2 \times 10^{-4}~\text{m}$:

$$n(L) \;=\; n^{0} \exp\!\left(-\frac{L}{G\tau}\right) \;=\; 10^{15} \exp\!\left(-\frac{2 \times 10^{-4}}{3.6 \times 10^{-4}}\right)$$

$$= 10^{15} \exp(-0.5556) \;=\; 10^{15} \times 0.5738 \;=\; 5.74 \times 10^{14}~\text{m}^{-4}$$

> $n(200~\mu\text{m}) \approx 5.74 \times 10^{14}~\text{m}^{-4}$ **(2 marks)**.

(ii) Dominant crystal size:

$$L_D \;=\; 3 G \tau \;=\; 3 \times 3.6 \times 10^{-4}~\text{m} \;=\; 1.08 \times 10^{-3}~\text{m} \;=\; 1080~\mu\text{m}$$

> $L_D = 1.08~\text{mm}$ **(2 marks)**.

(iii) Number of crystals in $0 \le L \le 200~\mu\text{m}$ per unit volume:

$$N_{0\to 200} \;=\; \int_{0}^{L} n^{0} \exp\!\left(-\frac{L'}{G\tau}\right) dL' \;=\; n^{0}\, G\tau \left[1 - \exp\!\left(-\frac{L}{G\tau}\right)\right]$$

$$= 10^{15} \times 3.6 \times 10^{-4} \times (1 - 0.5738)$$

$$= 3.6 \times 10^{11} \times 0.4262 \;=\; 1.534 \times 10^{11}~\text{m}^{-3}$$

> $N_{0\to 200} \approx 1.53 \times 10^{11}~\text{crystals m}^{-3}$ **(3 marks)**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Block — Read Twice Before the Exam**

* [ ] **Crystallization is driven by supersaturation** — without it nothing happens.
* [ ] Three equivalent forms of supersaturation: $S = C/C^{*}$, $\sigma = S-1$, $\Delta C = C-C^{*}$.
* [ ] **Miers diagram:** undersaturated → saturated → meta-stable → labile; MSZW is the design window.
* [ ] Nucleation is of two families: **primary (homogeneous, heterogeneous)** and **secondary**.
* [ ] Heterogeneous nucleation: free-energy barrier reduced by factor $\phi = (2 - 3\cos\theta + \cos^{3}\theta)/4$.
* [ ] Critical radius: $r_c = 2\gamma V_m / (R T \ln S)$ — **decreases** with $S$ and $T$.
* [ ] Critical free-energy barrier: $\Delta G_c = 16 \pi \gamma^{3} V_m^{2} / [3 (RT)^{2} (\ln S)^{2}]$ — equals $\frac{1}{3} \times$ surface energy of the critical cluster.
* [ ] Primary nucleation rate: $J = A \exp[-\Delta G_c / (kT)]$ — extremely sensitive to $\ln S$.
* [ ] Secondary nucleation (industrial): $B^{0} = k_N M_T^{\,j} N^{k} \Delta C^{n}$ — *empirical power-law*.
* [ ] Crystal growth is a **two-step** process — diffusion + surface integration.
* [ ] Combined resistance: $1/K_G = 1/k_d + 1/k_r$.
* [ ] **McCabe's $\Delta L$ law**: $G = dL/dt = \text{const}$ — *the* foundation of CSD analysis.
* [ ] **Kelvin (Gibbs–Thomson) equation**: $\ln S = 2 \gamma V_m / (r R T)$ — solubility rises for very small particles.
* [ ] **MSMPR population balance:** $n(L) = n^{0} \exp[-L / (G \tau)]$; dominant size $L_D = 3 G \tau$.
* [ ] **Yield formula (hydrate)**: $Y = [V_1 C_1 - V_2 C_2] / [1 - (R-1)W/M_s^{\text{stuff}}]$; for anhydrate $Y \approx V_1 C_1 - V_2 C_2$.
* [ ] Engineering use cases: **pharma proteins, antibiotics, sugar, salt, citric acid, amino acids**.
* [ ] Industrial crystallizer types to recognise: **MSMPR, DTB, forced-circulation, Oslo (fluidised-bed)**.
* [ ] Examiner expects *derivation steps written out*, not just the final formula.
* [ ] Units: $J$ in m⁻³ s⁻¹; $G$ in m s⁻¹; $L$ in m or µm; $K_G$ in m s⁻¹.
* [ ] Don't mix $S$ (dimensionless) with $\sigma$ (also dimensionless but used in growth law).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Batch Crystallizers, Process Crystallization of Proteins, and Recrystallization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT525 - BIOSEPARATION TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Batch Crystallizers, Process Crystallization of Proteins, and Recrystallization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4 — Batch Crystallizers, Process Crystallization of Proteins, and Recrystallization

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Batch Crystallizer — Formal Definition
A **batch crystallizer** is a closed, non-steady-state crystallization vessel in which a fixed mass of solution is charged, brought to supersaturation by a programmed change in a process variable (temperature, solvent composition, or anti-solvent/precipitant addition), allowed to undergo controlled **nucleation and crystal growth**, and finally discharged for filtration, washing, and drying. The entire operation is repeated in discrete "batches" rather than continuous feed/discharge.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Batch crystallization is the *dominant industrial mode* for pharmaceutical proteins, fine chemicals, and bio-actives because it allows tight control over the **metastable zone**, **polymorphic form**, and **product purity** — three things that downstream drug approval (FDA/EMA) critically depends on.

### 1.2 Process Crystallization of Proteins — Formal Definition
**Process crystallization of proteins** is the controlled, large-scale induction of ordered solid-state aggregation of purified protein molecules from a supersaturated aqueous mother liquor, achieved by manipulating physical (temperature, concentration) and chemical (pH, ionic strength, precipitant type) parameters so that the protein rejects the disordered bulk solvent phase and packs into a 3-D lattice.

### 1.3 Recrystallization — Formal Definition
**Recrystallization** is a purification technique in which an impure crystalline solid is re-dissolved in a minimum volume of a *fresh* (or recycled) hot solvent and re-precipitated under controlled cooling, with the goal of leaving the impurities behind in the mother liquor while the purified crystals are recovered. Each recrystallization step multiplies purity but sacrifices a fraction of the yield — a fundamental **yield–purity trade-off** of downstream processing.

---

### 1.4 Intuitive Analogy — "The Sugar, the Snowflake, and the Honey Jar"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: Crystallization = Snow Making in a Cloud**
> Imagine a cloud full of water vapor. The vapor is "dissolved water" in air (analogous to a **supersaturated solution**). When temperature drops, water must leave the air and form ice — but it cannot form everywhere at once. Tiny "seeds" of dust act as **nuclei**, and water stacks on these seeds as **growth**. A **batch crystallizer** is a controlled cloud chamber: you add the vapor (charge), you cool the chamber (create supersaturation), and you let the snowflakes (crystals) grow for a fixed time before harvesting.
>
> **For proteins** — picture a bee's honeycomb. The bees (protein molecules) refuse to swim in pure water; they want to huddle together in a tight hexagonal arrangement. We give them the right *trigger* (a pinch of salt, PEG, or a temperature drop) to encourage them to huddle. A **batch protein crystallizer** is the controlled hive where the bees are gently nudged into a perfect lattice.
>
> **Recrystallization** is like melting and refreezing ice. The first freeze traps some impurities (twigs, dust) inside. If you melt the ice, the impurities dissolve more easily than the pure water, so refreezing gives you cleaner ice. Repeat this and the ice becomes pure — but every melt loses a little water (yield loss).

---

### 1.5 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Metric | Typical Value | Units | Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Solubility of a typical protein | **0.1 – 50** | mg/mL | Sets the upper limit of working concentration |
| Induction time for protein crystals | **hours – weeks** | h | Determines batch residence time |
| Optimal supersaturation (S = C/C\*) | **1.05 – 1.5** | dimensionless | Prevents amorphous vs crystalline path |
| Operating pH for protein crystallization | **4.5 – 9.0** | pH units | Near pI minimizes solubility |
| McCabe's ΔL law constant | size-independent growth | — | Geometric similarity of crystals |
| Saturation temperature (T_sat) | solute-specific | °C | Onset of first nucleation |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
