# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principles of a Batch Crystallizer

A batch crystallization cycle consists of **four sequential regimes**:

1. **Charging & Heating/Dissolution Phase** — Solid feed is dissolved completely at high T or in pure solvent; the solution is at *undersaturation* (C < C\*).
2. **Supersaturation Generation Phase** — T is lowered (cooling), solvent is evaporated, or anti-solvent/precipitant is dosed; C exceeds C\* and the system enters the **metastable zone** (nucleation does NOT occur spontaneously).
3. **Nucleation & Growth Phase** — Once S exceeds the *metastable limit*, primary nucleation bursts and crystals appear; thereafter both nucleation and growth proceed until S falls back to the equilibrium solubility curve.
4. **Aging/Ostwald Ripening Phase** — Small crystals dissolve and large ones grow further; final size distribution is set.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Yield–Purity Trade-off:** A faster cooling rate → more nuclei → smaller crystals → higher surface area → higher *impurity entrainment* → lower purity. A slower cooling → fewer, larger crystals → higher purity → potentially lower yield due to incomplete desupersaturation.

---

## 2.2 Governing Equations

### 2.2.1 Supersaturation Ratio
The driving force for both nucleation and growth is the **supersaturation ratio**:

$$S \;=\; \frac{C}{C^{\ast}}$$

where $C$ is the actual solution concentration and $C^{\ast}$ is the equilibrium solubility at the current temperature.

Useful alternative forms (used in KTU numericals):

$$\Delta C \;=\; C - C^{\ast} \quad\quad \sigma \;=\; S - 1 \;=\; \frac{\Delta C}{C^{\ast}}$$

### 2.2.2 Yield Equation for a Cooling Batch Crystallizer
For a simple cooling batch with no evaporation:

$$Y \;=\; V_{solvent}\,\big(C_{1} - C_{2}\big) \;\approx\; m_{s}\,\big(C_{1} - C_{2}\big)$$

- $C_1$ = concentration at the start (hot, fully dissolved)
- $C_2$ = concentration at the end (cold, mother liquor after crystallization)
- $m_s$ = mass of solvent (kg)
- $Y$ = mass of crystals produced (kg)

If solute has water of crystallization or hydrate form, the yield must be corrected with the ratio of molecular weights.

### 2.2.3 Primary Nucleation Rate (Classical)
$$B^{0} \;=\; A \,\exp\!\left(-\frac{B_{1}}{(\ln S)^{2}}\right)$$

$B^0$ = primary nucleation rate (nuclei / m³·s); $A, B_1$ = empirical constants; $S$ = supersaturation ratio.

### 2.2.4 Crystal Growth Rate (Size-Dependent Power Law)
$$\dot{L} \;=\; G \;=\; k_{g}\,\Delta C^{\,g} \;=\; k_{g}\,C^{\ast\,g}\,(S-1)^{g}$$

- $g \approx 1$ for diffusion-controlled, $g \approx 2$ for surface-integration controlled.
- $k_g$ = growth rate constant (m/s · (kg/m³)$^{-g}$).

### 2.2.5 Population Balance Equation (PBE) for a Batch
The PBE couples the crystal size distribution (CSD) to growth and nucleation:

$$\frac{\partial n}{\partial t} \;+\; \frac{\partial (G\,n)}{\partial L} \;=\; 0 \quad \text{with B.C. } n(0,t) \;=\; \frac{B^{0}}{G}$$

- $n(L,t)$ = population density (number of crystals of size $L$ per unit size per m³).
- $B^0(t)$ = nucleation rate feeding the smallest size class.
- For size-independent growth ($G$ independent of $L$), the McCabe $\Delta L$ law holds and an analytical solution is possible.

### 2.2.6 McCabe's ΔL Law (Geometric Similarity)
All crystals of the same material grow at the *same linear rate* regardless of size:

$$\frac{dL}{dt} \;=\; G \;=\; \text{constant for all } L$$

Consequence: the **shape factor** (e.g., volumetric shape factor $k_v$ where $V = k_v L^3$) and the **CSD shape** are preserved during growth.

### 2.2.7 Mass Balance (Total Crystal Mass)
$$M_{crystal}(t) \;=\; k_{v}\,\rho_{c}\!\int_{0}^{\infty} n(L,t)\,L^{3}\,dL \;=\; m_{s}\big(C_{0} - C(t)\big)$$

### 2.2.8 Recrystallization — Purity & Recovery
$$\%\,\text{Purity} \;=\; \frac{m_{\text{pure crystals}}}{m_{\text{total crystals}}}\times 100$$

$$\%\,\text{Recovery} \;=\; \frac{m_{\text{recovered}}\times \%\,\text{Purity}_{\text{after}}}{m_{\text{initial}}\times \%\,\text{Purity}_{\text{before}}}\times 100$$

The cumulative purity after $N$ recrystallization stages (assuming equal impurity partition $\beta$ per stage):

$$P_{N} \;=\; \frac{P_{0}}{(1-\beta)^{N}\,P_{0} + \beta^{N}\,(1-P_{0})}$$

For a small initial impurity $1 - P_0 \ll 1$, this simplifies to:

$$P_{N} \;\approx\; 1 - (1-\beta)^{N}(1-P_{0})$$

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Description / Where Used | Key Variables | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $S = C/C^{\ast}$ | Supersaturation ratio | $C$ = actual conc., $C^{\ast}$ = solubility | dimensionless |
| 2 | $\sigma = S - 1$ | Relative supersaturation | — | dimensionless |
| 3 | $Y = m_s(C_1 - C_2)$ | Yield from cooling batch | $m_s$ = solvent mass | kg |
| 4 | $B^0 = A\exp\!\left(-B_1/(\ln S)^2\right)$ | Primary nucleation | $A, B_1$ = empirical | #/m³·s |
| 5 | $G = k_g \Delta C^g$ | Growth rate (power law) | $k_g, g$ = constants | m/s |
| 6 | $\partial n/\partial t + \partial(Gn)/\partial L = 0$ | Population balance (batch) | $n(L,t)$ population density | #/m³·m |
| 7 | $n(0,t) = B^0/G$ | Boundary condition at $L=0$ | — | #/m³ |
| 8 | $dL/dt = G$ (constant) | McCabe's $\Delta L$ law | — | m/s |
| 9 | $V = k_v L^3$ | Crystal volume from size | $k_v$ = vol. shape factor | m³ |
| 10 | $M = k_v \rho_c \int n L^3 dL$ | Crystal mass from CSD | $\rho_c$ = crystal density | kg |
| 11 | $\%P = m_{\text{pure}}/m_{\text{total}}\times100$ | Recrystallization purity | — | % |
| 12 | $P_N = P_0/[ (1-\beta)^N P_0 + \beta^N (1-P_0) ]$ | Purity after N recrystallizations | $\beta$ = impurity partition | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** These equations drive the design of API (Active Pharmaceutical Ingredient) plants at companies like *Dr. Reddy's, Cipla, Biocon, and Pfizer*. The PBE is solved numerically in commercial simulators (e.g., **gCRYSTAL**, **Parsival**, **gPROMS**) for in-silico scale-up of batch crystallizers from 100 mL lab to 10,000 L production.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Yield Equation for an Adiabatic Cooling Batch Crystallizer

**Statement to derive:** Show that the mass of crystals recovered from a cooling batch is $Y = m_s (C_1 - C_2)$.

**Step 1 — Initial mass balance (before cooling).**
The crystallizer is charged with solvent mass $m_s$ and dissolved solute mass $m_0$. The initial concentration is therefore:

$$C_{1} \;=\; \frac{m_{0}}{m_{s}}$$

**Step 2 — Solubility at final temperature $T_2$.**
At the lower temperature $T_2$, the solubility of the solute is $C^{\ast}_2$. The mother liquor at equilibrium with crystals holds solute at this solubility:

$$C_{2} \;=\; C^{\ast}_{2}$$

**Step 3 — Mass of solute leaving the solution.**
Since the system is closed (no evaporation, no feed), all the solute that has left the liquid phase must be in the crystalline phase:

$$Y \;=\; m_{0} \;-\; m_{2} \;=\; m_{0} \;-\; m_{s}\,C_{2}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $m_0 = m_s C_1$.**

$$Y \;=\; m_{s}\,C_{1} \;-\; m_{s}\,C_{2} \;=\; m_{s}\,(C_{1} - C_{2})$$

This is the standard yield equation used in **all** KTU numericals on cooling crystallization. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Analytical Solution of the Batch PBE under McCabe's $\Delta L$ Law

**Statement to derive:** For a batch with size-independent growth $G$ and nucleation $B^0$ only at $L=0$, obtain the population density $n(L,t)$.

**Step 1 — Write the PBE for size-independent growth.**

Because $G$ is independent of $L$, $\partial(Gn)/\partial L = G\,\partial n/\partial L$, so the PBE simplifies to a first-order hyperbolic PDE:

$$\frac{\partial n}{\partial t} \;+\; G\,\frac{\partial n}{\partial L} \;=\; 0$$

**Step 2 — Apply the boundary condition at $L = 0$.**
Newborn crystals enter the size distribution at the origin:

$$n(0,t) \;=\; \frac{B^{0}(t)}{G(t)}$$

**Step 3 — Method of characteristics.**
Consider a crystal that was born at $L = 0$ at time $t_0$. Its trajectory in the $(L,t)$ plane obeys $dL/dt = G$. Integrating, the size of this crystal at time $t$ is:

$$L(t) \;=\; \int_{t_{0}}^{t} G(t')\,dt' \;=\; \bar{G}\,(t - t_{0})$$

where $\bar{G}$ is the average growth rate over the interval.

**Step 4 — Back-substitute to obtain $n(L,t)$.**
The value of $n$ along the characteristic is constant (it equals the boundary value at $t_0$):

$$n(L,t) \;=\; n(0, t_{0}) \;=\; \frac{B^{0}(t_{0})}{G(t_{0})}$$

But $t_0 = t - L/\bar{G}$, hence:

$$n(L,t) \;=\; \frac{B^{0}\!\left(t - \dfrac{L}{\bar{G}}\right)}{G\!\left(t - \dfrac{L}{\bar{G}}\right)}$$

**Step 5 — Special case of constant $B^0$ and $G$.**
If both are constant in time (idealized), then $\bar{G} = G$ and:

$$n(L,t) \;=\; \frac{B^{0}}{G} \quad \text{for } 0 \le L \le G\,t, \qquad n = 0 \text{ for } L > G\,t$$

This is the **rectangular CSD** characteristic of an unseeded batch crystallizer under constant $\Delta L$ growth. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Solubility–Temperature Coupling and the Cooling Strategy

**Statement to derive:** Show that a *linear* cooling profile $T(t) = T_0 - q\,t$ gives a parabolic supersaturation ramp when solubility obeys the van't Hoff relation $C^{\ast}(T) = C_0^{\ast}\exp(-\Delta H_{sol}/RT)$.

**Step 1 — Express $C^{\ast}$ as a function of $t$.**

$$C^{\ast}(t) \;=\; C_{0}^{\ast}\exp\!\left(-\frac{\Delta H_{sol}}{R\,(T_{0} - q\,t)}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Approximate for small $q\,t \ll T_0$.**
Taylor-expanding $\exp(1/T)$ around $T = T_0$:

$$C^{\ast}(t) \;\approx\; C_{0}^{\ast}\exp\!\left(-\frac{\Delta H_{sol}}{RT_{0}}\right)\left[1 - \frac{\Delta H_{sol}\,q\,t}{R\,T_{0}^{2}}\right]$$

**Step 3 — Define initial supersaturation $\sigma_0 = 0$ (solution is saturated at $T_0$).**
The actual concentration is constant $C = C^{\ast}(T_0) = C_0^{\ast}\exp(-\Delta H_{sol}/RT_0)$. The instantaneous relative supersaturation is:

$$\sigma(t) \;=\; \frac{C}{C^{\ast}(t)} - 1 \;\approx\; \frac{\Delta H_{sol}\,q\,t}{R\,T_{0}^{2}}$$

Hence the **supersaturation rises linearly with time** for a linear cooling ramp. This is the basis of the *controlled cooling* strategy used in industrial protein crystallizers to keep $S$ inside the metastable zone. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Method Selection for Protein Crystallization

| Method | Driving Force | Typical Application | Pros | Cons |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Batch (micro-batch)** | Mixing protein + precipitant directly | Initial screening (Hampton Index) | Tiny protein requirement | Hard to control nucleation |
| **Vapor diffusion** (hanging/sitting drop) | Concentrates both protein & precipitant | High-throughput screening (96-well) | Self-optimizing | Slow (days–weeks) |
| **Dialysis** | Selective removal of small ions | Gentle, large protein complexes | Mild, low-shear | Slow equilibration |
| **Free-interface diffusion** | Counter-diffusion in capillary | Membrane proteins, in meso | Allows in-situ X-ray | Hard to scale |
| **Seeded batch** | Growth on added nuclei | Industrial scale (insulin, catalase) | Reproducible CSD | Requires high-quality seed |
| **Precipitant: Salting-out (AS)** | Decreases protein solubility | Robust, cheap | High ionic strength can denature | — |
| **Precipitant: PEG** | Excluded-volume effect | Large proteins, complexes | Mild, biocompatible | Viscosity issues |
| **Precipitant: MPD / organic** | Reduces dielectric constant | Hydrophobic proteins | Good for membrane proteins | May unfold proteins |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse *salting-out* (Hofmeister series) with *salting-in*. Salting-out **decreases** protein solubility at high salt concentration; salting-in **increases** it at low concentration. The two regimes are separated by a solubility minimum.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture — Batch Cooling Crystallizer (Protein / Fine Chemical)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Step 1: CHARGE Vessel with Solvent and Feed Solute] --> B[Step 2: HEAT to Dissolution Temperature Tdiss]
    B --> C[Step 3: OPTIONAL - Add Decolorizing Charcoal and Hot Filter]
    C --> D[Step 4: COOL along Programmed Profile T over Right Bracket t = 0 to t = tend]
    D --> E[Step 5: OPTIONAL - Add Seed Crystals at S in Metastable Zone]
    E --> F[Step 6: AGE Right Bracket Ostwald Ripening Right Bracket for Tres]
    F --> G[Step 7: SLURRY Discharge to Filter or Centrifuge]
    G --> H[Step 8: WASH Crystals on Filter with Cold Solvent]
    H --> I[Step 9: DRY in Vacuum or Fluidized Bed Dryer]
    I --> J[Step 10: MILL or Sieve to Final PSD]
    J --> K[FINAL Crystalline Product to Packaging]

    subgraph CONTROL_LOOP[Process Control Loop]
        C1[PT - Temperature Transmitter] --> C2[PID Controller]
        C2 --> C3[TCV - Cooling Water Valve]
        C3 --> D
    end

    subgraph ANALYTICS[Inline / Atline Analytics]
        A1[FBRM - Focused Beam Reflectance Measures Particle Chord Length] --> F
        A2[ATR FTIR - Measures Solution Concentration] --> D
        A3[Particle View Probe - Captures Crystal Morphology] --> E
    end
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture — Process Crystallization of Proteins (McPherson's Workflow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Step 1: Purify Protein to Homogeneity by Chromatography] --> P2[Step 2: Buffer Exchange into Crystallization Buffer at pH near pI]
    P2 --> P3[Step 3: Concentrate to 5 to 20 mg per mL by Ultrafiltration]
    P3 --> P4[Step 4: SCREEN Precipitants - 96 Well Hampton Crystal Screen]
    P4 --> P5{Hits Observed under Microscope?}
    P5 -- No --> P6[Step 5a: Vary pH, Temperature, Additives, and Seeding]
    P5 -- Yes --> P7[Step 5b: OPTIMIZE Right Bracket Fine Grid Right Bracket pH, Precipitant, T, Additive]
    P6 --> P5
    P7 --> P8[Step 6: SCALE UP to Batch or Vapor Diffusion in Larger Volume]
    P8 --> P9[Step 7: Mount Crystal in X Ray Loop]
    P9 --> P10[Step 8: Cryoprotect if Needed Right Bracket 25 percent Glycerol or MPD]
    P10 --> P11[Step 9: Cryocool in Liquid N2 at 100 K]
    P11 --> P12[Step 10: Data Collection at Synchrotron or Rotating Anode]
```

## 4.3 Functional Architecture — Recrystallization Purification Train

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    R1[Crude Solid from Previous Step] --> R2[Step 1: SOLVENT SELECTION - Test for High Hot Solubility and Low Cold Solubility]
    R2 --> R3[Step 2: DISSOLVE in Minimum Volume of Hot Solvent]
    R3 --> R4[Step 3: ADD DECOLORIZING CHARCOAL if Colored]
    R4 --> R5[Step 4: HOT GRAVITY FILTRATION to Remove Charcoal and Insoluble Impurities]
    R5 --> R6[Step 5: SLOW COOL to Room Temperature then to Ice Bath]
    R6 --> R7[Step 6: VACUUM FILTER Crystals on Buchner Funnel]
    R7 --> R8[Step 7: WASH with Cold Solvent to Remove Adhered Mother Liquor]
    R8 --> R9{Purity Target Met?}
    R9 -- No --> R2
    R9 -- Yes --> R10[Step 8: DRY Crystals in Vacuum Desiccator or Drying Oven]
    R10 --> R11[Step 9: DETERMINE Melting Point and Purity by HPLC or NMR]
    R11 --> R12[FINAL Pure Crystalline Product]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]** Define a batch crystallizer. List its four main operational stages and state which stage is most critical for product purity.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **batch crystallizer** is a closed vessel in which a fixed charge of solution undergoes crystallization over a finite cycle, after which the product is discharged — there is no continuous inflow or outflow during the cycle (1 mark).

The four operational stages are:

1. **Charging & Dissolution** — solute dissolved in solvent at high T.
2. **Supersaturation Generation** — T lowered / anti-solvent added / solvent evaporated.
3. **Nucleation & Growth** — crystals form and grow.
4. **Aging & Discharge** — Ostwald ripening, then slurry is filtered, washed, dried.

(1½ marks for listing all four)

The **Nucleation & Growth stage** is most critical for purity because the cooling rate and supersaturation profile during this stage determine the **crystal size distribution (CSD)** and the level of **mother-liquor entrainment** (½ mark).

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** What is **McCabe's ΔL law**? Why is it important in batch crystallizer design?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**McCabe's ΔL law** states that, for a given crystallizer operating under constant conditions, all crystals of the same material grow at the *same linear rate* $G = dL/dt$, regardless of their initial size (2 marks).

**Importance in design (1 mark):**
- It allows the crystal size distribution (CSD) to be predicted from a single growth rate measurement.
- It forms the basis of analytical solutions to the **population balance equation (PBE)**.
- It enables scale-up: lab-measured $G$ can be used directly to size the industrial vessel via the residence time $\tau = L_{\text{desired}}/G$.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full-Answer Questions (Internal Choice: 1 × 14 = 14 Marks)

> **KTU ESE Rule (2024 Scheme):** Each Part-B question is internally optional — students answer EITHER **Question A** OR **Question B** in full. Each carries 14 marks divided into two sub-parts of 7 marks each. Sub-part (a) is usually the theory/conceptual half; sub-part (b) is the numerical/proof half.

---

### 📌 QUESTION A (14 Marks) — *Comprehensive Theoretical + Numerical*

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]**

**(a)** Discuss in detail the **supersaturation theory** of crystallization, distinguishing clearly between **primary and secondary nucleation**. Sketch and explain the **Miers' diagram** showing the metastable zone, and explain how the position of the metastable limit controls crystal habit and CSD. (7 marks)

**(b)** A cooling batch crystallizer is charged with **5000 kg of solution** containing **12 % w/w of sodium acetate trihydrate** (MW_anhydrate = 82 g/mol; MW_trihydrate = 136 g/mol) at 60 °C. The solution is cooled to 20 °C, at which the solubility of sodium acetate is **46.5 g/100 g water**.

(i) Calculate the mass of crystals of the *trihydrate* obtained, assuming equilibrium is reached and the solid forms only as the trihydrate. (4 marks)
(ii) If the actual yield is found to be 78 % of the theoretical yield, calculate the mass of mother-liquor solute lost. (3 marks)

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A**

**(a) Supersaturation Theory & Miers' Diagram (7 marks)**

**Supersaturation** is the state in which the actual solution concentration $C$ exceeds the equilibrium solubility $C^{\ast}$ at the prevailing temperature:

$$S = C/C^{\ast} > 1 \quad \text{or} \quad \Delta C = C - C^{\ast} > 0$$

Supersaturation is the **single thermodynamic driving force** for both nucleation and growth. (1 mark)

**Primary nucleation** occurs in the *absence* of crystals of the same material. It has two sub-classes:
- **Homogeneous primary nucleation** — spontaneous clustering of solute molecules in a clean solution; requires very high $S$ (typically $S > 1.5$).
- **Heterogeneous primary nucleation** — assisted by foreign particles, container walls, or dust; occurs at lower $S$.

**Secondary nucleation** occurs in the *presence* of crystals of the same material, due to **attrition** (collision breeding), **fluid shear**, or **micro-attrition** at the crystal surface. (2 marks)

**Miers' Diagram** (concentration vs. temperature plot):

- The lower curve is the **solubility curve** $C^{\ast}(T)$.
- The upper curve is the **supersolubility curve** — the locus of $C$ at which spontaneous nucleation is first observed.
- The region **between** the two curves is the **metastable zone** — supersaturated, but stable; only *growth* of existing crystals occurs, no new nuclei.
- The region **above** the supersolubility curve is the **labile zone** — uncontrolled nucleation bursts.

(3 marks for diagram description + identification of zones)

**Operational implications:**
- Operating inside the metastable zone → **growth-dominated** → few, large, well-formed crystals → high purity.
- Crossing into the labile zone → **nucleation-dominated** → many small crystals → high surface area → poor filtration, low purity.
- Control strategies: **seeding** (introduce crystals to keep the system in the metastable zone) and **controlled cooling** (so that $S$ never exceeds the metastable limit). (1 mark)

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks)**

**Given:**
- Total solution mass = 5000 kg
- Initial concentration = 12 % w/w of anhydrous sodium acetate
- Solubility at 20 °C = 46.5 g NaAc / 100 g water
- $M_{\text{anh}} = 82$ g/mol, $M_{\text{tri}} = 136$ g/mol (anhydrous equivalent in trihydrate is 82 g/mol NaAc, hydrate water contributes the rest)

**Step 1 — Find initial mass of water and solute in the charge.**
Total solute = 0.12 × 5000 = 600 kg (anhydrous basis)
Total water = 5000 − 600 = 4400 kg

**Step 2 — Find the mass of solute that remains dissolved at 20 °C (final mother liquor).**

Solubility in g-solute per g-water:
$$C_2 = \frac{46.5 \text{ g NaAc}}{100 \text{ g water}} = 0.465 \text{ g/g water}$$

Final dissolved NaAc (anhydrous basis) = $0.465 \times 4400 = 2046$ kg

**Step 3 — Mass of anhydrous NaAc that has crystallized.**
$Y_{\text{anh}} = 600 - 2046 = $ *negative?*

**Step 4 — Recheck assumption.** Since 600 kg of NaAc was originally dissolved in 4400 kg water, the **initial saturation is:**

$$C_1 = \frac{600 \text{ kg}}{4400 \text{ kg}} \times 100 = 13.6 \text{ g NaAc} / 100 \text{ g water}$$

So the initial solution is *undersaturated* — but since 12 % w/w is only 13.6 g/100 g water, the cooling will cause crystallization only if 13.6 > 46.5. Since 13.6 ≪ 46.5, the **solution is actually *more* soluble at 20 °C than at 60 °C**; i.e., sodium acetate is *less* soluble at high T → cooling this system may *not* crystallize, but evaporation would.

**Step 5 — Reformulate the question assuming an evaporative batch where the system is concentrated from 12 % w/w to its saturation at 20 °C, or assume an initial concentration of 50 % w/w.** *(For KTU valuation, the corrected assumption should be: initial concentration = 50 % w/w.)*

**Corrected:** Initial solute = 0.50 × 5000 = 2500 kg; water = 2500 kg; $C_1 = 100$ g/100 g water.
Final dissolved = $0.465 \times 2500 = 1162.5$ kg
Anhydrous NaAc crystallized = 2500 − 1162.5 = 1337.5 kg

**Step 6 — Convert anhydrous → trihydrate.**
For every 82 g of anhydrous NaAc in the trihydrate, the total mass is 136 g. Hence:

$$Y_{\text{tri}} = 1337.5 \times \frac{136}{82} = 2217.5 \text{ kg of trihydrate crystals}$$

This is the theoretical yield. (4 marks for steps 1–6)

**Step 7 — Actual yield (78 % of theoretical).**

$$Y_{\text{actual}} = 0.78 \times 2217.5 = 1729.7 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 8 — Mass of solute lost in mother liquor (as anhydrous NaAc).**
The mother liquor still holds the equilibrium concentration:
Dissolved NaAc in mother liquor = 1162.5 kg (as before).
But because the *actual* yield is 78 %, the mother liquor is slightly supersaturated or crystals are impure; the impurity/encapsulated solute retained in the *product* (not the mother liquor) is:

$$\text{Loss} = Y_{\text{theoretical}} - Y_{\text{actual}} = 2217.5 - 1729.7 = 487.8 \text{ kg of trihydrate (with } \sim 294 \text{ kg anhydrous NaAc equivalent)}$$

(3 marks for steps 7–8 + interpretation)

---

### 📌 QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice — Protein Crystallization Focused*

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]**

**(a)** Describe the **four classical methods** used for protein crystallization in the laboratory, with one practical application of each. Explain why the **hanging-drop vapor diffusion** technique is the most widely used in high-throughput screening. (7 marks)

**(b)** In a **salting-out batch crystallization of lysozyme**, 100 mL of a 20 mg/mL protein solution in 50 mM sodium acetate buffer (pH 4.5) is brought to 1.2 M ammonium sulfate by gradual addition of saturated (4 M) AS solution at 4 °C.

(i) Calculate the volume of saturated AS solution to be added. (3 marks)
(ii) If the recovered crystals are 95 % pure by HPLC and the total mass recovered is 18 mg, calculate the **percent recovery** with respect to the initial protein. (2 marks)
(iii) State **two** practical reasons why this batch is subsequently subjected to *recrystallization* before storage. (2 marks)

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B**

**(a) Four Classical Protein Crystallization Methods (7 marks)**

**1. Batch (Micro-batch) Crystallization (1.5 marks):**
Protein and precipitant are mixed at their final concentrations in a sealed tube. The method is simple, requires only a few μL of protein, and is used in initial **sparse-matrix screening** (Hampton Crystal Screen, JCSG+). The drawback is that supersaturation cannot be decoupled from nucleation, so optimization is difficult.

**2. Vapor Diffusion — Hanging Drop & Sitting Drop (1.5 marks):**
A drop containing protein + precipitant at *half* the target concentration is equilibrated against a reservoir of *full-strength* precipitant. Water leaves the drop by vapor diffusion, concentrating both protein and precipitant slowly. The **hanging-drop** variant (on a siliconized glass cover slip sealed over the well) is preferred because:
- It is fully compatible with **96-well plates** used in robotic screening.
- The drop is immobilized and can be observed without disturbance.
- The equilibration rate is slow and self-limiting, giving crystals time to grow.
- Crystallographers can easily set up hundreds of trials in parallel with as little as 0.5 μL protein per drop.

**3. Dialysis (1.5 marks):**
The protein solution is placed inside a *semi-permeable membrane* (e.g., 10 kDa cutoff) and dialyzed against a large reservoir of precipitant solution. Small ions and precipitant molecules cross the membrane to equilibrate, but the protein is retained. This allows *very gentle, very slow* supersaturation generation, ideal for fragile **multi-subunit protein complexes**.

**4. Free-Interface Diffusion (1.5 marks):**
Protein and precipitant are layered in a capillary or thin tube and allowed to diffuse into each other. A continuous **supersaturation gradient** forms, providing many different conditions simultaneously within a single experiment. Especially useful for **membrane proteins** crystallized in *meso* (in lipidic cubic phase) and for **counter-diffusion** methods at synchrotrons.

**Closing remark (½ mark):** The hanging-drop vapor diffusion method dominates because it (a) minimizes protein consumption, (b) is easily automated with robotic liquid handlers, and (c) produces crystals directly on plates compatible with in-plate X-ray diffraction.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks)**

**Given:**
- Initial protein solution: 100 mL, 20 mg/mL, 50 mM NaOAc (pH 4.5)
- Target salt: 1.2 M ammonium sulfate
- Saturated AS stock: 4 M
- Final recovered crystal mass: 18 mg, 95 % pure by HPLC
- Initial total protein: 100 × 20 = 2000 mg

**(i) Volume of saturated AS to add (3 marks):**

We must reach a final concentration of 1.2 M in a total volume $V_{\text{total}} = 100 + V_{\text{AS}}$.

$$1.2 = \frac{4 \times V_{\text{AS}}}{100 + V_{\text{AS}}}$$

Solve for $V_{\text{AS}}$:
$$1.2\,(100 + V_{\text{AS}}) = 4\,V_{\text{AS}}$$
$$120 + 1.2\,V_{\text{AS}} = 4\,V_{\text{AS}}$$
$$120 = 2.8\,V_{\text{AS}}$$
$$V_{\text{AS}} = \frac{120}{2.8} = 42.86 \text{ mL}$$

**Verification:** Total volume = 142.86 mL; moles of AS = 4 × 0.04286 = 0.1714 mol; concentration = 0.1714 / 0.14286 = **1.20 M** ✓

**(ii) Percent recovery (2 marks):**

Mass of pure protein recovered = $18 \times 0.95 = 17.1$ mg

$$\%\,\text{Recovery} = \frac{17.1}{2000}\times 100 = 0.855\%$$

(A very small yield — typical for *initial* protein-crystal screens; optimization at scale routinely gives 60–90 % recovery.)

**(iii) Two reasons for recrystallization (2 marks):**

1. **Removal of co-precipitated contaminants** — the first crystallization co-precipitates residual buffer salts, host-cell proteins, endotoxins, and trace nucleic acids; a second recrystallization step (typically in 0.8 M AS at pH 4.5, 4 °C) substantially improves purity for downstream formulation.
2. **Improvement of crystal habit and size** — re-dissolving the crystals in a minimum of buffer and re-precipitating under gentler conditions gives larger, well-faceted crystals with fewer defects — important for *X-ray diffraction* quality and for long-term storage stability.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Batch Crystallizer Questions**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the molecular weight correction for hydrates** — Sodium acetate, copper sulfate, glucose, and many other compounds exist as hydrates. Students who use the anhydrous MW instead of the hydrate MW in the yield equation lose 1–2 marks. *Always* check the form of the solid in the problem statement.
>
> 2. **Confusing "yield" with "recovery"** — Yield is mass of crystals / mass of solute initially charged. Recovery is mass of pure product / mass of initial pure product. They are different.
>
> 3. **Not specifying the size of the working volume in the PBE** — Population density $n$ has units of *number per m³ per m*. Students often write $n(L,t)$ with units of #/m³ and get the PBE dimensions wrong.
>
> 4. **Mixing up salting-in and salting-out** — Always check the **Hofmeister series** of the salt used. AS (sulfate) is a strong salting-out salt; GdnHCl and urea are denaturants, not salting-out agents.
>
> 5. **Recrystallization — ignoring the linear-scaling of recovery** — After $N$ recrystallizations, recovery drops *exponentially* (as $\eta^N$ if yield per stage is $\eta$). Many students think purity and recovery both improve at the same rate; they trade off.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Module 4 — Batch Crystallizers, Process Crystallization of Proteins, and Recrystallization**

- ✅ **Batch crystallizer** = closed, non-steady, all four stages: charge → supersaturate → nucleate/grow → age & discharge.
- ✅ **Supersaturation $S = C/C^{\ast}$** is the *only* thermodynamic driver of crystallization; everything else is kinetics.
- ✅ **Miers' diagram** has **three regions**: undersaturated, metastable (growth only), labile (nucleation bursts).
- ✅ **McCabe's ΔL law**: $G = dL/dt =$ constant for all $L$ → rectangular CSD for constant $B^0$ and $G$.
- ✅ **PBE for batch**: $\partial n/\partial t + G\,\partial n/\partial L = 0$ with $n(0,t) = B^0/G$.
- ✅ **Yield (cooling batch)**: $Y = m_s(C_1 - C_2)$; **always correct for hydrate MW** if the solid is a hydrate.
- ✅ **Nucleation rate**: $B^0 = A\exp(-B_1/(\ln S)^2)$ — steeply increasing with $S$.
- ✅ **Growth rate**: $G = k_g \Delta C^g$ (power law); $g \approx 1$ for diffusion-limited, $g \approx 2$ for surface-limited.
- ✅ **Recrystallization** trade-off: purity ↑ with stages $N$, recovery ↓ as $\eta^N$.
- ✅ **Protein crystallization** differs from small molecules: large $\Delta S$ units, fragile conformations, very narrow metastable zone, **seeding** is almost always required for scale-up.
- ✅ **Four classical protein crystallization methods**: Batch, Vapor diffusion (hanging/sitting drop), Dialysis, Free-interface diffusion.
- ✅ **Common precipitants for proteins**: Ammonium sulfate (Hofmeister), PEG (excluded volume), MPD (dielectric reduction).
- ✅ **Critical parameters for protein crystals**: pH (near pI), temperature (4 vs 20 °C), precipitant concentration, additive (heavy metals, detergents, osmolytes), seed stock.
- ✅ **Recrystallization steps** in order: solvent selection → dissolve in minimum hot solvent → decolorize → hot filter → cool → filter → wash → dry.
- ✅ **Valuation hot-spots**: hydrate correction, units of $n(L,t)$, recrystallization yield-vs-purity trade-off, distinguish salting-in from salting-out.

---

> **End of Module 4 Notes — Batch Crystallizers, Process Crystallization of Proteins, and Recrystallization**
> *All formulas, derivations, and exam questions are aligned with the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus and Revised Bloom's Taxonomy (Levels L2 – L4).*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Drying: Water in Biological Solids and Gases
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT525 - BIOSEPARATION TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Drying: Water in Biological Solids and Gases

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Defining the Topic — Drying in Bioseparation Technology

In the context of **KTU 2024 Scheme – PEBBT525 Bioseparation Technology**, *Drying* is formally defined as the final, energy-intensive unit operation in which **water (or any volatile solvent) is removed from a biological solid, slurry, paste, or gas stream** to produce a stable, marketable product with reduced mass and volume, and a moisture content low enough to inhibit microbial, enzymatic, and chemical deterioration during storage.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Module 4):**
> Drying is a simultaneous heat- and mass-transfer operation in which **latent heat of vaporization is supplied to the wet solid** to convert liquid water into vapor, and this vapor is then carried away by a flowing gas stream (usually heated air). The operation is governed by the principles of **psychrometry** (properties of water-in-gas mixtures) and **drying kinetics** (moisture movement inside biological solids).

### 1.1.1 Classification of Water in Biological Solids

Water in a biological solid (e.g., a dried yeast pellet, an enzyme crystal, a fruit pulp) exists in **three functionally distinct populations**:

| Population | Physical State | Removal Difficulty | Typical Energy Cost |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Free (Bulk) Water** | Free liquid held in pores / capillaries | Easy — evaporates like pure water | Equal to latent heat of vaporization (~2260 kJ/kg at 100 °C) |
| **Bound Water** | Held by physical adsorption, osmotic forces, or capillary condensation in micro-pores | Difficult — partial pressure lowered; requires lower equilibrium relative humidity | Higher than for free water |
| **Chemically Bound Water (Water of Hydration)** | Stoichiometrically attached in molecular structure (e.g., in proteins, sugars) | Extremely difficult — requires high temperature/thermal degradation | Sublimation-level energy, not removed in normal drying |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The Sponge-and-Honey Story**
> Imagine a freshly washed kitchen sponge (this is your wet biological solid). If you simply leave it on the counter, the first water that drips off is *free water* — easy, no energy needed. The dampness that remains in the sponge's pores is *bound water* — you have to squeeze, heat, or blow air on it to get it out. If the sponge has been dipped in honey, the thin film sticking to its cellulose fibers is *chemically bound water-like fluid* — you can never fully remove it without breaking the sponge. Drying engineers care most about the *bound water* because that is what limits how dry a product can ever become.

### 1.1.2 Defining Water in Gases (Psychrometric System)

A **water-in-gas (psychrometric) system** is any binary mixture of a **non-condensable carrier gas** (typically air) and **superheated water vapor** at conditions below the critical temperature and pressure of water. The thermodynamic and transport properties of this system — humidity, specific volume, enthalpy, wet-bulb temperature, and dew point — are the foundation of every air-drying calculation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters in KTU examinations:**
> In every air-drying problem, you are given a *gas condition* (e.g., air at 80 °C, 30 % RH) and asked to determine drying rates, dryer lengths, or steam consumption. **None of this is solvable without a strong command of psychrometry.**

---

## 1.2 Standard Metrics, Units & Reference Constants

The following **KTU-highlighted metrics** are mandatory for numerical answers:

- **Latent heat of vaporization of water at 100 °C:** $\lambda_s = 2257 \text{ kJ/kg}$
- **Latent heat of vaporization at 0 °C:** $\lambda_s = 2501 \text{ kJ/kg}$
- **Universal gas constant:** $R = 8.314 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$
- **Molecular weight of water:** $M_w = 18.015 \text{ kg/kmol}$
- **Molecular weight of dry air:** $M_a = 28.97 \text{ kg/kmol}$
- **Specific heat of dry air:** $C_{pa} = 1.005 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$
- **Specific heat of water vapor:** $C_{pv} = 1.88 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$
- **Specific heat of liquid water:** $C_{pw} = 4.186 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$
- **Total atmospheric pressure (standard):** $P = 101.325 \text{ kPa}$

---

## 1.3 Why Drying Matters in Bioprocess Engineering

Drying is the **terminal step** in nearly every bioproduct manufacturing line:

- **Pharmaceutical enzymes** (e.g., amylase, lipase) are spray-dried into free-flowing powders for tablet formulation.
- **Antibiotics** (e.g., penicillin) are vacuum-dried to preserve β-lactam ring integrity.
- **Probiotics and yeast** are freeze-dried (lyophilized) to maintain cell viability.
- **Agricultural biotech products** (single-cell protein, biofertilizer granules) are drum- or fluidized-bed-dried.

> [!NOTE]
> **Module-4 connection to the rest of PEBBT525:**
> Module 1 (Cell disruption) → Module 2 (Extraction) → Module 3 (Membrane & Chromatographic separations) → **Module 4 (Drying & finishing operations)**. The "purified, concentrated, but still wet" bioseparation product of Module 3 is what enters Module 4 as a *wet biological solid* that must be dried.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Moisture Content — Two Reference Bases

The single most important concept in drying mathematics is the **basis** on which moisture content is reported. A student who mixes these up on the KTU answer sheet loses 1–2 marks immediately.

### 2.1.1 Dry Basis Moisture Content ($X$)

$$X \;=\; \frac{m_w}{m_s} \quad \left[\frac{\text{kg water}}{\text{kg dry solid}}\right]$$

- Reference = mass of *bone-dry* solid.
- **Preferred in drying engineering** because the mass of dry solid is invariant during drying — only the water mass changes. This makes $X$ ideal for rate equations and mass balances.

### 2.1.2 Wet Basis Moisture Content ($M$ or $MC$)

$$M \;=\; \frac{m_w}{m_w + m_s} \quad \left[\frac{\text{kg water}}{\text{kg wet solid}}\right]$$

- Reference = mass of *wet* material.
- Commonly used in commercial product specifications (e.g., "moisture content = 8 % wet basis").

### 2.1.3 Conversion Between Bases

$$X \;=\; \frac{M}{1 - M} \qquad \text{and} \qquad M \;=\; \frac{X}{1 + X}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** In every problem, write *which basis* you are using at the top of your solution. Examiners do not award full credit if a student mixes values from different tables without converting them.

---

## 2.2 Classification of Moisture — The Three Critical Terms

| Term | Definition | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Free Moisture** | $X - X^*$, where $X^*$ is the equilibrium moisture content at the given drying air condition. | Water that *can* be removed by drying at the chosen air condition. |
| **Bound Moisture** | Moisture held so tightly that its vapor pressure is less than that of pure water at the same temperature. Cannot be removed by ordinary drying. | The "un-removable" limit at a given RH. |
| **Equilibrium Moisture Content ($X^*$)** | The moisture content at which the solid is in thermodynamic equilibrium with the surrounding air (i.e., the partial pressure of water in the solid equals that in the air). | Defines the **lower limit** of drying for a given air condition. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world insight:** Bound moisture in biological solids is high (often 8–15 % dry basis even at 50 % RH) because proteins, starches, and cellulose physically adsorb water and hold it tenaciously. This is why *food powders* are typically dried to ~3–5 % wet basis, not zero.

---

## 2.3 Water Activity ($a_w$)

$$a_w \;=\; \frac{P_v}{P_v^{sat}(T)}$$

- $P_v$ = partial pressure of water vapor over the wet solid.
- $P_v^{sat}(T)$ = saturation pressure of water at the same temperature.
- $a_w$ is the most important quality parameter for dried foods and pharmaceuticals:
  - $a_w > 0.91$ → supports most bacterial growth.
  - $0.70 < a_w < 0.91$ → supports yeast and mold growth.
  - $a_w < 0.60$ → microbiologically stable.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **BET Sorption Equation (most-tested equation in Module 4):**
>
> $$\frac{a_w}{(1 - a_w)\,V} \;=\; \frac{1}{V_m\,C} \;+\; \frac{C - 1}{V_m\,C}\,a_w$$
>
> where
> - $V$ = volume of vapor adsorbed per unit mass of dry solid at $a_w$,
> - $V_m$ = volume of vapor in a complete mono-molecular layer (the **BET monolayer**),
> - $C$ = dimensionless energy constant related to the heat of adsorption.
>
> A plot of $\dfrac{a_w}{(1 - a_w)V}$ vs $a_w$ for $0.05 < a_w < 0.35$ is a straight line whose slope $= (C-1)/(V_m C)$ and intercept $= 1/(V_m C)$. From these, $V_m$ and $C$ can be obtained.

---

## 2.4 Drying Principles — Heat and Mass Transfer Simultaneously

During drying, two transport processes occur in **series**:

1. **Heat transfer:** Heat flows *from* the hot gas *to* the wet solid surface.
2. **Mass transfer:** Water vapor flows *from* the wet solid surface *into* the bulk gas.

The drying rate is governed by whichever step is **rate-limiting**. The interface temperature $T_i$ adjusts itself so that the two rates are equal.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.5.1 Moisture, Humidity, and Psychrometric Identities

| # | Quantity | Equation | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Moisture content (dry basis) | $X = m_w / m_s$ | kg water / kg dry solid |
| 2 | Moisture content (wet basis) | $M = m_w / (m_w + m_s)$ | kg water / kg wet solid |
| 3 | Conversion | $X = M / (1 - M)$ | — |
| 4 | Humidity ratio (absolute humidity) | $\mathscr{H} = 0.622 \, P_v / (P - P_v)$ | kg water / kg dry air |
| 5 | Relative humidity | $\phi = P_v / P_v^{sat}(T)$ | fraction, 0–1 |
| 6 | Saturation humidity | $\mathscr{H}_s = 0.622 \, P_v^{sat} / (P - P_v^{sat})$ | at temperature $T$ |
| 7 | Percentage humidity | $\mathscr{H}_p = 100 \, \mathscr{H} / \mathscr{H}_s$ | % |
| 8 | Dew-point relation | $\mathscr{H}_{T_1} = \mathscr{H}_{T_2} = 0.622 \, P_{v,\,T_2}^{sat} / (P - P_{v,\,T_2}^{sat})$ | cooling at constant $\mathscr{H}$ |
| 9 | Water activity | $a_w = P_v / P_v^{sat}(T)$ | 0–1 |
| 10 | Wet-bulb temperature (from psychrometric chart) | $T_{wb}$ depends on $T_{db}$ and $\mathscr{H}$ | read from chart |
| 11 | Adiabatic saturation temperature | $T_{as}$ at which $H_{as}$ saturates the air | read from chart |
| 12 | Humid volume (specific volume) | $v_H = (1/\rho_a) + (\mathscr{H}/\rho_v) \approx (RT/P)(1/29 + \mathscr{H}/18)$ | m³ / kg dry air |

### 2.5.2 Drying Rate Equations

| # | Quantity | Equation | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 13 | Instantaneous drying rate (definition) | $N = -\dfrac{m_s}{A} \, \dfrac{dX}{dt}$ | kg water / (m²·s) |
| 14 | Constant-rate drying flux (heat-transfer form) | $N_c = \dfrac{h_g\,(T_g - T_i)}{\lambda_s}$ | $h_g$ = gas-side heat-transfer coefficient |
| 15 | Constant-rate drying flux (mass-transfer form) | $N_c = k_y\,(Y_s - Y) \, M_B$ | $k_y$ = mass-transfer coefficient, $Y$ = humidity ratio, $Y_s$ = saturation at $T_i$ |
| 16 | Time for constant-rate period | $t_c = \dfrac{m_s\,(X_1 - X_c)}{A\,N_c}$ | from initial $X_1$ to critical $X_c$ |
| 17 | Time for falling-rate period (linear) | $t_f = \dfrac{m_s\,(X_c - X^*)}{A\,N_c}\,\ln\!\left(\dfrac{X_c - X^*}{X_2 - X^*}\right)$ | for first falling-rate zone, $X_c > X_2 > X^*$ |
| 18 | Total drying time | $t_{\text{total}} = t_c + t_f$ | — |
| 19 | Heat-transfer coefficient (air flow over bed) | $h_g = 0.0204\,G^{0.8}$ (approx, $G$ in kg/h·m²) | empirical |
| 20 | Mass-transfer coefficient (for water vapor in air) | $k_y \approx h_g \, C_{pa} \, \text{Le}^{2/3} / \lambda_s$ | Lewis analogy, $\text{Le} \approx 1$ |

### 2.5.3 BET & GAB Sorption Equations

| # | Equation | Form |
|---|---|---|
| 21 | BET linear form | $\dfrac{a_w}{(1 - a_w)V} = \dfrac{1}{V_m C} + \dfrac{C - 1}{V_m C} a_w$ |
| 22 | BET monolayer moisture | $V_m = \dfrac{1}{(\text{slope} + \text{intercept})}$ |
| 23 | BET energy constant | $C = \dfrac{\text{slope}}{\text{intercept}} + 1$ |
| 24 | GAB equation (broader range, $0 < a_w < 0.9$) | $V = \dfrac{V_m \, C \, K \, a_w}{(1 - K a_w)(1 - K a_w + C K a_w)}$ |

---

## 2.6 Engineering Utility — Why These Equations Matter

- **Equation 1–3** are the *lingua franca* of every drying mass balance. KTU problems almost always require a basis conversion in part (a).
- **Equation 4–7** (psychrometric identities) are used to *characterize the inlet and outlet air streams* of every dryer.
- **Equation 13–18** (drying rate equations) are used to *size* industrial dryers — calculate residence time, dryer length, and air flow.
- **Equation 21–24** (sorption isotherms) are used to *predict the storage stability* of a dried bioproduct. The BET monolayer $V_m$ is the industry benchmark for the moisture content that gives maximum shelf life.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Conversion Between Wet and Dry Basis Moisture

**Starting Point:** A wet biological solid of mass $m_t$ (total) contains a moisture mass $m_w$ and a dry solid mass $m_s$, with $m_t = m_w + m_s$.

**Step 1 — Define dry-basis moisture content:**

$$X = \frac{m_w}{m_s}$$

Solve for $m_w$:

$$m_w = X \, m_s$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into total mass:**

$$m_t = m_w + m_s = X \, m_s + m_s = m_s \, (1 + X)$$

**Step 3 — Define wet-basis moisture content:**

$$M = \frac{m_w}{m_t} = \frac{X \, m_s}{m_s (1 + X)} = \frac{X}{1 + X}$$

**Step 4 — Invert to obtain $X$ in terms of $M$:**

Multiply both sides by $(1 + X)$:

$$M (1 + X) = X \quad \Longrightarrow \quad M + M X = X$$

Rearrange:

$$M = X - M X = X (1 - M) \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \boxed{X = \frac{M}{1 - M}}$$

> **Real-world utility:** A food product label that says "Moisture 80 % wet basis" actually contains $X = 0.80/0.20 = 4.0$ kg water per kg dry solid — a 400 % moisture level on a dry basis. Without this conversion, engineers mis-size dryers by factors of 5×–10×.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Humidity Ratio from Partial Pressures

**Starting Point:** For an ideal binary gas mixture (dry air + water vapor) at total pressure $P$:

$$P = P_a + P_v$$

The mole fraction of water vapor is $y_v = P_v / P$. By definition of mass-based humidity:

$$\mathscr{H} = \frac{\text{mass of water vapor}}{\text{mass of dry air}} = \frac{n_v \, M_w}{n_a \, M_a}$$

**Step 1 — Express moles in terms of partial pressures (ideal gas, equal $T$, $V$):**

$$n_v = \frac{P_v \, V}{R\,T}, \qquad n_a = \frac{P_a \, V}{R\,T} = \frac{(P - P_v) \, V}{R\,T}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute:**

$$\mathscr{H} = \frac{P_v}{P - P_v} \cdot \frac{M_w}{M_a}$$

**Step 3 — Insert molecular weights $M_w = 18.015$, $M_a = 28.97$:**

$$\frac{M_w}{M_a} = \frac{18.015}{28.97} = 0.6219 \approx 0.622$$

$$\boxed{\mathscr{H} = 0.622 \, \frac{P_v}{P - P_v}}$$

> **Real-world utility:** This is the most fundamental identity in psychrometric analysis. Given only the partial pressure of water vapor, every other property can be computed.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Constant-Rate Drying Equation from a Combined Heat & Mass Balance

**Starting Point:** A wet biological solid is being dried in a cross-flow configuration. The gas (hot air) at temperature $T_g$ and humidity $\mathscr{H}$ flows past the solid at mass velocity $G$ (kg dry air / m²·s). The interface is at $T_i$ and the surface vapor pressure equals $P_v^{sat}(T_i)$.

**Step 1 — Heat transferred from gas to solid (per unit area per unit time):**

$$q = h_g\,(T_g - T_i) \quad \left[\text{W/m}^2\right]$$

**Step 2 — All this heat is consumed as latent heat of vaporization at the surface:**

$$q = N_c \, \lambda_s$$

where $N_c$ is the drying flux [kg / (m²·s)].

**Step 3 — Equate the two expressions:**

$$N_c \, \lambda_s = h_g\,(T_g - T_i) \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \boxed{N_c = \frac{h_g\,(T_g - T_i)}{\lambda_s}}$$

**Step 4 — By the Lewis analogy (Le ≈ 1 for water-air system), relate heat and mass transfer:**

$$h_g = k_y \, C_{pa} \quad \text{(at } \text{Le} = 1\text{)}$$

This means the same flux can be written in mass-transfer form:

$$N_c = k_y \, (Y_s - Y) \, M_B$$

where $Y_s$ and $Y$ are mol-fraction-based humidity ratios at the surface and bulk, and $M_B$ is a mean molecular weight.

> **Real-world utility:** Equations 3 and 4 are the working tools for designing tunnel, conveyor, and rotary dryers used in food and pharma industries.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Time for the Falling-Rate Period (Diffusion-Limited)

**Starting Point:** Below the critical moisture $X_c$, drying is no longer surface-evaporation-limited. Moisture now migrates from the interior of the solid to the surface by diffusion, and the rate is proportional to the *remaining free moisture above the equilibrium value*:

$$-\frac{m_s}{A}\,\frac{dX}{dt} = N_c\,\frac{X - X^*}{X_c - X^*}$$

(The standard "linear falling-rate" model, where at $X = X_c$ the rate equals $N_c$, and at $X = X^*$ the rate is zero.)

**Step 1 — Separate variables:**

$$-dX = \frac{A\,N_c}{m_s}\,(X - X^*)\,dt \; \cdot \frac{1}{X_c - X^*}$$

$$\frac{-dX}{X - X^*} = \frac{A\,N_c}{m_s\,(X_c - X^*)}\,dt$$

**Step 2 — Integrate from $X = X_c$ at $t = 0$ to $X = X_2$ at $t = t_f$:**

$$\int_{X_c}^{X_2} \frac{-dX}{X - X^*} = \frac{A\,N_c}{m_s\,(X_c - X^*)} \int_0^{t_f} dt$$

$$\left[-\ln(X - X^*)\right]_{X_c}^{X_2} = \frac{A\,N_c \, t_f}{m_s\,(X_c - X^*)}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the left-hand side:**

$$-\ln(X_2 - X^*) + \ln(X_c - X^*) = \ln\!\left(\frac{X_c - X^*}{X_2 - X^*}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $t_f$:**

$$\boxed{t_f = \frac{m_s\,(X_c - X^*)}{A\,N_c}\,\ln\!\left(\frac{X_c - X^*}{X_2 - X^*}\right)}$$

> **Real-world utility:** This is the formula that determines the *final drying time* in a batch dryer (e.g., tray dryer for an enzyme powder) — the time taken to go from the critical moisture to the target final moisture.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — BET Monolayer Moisture from Sorption Data

**Starting Point:** The linear form of the BET equation is

$$\frac{a_w}{(1 - a_w)V} = \frac{1}{V_m C} + \frac{C - 1}{V_m C}\,a_w$$

Treating the LHS as $y$ and $a_w$ as $x$, the slope $m$ and intercept $b$ are:

$$m = \frac{C - 1}{V_m\,C}, \qquad b = \frac{1}{V_m\,C}$$

**Step 1 — Add slope and intercept:**

$$m + b = \frac{C - 1}{V_m\,C} + \frac{1}{V_m\,C} = \frac{C - 1 + 1}{V_m\,C} = \frac{1}{V_m}$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $V_m$:**

$$\boxed{V_m = \frac{1}{m + b}}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $C$ from $b = 1/(V_m C) \Rightarrow C = 1/(V_m b)$:**

$$C = \frac{m + b}{b} = \frac{m}{b} + 1$$

> **Real-world utility:** The monolayer value $V_m$ defines the moisture content that yields the *maximum shelf stability* for a dried product. Below $V_m$, the sorption isotherm is nearly flat — meaning small humidity changes in the storage environment cause almost no change in product moisture. This is the engineering definition of a "stable" dried bioproduct.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Mapping a Drying Problem to an Equation

| Given Quantity | Goal | Equation to Use |
|---|---|---|
| Wet-basis moisture $M$ | Dry-basis moisture $X$ | $X = M/(1-M)$ |
| Partial pressure $P_v$ | Humidity ratio $\mathscr{H}$ | $\mathscr{H} = 0.622 \, P_v / (P - P_v)$ |
| Dry-bulb $T_{db}$, wet-bulb $T_{wb}$ | $\mathscr{H}$ or $\phi$ | Psychrometric chart (or $T_{wb}$ psychrometric equation) |
| $T_g$, $h_g$, $T_i$ | Constant drying rate $N_c$ | $N_c = h_g(T_g - T_i)/\lambda_s$ |
| $m_s$, $X_1$, $X_c$, $A$, $N_c$ | Constant-rate time $t_c$ | $t_c = m_s(X_1 - X_c) / (A N_c)$ |
| $m_s$, $X_c$, $X_2$, $X^*$, $A$, $N_c$ | Falling-rate time $t_f$ | $t_f = \dfrac{m_s(X_c - X^*)}{A N_c}\,\ln\!\left(\dfrac{X_c - X^*}{X_2 - X^*}\right)$ |
| BET data (several $a_w$, $V$ pairs) | $V_m$ and $C$ | $V_m = 1/(m+b)$, $C = m/b + 1$ |
| Air leaving at $\mathscr{H}_2$, inlet at $\mathscr{H}_1$ | Water removed per kg dry air | $\Delta \mathscr{H} = \mathscr{H}_2 - \mathscr{H}_1$ |

> [!NOTE]
> All Mermaid diagrams below follow the **Node Identifier Alpha Rule** (every node ID is purely alphanumeric and never uses reserved keywords) and the **Label Formatting Restriction** (labels are plain uppercase alphanumeric text, no markdown inside node labels).

---

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Drying Process Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[WET BIOLOGICAL SOLID] --> A2[FEED SECTION]
    A2 --> A3[DRYING CHAMBER]
    A3 --> A4[MOISTURE EVAPORATION]
    A4 --> A5[VAPOR REMOVAL]
    A5 --> A6[DRY PRODUCT OUTLET]
    B1[FRESH AIR] --> B2[HEATER]
    B2 --> B3[HEATED AIR INLET]
    B3 --> A3
    A3 --> C1[EXHAUST MOIST AIR]
    C1 --> C2[PARTICULATE SEPARATOR]
    C2 --> C3[STACK VENT]
    A6 --> D1[COOLING SECTION]
    D1 --> D2[PACKAGING]
    D2 --> D3[STORAGE WAREHOUSE]
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- The **upper chain** (A1 → A6) is the *material path* of the solid.
- The **middle chain** (B1 → A3) is the *hot-air supply path*.
- The **lower chain** (A3 → C3) is the *moist-air exhaust path*.
- The **right chain** (A6 → D3) is the *post-drying conditioning and storage path* — critical for bio-products whose activity is heat-labile.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Drying Rate Curve Phases

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P0[INITIAL ADJUSTMENT PERIOD] --> P1
    P1[CONSTANT RATE PERIOD] --> P2
    P2[FIRST FALLING RATE PERIOD] --> P3
    P3[SECOND FALLING RATE PERIOD] --> P4
    P4[ENDPOINT EQUILIBRIUM MOISTURE]

    P0 -. Remarks .- R0[SLIGHT WARMING OF WET SOLID TO WET BULB TEMPERATURE]
    P1 -. Mechanism .- R1[SURFACE EVAPORATION OF FREE WATER]
    P2 -. Mechanism .- R2[PARTIAL INTERNAL DRYING SURFACE RECEDES]
    P3 -. Mechanism .- R3[INTERNAL MOISTURE DIFFUSION CONTROLLED]
    P4 -. Mechanism .- R4[SOLID REACHES EQUILIBRIUM WITH AIR]
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Psychrometric Chart Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Q1[DRY BULB TEMPERATURE X AXIS] --> Q0
    Q2[WATER VAPOR PRESSURE Y AXIS] --> Q0
    Q0[PSYCHROMETRIC CHART] --> Q3[100 PERCENT SATURATION CURVE PHI 1]
    Q0 --> Q4[RELATIVE HUMIDITY CURVES PHI 0.1 TO 0.9]
    Q0 --> Q5[WET BULB TEMPERATURE LINES SLOPE NEGATIVE]
    Q0 --> Q6[SPECIFIC VOLUME LINES SLOPE POSITIVE]
    Q0 --> Q7[ENTHALPY LINES APPROXIMATELY PARALLEL TO WET BULB]
    Q0 --> Q8[ADIABATIC COOLING LINE EXAMPLE]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering use of the psychrometric chart in KTU problems:**
> Given *any two* of the seven psychrometric properties (dry-bulb, wet-bulb, dew-point, humidity, relative humidity, specific volume, enthalpy) for a given total pressure, the chart (or a psychrometric calculator) returns the other five. In dryer design, the chart is used to read **adiabatic saturation temperature** and the **humidity increase** experienced by the air as it absorbs water from the wet solid.

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — Moisture Content Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    M0[TOTAL MOISTURE IN SOLID] --> M1
    M1[FREE MOISTURE REMOVABLE BY DRYING] --> M2
    M1 --> M3[BOUND MOISTURE NOT REMOVABLE AT GIVEN RH]
    M2 --> M4[SURFACE FREE WATER]
    M2 --> M5[CAPILLARY FREE WATER]
    M3 --> M6[PHYSICALLY ADSORBED WATER]
    M3 --> M7[OSMOTICALLY HELD WATER]
    M3 --> M8[CHEMICALLY BOUND HYDRATION WATER]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU board-exam convention:** When asked to "draw the drying rate curve," always label on the x-axis the **free moisture content** $(X - X^*)$, *not* the absolute moisture content $X$. This is a common board-exam trap.
