# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Model Paper]**
>
> **Q1.** Distinguish between *bioprocess integration* and *bioprocess intensification*. Give one industrial example for each.
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> - **Bioprocess integration** is the engineering strategy of *coupling* two or more unit operations (e.g., reaction + separation) into a single synergistic workflow so that the output of one becomes the input of the next without intermediate storage. *Example: extractive fermentation where in-situ solvent extraction is integrated inside the bioreactor.*
> - **Bioprocess intensification** is the deployment of innovative techniques to *radically improve* productivity, reduce equipment size, and lower waste. *Example: replacing a 10,000 L fermenter + centrifuge train with a 1,000 L fermenter + ISPR loop producing the same annual output.*

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]**
>
> **Q2.** What is *Whole Broth Processing (WBP)*? List any two advantages it offers over conventional downstream processing.
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> - **Definition (1.5 marks):** Whole Broth Processing is a downstream strategy in which the entire fermentation broth — including cells, cell debris, dissolved solids, residual nutrients, and the target product — is fed directly into the primary recovery step (e.g., extraction or adsorption) **without prior cell separation by centrifugation or filtration**.
> - **Advantages (1.5 marks — any two):**
>   1. Eliminates the centrifugation / microfiltration bottleneck, reducing CAPEX and OPEX.
>   2. Reduces product loss (intracellular product recovered without an inefficient cell disruption + solid–liquid separation sequence).
>   3. Shortens overall process time (fewer unit operations in series).
>   4. Better suited for high-cell-density broths that would foul centrifuges.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, ESE Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, ESE Module 4 - Choice A]**
>
> **Q3 (a).** With the aid of a neat block diagram, explain the concept of **In-situ Product Recovery (ISPR)**. Discuss the various techniques of ISPR with emphasis on extractive fermentation and adsorption. **(7 marks)**
>
> **Model Answer:**
> 1. **Definition and principle (2 marks):** ISPR is a process intensification strategy in which the target product is selectively removed from the bioreactor the moment it is formed, using a coupled recovery unit. The thermodynamic driving force is Le Chatelier's principle: continuous removal shifts the biochemical equilibrium in favor of further product formation, relieving product inhibition.
> 2. **Block diagram description (1 mark):** (See Section 4.1, Diagram A — Fermenter → External Recovery Unit → Regeneration → Polishing → Product. Recovery phase is recycled.)
> 3. **Extractive fermentation (2 marks):** An immiscible organic solvent (e.g., oleyl alcohol for ethanol, tri-n-octylamine in dodecane for organic acids) is placed either in a separate layer above the broth or circulated through an external loop. The product partitions into the solvent according to the partition coefficient $K_D$. The solvent is regenerated by back-extraction, distillation, or pH swing, and the concentrated aqueous stream proceeds to polishing.
> 4. **Adsorption (2 marks):** Hydrophobic polymeric resins (e.g., Amberlite XAD-4, XAD-7) or ion-exchange resins are placed inside the fermenter or in an external column. The product binds to the resin surface (Langmuir-type isotherm). Once loaded, the resin is filtered or settled out, washed, and eluted with an appropriate solvent (methanol, dilute acid, or pH-adjusted buffer). The eluate contains the concentrated product.
>
> ---
>
> **Q3 (b).** A fermentation broth (volume $V_{aq}$ = 100 L) contains an antibiotic at $C_0$ = 2 g L⁻¹. It is extracted with an organic solvent of volume $V_{org}$ = 50 L. The partition coefficient $K_D$ = 8. Calculate: (i) the single-stage recovery, (ii) the recovery in three cross-current stages using fresh solvent each time. **(7 marks)**
>
> **Model Answer:**
> 1. **Given data (1 mark):** $V_{aq} = 100 \text{ L}$, $V_{org} = 50 \text{ L}$, $C_0 = 2 \text{ g L}^{-1}$, $K_D = 8$, phase ratio $\theta = V_{org}/V_{aq} = 50/100 = 0.5$.
> 2. **Part (i) — single-stage recovery (3 marks):**
>    - Apply the single-stage extraction formula:
>    $$ E_1 = \dfrac{K_D \cdot \theta}{1 + K_D \cdot \theta} = \dfrac{8 \cdot 0.5}{1 + 8 \cdot 0.5} = \dfrac{4}{1 + 4} = \dfrac{4}{5} = 0.80 $$
>    - Therefore, **80%** of the antibiotic is recovered in a single stage. Mass recovered = $0.80 \cdot 2 \text{ g L}^{-1} \cdot 100 \text{ L} = 160 \text{ g}$.
> 3. **Part (ii) — three-stage cross-current recovery (3 marks):**
>    - Apply the multi-stage cross-current formula:
>    $$ E_{total} = 1 - \left( \dfrac{1}{1 + K_D \theta} \right)^N = 1 - \left( \dfrac{1}{5} \right)^3 = 1 - \dfrac{1}{125} = 1 - 0.008 = 0.992 $$
>    - Therefore, **99.2%** of the antibiotic is recovered in three stages. Mass recovered = $0.992 \cdot 2 \text{ g L}^{-1} \cdot 100 \text{ L} = 198.4 \text{ g}$.
> 4. **Conclusion (text):** Going from 1 stage to 3 stages boosts recovery from 80% to 99.2%, an additional 38.4 g of product — a key justification for multi-stage design.

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, ESE Module 4 - Choice B (Alternative)]**
>
> **Q4 (a).** What is **Whole Broth Processing (WBP)**? With the help of a comparative block diagram, explain how it differs from conventional downstream processing of fermentation broth. List the engineering and economic advantages of WBP. **(7 marks)**
>
> **Model Answer:**
> 1. **Definition (1 mark):** WBP is a downstream processing strategy in which the entire fermentation broth — containing intact cells, cell debris, dissolved salts, residual nutrients, and the target product — is fed directly to the primary recovery step (e.g., extraction column, adsorption bed, or membrane module) without prior cell removal by centrifugation or filtration.
> 2. **Block diagram (1.5 marks):** (Refer to Section 4.2, Diagram B.)
>    - *Traditional:* Fermenter → Centrifuge → (Cell disruption) → Capture → Chromatography → Polishing → Product (6–7 stages).
>    - *WBP:* Fermenter → Whole broth extraction/adsorption → Single clarification → Chromatography → Product (3–4 stages).
> 3. **Engineering differences (2 marks):**
>    - Centrifugation / microfiltration step is **eliminated or deferred**.
>    - Solids (cells, debris) pass through the recovery unit, requiring the extractant / adsorbent to tolerate particulates (robust contacting devices such as pulsed columns or moving-bed adsorbers).
>    - Process Analytical Technology (PAT) is required to monitor solids and product concentration in real time.
> 4. **Economic advantages (2.5 marks — any 3):**
>    - CAPEX reduced (no centrifuge, no associated utilities, less floor space).
>    - OPEX reduced (lower energy and maintenance burden).
>    - Lower product loss (no loss to cell paste or filter cake).
>    - Faster turnaround time (fewer unit operations in series).
>    - Better handling of high-cell-density broths (no centrifuge capacity limit).
>    - Suitable for intracellular products when combined with whole-broth lysis.
>
> ---
>
> **Q4 (b).** A 2000 L fermenter produces a bio-product at a final batch titer of 25 g L⁻¹. The conventional batch process takes 40 h including 8 h of downstream recovery. An ISPR process achieves a sustained volumetric productivity of 1.2 g L⁻¹ h⁻¹ with the same fermenter volume. Compare the two processes in terms of (i) volumetric productivity, (ii) total cycle time, and (iii) total product per cycle. **(7 marks)**
>
> **Model Answer:**
> 1. **Given data (1 mark):** $V = 2000 \text{ L}$; $P_{batch} = 25 \text{ g L}^{-1}$; $t_{batch} = 40 \text{ h}$; $Q_{P,ISPR} = 1.2 \text{ g L}^{-1}\text{ h}^{-1}$.
> 2. **Part (i) — Volumetric productivity comparison (2 marks):**
>    - Conventional: $Q_{P,batch} = \dfrac{P_{batch}}{t_{batch}} = \dfrac{25 \text{ g L}^{-1}}{40 \text{ h}} = 0.625 \text{ g L}^{-1}\text{ h}^{-1}$.
>    - ISPR: $Q_{P,ISPR} = 1.2 \text{ g L}^{-1}\text{ h}^{-1}$.
>    - Enhancement ratio: $\dfrac{1.2}{0.625} = 1.92$. So ISPR is **1.92× more productive** by volume.
> 3. **Part (ii) — Total cycle time for 50 g L⁻¹ equivalent (2 marks):**
>    - Conventional: at 25 g L⁻¹ in 40 h, achieving 50 g L⁻¹ would require either doubling titer (often not possible due to inhibition) or running two cycles: total time = $2 \times 40 = 80 \text{ h}$.
>    - ISPR: at 1.2 g L⁻¹ h⁻¹, achieving 50 g L⁻¹ cumulative: $t = 50 / 1.2 = 41.67 \text{ h}$ — almost **half the time** of two conventional cycles.
> 4. **Part (iii) — Total product per cycle (2 marks):**
>    - Conventional: $m_{batch} = 25 \text{ g L}^{-1} \times 2000 \text{ L} = 50,000 \text{ g} = 50 \text{ kg}$.
>    - ISPR: in 40 h (same as conventional cycle): $m_{ISPR} = 1.2 \text{ g L}^{-1}\text{ h}^{-1} \times 40 \text{ h} \times 2000 \text{ L} = 96,000 \text{ g} = 96 \text{ kg}$.
>    - **Net gain: 46 kg per cycle**, a **92% increase** in product output from the same vessel.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in this Module**
> 1. **Conflating "Integration" with "Intensification":** Examiners specifically award a 1-mark distinction. Always write: *Integration = coupling; Intensification = order-of-magnitude improvement.*
> 2. **Confusing WBP with ISPR:** WBP is a *recovery* strategy (operates at the downstream end, broth not separated). ISPR is a *reaction-coupled* strategy (operates simultaneously with fermentation). They are not synonyms.
> 3. **Forgetting solvent biocompatibility in extractive fermentation:** If the solvent is toxic to cells, the extraction is not "in-situ" — it becomes a two-step process. Always mention biocompatibility as a design criterion.
> 4. **Not specifying units in numerical answers:** The expression $K_D = 5$ without units is acceptable (dimensionless), but $J = 0.5$ without units will lose a mark. Always write g L⁻¹ h⁻¹ for productivity, kg m⁻² h⁻¹ for flux.
> 5. **Skipping the Le Chatelier justification:** The thermodynamic reason (inhibition relief via continuous removal) is the conceptual core of ISPR. Omitting it costs at least 2 marks in long answers.
> 6. **Mismeasuring productivity:** Volumetric productivity $Q_P$ uses *reactor volume* (not extractant volume). WBP does not change the reactor volume — only the recovery train.
> 7. **Forgetting the cell washout criterion:** In an external ISPR loop, the circulation rate must keep the dilution rate $D < D_c$, or cells wash out. A complete ISPR design always includes this check.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 4: Bioprocess Integration and Intensification**
>
> - **Bioprocess Integration** = *coupling* of unit operations; **Bioprocess Intensification** = *radical improvement* in productivity / size / cost.
> - **ISPR** removes product *during* fermentation via a coupled separation unit; driven by **Le Chatelier's principle** to relieve product inhibition.
> - **WBP** feeds *whole broth* (with cells) directly to the recovery step, **eliminating centrifugation**.
> - **Single-stage extraction recovery:** $E_1 = \dfrac{K_D \theta}{1 + K_D \theta}$.
> - **N-stage cross-current recovery:** $E_{total} = 1 - \left( \dfrac{1}{1 + K_D \theta} \right)^N$.
> - **Pervaporation flux:** $J = (P/\ell) \cdot (p_{feed} - p_{perm})$; selectivity $\alpha_{i/j} = (y_i/y_j)_{perm} / (x_i/x_j)_{feed}$.
> - **Membrane distillation flux:** $J_{MD} = K_{MD} \cdot \Delta T$.
> - **Langmuir adsorption capacity:** $q = q_{max} b C / (1 + b C)$.
> - **ISPR techniques:** Liquid–liquid extraction, perstraction, adsorption (XAD resins), ion exchange, pervaporation, membrane distillation, precipitation, gas stripping.
> - **ISPR placement:** *Internal* (in fermenter) or *external* (circulation loop).
> - **WBP advantages:** no centrifuge, no filter, lower CAPEX/OPEX, faster turnaround, suits high-cell-density broths.
> - **Washout criterion in external loop:** $D < D_c = \mu_{max} S_{in} / (K_S + S_{in})$.
> - **ISPR productivity enhancement** of **2–10×** is typical for inhibitory products; **1.5–3×** is typical for non-inhibitory products.
> - **Industrial examples:** Ethanol (pervaporation/vacuum stripping), lactic acid (reactive extraction with TOA), ABE (gas stripping), antibiotics (solvent extraction + adsorption), amino acids (ion exchange).
> - **Most-tested KTU formulas:** $E_1$, $E_{total}$, $Q_P$, $Y_{P/S}$, $J_{PV}$, $\alpha$, Langmuir $q$.
> - **Always state assumptions:** dilute solution, ideal Raoult's law (where used), single-stage equilibrium, no solvent loss, no cell death in extractant.
> - **Always provide units** in numerical answers: g L⁻¹ h⁻¹, kg m⁻³ day⁻¹, m³ h⁻¹, dimensionless for $K_D$ and $\alpha$.
> - **Always end with a conclusion sentence** in 14-mark answers — examiners reward synthesis.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Principles and Advantages of Process Intensification; Economics of Industrial Bioseparations: Capital Cost and Operating Cost Estimation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEBBT525 - BIOSEPARATION TECHNOLOGY > - **Module:** Module Module 4 > - **Topic:** Principles and Advantages of Process Intensification; Economics of Industrial Bioseparations: Capital Cost and Operating Cost Estimation # MODULE 4 — SECTION 1: CONCEPTUAL FOUNDATIONS & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW ## 1.1 Process Intensification (PI) — Formal Definition > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight — Definition (Stankiewicz & Moulijn, 2000)** > *Process Intensification (PI) is defined as any chemical engineering development that leads to a substantially smaller, cleaner, safer, and more energy-efficient technology.* It is the systematic methodology of making dramatic reductions in **plant volume** (typically a factor of **100 to 1000×**) while maintaining or improving the conversion, selectivity, and throughput of a process. In the context of **bioseparations**, PI refers to the redesign and integration of unit operations—such as chromatography, extraction, membrane filtration, crystallization, and distillation—to achieve order-of-magnitude improvements in equipment size, energy consumption, waste generation, and overall process economics. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Shrinking Factory" > [!IMPORTANT] > **Plain-English Analogy** > Imagine you own a massive 10,000 sq. ft. traditional bakery that produces 1,000 loaves per hour. Process Intensification is like **redesigning that bakery into a smart 200 sq. ft. automated micro-bakery** that produces the *same* 1,000 loaves per hour — but uses **90 % less floor space, 70 % less energy, fewer workers, and generates almost zero waste** because every unit operation (mixing → proofing → baking → cooling → packaging) is now coupled, miniaturized, and made smarter through novel equipment and techniques. > > In bioseparations, the *factory* is your **purification train** (centrifuge → chromatography → UF/DF → viral filtration), and PI seeks to compress this train into a footprint that is **orders of magnitude smaller** while delivering the same purity, yield, and throughput. --- ## 1.3 Core Principles of Process Intensification According to the universally accepted framework proposed by **Stankiewicz & Moulijn (2000)**, PI is governed by two overarching domains, each containing multiple principles: ### A. Equipment-Based Intensification | # | Principle | What It Means in Bioseparations | |---|-----------|--------------------------------| | 1 | **Equipment size reduction** | Using high-gravity fields, centrifugal/electric fields, or micro-fluidics to shrink equipment (e.g., disk-stack vs. tubular centrifuge). | | 2 | **Use of alternative energy fields** | Replacing gravity with centrifugal, electrical, ultrasonic, or magnetic fields (e.g., magnetic bead capture of proteins instead of packed-bed chromatography). | | 3 | **Combining functions / reaction–separation integration** | Performing two or more operations simultaneously in one device (e.g., reactive extraction, *in-situ* product removal). | ### B. Method-Based Intensification | # | Principle | What It Means in Bioseparations | |---|-----------|--------------------------------| | 4 | **Process dynamics / optimal control** | Using advanced control strategies to push processes closer to their thermodynamic limits (e.g., Model-Predictive Control of chromatography gradients). | | 5 | **Novel operating modes** | Operating in transient, unsteady-state, or periodic regimes rather than steady state (e.g., simulated moving bed (SMB) chromatography vs. batch elution). | | 6 | **Multifunctional reactors / hybrid separations** | Combining reaction and separation in one step (e.g., extractive fermentation, membrane bioreactors). | > [!NOTE] > **KTU High-Yield Mnemonic — "EEM + DON"** > **E**quipment, **E**nergy fields, **M**ulti-function (Equipment side) + > **D**ynamics, **O**perating modes, **N**ovel reactors (Method side). --- ## 1.4 Economics of Industrial Bioseparations — Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme)** > The economics of industrial bioseparations deals with the systematic estimation and analysis of **capital expenditure (CAPEX)** — the one-time money needed to design, build, and commission a separation facility — and **operating expenditure (OPEX)** — the recurring money spent every year to run the plant, including raw materials, utilities, labor, and maintenance. > > The objective is to determine the **total cost of a purified bioproduct** (e.g., ₹/gram of therapeutic antibody) and to assess the **profitability** of the process via metrics such as Payback Period, Return on Investment (ROI), Net Present Value (NPV), and Discounted Cash Flow Rate of Return (DCFRR). --- ## 1.5 Conceptual Analogy — "Buying vs. Running a Car" > [!IMPORTANT] > **Plain-English Analogy** > Buying a process plant is like **buying a luxury car**: > - **Capital Cost (CAPEX)** = the *showroom price* of the car (one-time). > - **Operating Cost (OPEX)** = the *fuel, insurance, service, tyres* you pay every year. > - The **total cost per kilometre driven** is your "unit cost" — and you want it **as low as possible**. > - The **payback period** is how many kilometres you must drive before the car's productivity "earns back" its purchase price. > > In a **bioseparation plant**, CAPEX includes fermentor-broth hold tanks, centrifuges, chromatography skids, UF/DF systems, glassware, piping, instrumentation, and buildings. OPEX includes resins, buffers, membranes, steam, electricity, chilled water, clean-in-place (CIP) chemicals, labour, and quality-control consumables. # MODULE 4 — SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU FORMULA SHEET ## 2.1 The Principles of PI — Detailed Engineering Logic ### 2.1.1 Equipment-Based Intensification **Step 1 — Size Reduction:** All mass-transfer operations obey the *transfer equation*: $$J = k_L \, a \, (C^* - C)$$ where $k_L$ is the mass-transfer coefficient, $a$ is the interfacial area per unit volume, and $(C^* - C)$ is the driving force. To achieve the **same flux in a smaller volume**, PI increases $k_L$ and $a$ by orders of magnitude using: - **High centrifugal fields** (e.g., disk-stack centrifuges operating at **10,000 × g**). - **Static mixers and microreactors** (specific surface area up to **10,000 m²/m³** vs. ~100 m²/m³ in conventional vessels). - **Monolithic chromatography columns** (area ~400 m²/m³ vs. ~20 m²/m³ for packed beads). **Step 2 — Alternative Energy Fields:** Classical separations use *gravity* or *pressure*. PI replaces these with: - **Centrifugal fields** → high-speed disk-stack and tubular centrifuges. - **Electrical fields** → electrodialysis, membrane chromatography with charged ligands. - **Magnetic fields** → MagSep™ bead-based affinity capture. - **Ultrasonic fields** → acoustic cell-disruption for intracellular product release. **Step 3 — Combining Functions (Reactive Separations):** In bioprocessing, the best example is **extractive fermentation**, where product removal occurs *in situ*, eliminating feedback inhibition and removing a downstream recovery step. ### 2.1.2 Method-Based Intensification **Step 4 — Process Dynamics & Optimal Control:** Uses real-time sensors (PAT — Process Analytical Technology) and Model-Predictive Control (MPC) to operate at the **edge of feasible operating windows**, maximizing mass-transfer driving forces. **Step 5 — Novel Operating Modes:** A textbook example is **Simulated Moving Bed (SMB) chromatography**, which mimics a *continuous counter-current* bed using a *periodic* port-switching pattern — yielding 2–5× higher productivity than batch elution chromatography. **Step 6 — Multifunctional / Hybrid Devices:** **Membrane chromatography** (e.g., Sartobind® Q) merges *filtration* and *ion-exchange* into a single device, replacing two unit operations. --- ## 2.2 Advantages of Process Intensification — Engineering Payoff Table | # | Advantage | Quantitative Benchmark | Relevance to Bioseparations | |---|-----------|------------------------|------------------------------| | 1 | **Smaller plant footprint** | 100 – 1000× volume reduction | Classified buffer-prep & hold tanks shrink dramatically. | | 2 | **Lower energy consumption** | 30 – 90 % energy savings | Reduced chilled-water and steam loads in chromatography. | | 3 | **Higher mass-transfer rates** | $k_La$ increases 10–100× | Centrifugal extractors instead of gravity settlers. | | 4 | **Reduced waste / greener processing** | 50 – 80 % solvent reduction | Single-pass membrane chromatography vs. gradient elution. | | 5 | **Improved safety** | Inventory of hazardous solvents ↓ | Continuous flow instead of large batch solvent drums. | | 6 | **Faster R&D scale-up** | Time-to-market ↓ 30 – 50 % | Modular skid-based PI units. | | 7 | **Lower CAPEX & OPEX** | Up to 40 % total cost reduction | Smaller clean rooms, fewer operators, less utility piping. | > [!TIP] > **KTU Memory Hook — "7 Sins of Conventional Processing Solved by PI"** > *Size, Solvent, Slowness, Safety, Spillage, Set-up cost, Sustainability* → all improved by PI. --- ## 2.3 Economics of Industrial Bioseparations — Cost Classification The **total annual cost of a bioproduct** is split into two main blocks: ### A. Capital Cost (CAPEX) Components | Item | Description | |------|-------------| | **ISBL** | Inside Battery Limits — major process equipment (centrifuges, columns, membranes). | | **OSBL** | Outside Battery Limits — utilities, storage, waste treatment. | | **Engineering cost** | Design, procurement, construction management (~10–30 % of ISBL). | | **Contingency** | Risk allowance (~5–15 % of direct cost). | | **Working capital** | 10–20 % of total CAPEX, recovered at end-of-life. | ### B. Operating Cost (OPEX) Components | Category | Examples in Bioseparation | |----------|---------------------------| | **Direct manufacturing** | Raw materials (resins, membranes, buffers), utilities (WFI, steam, chilled water), operating labour. | | **Fixed manufacturing** | Maintenance (3–10 % of ISBL/yr), depreciation, insurance, local taxes. | | **General expenses** | Administration, R&D, marketing, distribution. | --- ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **HIGH-YIELD FORMULA TABLE — Memorise this for Part-A and Part-B** | # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Units | |---|---------|---------|---------------| | 1 | $C = F_L \cdot C_{eq}$ | **Lang Factor Method** (CAPEX) | ₹, \$ | | 2 | $C_2 = C_1 \, (Q_2 / Q_1)^{0.6}$ | **Six-Tenths Rule** (capacity scaling) | ₹, \$ | | 3 | $C = C_{eq} \, (1 + \sum f_i)$ | **Factorial Method** (CAPEX with f1…f5) | ₹, \$ | | 4 | $C_{eq} = a + b \, S^n$ | **Six-Tenths variant** with equipment-cost correlation | ₹ vs. capacity S | | 5 | $\text{Unit Cost} = \dfrac{C_{capital}/n + C_{operating}}{P_{annual}}$ | **Per-unit production cost** | ₹/kg, \$/g | | 6 | $\text{Payback} = \dfrac{\text{Fixed Capital Investment}}{\text{Net Annual Cash Flow}}$ | Years to recover CAPEX | years | | 7 | $\text{ROI (\%)} = \dfrac{\text{Annual Profit}}{\text{Fixed Capital}} \times 100$ | Return on Investment | % | | 8 | $NPV = \sum_{t=0}^{N} \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+i)^t}$ | Net Present Value | ₹, \$ | | 9 | $NPV = 0 \Rightarrow i = \text{DCFRR}$ | Discounted Cash Flow Rate of Return | % | | 10 | $C_{working} = 0.10 \, C_{TCI} \text{ to } 0.20 \, C_{TCI}$ | Working capital estimate | ₹, \$ | | 11 | $\text{CEPCI ratio} = \dfrac{C_{now}}{C_{past}} \cdot \dfrac{\text{CEPCI}_{past}}{\text{CEPCI}_{now}}$ | Inflation adjustment using Chemical Engineering Plant Cost Index | – | --- ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Lang Factor ($F_L$)** is widely used in **conceptual design** when only the major equipment cost is known. For bioprocess plants (mostly fluids & solids handling), **$F_L \approx 4.28$ to 5.85**. - **Six-Tenths Rule** is used by process engineers to quickly *estimate* the cost of a larger plant based on a smaller reference plant. - **DCFRR** is the most rigorous profitability metric demanded by KTU 2024 long-answer questions. # MODULE 4 — SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Lang Factor CAPEX Equation ### 3.1.1 Concept When only the **purchased cost of major process equipment** ($C_{eq}$, in ₹ or \$) is known, the **total capital investment (TCI)** is approximated by multiplying $C_{eq}$ by a dimensionless **Lang factor $F_L$**: $$C_{TCI} = F_L \cdot C_{eq}$$ ### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Construction of $F_L$ The Lang factor is built up from five multiplicative contributions, each representing a fraction of the equipment cost: $$ \begin{aligned} F_L &= f_1 \cdot f_2 \cdot f_3 \cdot f_4 \cdot f_5 \\ \text{where: } & \\ f_1 &= \text{Equipment erection factor (piping + installation)} \approx 1.5 \\ f_2 &= \text{Instrumentation and control factor} \approx 1.3 \\ f_3 &= \text{Buildings and process structures factor} \approx 1.3 \\ f_4 &= \text{Utilities factor} \approx 1.3 \\ f_5 &= \text{Outside battery limits (OSBL) factor} \approx 1.3 \end{aligned} $$ Therefore, for a typical **fluid-processing bioseparation plant**: $$ \begin{aligned} F_L &= 1.5 \times 1.3 \times 1.3 \times 1.3 \times 1.3 \\ &= 1.5 \times (1.3)^4 \\ &= 1.5 \times 2.8561 \\ &\approx 4.28 \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > **KTU Interpretation** > A Lang factor of 4.28 means the total plant investment is approximately **4.28 times** the purchased cost of the major equipment alone. For **solid-handling bioprocess plants** (e.g., crystallization + drying), $F_L \approx 5.85$. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Factorial Method (Hand's Detailed Method) ### 3.2.1 Concept The **factorial method** decomposes CAPEX into *direct* and *indirect* cost factors, each expressed as a fraction of the **purchased-equipment cost delivered to site** ($C_{eq}$): $$ \begin{aligned} C_{TCI} &= C_{eq} \left[ 1 + f_1 + f_2 + f_3 + \ldots + f_n \right] \\ &= C_{eq} \left[ 1 + \sum_{i=1}^{n} f_i \right] \end{aligned} $$ ### 3.2.2 Standard KTU Factorial Factors | Factor | Description | Typical Range | |--------|-------------|---------------| | $f_1$ | Equipment installation | 0.30 – 0.60 | | $f_2$ | Piping | 0.30 – 0.60 | | $f_3$ | Instrumentation & control | 0.10 – 0.30 | | $f_4$ | Electrical | 0.10 – 0.20 | | $f_5$ | Buildings (process + auxiliary) | 0.20 – 0.60 | | $f_6$ | Yard improvement | 0.05 – 0.15 | | $f_7$ | Service facilities (utilities) | 0.20 – 0.60 | | $f_8$ | Engineering & supervision | 0.20 – 0.35 | | $f_9$ | Construction | 0.30 – 0.50 | | $f_{10}$ | Contingency | 0.05 – 0.15 | | $f_{11}$ | Working capital | 0.10 – 0.20 (often added separately) | > **Engineering logic** — every $f_i$ is empirically regressed from hundreds of installed plants and represents the *fractional overhead* on top of equipment cost. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Six-Tenths Rule (Capacity Scaling) ### 3.3.1 Concept A well-known empirical rule used in early-stage bioseparation plant sizing: $$C_2 = C_1 \, \left( \dfrac{Q_2}{Q_1} \right)^{0.6}$$ where $Q_1, Q_2$ are the capacities (e.g., kg/h of throughput). ### 3.3.2 Derivation of the 0.6 Exponent The cost of chemical equipment scales roughly with the *surface area* (heat exchangers) or *volume* (tanks). For a process where cost ∝ characteristic dimension$^x$: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{If } C &\propto L^x \\ \frac{C_2}{C_1} &= \left(\frac{L_2}{L_1}\right)^x \end{aligned} $$ For vessels: Volume ∝ $L^3$, and Cost ∝ Surface Area ∝ $L^2$. So: $$ \begin{aligned} C &\propto V^{2/3} \\ \text{Since } V &\propto Q \\ \therefore \frac{C_2}{C_1} &= \left(\frac{Q_2}{Q_1}\right)^{2/3} \approx \left(\frac{Q_2}{Q_1}\right)^{0.67} \end{aligned} $$ The empirical **0.6 exponent** is a *slight underestimate* of $2/3$ that gives better fits across multiple equipment classes. --- ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Payback Period, ROI, NPV, DCFRR ### 3.4.1 Payback Period (Years) $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Payback} &= \frac{\text{Total Fixed Capital Investment (FCI)}}{\text{Net Annual Cash Flow (NACF)}} \\ \text{units: years} \end{aligned} $$ ### 3.4.2 Return on Investment (%) $$ \begin{aligned} \text{ROI (\%)} &= \left( \frac{\text{Annual Profit}}{\text{FCI}} \right) \times 100 \end{aligned} $$ ### 3.4.3 Net Present Value (NPV) $$ \begin{aligned} NPV &= \sum_{t=0}^{N} \frac{CF_t}{(1+i)^t} \\ &= -C_{FCI} + \sum_{t=1}^{N} \frac{CF_t}{(1+i)^t} \end{aligned} $$ where $i$ is the discount rate (e.g., **10 % per annum** for typical pharma projects) and $CF_t$ is the cash flow in year $t$. ### 3.4.4 DCFRR (Internal Rate of Return) By definition, DCFRR is the value of $i$ that satisfies $NPV = 0$: $$ \begin{aligned} 0 &= -C_{FCI} + \sum_{t=1}^{N} \frac{CF_t}{(1+i_{DCFRR})^t} \\ i_{DCFRR} &= \text{DCFRR (solve by trial or interpolation)} \end{aligned} $$ --- ## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Board Pattern > [!NOTE] > **Numerical (14-Mark pattern, simplified)** — A bioseparation pilot plant has $C_{eq}$ = **₹ 50 lakh** (purchased equipment cost). Using Lang's method with $F_L = 4.28$, estimate the **Total Capital Investment (TCI)**. **Solution:** $$ \begin{aligned} C_{TCI} &= F_L \times C_{eq} \\ &= 4.28 \times 50 \text{ lakh} \\ &= 214 \text{ lakh} \\ &= ₹ \, 2.14 \, \text{Crore} \end{aligned} $$ If working capital is **15 %** of TCI: $$ \begin{aligned} C_{working} &= 0.15 \times 214 = 32.1 \text{ lakh} \end{aligned} $$ Therefore: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Grand Total Investment} &= 214 + 32.1 = 246.1 \text{ lakh} \\ &= ₹ \, 2.461 \text{ Crore} \end{aligned} $$ > [!TIP] > **Working Capital** is the *additional* cash a company must hold to keep the plant running day-to-day (inventories, accounts receivable, spare parts). It is **not part of fixed capital** but is part of *total capital*. --- ## 3.6 Numerical Example 2 — Payback Period A biopharma plant has **CAPEX = ₹ 100 Crore**, and the expected **annual net cash flow = ₹ 18 Crore**. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Payback Period} &= \frac{100}{18} \text{ years} \\ &= 5.56 \text{ years} \end{aligned} $$ **Engineering judgement:** Payback periods < 6 years are typically attractive in pharma; > 10 years are usually rejected. # MODULE 4 — SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Principles of Process Intensification ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph A["EQUIPMENT-BASED INTENSIFICATION"] A1["Equipment Size Reduction
high gravity fields, microreactors"] A2["Alternative Energy Fields
centrifugal, electric, magnetic, ultrasonic"] A3["Combined Functions
reaction plus separation in one device"] end subgraph B["METHOD-BASED INTENSIFICATION"] B1["Process Dynamics
PAT and Model Predictive Control"] B2["Novel Operating Modes
SMB chromatography, periodic flow"] B3["Multifunctional Reactors
membrane bioreactors, extractive fermentation"] end PI["PROCESS INTENSIFICATION
100 to 1000 times smaller plant
30 to 90 percent energy saving"] OUT["OUTCOMES
Smaller Footprint
Lower CAPEX and OPEX
Higher Mass Transfer Rates
Improved Safety
Reduced Waste"] A1 --> PI A2 --> PI A3 --> PI B1 --> PI B2 --> PI B3 --> PI PI --> OUT ``` ## 4.2 Block Diagram — Capital Cost (CAPEX) Estimation Hierarchy ```mermaid flowchart TD CEQ["Purchased Equipment Cost
Ceq"] ISBL["ISBL Inside Battery Limits
major process equipment cost"] OSBL["OSBL Outside Battery Limits
utilities storage waste"] ENG["Engineering and Supervision
10 to 30 percent of ISBL"] CONT["Contingency
5 to 15 percent allowance"] WC["Working Capital
10 to 20 percent of TCI"] TCI["TOTAL CAPITAL INVESTMENT TCI"] CEQ --> ISBL ISBL --> OSBL OSBL --> ENG ENG --> CONT CONT --> WC WC --> TCI LF["Lang Factor Method
F sub L = 4.28 to 5.85"] -.-> CEQ FM["Factorial Method
C = Ceq times 1 plus sum f sub i"] -.-> CEQ SX["Six Tenths Rule
C2 = C1 times Q2 by Q1 to 0.6"] -.-> CEQ ``` ## 4.3 Block Diagram — Operating Cost (OPEX) Breakdown ```mermaid flowchart LR OPEX["OPERATING COST OPEX"] subgraph DM["DIRECT MANUFACTURING COST"] RM["Raw Materials
resins, membranes, buffers"] UT["Utilities
steam, chilled water, WFI, electricity"] OL["Operating Labour
operators and supervisors"] end subgraph FM["FIXED MANUFACTURING COST"] MAINT["Maintenance
3 to 10 percent of ISBL per year"] DEP["Depreciation
straight line or declining balance"] INS["Insurance and Local Taxes"] end subgraph GE["GENERAL EXPENSES"] ADM["Administration"] RD["Research and Development"] MKT["Marketing and Distribution"] end OPEX --> DM OPEX --> FM OPEX --> GE DM --> UC["UNIT PRODUCTION COST
Rs per kg or Dollar per gram"] FM --> UC GE --> UC ``` ## 4.4 Block Diagram — Profitability Analysis Workflow (DCFRR / NPV) ```mermaid flowchart TD INV["CAPEX and Working Capital Investment
at t = 0"] CF1["Year 1 Cash Flow"] CF2["Year 2 Cash Flow"] CFN["Year N Cash Flow"] DISC["Discount Factor 1 by 1 plus i to power t"] SUM["Summation of Discounted Cash Flows"] NPV["NPV = sum minus Investment"] DCFRR["Solve NPV = 0 for i
i equals DCFRR"] DEC["Decision
Accept if DCFRR greater than hurdle rate
Reject otherwise"] INV --> SUM CF1 --> DISC --> SUM CF2 --> DISC CFN --> DISC SUM --> NPV --> DCFRR --> DEC ``` ## 4.5 Schematic — How PI Improves Bioseparation Economics ```mermaid flowchart LR CONV["CONVENTIONAL PURIFICATION TRAIN
centrifuge then chromatography then UF
Large footprint
High buffer volumes
Long cycle times"] PI["INTENSIFIED TRAIN
monolithic columns plus membrane chromatography plus single pass TFF
10 to 100 times smaller
Continuous operation
Lower buffer consumption"] CAPEX["CAPEX Reduction 30 to 50 percent"] OPEX["OPEX Reduction 40 to 70 percent"] TIME["Cycle Time Reduction 50 to 80 percent"] QOQ["Quality of Product Maintained or Improved"] CONV -.-> CAPEX CONV -.-> OPEX CONV -.-> TIME PI --> CAPEX PI --> OPEX PI --> TIME PI --> QOQ ``` # MODULE 4 — SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP --- ## PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions (Module 4) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Define Process Intensification. List any two principles of PI.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (2 marks):** Process Intensification (PI) is any chemical-engineering development that leads to a substantially **smaller, cleaner, safer, and more energy-efficient** technology. As proposed by Stankiewicz and Moulijn, PI aims for a plant-volume reduction of **100 to 1000 times** while maintaining or improving conversion, selectivity, and throughput. **Any two principles (1 mark):** 1. *Equipment-based*: Use of alternative energy fields such as centrifugal, electrical, or magnetic fields in place of gravity. 2. *Method-based*: Adoption of novel operating modes such as Simulated Moving Bed (SMB) chromatography in place of conventional batch elution. --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]` **Distinguish between Capital Cost (CAPEX) and Operating Cost (OPEX) for an industrial bioseparation plant.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | CAPEX | OPEX | |--------|-------|------| | **Meaning** | One-time investment to design, build and commission the plant. | Recurring annual expenditure to operate the plant. | | **Examples** | Centrifuges, chromatography columns, UF membranes, buildings, engineering, contingency. | Raw materials (resins, buffers), utilities (steam, WFI, electricity), labour, maintenance, insurance. | | **Treatment in books** | Depreciated over plant life. | Expensed every year. | (2 marks for distinction + 1 mark for example lists.) --- ## PART B — 14-Mark Long-Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice) ### QUESTION A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]` **(a)** *Explain the principles and advantages of Process Intensification with suitable bioseparation examples.* **(7 marks)** **(b)** *Describe the Lang Factor method and the Factorial method for estimating the capital cost of a bioseparation plant. State typical values used.* **(7 marks)** --- ### Model Solution — Q A (a) — Principles & Advantages of PI (7 marks) **1. Definition (1 mark):** PI is the engineering strategy to achieve order-of-magnitude reduction in plant volume while improving safety, energy efficiency, and sustainability. **2. Equipment-Based Principles (2 marks):** - **Equipment size reduction** — disk-stack centrifuges and monolithic columns shrink equipment size by 10–100×. - **Alternative energy fields** — centrifugal (10,000 × g), electrical (electrodialysis), magnetic (MagSep™ bead capture), ultrasonic (acoustic disruption). - **Combining functions** — extractive fermentation merges reaction and product recovery in one unit. **3. Method-Based Principles (2 marks):** - **Process dynamics / PAT-based control** — Model Predictive Control (MPC) of chromatography. - **Novel operating modes** — Simulated Moving Bed (SMB) chromatography gives 2–5× higher productivity than batch. - **Multifunctional reactors / hybrid separations** — membrane chromatography (e.g., Sartobind Q) merges filtration with ion exchange. **4. Advantages (2 marks):** - Smaller plant footprint (100–1000×) — reduces classified cleanroom area. - Energy savings of 30–90 %. - 50–80 % solvent/buffer reduction → greener, cheaper processing. - Improved safety (smaller solvent inventories). - Faster scale-up → reduced time-to-market for biologics. **Conclusion line (optional):** *In bioseparations, PI is no longer optional — it is the strategic lever for low-cost biologics manufacturing.* --- ### Model Solution — Q A (b) — CAPEX Estimation Methods (7 marks) **1. Lang Factor Method (3 marks):** The total capital investment (TCI) is related to the purchased cost of major process equipment ($C_{eq}$) by the *Lang factor* $F_L$: $$C_{TCI} = F_L \cdot C_{eq}$$ The factor is constructed as the product of five sub-factors (erection, instrumentation, buildings, utilities, OSBL): $$F_L = f_1 \cdot f_2 \cdot f_3 \cdot f_4 \cdot f_5 \approx 1.5 \times 1.3^4 \approx 4.28 \text{ (fluids)}$$ $$F_L \approx 5.85 \text{ (solids)}$$ **2. Factorial Method (3 marks):** A more detailed method where each cost category (piping, instrumentation, electrical, buildings, yard, utilities, engineering, construction, contingency) is expressed as a fraction $f_i$ of $C_{eq}$: $$C_{TCI} = C_{eq} \, \left[ 1 + \sum_{i=1}^{n} f_i \right]$$ Typical values (state 4–5 of them): | Factor | Range | |--------|-------| | Installation $f_1$ | 0.30 – 0.60 | | Piping $f_2$ | 0.30 – 0.60 | | Instrumentation $f_3$ | 0.10 – 0.30 | | Buildings $f_5$ | 0.20 – 0.60 | | Contingency $f_{10}$ | 0.05 – 0.15 | **3. Comparison (1 mark):** Lang factor is *quick* (used in preliminary screening), Factorial is *detailed* (used in feasibility-stage cost engineering). --- ### QUESTION B — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **(a)** *Explain the concept and significance of Process Intensification in the context of bioseparation technology.* **(7 marks)** **(b)** *Describe the different components of operating cost (OPEX) in an industrial bioseparation plant and explain how unit production cost is calculated.* **(7 marks)** --- ### Model Solution — Q B (a) — Concept & Significance of PI (7 marks) **1. Concept (2 marks):** PI is the strategic redesign of equipment and methods to achieve dramatic reductions in plant size, energy, waste, and cost while improving safety and product quality. It is operationalised through two complementary domains — equipment-based and method-based intensification (Stankiewicz & Moulijn framework). **2. Significance in Bioseparations (3 marks):** - *Cost* — Therapeutic proteins cost ~₹ 50,000–5,00,000 per gram; even 30 % OPEX reduction translates into crores of rupees saved annually. - *Sustainability* — Buffer-preparation accounts for ~40 % of a downstream plant's water footprint; PI technologies (single-pass TFF, membrane chromatography) cut this by up to 70 %. - *Speed* — Continuous capture (e.g., periodic counter-current chromatography) reduces bottleneck in manufacturing campaigns, accelerating time-to-market for new biologics. - *Quality* — PI often operates at higher selectivity, reducing impurities and improving product purity. **3. Examples (2 marks):** - Monolithic chromatography columns replacing soft agarose beads. - Single-pass tangential-flow filtration replacing multi-stage UF/DF. - MagSep™ bead-based affinity capture replacing Protein A columns. --- ### Model Solution — Q B (b) — OPEX Components & Unit Cost (7 marks) **1. Classification (3 marks):** Operating cost is grouped into three categories: **(i) Direct Manufacturing Costs (variable with throughput):** - *Raw materials* — chromatography resins, membranes, salts, buffers. - *Utilities* — WFI (Water-for-Injection), pure steam, chilled water, electricity. - *Operating labour* — shift operators, supervisors, QA/QC technicians. **(ii) Fixed Manufacturing Costs (independent of throughput):** - *Maintenance* — typically 3–10 % of ISBL per year. - *Depreciation* — straight-line over plant life (~10 years for pharma). - *Insurance and local taxes*. **(iii) General Expenses (overheads):** - Administration, R&D, marketing and distribution. **2. Unit Production Cost Formula (3 marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Unit Cost} = \frac{(C_{capital} / n) + C_{operating,annual}}{P_{annual}} \end{aligned} $$ where $C_{capital}/n$ is the *annualised* capital cost (depreciation), $C_{operating,annual}$ is the annual OPEX, and $P_{annual}$ is the annual production (kg/yr). Worked example (1 mark): If $C_{capital} = 100$ lakh, $n = 10$ years, $C_{operating} = 40$ lakh/yr, $P = 50,000$ units/yr: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Unit Cost} &= \frac{(100/10) + 40}{50{,}000} \\ &= \frac{10 + 40}{50{,}000} \\ &= \frac{50}{50{,}000} \\ &= 0.001 \text{ lakh/unit} = ₹ 100 \text{ per unit} \end{aligned} $$ **3. Conclusion (1 mark):** Reducing OPEX (especially buffer and resin consumption) via PI technologies is the single biggest lever for reducing unit production cost in bioseparations. --- ## KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING / PITFALL CALLOUT > [!WARNING] > **Where KTU Students Lose Marks — Read Carefully!** > > 1. **Confusing CAPEX with Working Capital** — Working capital is *not* depreciated. It is recovered at the end of project life. Listing it under depreciation will cost **1–2 marks**. > 2. **Forgetting units in numerical answers** — KTU examiners strictly deduct **0.5 marks** per answer if units are missing. Always write ₹/kg, years, or %. > 3. **Wrong Lang factor value** — For *fluid* bioprocess plants, $F_L \approx 4.28$; for *solids* plants, $F_L \approx 5.85$. Writing a single Lang factor without specifying the plant type loses **0.5 marks**. > 4. **Mixing Payback Period and ROI** — Payback is in *years*, ROI is in *%*. Confusing them is a common error. > 5. **Skipping the derivation of NPV** — When solving DCFRR problems, always show the *equation* and the *iterative trial* or *interpolation formula*. Skipping this costs **2–3 marks**. > 6. **In PI questions, students often forget to give an example** — Stating "SMB chromatography" or "disk-stack centrifuge" without naming it loses a mark. **Always anchor PI principles with a concrete bioseparation example.** > 7. **OPEX direct vs fixed** — Direct OPEX scales with throughput; fixed OPEX does not. Examiners frequently test this distinction. --- ## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Module 4 — One-Page Revision Card** ### A. Process Intensification (PI) - [ ] **Definition** — Stankiewicz & Moulijn: substantially smaller, cleaner, safer, more energy-efficient technology (100–1000× volume reduction). - [ ] **Equipment-based principles** — Size reduction, alternative energy fields, combined functions. - [ ] **Method-based principles** — Process dynamics (PAT/MPC), novel operating modes (SMB), multifunctional reactors. - [ ] **Advantages** — Smaller footprint, 30–90 % energy saving, higher mass-transfer rates, reduced waste, lower CAPEX & OPEX, improved safety. - [ ] **Bioseparation examples** — Monolithic chromatography, single-pass TFF, membrane chromatography, magnetic-bead capture, SMB chromatography, disk-stack centrifuges, extractive fermentation. ### B. Economics — CAPEX - [ ] **Lang Factor method** — $C_{TCI} = F_L \cdot C_{eq}$ with $F_L \approx 4.28$ (fluid) or $5.85$ (solid). - [ ] **Factorial method** — $C_{TCI} = C_{eq} \left[ 1 + \sum f_i \right]$; remember typical ranges for $f_1$–$f_{11}$. - [ ] **Six-tenths rule** — $C_2 = C_1 (Q_2/Q_1)^{0.6}$. - [ ] **CAPEX components** — ISBL + OSBL + Engineering + Contingency + Working Capital. ### C. Economics — OPEX - [ ] **Direct manufacturing costs** — Raw materials, utilities, operating labour (variable with throughput). - [ ] **Fixed manufacturing costs** — Maintenance, depreciation, insurance, taxes. - [ ] **General expenses** — Administration, R&D, marketing, distribution. - [ ] **Unit production cost** — $[(C_{capital}/n) + C_{operating}] / P_{annual}$. ### D. Profitability Metrics - [ ] **Payback Period (yr)** = FCI / NACF. - [ ] **ROI (%)** = (Annual Profit / FCI) × 100. - [ ] **NPV** = $\sum CF_t / (1+i)^t - C_{FCI}$. - [ ] **DCFRR** — Discount rate at which NPV = 0. - [ ] **Decision rule** — Accept if DCFRR > hurdle rate (typically 12–15 %). ### E. KTU Board Exam Pattern Pointers - [ ] Always mention *units* in numerical answers. - [ ] Always *give a bioseparation example* when explaining PI principles. - [ ] Always show *derivation steps* in DCFRR / NPV problems. - [ ] Always state the *plant type* (fluid vs. solid) when using the Lang factor.

topics.title: Profitability Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT525 - BIOSEPARATION TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Profitability Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Profitability Analysis** in the context of Bioseparation Process Engineering is defined as the systematic quantitative evaluation of the economic returns generated by a bioproduct recovery and purification train, expressed as the ratio of net earnings (revenues minus total costs including capital recovery, operating costs, and taxes) to the total capital investment over a defined project life. It encompasses all engineering-economic techniques used to judge whether a downstream processing (DSP) scheme — comprising unit operations such as cell disruption, centrifugation, chromatography, membrane filtration, precipitation, crystallization, and drying — is financially viable over its operating horizon.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Examiner Tone):**
> *"Profitability analysis is the engineering-economic technique that quantifies the financial performance of a chemical or bioprocess plant by relating its annual net profit (gross income minus total annual cost) to the total capital investment, yielding decision-making metrics such as Rate of Return on Investment (ROI), Payback Period (PBP), Net Present Value (NPV), Internal Rate of Return (IRR), and Discounted Cash Flow Rate of Return (DCFRR)."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Street-Food Cart"**
>
> Imagine you set up a small *masala-dosa* cart. Before you start frying dosas, you need money to buy a cart (fixed capital), a gas cylinder, oil, and batter ingredients (working capital). Every day you earn ₹1,500 in sales but spend ₹900 on batter, gas, and rent. The **profitability analysis** is like a daily money-check:
> - *How many days will it take to recover the ₹25,000 you spent on the cart?* → **Payback Period**
> - *After one year, will you actually be richer, or just break even?* → **Net Present Value**
> - *What's the percentage return on your ₹25,000 investment?* → **Rate of Return**
>
> Apply the same idea to a **bioseparation plant** purifying insulin or a monoclonal antibody: the "cart" is the chromatography column, the centrifuges, the ultrafiltration skid; the "batter" is the buffer, resin, and electricity; and the "dosa sales" are the purified bioproduct revenue.

## 1.3 Why Profitability Analysis is Critical in Bioseparation

In **PEBBT525 — Bioseparation Technology**, downstream processing typically consumes **60–80 %** of the total manufacturing cost of a biopharmaceutical product. Therefore, any process scheme — whether it is a packed-bed Protein A chromatography train or a multi-stage aqueous two-phase extraction (ATPS) system — must clear a strict profitability threshold before commercial scale-up. The metrics used in this module are evaluated at **constant money (before inflation adjustment)** unless otherwise stated.

## 1.4 Key Physical / Financial Constants and Standard Metrics

| Standard Metric | Symbol | Typical Range / Value |
|---|---|---|
| Minimum Acceptable Rate of Return (MARR) / Discount Rate | $i$ or $r$ | **8 % – 15 %** per annum for biopharma |
| Project Life | $n$ | **10 – 15 years** |
| Corporate Tax Rate (India, old regime) | $t$ | **25.168 % – 30 %** |
| Working Capital Fraction | $W$ | **10 % – 20 %** of Fixed Capital |
| Salvage Value Fraction | $s$ | **5 % – 10 %** of Fixed Capital |
| Lang Factor (for fluid-processing plants) | $f_L$ | **3.0 – 4.0** |
| Operating Labour | $C_{OL}$ | Plant-specific |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Convention:** All currency values are quoted in **₹ (INR)** or **\$ (USD)**. All time periods are in **years** unless explicitly stated. Discounting convention: **end-of-year cash flows** (ordinary annuity), unless the problem says "beginning-of-year" (annuity-due).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Two Pillars of Profitability Analysis

Profitability of any bioseparation plant rests on **two pillars**:

1. **Capital Investment (I)** — the one-time, up-front money required to design, build, and start the plant.
2. **Total Product Cost (C)** — the recurring annual cost of converting raw fermentation broth into purified bioproduct.

The **gross income (S)** is the annual revenue from selling the purified product. The **net profit** is what remains after subtracting costs and taxes.

## 2.2 Capital Investment — Detailed Breakdown

The **total capital investment** $I_{TC}$ is the sum of **fixed capital investment ($I_{FC}$)** and **working capital ($I_{WC}$)**.

$$
I_{TC} = I_{FC} + I_{WC}
$$

### 2.2.1 Fixed Capital Investment ($I_{FC}$)

$$
I_{FC} = I_{PE} + I_{EL} + I_{I} + I_{C} + I_{S} + I_{L}
$$

| Term | Full Form | Typical % of $I_{FC}$ |
|---|---|---|
| $I_{PE}$ | Purchased Equipment (columns, centrifuges, UF skids) | **15 % – 40 %** |
| $I_{EL}$ | Equipment Installation | **~ 8 % – 10 %** of $I_{PE}$ |
| $I_{I}$ | Instrumentation & Controls | | 
| $I_{C}$ | Piping (very high for biopharma sanitary tubing) | **~ 10 % – 20 %** of $I_{PE}$ |
| $I_{S}$ | Structures & Buildings (cleanrooms, GMP suites) | | 
| $I_{L}$ | Land | | 

### 2.2.2 Working Capital ($I_{WC}$)

This is the capital tied up in:
- Finished product inventory (often cold-stored at 2–8 °C)
- Raw-material inventory (buffers, resins, filters awaiting use)
- Accounts receivable (money owed by buyers)
- Cash for day-to-day operations
- Minus accounts payable

> [!NOTE]
> **Six-Tenths Rule (Williams' Law):** For scaling equipment cost from a known base size to a new size:
> $$
> C_B = C_A \left( \frac{S_B}{S_A} \right)^{0.6}
> $$
> where exponent **0.6** is a typical average for process equipment; bioprocess chromatography columns use **0.7 – 0.8**.

### 2.2.3 The Lang Factor Shortcut

$$
I_{FC} = f_L \times C_{PE}
$$

For a **bioseparation plant** (mostly fluid handling, sanitary piping, stainless steel vessels), $f_L \approx 3.0$ for a grassroots plant, 3.5–4.0 if the equipment is largely fabricated on site.

## 2.3 Total Product Cost (Manufacturing Cost)

The **total annual product cost** $C$ (₹/year) is the sum of **manufacturing cost ($C_{M}$)** and **general expenses ($G_E$)**:

$$
C = C_M + G_E
$$

### 2.3.1 Manufacturing Cost

$$
C_M = C_{RM} + C_{UT} + C_{OL} + C_{SUP} + C_{MT} + C_{DP} + C_{FC}
$$

| Symbol | Meaning | Note |
|---|---|---|
| $C_{RM}$ | Raw Materials | **Major cost in bioseparation**: chromatography resins, buffer salts, single-use filters, water-for-injection (WFI) |
| $C_{UT}$ | Utilities (Utilities Treatment) | Cooling water, clean steam, WFI, HVAC for cleanrooms |
| $C_{OL}$ | Operating Labour | Direct labour |
| $C_{SUP}$ | Supervisory & Clerical Labour | |
| $C_{MT}$ | Maintenance & Repairs | **2 % – 6 %** of $I_{FC}$ |
| $C_{DP}$ | Depreciation | |
| $C_{FC}$ | Fixed Charges (insurance, rent, local taxes) | |

### 2.3.2 Depreciation — Straight-Line Method

$$
C_{DP} = \frac{I_{FC} - S_v}{n}
$$

where $S_v$ = salvage value, $n$ = project life in years. The **book value** at the end of year $k$ is:

$$
B_k = I_{FC} - k \cdot C_{DP}
$$

### 2.3.3 General Expenses

$$
G_E = A_d + D_R + T + R_{sales}
$$

- $A_d$: Administration
- $D_R$: Distribution & Marketing (often huge for biopharma — **15 % – 25 %** of sales)
- $T$: Transportation
- $R_{sales}$: R&D funded from current sales

## 2.4 Gross Profit, Net Profit, and Cash Flow

$$
\text{Gross Profit } (G_P) = S - C_M
$$

$$
\text{Net Profit before Tax} = S - C = S - (C_M + G_E)
$$

$$
\text{Income Tax} = (S - C - D_{allowable}) \times t
$$

$$
\text{Net Profit after Tax (NPAT)} = S - C - \text{Tax}
$$

### 2.4.1 Cash Flow Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cash flow is NOT profit.** Cash flow = Net Profit after Tax + Depreciation (a non-cash expense is added back).

$$
\text{Annual Cash Flow (CF)} = \text{NPAT} + C_{DP}
$$

## 2.5 The Five Core Profitability Metrics — KTU Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Killer Exam Tip:** KTU board questions **always** ask for these five. Memorize the exact formula and the units.

| # | Metric | Formula | Unit | Decision Rule |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Rate of Return on Investment (ROI)** | $\text{ROI} = \dfrac{\text{Net Profit per year}}{\text{Total Capital Investment}} \times 100$ | **% per year** | ROI > MARR → Accept |
| 2 | **Payback Period (PBP)** | $\text{PBP} = \dfrac{\text{Fixed Capital Investment}}{\text{Annual Cash Flow}}$ | **years** | Lower is better; PBP < 5 yrs typical for biopharma |
| 3 | **Net Present Value (NPV)** | $\text{NPV} = \sum_{k=0}^{n} \dfrac{\text{CF}_k}{(1+i)^k}$ | **₹ or \$** | NPV > 0 → Accept |
| 4 | **Internal Rate of Return (IRR)** | $\sum_{k=0}^{n} \dfrac{\text{CF}_k}{(1+\text{IRR})^k} = 0$ | **%** | IRR > MARR → Accept |
| 5 | **Return on Investment (Towell's)** | $\text{ROI}_{T} = \dfrac{\text{Annual Cash Flow}}{\text{Total Capital Investment}} \times 100$ | **% per year** | Similar to #1 but uses cash flow |

### 2.5.1 The Present-Worth Single-Payment Factor (P / F, i, n)

$$
(P / F, i, n) = \frac{1}{(1+i)^n}
$$

### 2.5.2 The Uniform-Series Present-Worth Factor (P / A, i, n)

$$
(P / A, i, n) = \frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i(1+i)^n}
$$

### 2.5.3 The Capital-Recovery Factor (A / P, i, n)

$$
(A / P, i, n) = \frac{i(1+i)^n}{(1+i)^n - 1}
$$

### 2.5.4 The Sinking-Fund Factor (A / F, i, n)

$$
(A / F, i, n) = \frac{i}{(1+i)^n - 1}
$$

## 2.6 Engineering Utility of Profitability Analysis in Bioseparation

- **Selection of downstream scheme:** Protein A vs. mixed-mode chromatography for monoclonal antibody capture — same purity, very different resin cost.
- **Make-or-buy decisions:** Should we buy bulk intermediate or purify in-house?
- **Capacity expansion:** Double the chromatography skid — is ROI still attractive?
- **Comparison of unit operations:** Centrifugation vs. microfiltration for cell harvest.
- **R&D project screening:** A novel aqueous two-phase extraction method is compared against packed-bed chromatography on cost-benefit grounds.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of the Time Value of Money Factor (P/F, i, n)

A rupee received at the end of year $n$ is worth less than a rupee received today because the rupee today could be invested at an interest rate $i$ and would grow to $(1+i)^n$ rupees by year $n$. To "discount" a future amount $F$ back to its present equivalent $P$, we divide by the growth factor:

$$
F = P (1 + i)^n
$$

Rearranging for $P$:

$$
P = \frac{F}{(1 + i)^n}
$$

This gives the single-payment present-worth factor:

$$
\boxed{\,P = F \cdot (P/F, i, n) = F \cdot \frac{1}{(1+i)^n}\,}
$$

> The factor $(1+i)^n$ is called the **single-payment compound-amount factor**, and its reciprocal is the **single-payment present-worth factor**.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Uniform-Series Present-Worth Factor (P/A, i, n)

Consider a uniform end-of-year cash flow $A$ occurring for $n$ years. The present worth at year 0 is the sum of $n$ discounted payments:

$$
P = \frac{A}{1+i} + \frac{A}{(1+i)^2} + \cdots + \frac{A}{(1+i)^n}
$$

$$
P = A \cdot \sum_{k=1}^{n} \frac{1}{(1+i)^k}
$$

This is a finite geometric series with first term $a_1 = \dfrac{1}{1+i}$ and common ratio $r = \dfrac{1}{1+i}$. Using the geometric-series sum formula $\sum_{k=0}^{n-1} a_1 r^k = a_1 \dfrac{1 - r^n}{1 - r}$ (re-indexed to $k=1$ to $n$):

$$
\sum_{k=1}^{n} \frac{1}{(1+i)^k} = \frac{\dfrac{1}{1+i}\left[ 1 - \left(\dfrac{1}{1+i}\right)^{n} \right]}{1 - \dfrac{1}{1+i}}
$$

Simplify the denominator: $1 - \dfrac{1}{1+i} = \dfrac{i}{1+i}$

$$
\sum_{k=1}^{n} \frac{1}{(1+i)^k} = \frac{\dfrac{1}{1+i}\left[ 1 - (1+i)^{-n} \right]}{\dfrac{i}{1+i}} = \frac{1 - (1+i)^{-n}}{i}
$$

Multiplying numerator and denominator by $(1+i)^n$:

$$
= \frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i(1+i)^n}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\,P = A \cdot (P/A, i, n) = A \cdot \frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i(1+i)^n}\,}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation of Net Present Value (NPV)

A bioseparation project has:
- An initial capital outlay $I_{TC}$ at year 0 (cash **outflow**).
- Annual cash inflows $\text{CF}_k$ for $k = 1, 2, \ldots, n$.
- A salvage value $S_v$ recovered at year $n$.

The NPV at discount rate $i$ is the sum of all present-valued cash flows:

$$
\text{NPV} = -I_{TC} + \sum_{k=1}^{n} \frac{\text{CF}_k}{(1+i)^k} + \frac{S_v}{(1+i)^n}
$$

When the cash flows are level (constant) for all $n$ years, the sum becomes a uniform series:

$$
\sum_{k=1}^{n} \frac{\text{CF}}{(1+i)^k} = \text{CF} \cdot (P/A, i, n)
$$

Hence for uniform cash flow:

$$
\boxed{\,\text{NPV} = -I_{TC} + \text{CF} \cdot \frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i(1+i)^n} + \frac{S_v}{(1+i)^n}\,}
$$

> **Decision:** Accept project if NPV $> 0$. Reject if NPV $< 0$. Indifferent if NPV $= 0$.

## 3.4 Derivation of the Internal Rate of Return (IRR)

By definition, IRR is the discount rate $i^* = \text{IRR}$ at which the NPV equals zero:

$$
0 = -I_{TC} + \text{CF} \cdot \frac{(1+\text{IRR})^n - 1}{\text{IRR}\,(1+\text{IRR})^n} + \frac{S_v}{(1+\text{IRR})^n}
$$

If salvage value is zero and cash flow is uniform, the equation reduces to:

$$
I_{TC} = \text{CF} \cdot \frac{(1+\text{IRR})^n - 1}{\text{IRR}\,(1+\text{IRR})^n}
$$

This is a transcendental equation in IRR and **cannot be solved analytically**. KTU board practice is to use **trial-and-error interpolation**:

$$
\text{IRR} = i_1 + \frac{\text{NPV}_1}{\text{NPV}_1 - \text{NPV}_2} \cdot (i_2 - i_1)
$$

where $i_1$ and $i_2$ are the two bracketing rates with NPV of opposite signs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Alternative Form (Towell's Approximation):** When $n$ is large, $\dfrac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i(1+i)^n} \to \dfrac{1}{i}$. So for long-life bioprocess plants, IRR $\approx \dfrac{\text{CF}}{I_{TC}}$, which is exactly the **Rate of Return on Investment** (ROI). KTU expects students to recognise this equivalence and quote it explicitly.

## 3.5 Derivation of the Payback Period (PBP)

The payback period is the time $T$ at which the cumulative undiscounted cash flow recovers the original investment. For a uniform annual cash flow CF:

$$
I_{FC} = \text{CF} \times T
$$

$$
\boxed{\,T = \frac{I_{FC}}{\text{CF}}\,}
$$

For non-uniform cash flows, the **cumulative cash flow** is tabulated year by year, and $T$ is found by linear interpolation between the last negative and the first positive cumulative balance.

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Board Style

**Problem (15 marks).** A biotech firm is evaluating a **packed-bed Protein A chromatography** capture step for monoclonal antibody (mAb) purification. The data are:

- Purchased cost of chromatography skid (column + skid + control system): $C_{PE} = ₹$ **3,00,00,000** (3 crore)
- Lang factor for a biopharma DSP plant: $f_L = 3.5$
- Working capital: **12 %** of fixed capital
- Annual revenue from purified mAb: $S = ₹$ **5,50,00,000**
- Total annual product cost: $C = ₹$ **3,80,00,000**
- Straight-line depreciation over $n = 10$ years, salvage negligible
- Tax rate: $t = 25$ %
- Discount rate: $i = 12$ % per annum

Find: (a) Total capital investment, (b) Annual cash flow, (c) ROI, (d) PBP, (e) NPV.

**Solution (Step-by-Step).**

**Step 1 — Fixed capital investment:**

$$
I_{FC} = f_L \times C_{PE} = 3.5 \times 3,00,00,000 = 10,50,00,000
$$

Numerically, $3.5 \times 3 \times 10^7 = 10.5 \times 10^7 = 1.05 \times 10^9$ (i.e., ₹ 10.5 crore).

**Step 2 — Working capital:**

$$
I_{WC} = 0.12 \times I_{FC} = 0.12 \times 10,50,00,000 = 1,26,00,000
$$

**Step 3 — Total capital investment:**

$$
I_{TC} = I_{FC} + I_{WC} = 10,50,00,000 + 1,26,00,000 = 11,76,00,000
$$

(₹ 11.76 crore).

**Step 4 — Annual depreciation (straight-line, salvage = 0):**

$$
C_{DP} = \frac{I_{FC}}{n} = \frac{10,50,00,000}{10} = 1,05,00,000
$$

**Step 5 — Net profit before tax:**

$$
G_{PT} = S - C = 5,50,00,000 - 3,80,00,000 = 1,70,00,000
$$

**Step 6 — Income tax:**

$$
\text{Tax} = G_{PT} \times t = 1,70,00,000 \times 0.25 = 42,50,000
$$

**Step 7 — Net profit after tax (NPAT):**

$$
\text{NPAT} = G_{PT} - \text{Tax} = 1,70,00,000 - 42,50,000 = 1,27,50,000
$$

**Step 8 — Annual cash flow:**

$$
\text{CF} = \text{NPAT} + C_{DP} = 1,27,50,000 + 1,05,00,000 = 2,32,50,000
$$

**Step 9 — Rate of Return on Investment (ROI):**

$$
\text{ROI} = \frac{\text{NPAT}}{I_{TC}} \times 100 = \frac{1,27,50,000}{11,76,00,000} \times 100 \approx 10.84 \,\%
$$

$$
\text{ROI}_{Towell} = \frac{\text{CF}}{I_{TC}} \times 100 = \frac{2,32,50,000}{11,76,00,000} \times 100 \approx 19.77 \,\%
$$

**Step 10 — Payback period (undiscounted, on fixed capital):**

$$
\text{PBP} = \frac{I_{FC}}{\text{CF}} = \frac{10,50,00,000}{2,32,50,000} \approx 4.52 \text{ years}
$$

**Step 11 — Net Present Value:**

Compute the present-worth annuity factor at $i = 12\%$, $n = 10$:

$$
(P/A, 12\%, 10) = \frac{(1.12)^{10} - 1}{0.12 \times (1.12)^{10}}
$$

$(1.12)^{10} = 3.10585$ (from standard tables).

$$
(P/A, 12\%, 10) = \frac{3.10585 - 1}{0.12 \times 3.10585} = \frac{2.10585}{0.37270} = 5.6502
$$

$$
\text{NPV} = -I_{TC} + \text{CF} \times (P/A, 12\%, 10) = -11,76,00,000 + 2,32,50,000 \times 5.6502
$$

$$
= -11,76,00,000 + 1,31,36,711 = + 13,36,711
$$

(approximately ₹ 13.37 lakh). Since NPV > 0, **accept the project**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiners love this two-table layout.** Always (a) state the formula, (b) substitute the numbers, (c) compute the result with units, and (d) end with a one-line decision.

## 3.7 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Parameter Change on Profitability

| If this parameter **increases** | ROI | PBP | NPV | IRR |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Purchased Equipment Cost ($C_{PE}$) | Decreases | Increases | Decreases | Decreases |
| Selling Price of Bioproduct ($S$) | Increases | Decreases | Increases | Increases |
| Discount Rate ($i$) | Unchanged | Unchanged | Decreases | Unchanged |
| Project Life ($n$) | Unchanged | Unchanged | Increases (if CF > 0) | Slight increase |
| Operating Cost ($C$) | Decreases | Increases | Decreases | Decreases |
| Tax Rate ($t$) | Decreases | Increases | Decreases | Decreases |
| Working Capital ($I_{WC}$) | Decreases | Increases | Decreases (at $t=0$) | Decreases |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common pitfall:** Discount rate $i$ does **not** affect ROI or PBP. It affects only NPV and IRR. KTU students often incorrectly mark ROI as "decreasing with higher $i$".

```

flowchart LR

flowchart TD

```

> [!NOTE]
