# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]
**Differentiate between cash flow and net profit in the context of a bioseparation plant. Why is cash flow (not net profit) used in the payback-period and NPV calculations?**

**Model Answer:**

> **Net Profit** is an accounting measure: revenue minus all expenses including the non-cash expense of depreciation. It is reported on the **Profit & Loss statement**.
>
> **Cash Flow** is the actual movement of money. It is computed as:
> $$\text{Cash Flow} = \text{Net Profit after Tax} + \text{Depreciation}$$
> because depreciation does not involve any outflow of cash, so it is added back to convert accounting profit into actual cash.
>
> **Why cash flow is used:** Payback period and NPV measure the *recovery of actual money* invested. Depreciation is merely a book entry; the cash has already gone out when the equipment was purchased. Hence, all time-value-based profitability metrics must be computed on cash flow, not on accounting profit. **(3 marks — 1 each for definition, formula, and reason.)**

### Question A2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]
**State the Six-Tenths Rule (Williams' Law) used in cost estimation of bioprocess equipment. Mention the typical exponent value for (a) general process equipment and (b) chromatography columns.**

**Model Answer:**

The Six-Tenths Rule relates the cost of an equipment $C$ to its size $S$:

$$
C_B = C_A \left( \frac{S_B}{S_A} \right)^{x}
$$

- (a) For general process equipment, the typical exponent is **0.6** (giving the rule its name).
- (b) For chromatography columns in bioseparation, the exponent is higher, typically **0.7 to 0.8**, because column cost scales nearly with bed volume (linear plus packing-cost contributions).

The rule is valid only for **similar equipment**, within a size range of roughly 0.5× to 2× the base. **(3 marks — 1 for formula, 1 for general exponent, 1 for chromatography exponent.)**

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Regulation:** Module-4 ESE Part B carries **14 marks**, split into two sub-parts of 7 marks each. The student answers **either** Option A **or** Option B. Both options are independent, complete, 14-mark questions.

---

### QUESTION A — 14 Marks

**Q. (A) [KTU University Exam — Sample Paper, Module 4]** A biopharmaceutical company is evaluating a **downstream processing plant** for the production of a recombinant therapeutic protein. The following data are given:

- Purchased equipment cost for the chromatography skid, centrifuge, UF/DF skid, and holding vessels: $C_{PE} = ₹$ **5,00,00,000**
- Lang factor for the bioprocess plant: $f_L = 4.0$
- Working capital: **15 %** of fixed capital investment
- Estimated annual sales (revenue): $S = ₹$ **9,00,00,000**
- Total annual operating cost: $C = ₹$ **6,20,00,000**
- Project life: $n = 10$ years
- Salvage value: $S_v = ₹$ **40,00,000**
- Income tax rate: $t = 25$ %
- Discount rate: $i = 10$ % per annum
- Depreciation: straight-line method

**Compute:**
**(a)** Total capital investment, annual depreciation, and annual cash flow. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Rate of Return on Investment (ROI), Payback Period (on fixed capital), and Net Present Value (NPV) at 10 % discount rate. State whether the project should be accepted. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Fixed Capital Investment (using Lang factor):**

$$
I_{FC} = f_L \times C_{PE} = 4.0 \times 5,00,00,000 = 20,00,00,000 \quad \text{(₹ 20 crore)}
$$

**Step 2 — Working Capital:**

$$
I_{WC} = 0.15 \times I_{FC} = 0.15 \times 20,00,00,000 = 3,00,00,000
$$

**Step 3 — Total Capital Investment:**

$$
I_{TC} = I_{FC} + I_{WC} = 20,00,00,000 + 3,00,00,000 = 23,00,00,000
$$

**Step 4 — Annual Depreciation (straight-line, including salvage):**

$$
C_{DP} = \frac{I_{FC} - S_v}{n} = \frac{20,00,00,000 - 40,00,000}{10} = \frac{19,60,00,000}{10} = 1,96,00,000
$$

**Step 5 — Gross Profit before Tax:**

$$
G_{PT} = S - C = 9,00,00,000 - 6,20,00,000 = 2,80,00,000
$$

**Step 6 — Tax:**

$$
\text{Tax} = 0.25 \times 2,80,00,000 = 70,00,000
$$

**Step 7 — Net Profit after Tax (NPAT):**

$$
\text{NPAT} = 2,80,00,000 - 70,00,000 = 2,10,00,000
$$

**Step 8 — Annual Cash Flow:**

$$
\text{CF} = \text{NPAT} + C_{DP} = 2,10,00,000 + 1,96,00,000 = 4,06,00,000
$$

**(Sub-part (a) = 7 marks; full marks for clearly stating each formula and showing substitution.)**

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 9 — Rate of Return on Investment (ROI):**

$$
\text{ROI} = \frac{\text{NPAT}}{I_{TC}} \times 100 = \frac{2,10,00,000}{23,00,00,000} \times 100 \approx 9.13 \,\% \text{ per year}
$$

**Step 10 — Payback Period (on fixed capital, undiscounted):**

$$
\text{PBP} = \frac{I_{FC}}{\text{CF}} = \frac{20,00,00,000}{4,06,00,000} \approx 4.93 \text{ years}
$$

**Step 11 — NPV calculation:**

Present-worth annuity factor at $i = 10\%$, $n = 10$:

$$
(P/A, 10\%, 10) = \frac{(1.10)^{10} - 1}{0.10 \times (1.10)^{10}} = \frac{2.5937 - 1}{0.10 \times 2.5937} = \frac{1.5937}{0.25937} \approx 6.1446
$$

Single-payment present-worth factor for the salvage at year 10:

$$
(P/F, 10\%, 10) = \frac{1}{(1.10)^{10}} = \frac{1}{2.5937} \approx 0.3855
$$

NPV equation:

$$
\text{NPV} = -I_{TC} + \text{CF} \times (P/A, 10\%, 10) + S_v \times (P/F, 10\%, 10)
$$

Substituting:

$$
\text{NPV} = -23,00,00,000 + 4,06,00,000 \times 6.1446 + 40,00,000 \times 0.3855
$$

$$
= -23,00,00,000 + 24,95,10,760 + 15,42,000
$$

$$
= + 2,10,52,760
$$

(approximately ₹ 2.11 crore positive).

**Step 12 — Decision:**

Since **NPV > 0** (₹ +2.11 crore), ROI ≈ 9.13 % (note: should be benchmarked against MARR; if MARR = 10 %, ROI is just below; if MARR = 8 %, ROI is acceptable), and **PBP = 4.93 years** (acceptable, < 5 years), the project is **ACCEPTABLE** in overall NPV terms and recommended for implementation.

**(Sub-part (b) = 7 marks; full marks for the three metrics + explicit decision.)**

---

### QUESTION B — 14 Marks (ALTERNATIVE CHOICE)

**Q. (B) [KTU University Exam — Sample Paper, Module 4]** An Indian biotech start-up plans to install a **monoclonal antibody (mAb) capture-and-polish train** consisting of Protein A chromatography, cation-exchange (CEX) chromatography, and an ultrafiltration/diafiltration (UF/DF) skid. The detailed cost data are:

- **Equipment costs:**
  - Protein A column + skid: $₹ 1,50,00,000$
  - CEX column + skid: $₹ 1,00,00,000$
  - UF/DF skid (membrane area 20 m²): $₹ 1,80,00,000$
  - Hold vessels, piping, instrumentation: $₹ 1,70,00,000$
- Installation factor: **35 %** of purchased equipment cost
- Working capital: **18 %** of fixed capital
- Annual sales revenue: $S = ₹ 7,50,00,000$
- Annual manufacturing cost: $C_M = ₹ 4,00,00,000$
- General expenses: $G_E = ₹ 80,00,000$
- Project life: $n = 12$ years
- Salvage value: $S_v = ₹ 50,00,000$
- Tax rate: $t = 30$ %
- Discount rate: $i = 12$ % per annum

**Compute:**
**(a)** Total purchased equipment cost, fixed capital investment, working capital, and total capital investment. Compute annual depreciation and annual cash flow. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** ROI, Payback Period, and NPV. Use the IRR-interpolation method to bracket IRR between **15 % and 20 %** and report IRR. State the decision. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Total Purchased Equipment Cost:**

$$
C_{PE} = 1,50,00,000 + 1,00,00,000 + 1,80,00,000 + 1,70,00,000 = 6,00,00,000
$$

**Step 2 — Installation Cost (35 % of $C_{PE}$):**

$$
I_{\text{inst}} = 0.35 \times 6,00,00,000 = 2,10,00,000
$$

**Step 3 — Fixed Capital Investment:**

$$
I_{FC} = C_{PE} + I_{\text{inst}} = 6,00,00,000 + 2,10,00,000 = 8,10,00,000
$$

**Step 4 — Working Capital:**

$$
I_{WC} = 0.18 \times I_{FC} = 0.18 \times 8,10,00,000 = 1,45,80,000
$$

**Step 5 — Total Capital Investment:**

$$
I_{TC} = I_{FC} + I_{WC} = 8,10,00,000 + 1,45,80,000 = 9,55,80,000
$$

**Step 6 — Annual Depreciation (straight-line):**

$$
C_{DP} = \frac{I_{FC} - S_v}{n} = \frac{8,10,00,000 - 50,00,000}{12} = \frac{7,60,00,000}{12} \approx 63,33,333
$$

**Step 7 — Total Annual Product Cost:**

$$
C = C_M + G_E = 4,00,00,000 + 80,00,000 = 4,80,00,000
$$

**Step 8 — Gross Profit before Tax:**

$$
G_{PT} = S - C = 7,50,00,000 - 4,80,00,000 = 2,70,00,000
$$

**Step 9 — Tax (30 %):**

$$
\text{Tax} = 0.30 \times 2,70,00,000 = 81,00,000
$$

**Step 10 — NPAT:**

$$
\text{NPAT} = 2,70,00,000 - 81,00,000 = 1,89,00,000
$$

**Step 11 — Annual Cash Flow:**

$$
\text{CF} = \text{NPAT} + C_{DP} = 1,89,00,000 + 63,33,333 \approx 2,52,33,333
$$

**(Sub-part (a) complete — 7 marks.)**

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 12 — ROI:**

$$
\text{ROI} = \frac{\text{NPAT}}{I_{TC}} \times 100 = \frac{1,89,00,000}{9,55,80,000} \times 100 \approx 19.77 \,\% \text{ per year}
$$

**Step 13 — Payback Period (on fixed capital):**

$$
\text{PBP} = \frac{I_{FC}}{\text{CF}} = \frac{8,10,00,000}{2,52,33,333} \approx 3.21 \text{ years}
$$

**Step 14 — NPV at 12 % discount rate:**

$(P/A, 12\%, 12)$ and $(P/F, 12\%, 12)$:

$$
(1.12)^{12} = 3.8960
$$

$$
(P/A, 12\%, 12) = \frac{3.8960 - 1}{0.12 \times 3.8960} = \frac{2.8960}{0.4675} \approx 6.1944
$$

$$
(P/F, 12\%, 12) = \frac{1}{3.8960} \approx 0.2567
$$

NPV:

$$
\text{NPV} = -I_{TC} + \text{CF} \times (P/A, 12\%, 12) + S_v \times (P/F, 12\%, 12)
$$

$$
= -9,55,80,000 + 2,52,33,333 \times 6.1944 + 50,00,000 \times 0.2567
$$

$$
= -9,55,80,000 + 15,62,52,914 + 12,83,500
$$

$$
\approx + 6,19,56,414
$$

(₹ 6.20 crore positive).

**Step 15 — IRR by interpolation (15 % and 20 %):**

At $i_1 = 15\%$:
$(1.15)^{12} = 5.3503$
$(P/A, 15\%, 12) = \dfrac{5.3503 - 1}{0.15 \times 5.3503} = \dfrac{4.3503}{0.8025} = 5.4206$
$(P/F, 15\%, 12) = 1/5.3503 = 0.1869$

$$
\text{NPV}_{15} = -9,55,80,000 + 2,52,33,333 \times 5.4206 + 50,00,000 \times 0.1869
$$

$$
= -9,55,80,000 + 13,67,79,915 + 9,34,500 \approx +4,21,34,415
$$

At $i_2 = 20\%$:
$(1.20)^{12} = 8.9161$
$(P/A, 20\%, 12) = \dfrac{8.9161 - 1}{0.20 \times 8.9161} = \dfrac{7.9161}{1.7832} = 4.4392$
$(P/F, 20\%, 12) = 1/8.9161 = 0.1122$

$$
\text{NPV}_{20} = -9,55,80,000 + 2,52,33,333 \times 4.4392 + 50,00,000 \times 0.1122
$$

$$
= -9,55,80,000 + 11,20,07,889 + 5,61,000 \approx +1,69,88,889
$$

Both NPVs are still positive even at 20 %, so IRR is **above 20 %**. To bracket properly, try $i_3 = 25\%$:

$(1.25)^{12} = 9.5456$
$(P/A, 25\%, 12) = \dfrac{9.5456 - 1}{0.25 \times 9.5456} = \dfrac{8.5456}{2.3864} = 3.5807$
$(P/F, 25\%, 12) = 1/9.5456 = 0.1048$

$$
\text{NPV}_{25} = -9,55,80,000 + 2,52,33,333 \times 3.5807 + 50,00,000 \times 0.1048
$$

$$
= -9,55,80,000 + 9,03,40,222 + 5,24,000 \approx -46,15,778
$$

NPV is now negative. Bracket is between 20 % and 25 %. Using the interpolation formula:

$$
\text{IRR} = i_2 + \frac{\text{NPV}_{20}}{\text{NPV}_{20} - \text{NPV}_{25}} \times (i_3 - i_2)
$$

$$
\text{IRR} = 20 + \frac{1,69,88,889}{1,69,88,889 - (-46,15,778)} \times (25 - 20)
$$

$$
= 20 + \frac{1,69,88,889}{2,16,04,667} \times 5 = 20 + 0.7863 \times 5 \approx 23.93 \,\%
$$

**Step 16 — Decision:**

- ROI ≈ 19.77 % per year
- PBP ≈ 3.21 years (excellent)
- NPV at 12 % = +₹ 6.20 crore (positive)
- IRR ≈ **23.93 %** > MARR (12 %)

**All four metrics recommend ACCEPTANCE** of the bioprocess scheme.

**(Sub-part (b) = 7 marks.)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps (avoid these to gain full 14 marks):**
>
> 1. **Using Net Profit instead of Cash Flow** in NPV and PBP. Depreciation is **non-cash**; it must be **added back** when computing CF.
> 2. **Forgetting Working Capital.** Many students compute PBP on $I_{TC}$ instead of $I_{FC}$ — the KTU standard is PBP on $I_{FC}$ unless the question states "total capital".
> 3. **Six-Tenths Rule exponent errors.** The exponent 0.6 is for general process equipment; for chromatography columns, the correct exponent is **0.7–0.8**. Mixing these up costs 1–2 marks.
> 4. **P/A factor miscalculation.** Always show both the numerator and denominator separately: $(1+i)^n - 1$ in the numerator, $i(1+i)^n$ in the denominator.
> 5. **NPV with salvage value.** The salvage $S_v$ is received at the **end of year $n$**, so it must be discounted by $(P/F, i, n)$, not added as a year-0 amount.
> 6. **IRR interpolation sign error.** NPV$_1$ and NPV$_2$ must have **opposite signs** for the interpolation formula to work. If both are positive, you must re-bracket at a higher rate.
> 7. **Missing decision statement.** Every profitability problem in KTU 2024 requires a final **Accept / Reject** line with justification (e.g., "NPV > 0, IRR > MARR → Accept"). A bare numerical answer loses 1 mark.
> 8. **Unit mismatch.** Always state currency (₹ or $) and time (years). A number without units is treated as ambiguous and penalized.

## 5.4 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

- [ ] **Profitability analysis** = quantitative comparison of net earnings to total capital invested in a bioseparation plant.
- [ ] **Total Capital Investment** $I_{TC} = I_{FC} + I_{WC}$. Fixed capital can be estimated via **Lang factor** $f_L \times C_{PE}$ or by **componentwise buildup** (installation, piping, instrumentation, structures, land, electrical, utilities).
- [ ] **Six-Tenths Rule** scales equipment cost with size: $C_B = C_A (S_B/S_A)^{0.6}$ — use **0.7–0.8 for chromatography columns**.
- [ ] **Total Product Cost** $C = C_M + G_E = (\text{RM} + \text{Util} + \text{Labour} + \text{Maint} + \text{Depr} + \text{Fixed Chgs}) + (\text{Admin} + \text{Distribution} + \text{Transport} + \text{R\&D})$.
- [ ] **Depreciation (SLM)** $C_{DP} = (I_{FC} - S_v)/n$.
- [ ] **Cash Flow** = NPAT + Depreciation (depreciation added back because it is non-cash).
- [ ] **ROI** = NPAT / $I_{TC}$ (in % per year).
- [ ] **Towell's ROI** = Cash Flow / $I_{TC}$ (in % per year).
- [ ] **Payback Period** = $I_{FC}$ / Annual Cash Flow (in years, undiscounted convention for KTU).
- [ ] **NPV** = $-I_{TC}$ + CF × (P/A, i, n) + $S_v$ × (P/F, i, n).
- [ ] **IRR** = discount rate at which NPV = 0; found by **trial-and-error interpolation**.
- [ ] **Decision Rules:** Accept if NPV > 0, IRR > MARR, ROI > MARR, PBP < acceptable life.
- [ ] **MARR (Minimum Acceptable Rate of Return)** is given in the problem — usually **8–15 %** for biopharma.
- [ ] **End-of-year cash flow convention** is the default; **beginning-of-year** uses annuity-due factors.
- [ ] For **long-life bioprocess plants** ($n \to \infty$), ROI $\approx$ IRR $\approx$ CF / $I_{TC}$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Case Studies in Bioseparations: Citric Acid, Insulin, and Therapeutic Monoclonal Antibody Production – Process Scheduling and Economic Evaluation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT525 - BIOSEPARATION TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Case Studies in Bioseparations: Citric Acid, Insulin, and Therapeutic Monoclonal Antibody Production – Process Scheduling and Economic Evaluation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Industrial Bioseparation Case Studies — The Big Picture

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** for *Bioseparation Technology* (PEBBT525), **Module 4** unifies three of the most commercially significant industrial case studies under a single analytical lens: a low-molecular-weight bulk product (**citric acid**), a recombinant therapeutic peptide (**insulin**), and a high-value complex biomolecule (**monoclonal antibodies / mAbs**). The unifying theme is how **downstream processing (DSP)** architecture, **process scheduling**, and **economic evaluation** co-design the final plant.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology):** A *bioseparation case study* is a comprehensive, plant-level evaluation of all unit operations from *cell disruption / clarification* to *final polishing*, coupled with a **time-resolved Gantt schedule** of batch/continuous operations and a **discounted cash flow (DCF) based economic model** that quantifies total capital and operating expenditures.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Kitchen to Restaurant" Analogy

Imagine three different "kitchens" producing three dishes of vastly different value:

| Dish | Kitchen Type | Cleanup Required | Customer |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Citric acid** (1 kg) | Bulk industrial canteen | Quick (precipitation + crystallization) | Food industry (low margin) |
| **Insulin** (mg) | Specialized boutique kitchen | Strict (chromatography + RP-HPLC) | Diabetic patients (regulated) |
| **mAb** (gram) | Pharmaceutical cleanroom | Extreme (Protein A + 2–3 polishing steps + viral clearance) | Cancer/autoimmune patients (high margin) |

Each kitchen's "cleaning crew" (downstream processing) is **sized, scheduled, and costed** differently — and that is the heart of this module.

---

## 1.2 The Three Case Studies at a Glance

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Module 4 Highlight:** All three case studies must be analyzed along the **same four-dimensional matrix**: *(i) Upstream feedstock → (ii) Recovery train → (iii) Purification cascade → (iv) Formulation/Polishing*.

### A. Citric Acid (Bulk, Low-Molecular-Weight)

- **Molecule:** 2-hydroxypropane-1,2,3-tricarboxylic acid; **M = 192.12 g/mol**.
- **Producer organism:** *Aspergillus niger* (aerobic submerged fermentation).
- **Titer (typical):** **100–200 g/L**.
- **Market price:** **≈ \$0.8–1.2 per kg** (commodity).
- **Key recovery step:** **Lime-sulfuric acid precipitation** (calcium citrate → citric acid).
- **Final purity:** USP/FCC grade ≥ 99.5%.

### B. Recombinant Human Insulin (Therapeutic Peptide)

- **Molecule:** 51-residue peptide (A-chain 21 aa + B-chain 30 aa, connected by 2 inter-chain + 1 intra-chain disulfide bond); **M ≈ 5808 Da**.
- **Producer organism:** *Escherichia coli* (inclusion bodies) **or** *Saccharomyces cerevisiae* (secreted).
- **Titer:** **1–5 g/L** (fed-batch).
- **Market price:** **≈ \$30–100 per gram** (API).
- **Key recovery step:** **Inclusion-body isolation → solubilization → refolding → enzymatic transpeptidation (trypsin/carboxypeptidase B)**.
- **Final purity:** ≥ 99.0% (HPLC) + bioassay.

### C. Therapeutic Monoclonal Antibodies (mAbs)

- **Molecule:** IgG-class immunoglobulin, **M ≈ 150 kDa**, Y-shaped, 2 H + 2 L chains, glycosylated.
- **Producer organism:** **Chinese Hamster Ovary (CHO) cells** (mammalian, fed-batch).
- **Titer:** **5–13 g/L** (current industrial standard).
- **Market price:** **≈ \$2,000–15,000 per gram** (high-value).
- **Key recovery step:** **Protein A affinity chromatography** (capture), followed by 2 polishing steps.
- **Final purity:** ≥ 99.5%, **< 10 pg/mL residual host-cell DNA**, **< 100 ppm aggregates**.

---

## 1.3 Process Scheduling and Economic Evaluation — The Two Integration Layers

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (Scheduling):** **Process scheduling** is the temporal mapping of all upstream and downstream unit operations onto a shared calendar to minimize the **critical-path cycle time** $\tau_c$, eliminate equipment idle time, and synchronize batch handoffs (via *campaign* or *overlap* strategies).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (Economic Evaluation):** **Economic evaluation** is the engineering quantification of plant profitability using **Capital Expenditure (CAPEX)**, **Operating Expenditure (OPEX)**, **Net Present Value (NPV)**, **Internal Rate of Return (IRR)**, and **Payback Period (PP)**, anchored to the *Cost of Goods (COG)* per unit of purified product.

**Intuition:** Scheduling asks *"how fast can we make it?"* — economics asks *"is it worth making it that fast?"* A faster schedule reduces batch time but usually raises CAPEX (parallel equipment, larger chromatography columns). The **optimal plant** is the Pareto front where cycle time × cost per gram is minimized.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Case Study A — Citric Acid Downstream Train

### A.1 Process Flow

**Step 1 — Fermentation Broth → Cell Removal:**
The broth from *A. niger* fermentation is filtered (rotary vacuum filter) or centrifuged. Mycelial cake is washed; **recovery of citric acid from the broth is ≈ 90–95%**.

**Step 2 — Lime Precipitation:**
Calcium hydroxide (lime) is added at **pH 7.0–8.5** and **60–70 °C** to precipitate **calcium citrate** (poorly soluble, $K_{sp} \approx 10^{-9}$):
$$
\text{Citric Acid (3 COOH)} \;+\; 1.5\,\text{Ca(OH)}_2 \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Ca}_{1.5}\text{Citrate}\,(s) \;+\; 3\,\text{H}_2\text{O}
$$

**Step 3 — Acidification (Sulfuric Acid):**
$$
\text{Ca}_{1.5}\text{Citrate} \;+\; 1.5\,\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Citric Acid} \;+\; 1.5\,\text{CaSO}_4\,(s) \downarrow
$$
Gypsum (CaSO₄·2H₂O) is removed by filtration.

**Step 4 — Ion-Exchange Decolorization:**
Cationic + anionic resins remove residual Ca²⁺, SO₄²⁻, and color bodies.

**Step 5 — Vacuum/Crystallization:**
Citric acid is concentrated to **≈ 70% w/w** and crystallized as the **monohydrate** (commercial form) below 36.6 °C, or as **anhydrous** above 36.6 °C.

**Step 6 — Drying & Packaging:**
Fluidized-bed or rotary dryer; final moisture < 0.1%.

### A.2 Why Lime-Sulfuric?

It is **cheap, scalable, and tolerant of impurities** — but produces **≈ 1.5 kg gypsum per kg citric acid**, a major solid-waste problem that drives the push toward *solvent extraction* and *electrodialysis* alternatives.

---

## 2.2 Case Study B — Recombinant Insulin Downstream Train

### B.1 E. coli Inclusion-Body Route (Humulin-style)

**Step 1 — Cell Disruption:**
High-pressure homogenization (≥ 800 bar) **or** chemical lysis. Inclusion bodies (IBs) are dense protein aggregates (0.5–1.5 μm).

**Step 2 — IB Isolation & Washing:**
Differential centrifugation. Washing with **0.5–1.0 M urea + Triton X-100** removes membrane proteins, nucleic acids, and LPS endotoxin.

**Step 3 — Solubilization:**
Strong denaturant: **6–8 M guanidine-HCl** or **8 M urea** + reducing agent (**DTT** or β-mercaptoethanol) at pH 8.5. Disulfide bonds are reduced.

**Step 4 — Refolding (Most Critical DSP Step):**
Dilution (1:10–1:100) into a refolding buffer containing:
- **Reduced/oxidized glutathione (GSH/GSSG) redox couple** (ratio ≈ 10:1 to 5:1)
- pH 8.0–10.5
- **L-arginine (0.5 M)** as a *molecular chaperone suppressor* to prevent aggregation.

**Step 5 — Separate A- and B-Chain Purification:**
The proinsulin analog is cleaved by **trypsin** (cleaves at Arg-Lys) and **carboxypeptidase B** (removes residual basic residues) to yield the mature 51-aa insulin.

**Step 6 — RP-HPLC Purification + Crystallization:**
Reversed-phase C18 chromatography, followed by **Zn²⁺-mediated crystallization** at pH 5.5–6.0 (insulin hexamer → crystal).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Yield Insight:** Refolding recovery in insulin is typically only **30–60%** because of off-pathway aggregation. This is the single largest determinant of overall DSP yield.

### B.2 Yeast Route (Saccharomyces cerevisiae — Novolin/Novo Nordisk)

Secretes a **single-chain insulin precursor (SCI)** to the periplasm. The periplasmic extract is purified by **cation-exchange + RP-HPLC** — no inclusion bodies, but a much lower titer and longer DSP.

---

## 2.3 Case Study C — Monoclonal Antibody (mAb) Downstream Train

This is the **most complex and economically significant** case study in the module.

### C.1 The mAb Platform Process (3-Column Template)

| Step | Column | Mode | Resin | Purpose | Typical Yield |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Capture** | Protein A | Affinity | MabSelect SuRe / Praesto | Bind Fc region, **> 95% purity** in one step | 85–95% |
| **Intermediate** | CEX (cation exchange) | Bind-Elute | SP Sepharose FF | Remove aggregates, HCP, leached Protein A | 80–90% |
| **Polishing** | AEX (anion exchange) | Flow-Through | Q Sepharose FF | Remove DNA, viruses, endotoxin | 90–98% |

### C.2 Viral Clearance — A Regulatory Mandate

Two orthogonal viral-inactivation/removal steps are **required by ICH Q5A**:
- **Low-pH inactivation** (pH 3.5–4.0, 30–60 min) immediately after Protein A elution.
- **Viral filtration** (20-nm or 35-nm asymmetric membrane, e.g., Planova) after intermediate purification.

### C.3 Why Protein A?

Protein A is a **bacterial cell-wall protein** from *Staphylococcus aureus* that binds the **Fc region of IgG** with $K_d \approx 10^{-7}$ M. It provides a **3-log clearance of impurities** in a single step, justifying its **high cost (≈ \$8,000–15,000 per liter of resin)**.

---

## 2.4 Process Scheduling — Gantt Charts and Cycle-Time Reduction

### 2.4.1 The Multiproduct Plant Problem

Modern plants produce **multiple mAbs in the same facility** (campaign scheduling). A typical **6-batch campaign** has:
- A **2–4 day changeover** between products (cleaning, sanitization, resin sanitization).
- An **8–14 day DSP** per batch.
- A **14–18 day upstream** fed-batch culture.

### 2.4.2 Critical-Path Cycle Time

The **minimum plant cycle time** $\tau_c$ is determined by the longest equipment occupancy:
$$
\tau_c \;=\; \max_{j}\left\{\,\sum_{i \in \text{path}(j)} t_{i}^{\text{occupancy}}\,\right\}
$$
where $\text{path}(j)$ is the critical path through equipment $j$.

### 2.4.3 Scheduling Strategies

1. **Sequential (no overlap):** $\tau_c$ = sum of all steps (worst).
2. **Pipeline / staggered:** Multiple batches are in different stages simultaneously, separated by a **dwell time** $t_d$.
3. **Continuous (e.g., periodic counter-current chromatography — PCC):** True steady-state, no batch boundary.

---

## 2.5 Economic Evaluation — The Engineer's Spreadsheet

### 2.5.1 CAPEX (Capital Expenditure)

Includes bioreactor, chromatography skids, UF/DF systems, buffer prep, building, QC labs. Estimated using **Lang's factor**:
$$
C_{\text{plant}} \;=\; F_{\text{Lang}} \cdot C_{\text{equipment}}
$$
with $F_{\text{Lang}} \approx 4.5$–$5.5$ for solid-processing biotech plants.

### 2.5.2 OPEX (Operating Expenditure)

Major components (mAb plant, typical split):
- **Resins & consumables (especially Protein A):** **≈ 25–35%**
- **Raw materials (media, buffers):** **≈ 20–30%**
- **Labor:** **≈ 15–25%**
- **Utilities, QC, waste:** **≈ 15–20%**

### 2.5.3 Cost of Goods (COG) per Gram

$$
\text{COG} \;=\; \frac{\text{Annual CAPEX amortization} \;+\; \text{Annual OPEX}}{\text{Annual kg of purified product}}
$$
For mAbs, typical COG = **\$50–200 per gram**; selling price = **\$2,000–15,000 per gram** → **gross margin ≈ 90–95%**.

### 2.5.4 Net Present Value (NPV)

$$
\text{NPV} \;=\; \sum_{t=0}^{N} \frac{R_t \;-\; C_t}{\,(1 + r)^{t}\,}
$$
where $R_t$ = revenue at year $t$, $C_t$ = costs, $r$ = discount rate.

### 2.5.5 Payback Period

$$
\text{PP} \;=\; \frac{\text{Total Initial Investment}}{\text{Annual Net Cash Inflow}}
$$

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master these formulas — they account for ≥ 70% of numerical problems in KTU 2024 ESE.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Symbols & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Stage yield | $Y_n = \prod_{i=1}^{n} y_i$ | $y_i$ = individual step recovery |
| 2 | Overall recovery | $R = \frac{m_{\text{pure out}}}{m_{\text{impure in}}}$ | dimensionless |
| 3 | Purification factor | $\text{PF} = \frac{(P/S)_{\text{out}}}{(P/S)_{\text{in}}}$ | $P$ = product purity, $S$ = impurity |
| 4 | Volumetric productivity | $P_v = \frac{m_{\text{product}}}{V_{\text{reactor}}\cdot t}$ | g·L⁻¹·h⁻¹ |
| 5 | Cycle time (critical path) | $\tau_c = \max_j \sum_{i} t_{ij}$ | h or d |
| 6 | Equipment occupancy | $t_{ij}^{\text{occ}} = t_{ij}^{\text{proc}} + t_{ij}^{\text{CIP/SIP}}$ | h |
| 7 | Lang's factor CAPEX | $C_{\text{plant}} = F_{\text{Lang}}\, C_{\text{eq}}$ | USD |
| 8 | COG per gram | $\text{COG} = \frac{A + O}{M_{\text{annual}}}$ | USD/g |
| 9 | NPV | $\text{NPV} = \sum_t \frac{R_t - C_t}{(1+r)^t}$ | USD |
| 10 | IRR | $\sum_t \frac{R_t - C_t}{(1 + \text{IRR})^t} = 0$ | dimensionless % |
| 11 | Payback period | $\text{PP} = \dfrac{C_0}{R - C}$ | years |
| 12 | Mass balance (single stage) | $V_{\text{in}} C_{\text{in}} = V_{\text{out}} C_{\text{out}} + m_{\text{loss}}$ | g, L, g/L |
| 13 | Protein-A dynamic capacity | $q_D \approx 35\text{–}50$ g IgG/L resin | g/L |
| 14 | Resin cycles (lifetime) | $N_{\text{cycles}} = \frac{C_{\text{resin}}}{\text{Cost per cycle}}$ | dimensionless |
| 15 | Breakeven price | $p_{\text{be}} = \text{COG} \cdot (1 + m)$ | $m$ = target margin |
| 16 | Citric-acid lime stoichiometry | $1.5\,\text{Ca(OH)}_2$ per mol citric acid | mol |
| 17 | Insulin refolding recovery | $R_{\text{refold}} \approx 0.30\text{–}0.60$ | dimensionless |
| 18 | mAb step recoveries | $0.85, 0.85, 0.95, 0.95$ (cap/inter/polish/viral) | dimensionless |
| 19 | Lang factor for fluids | $F_{\text{Lang}} \approx 3.5$ | dimensionless |
| 20 | Lang factor for solids | $F_{\text{Lang}} \approx 4.5$ | dimensionless |

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Plant design:** The COG equation forces engineers to *price every buffer, every column, every hour of CIP time* — a discipline that saved the insulin industry millions when continuous chromatography replaced batch Protein A in the 2010s.
- **Regulatory filings:** For mAb BLAs (Biologics License Applications), the FDA requires documented process economics as part of the **Process Performance Qualification (PPQ)** package.
- **Contract Manufacturing (CMO):** Economic evaluation enables **price-per-batch** quotations, typically \$2–8 million per mAb campaign.
- **Green engineering:** The **citric-acid gypsum waste** problem (≈ 1.5 kg/kg product) is a textbook case for circular-economy design — converting the waste to **plaster of Paris / agricultural gypsum** is now a standard environmental mitigation.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Citric Acid Overall Recovery (Lime Route)

**Problem (KTU-style, 7 marks):** A 100,000 L fermenter produces a broth containing **150 g/L citric acid**. The downstream train has the following stepwise yields:

| Step | Yield $y_i$ |
|---|---|
| Filtration | 0.95 |
| Lime precipitation | 0.92 |
| Acidification (H₂SO₄) | 0.97 |
| Ion exchange | 0.98 |
| Crystallization | 0.96 |
| Drying | 0.99 |

**Calculate:** (a) overall mass recovery, (b) kg of dry citric acid monohydrate produced per batch, and (c) overall process purity if the input broth purity is 85% and each purification step contributes to the listed yield (impurity removal is multiplicative on a log basis, with $10^{\Delta\log P}$ of contaminant removal per step).

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Compute the overall yield** (multiplicative cascade):
$$
R_{\text{overall}} \;=\; \prod_{i=1}^{6} y_i
$$

Substituting the values:
$$
R_{\text{overall}} \;=\; 0.95 \times 0.92 \times 0.97 \times 0.98 \times 0.96 \times 0.99
$$

Evaluate stepwise:
$$
0.95 \times 0.92 \;=\; 0.874
$$
$$
0.874 \times 0.97 \;=\; 0.84778
$$
$$
0.84778 \times 0.98 \;=\; 0.8308244
$$
$$
0.8308244 \times 0.96 \;=\; 0.7975914
$$
$$
0.7975914 \times 0.99 \;=\; 0.7896155
$$

$$
\boxed{R_{\text{overall}} \;\approx\; 0.7896 \;\text{ or }\; 78.96\%}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute mass of citric acid in the broth:**
$$
m_{\text{broth}} \;=\; 100{,}000\,\text{L} \;\times\; 150\,\text{g/L} \;=\; 1.5 \times 10^7\,\text{g} \;=\; 15{,}000\,\text{kg}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute mass of dry product:**
$$
m_{\text{product}} \;=\; m_{\text{broth}} \;\times\; R_{\text{overall}} \;=\; 15{,}000 \times 0.7896
$$

$$
m_{\text{product}} \;=\; 11{,}844\,\text{kg}
$$

$$
\boxed{m_{\text{product}} \;\approx\; 11{,}844\,\text{kg of dry citric acid per batch}}
$$

**Step 4 — Purity calculation (informational):** Each step is assumed to remove impurities multiplicatively on a log scale. With an initial log-purity of $\log_{10}(0.85/0.15) = 0.754$, and 6 purification steps each contributing $\approx 0.3$ log units of impurity removal, the final purity exceeds **99.9%** — well above USP grade.

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A 21% loss across 6 steps is *excellent* for a commodity biochemical — this is why the lime route, despite its waste, remains the global standard for **> 1.6 million tonnes/year** of citric acid production.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — mAb Process Cycle Time & Plant Throughput

**Problem (KTU-style, 7 marks):** A mAb plant has the following DSP unit-operation times (in hours):

| Step | $t_{\text{process}}$ | $t_{\text{CIP/SIP}}$ | Number of parallel units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Clarification (centrifuge) | 6 | 2 | 2 |
| Protein A capture | 8 | 4 | 3 |
| Low-pH viral inactivation | 2 | 1 | 2 |
| CEX (intermediate) | 6 | 3 | 2 |
| AEX (polishing) | 5 | 2 | 2 |
| Viral filtration | 4 | 2 | 1 |
| UF/DF + fill | 8 | 4 | 2 |

**Calculate:** (a) the critical-path cycle time $\tau_c$ for one batch, and (b) the maximum annual throughput (batches/yr) assuming 24/7 operation and a 30-day annual shutdown.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Effective occupancy per equipment type:**

The *cycle time contribution* per unit is the sum of process + CIP/SIP, *divided* by the number of parallel units (since the batch can use any one of them, but the batch only needs one):
$$
t_{j}^{\text{occ/batch}} \;=\; \frac{t_{j}^{\text{process}} + t_{j}^{\text{CIP/SIP}}}{n_{j}}
$$

Clarification:
$$
t_{\text{clarify}} \;=\; \frac{6 + 2}{2} \;=\; 4\,\text{h}
$$

Protein A capture:
$$
t_{\text{ProA}} \;=\; \frac{8 + 4}{3} \;=\; 4\,\text{h}
$$

Low-pH inactivation:
$$
t_{\text{LPV}} \;=\; \frac{2 + 1}{2} \;=\; 1.5\,\text{h}
$$

CEX:
$$
t_{\text{CEX}} \;=\; \frac{6 + 3}{2} \;=\; 4.5\,\text{h}
$$

AEX:
$$
t_{\text{AEX}} \;=\; \frac{5 + 2}{2} \;=\; 3.5\,\text{h}
$$

Viral filtration:
$$
t_{\text{VF}} \;=\; \frac{4 + 2}{1} \;=\; 6\,\text{h}
$$

UF/DF:
$$
t_{\text{UFDF}} \;=\; \frac{8 + 4}{2} \;=\; 6\,\text{h}
$$

**Step 2 — Identify the critical path (longest sum):**

DSP is a linear cascade — the critical path is the sum of *all* steps if parallelism is sequential within a batch. But because each step *follows* the previous, the per-batch DSP time is:
$$
\tau_c \;=\; t_{\text{clarify}} + t_{\text{ProA}} + t_{\text{LPV}} + t_{\text{CEX}} + t_{\text{AEX}} + t_{\text{VF}} + t_{\text{UFDF}}
$$

Substitute:
$$
\tau_c \;=\; 4 + 4 + 1.5 + 4.5 + 3.5 + 6 + 6
$$

$$
\boxed{\tau_c \;=\; 29.5\,\text{h per batch}}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute annual batches:**

Total operating hours:
$$
H_{\text{annual}} \;=\; (365 - 30)\,\text{d} \times 24\,\text{h/d} \;=\; 335 \times 24 \;=\; 8040\,\text{h}
$$

Annual batches:
$$
N_{\text{batches}} \;=\; \frac{H_{\text{annual}}}{\tau_c} \;=\; \frac{8040}{29.5} \;\approx\; 272.5
$$

$$
\boxed{N_{\text{batches}} \;\approx\; 272\,\text{batches per year}}
$$

**Step 4 — Annual mAb mass:**

With a 2,000 L bioreactor at **5 g/L** titer and overall yield of $0.85^4 \approx 0.522$:
$$
M_{\text{annual}} \;=\; 272 \times 2000 \times 5 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.522
$$

$$
M_{\text{annual}} \;=\; 272 \times 10 \times 0.522
$$

$$
M_{\text{annual}} \;\approx\; 1420\,\text{kg mAb per year}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industry benchmark:** This **1420 kg/yr** matches a typical **2,000-L scale commercial mAb plant** producing, for example, **trastuzumab** or **adalimumab**.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Economic Evaluation (NPV & Payback)

**Problem (KTU-style, 7 marks):** A company is investing **\$50 million** in a new insulin plant. Expected annual revenue is **\$18 million**, and annual operating cost is **\$10 million**. The plant has a useful life of **10 years** with a discount rate of **8%**. Compute (a) annual net cash flow, (b) NPV, and (c) payback period.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Annual net cash flow:**
$$
\text{NCF} \;=\; R - C \;=\; 18 - 10 \;=\; 8\,\text{million USD/yr}
$$

**Step 2 — NPV using the present-value annuity factor (PVAF):**

The PVAF for $n=10$, $r=0.08$ is:
$$
\text{PVAF} \;=\; \frac{1 - (1 + r)^{-n}}{r} \;=\; \frac{1 - (1.08)^{-10}}{0.08}
$$

Compute $(1.08)^{-10}$:
$$
(1.08)^{10} \;\approx\; 2.1589
$$
$$
(1.08)^{-10} \;\approx\; 0.4632
$$

Substitute:
$$
\text{PVAF} \;=\; \frac{1 - 0.4632}{0.08} \;=\; \frac{0.5368}{0.08} \;=\; 6.7101
$$

Now compute NPV:
$$
\text{NPV} \;=\; -C_0 + \text{NCF} \times \text{PVAF}
$$
$$
\text{NPV} \;=\; -50 + 8 \times 6.7101
$$
$$
\text{NPV} \;=\; -50 + 53.6808
$$
$$
\boxed{\text{NPV} \;\approx\; +\$3.68\,\text{million}}
$$

> **Decision rule:** Since $\text{NPV} > 0$, **the plant is economically viable**.

**Step 3 — Payback period (simple, undiscounted):**
$$
\text{PP} \;=\; \frac{C_0}{\text{NCF}} \;=\; \frac{50}{8} \;=\; 6.25\,\text{years}
$$

**Discounted payback** (when cumulative discounted NCF ≥ 50):

Cumulative discounted cash flow (DCF) table:
$$
\text{Year 1: } 8 / 1.08 = 7.407
$$
$$
\text{Year 2: } 7.407 + (8/1.1664) = 7.407 + 6.858 = 14.265
$$
$$
\text{Year 3: } 14.265 + (8/1.2597) = 14.265 + 6.350 = 20.615
$$
$$
\text{Year 4: } 20.615 + (8/1.3605) = 20.615 + 5.880 = 26.495
$$
$$
\text{Year 5: } 26.495 + (8/1.4693) = 26.495 + 5.445 = 31.940
$$
$$
\text{Year 6: } 31.940 + (8/1.5869) = 31.940 + 5.041 = 36.981
$$
$$
\text{Year 7: } 36.981 + (8/1.7138) = 36.981 + 4.668 = 41.649
$$
$$
\text{Year 8: } 41.649 + (8/1.8509) = 41.649 + 4.322 = 45.971
$$
$$
\text{Year 9: } 45.971 + (8/1.9990) = 45.971 + 4.002 = 49.973
$$
$$
\text{Year 10: } 49.973 + (8/2.1589) = 49.973 + 3.706 = 53.679
$$

Cumulative reaches 50 between **Year 9 and Year 10**:
$$
\boxed{\text{Discounted PP} \;\approx\; 9.0\text{ years (out of 10-year life)}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical insight:** The simple payback (6.25 yr) is *misleading* because it ignores the time value of money. The discounted payback (9.0 yr) shows that **most of the profit is back-loaded** — a real risk if a competitor launches a biosimilar at year 6.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Side-by-Side Comparison of the Three Case Studies

| Dimension | **Citric Acid** | **Insulin (E. coli)** | **mAb (CHO)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Molecule class** | Small organic acid | Recombinant peptide | Glycoprotein |
| **MW (Da)** | 192 | 5,808 | ~150,000 |
| **Host organism** | *A. niger* | *E. coli* | CHO (mammalian) |
| **Titer (g/L)** | 100–200 | 1–5 | 5–13 |
| **DSP complexity** | Low (3–4 steps) | Medium (5–7 steps) | High (7–10 steps) |
| **Critical recovery step** | Lime precipitation | Refolding | Protein A capture |
| **Overall yield** | 75–85% | 25–45% | 50–70% |
| **Cycle time (DSP)** | 24–48 h | 5–10 days | 8–14 days |
| **CAPEX (USD/g capacity)** | **≈ 1–3** | **≈ 50–150** | **≈ 500–1500** |
| **COG (USD/g)** | **≈ 0.5–1.0** | **≈ 20–60** | **≈ 50–200** |
| **Selling price (USD/g)** | **≈ 0.8–1.2** | **≈ 30–100** | **≈ 2000–15000** |
| **Gross margin** | 30–50% | 50–80% | 90–95% |
| **Regulatory burden** | Low (commodity) | High (drug) | Very high (biologic) |
| **Solid waste (kg/kg product)** | **1.5 (gypsum)** | 0.2–0.5 (cell debris) | 0.05–0.1 |
| **Typical plant capacity** | 10,000–100,000 t/yr | 500–5,000 kg/yr | 100–2,000 kg/yr |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pedagogical message for KTU students:** As you move **up the value chain** (commodity → peptide → mAb), CAPEX, cycle time, and regulatory burden rise *exponentially*, but so does the gross margin. **Process scheduling** and **economic evaluation** are the *only* tools that allow rational decisions across this entire spectrum.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 4]**
**List the four sequential steps involved in the lime-sulfuric acid recovery of citric acid from *Aspergillus niger* fermentation broth and state the chemical equation for the precipitation step.**

**Model Answer:**

The four sequential steps are:
1. **Filtration** of fermentation broth to remove mycelial cake.
2. **Lime precipitation** with Ca(OH)₂ at pH 7.5, 60 °C, forming insoluble calcium citrate.
3. **Sulfuric acid acidification** to liberate free citric acid and precipitate gypsum (CaSO₄·2H₂O).
4. **Ion-exchange decolorization** to remove residual color bodies, Ca²⁺, and SO₄²⁻.

Precipitation equation:
$$
3\,\text{CA} \;+\; 1.5\,\text{Ca(OH)}_2 \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Ca}_{1.5}\text{Citrate}\,(s) \;+\; 3\,\text{H}_2\text{O}
$$
where CA = citric acid. **(3 marks)**

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 4]**
**Why is Protein A affinity chromatography used as the capture step in nearly all industrial monoclonal antibody (mAb) processes, and what is its typical dynamic binding capacity?**

**Model Answer:**

Protein A, a cell-wall protein of *Staphylococcus aureus*, binds the **Fc region of IgG** with high affinity ($K_d \approx 10^{-7}$ M). Its advantages:
- **Single-step capture** of mAb from clarified CHO broth with **> 95% purity** in the eluate.
- **3-log clearance** of host-cell proteins (HCP), DNA, and media components.
- **Robust** across a wide range of feed conditions (pH 6–8, conductivity 5–15 mS/cm).

**Typical dynamic binding capacity** on modern resins (e.g., MabSelect SuRe LX): **35–50 g IgG per liter of resin**. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A — Citric Acid Process Economics **[14 Marks]**

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 4, Question 1(a)–(b)]**

**(a)** With a neat flow diagram, describe the **downstream processing of citric acid produced by *Aspergillus niger***. Explain why **lime precipitation followed by sulfuric acid** is the global industrial standard despite the **gypsum waste problem**. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A 50,000 L fermenter produces a broth at 120 g/L citric acid. The DSP train has the following yields: filtration 0.94, precipitation 0.91, acidification 0.96, ion exchange 0.97, crystallization 0.95, drying 0.98. Calculate (i) the **overall mass yield**, (ii) the **kg of dry citric acid monohydrate per batch**, and (iii) the **cost of goods (COG) per kg** if the annual CAPEX amortization is \$0.4 M, annual OPEX is \$1.6 M, and 200 batches are run per year. **[7 Marks]**

### Model Answer — Question A

**(a) [7 Marks]**

**Flow diagram** (describe as in **Section 4.1** above): Fermenter → Rotary vacuum filter → Lime precipitation tank → Acidification + gypsum filtration → Ion exchange columns (cationic + anionic) → Activated carbon → Vacuum evaporator → Crystallizer → Centrifuge → Fluidized-bed dryer → Packed product.

**Why lime-sulfuric is the global standard** (3 sub-points, 5 marks):
1. **Economy of scale:** Lime and sulfuric acid are **commodity chemicals**, orders of magnitude cheaper than chromatographic resins. The cost per kg of product is **\$0.05–0.10** for chemicals vs. **\$50–200** for chromatographic media — a **1000× advantage** for a low-margin product like citric acid.
2. **Process robustness:** The lime route tolerates variable broth composition, suspended solids, and color bodies without the precision required by chromatography.
3. **Gypsum mitigation:** The 1.5 kg/kg gypsum waste is now **valorized** — sold as **plaster of Paris, agricultural soil conditioner, or cement retarder** — turning a liability into a co-product revenue stream.

**Key limitation:** The route is **energy-intensive** (centrifuge, evaporator, dryer) and produces **large CO₂ and water footprints** per kg of product. (2 marks)

**(b) [7 Marks]**

**(i) Overall yield** (multiplicative cascade):
$$
R = 0.94 \times 0.91 \times 0.96 \times 0.97 \times 0.95 \times 0.98
$$

Step-by-step:
$$
0.94 \times 0.91 = 0.8554
$$
$$
0.8554 \times 0.96 = 0.8212
$$
$$
0.8212 \times 0.97 = 0.7966
$$
$$
0.7966 \times 0.95 = 0.7568
$$
$$
0.7568 \times 0.98 = 0.7416
$$

$$
\boxed{R \approx 0.7416 \;\text{ or }\; 74.16\%}
$$

**(ii) Mass of dry product:**
$$
m_{\text{broth}} = 50{,}000 \times 120 = 6.0 \times 10^6\,\text{g} = 6000\,\text{kg}
$$
$$
m_{\text{product}} = 6000 \times 0.7416 = 4449.6\,\text{kg}
$$
$$
\boxed{m_{\text{product}} \approx 4450\,\text{kg per batch}}
$$

**(iii) COG per kg:**

Annual cost:
$$
C_{\text{annual}} = 0.4 + 1.6 = 2.0\,\text{M USD/yr}
$$

Annual product:
$$
M_{\text{annual}} = 4450 \times 200 = 890{,}000\,\text{kg/yr}
$$

$$
\text{COG} = \frac{2.0 \times 10^6}{890{,}000} = 2.25\,\text{USD/kg}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{COG} \approx \$2.25\,\text{per kg}}
$$

This is **commercially realistic** for food-grade citric acid.

---

### QUESTION B — mAb Plant Scheduling & Economic Evaluation **[14 Marks]**

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 4, Question 2(a)–(b)] (Internal choice alternative)**

**(a)** With a **neat Gantt chart concept**, explain the **process scheduling strategy** for a multi-product mAb plant running **3 consecutive batches in a staggered (overlap) mode**. Show the effect of *dwell time* $t_d$ on equipment utilization and the critical-path cycle time. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A biotech company is investing **\$120 M** in a new mAb plant. The expected annual revenue is **\$60 M** and annual operating cost is **\$25 M**. The plant life is **12 years**, and the discount rate is **10%**. Calculate (i) the **annual net cash flow**, (ii) the **NPV**, and (iii) the **discounted payback period**. Comment on whether the project should proceed. **[7 Marks]**

### Model Answer — Question B

**(a) [7 Marks]**

**Strategy description:**

In a **staggered (overlap / pipeline) schedule**, consecutive batches are started at fixed time intervals (the **dwell time $t_d$**). While batch $n$ is undergoing DSP (e.g., Protein A capture), batch $n+1$ is at an earlier stage (e.g., clarification), and batch $n+2$ is still in the upstream bioreactor. This decouples the bottleneck and ensures that the **most expensive equipment (Protein A columns, bioreactors) is utilized near 100%** instead of sitting idle between batches.

**Effect of $t_d$:**

- If $t_d \to 0$: batches start simultaneously — DSP equipment is over-queued, upstream dominates the critical path.
- If $t_d = t_{\text{DSP}}$: each batch finishes just as the next enters the same equipment — perfect *single-train* pipelining.
- If $t_d > t_{\text{DSP}}$: equipment is **underutilized**; cost per gram rises.

**Critical-path cycle time per batch** $\tau_c$ is unchanged (set by the longest serial chain), but **throughput increases** because the next batch enters the line before the previous one leaves.

**Gantt chart** (see **Section 4.4** above): 3 batches spaced 3 days apart. (3 marks for diagram and 4 for explanation.)

**(b) [7 Marks]**

**(i) Annual net cash flow:**
$$
\text{NCF} = R - C = 60 - 25 = 35\,\text{M USD/yr}
$$

**(ii) NPV:** Compute the PVAF for $n = 12$, $r = 0.10$:
$$
\text{PVAF} = \frac{1 - (1.10)^{-12}}{0.10}
$$

Compute $(1.10)^{12}$:
$$
(1.10)^{12} \approx 3.1384
$$
$$
(1.10)^{-12} \approx 0.3186
$$
$$
\text{PVAF} = \frac{1 - 0.3186}{0.10} = \frac{0.6814}{0.10} = 6.8137
$$

NPV:
$$
\text{NPV} = -120 + 35 \times 6.8137
$$
$$
\text{NPV} = -120 + 238.48
$$
$$
\boxed{\text{NPV} \approx +\$118.5\,\text{M}}
$$

**(iii) Discounted payback:** cumulative discounted cash flow reaches 120 M.

Discount each year by dividing by $(1.10)^t$:
$$
\text{Year 1: } 35/1.10 = 31.82
$$
$$
\text{Year 2: } 35/1.21 = 28.93
$$
$$
\text{Year 3: } 35/1.331 = 26.30
$$
$$
\text{Year 4: } 35/1.4641 = 23.91
$$
$$
\text{Year 5: } 35/1.6105 = 21.73
$$
$$
\text{Year 6: } 35/1.7716 = 19.76
$$

Cumulative through Year 4: $31.82 + 28.93 + 26.30 + 23.91 = 110.96$.
Cumulative through Year 5: $110.96 + 21.73 = 132.69$.

The project recovers \$120 M between Years 4 and 5:
$$
\text{Year 5 needed} = 120 - 110.96 = 9.04
$$
$$
\text{Fraction of Year 5} = 9.04 / 21.73 = 0.416
$$
$$
\boxed{\text{Discounted PP} \approx 4.42 \text{ years (out of 12-yr life)}}
$$

**Comment (1 mark):** With $\text{NPV} = +\$118.5\,\text{M}$ and $\text{PP} \approx 4.4$ years, **the project is strongly recommended**. However, sensitivity analysis on Protein A resin cost (which dominates OPEX) and on selling price (biosimilar competition) should be performed before final commitment.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Read Carefully**
> 1. **Confusing PVAF with simple division:** Many students write $\text{NPV} = -120 + 35 \times 12 = +\$300$ M — **this loses 2–3 marks** because it ignores the time value of money.
> 2. **Adding yields instead of multiplying:** A common error is $0.95 + 0.94 + 0.96 = 2.85$ — **always multiply** for cascade yields.
> 3. **Forgetting CIP/SIP in cycle time:** Students often report *only* the process time, ignoring the cleaning time — **this understates $\tau_c$ by 30–50%**.
> 4. **Confusing mass of broth with mass of product:** Always apply the overall yield to compute final product mass.
> 5. **Forgetting Lang's factor:** CAPEX estimates without $F_{\text{Lang}}$ are *bare-equipment* costs, **not plant costs** — a frequent mistake that understates CAPEX by 4–5×.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save this section for last-minute exam revision — every bullet is high-yield for KTU 2024 ESE.**

- **Citric acid DSP:** Filter → Lime precipitation (Ca(OH)₂, pH 7.5) → H₂SO₄ acidification (gypsum removal) → Ion exchange → Crystallization (monohydrate < 36.6 °C). Overall yield ≈ 75–85%.
- **Citric-acid chemistry:** $1.5\,\text{Ca(OH)}_2$ per mol citric acid; produces **1.5 kg CaSO₄ per kg product** (waste problem).
- **Insulin DSP (E. coli IB route):** Disrupt → Wash IB → Solubilize (8 M urea + DTT) → Refold (GSH/GSSG + arginine) → Cleave (trypsin + CPB) → RP-HPLC → Zn crystallization.
- **Insulin refolding recovery = 30–60%** — the biggest yield-loss step.
- **mAb DSP platform (3 columns):** Protein A (capture, > 95% purity) → CEX (intermediate) → AEX (polish), with **low-pH viral inactivation** after Protein A and **viral filtration** after intermediate.
- **Protein A dynamic capacity:** **35–50 g IgG per L resin**; cost ≈ **\$8,000–15,000 per L** (drives mAb COG).
- **Yield cascade formula:** $R_{\text{overall}} = \prod_i y_i$ (always multiply).
- **Cycle time formula:** $\tau_c = \max_j \sum_i t_{ij}^{\text{occ}}$, where $t^{\text{occ}} = t^{\text{proc}} + t^{\text{CIP/SIP}}$.
- **Staggered scheduling** (dwell time $t_d$) maximizes utilization of Protein A columns.
- **CAPEX formula:** $C_{\text{plant}} = F_{\text{Lang}} \cdot C_{\text{eq}}$, with $F_{\text{Lang}} = 4.5$ for solids, $3.5$ for fluids, $5.5$ for biotech.
- **OPEX major components (mAb):** Resins 30%, raw materials 25%, labor 20%, utilities/QC 25%.
- **COG formula:** $\text{COG} = \dfrac{\text{CAPEX annual} + \text{OPEX annual}}{\text{kg of pure product per year}}$.
- **NPV formula:** $\text{NPV} = -C_0 + \text{NCF} \times \dfrac{1 - (1+r)^{-n}}{r}$.
- **PVAF** for $n = 10$, $r = 0.08$ = **6.7101**; for $n = 12$, $r = 0.10$ = **6.8137** (memorize for speed).
- **Decision rule:** $\text{NPV} > 0$ AND $\text{IRR} > r$ → **proceed**.
- **Payback period:** simple = $C_0 / \text{NCF}$; discounted = first year where cumulative discounted NCF ≥ $C_0$.
- **Comparative economics (must memorize for comparison questions):**
   - Citric acid: COG ≈ \$0.5–1.0/g, margin 30–50%
   - Insulin: COG ≈ \$20–60/g, margin 50–80%
   - mAb: COG ≈ \$50–200/g, margin 90–95%
- **Regulatory hook:** mAb processes need **two orthogonal viral clearance steps** (ICH Q5A).
- **Solid-waste hierarchy (citric acid):** gypsum → plasterboard / agricultural gypsum → landfill.
- **Process-intensity ranking** (kcal/kg product, energy footprint): citric acid < insulin < mAb.
- **Multiproduct plants** require *campaign scheduling* with **2–4 day changeovers** between products.
- **Green metrics to remember:** kg waste / kg product; m³ water / kg product; kWh / kg product.
- **KTU Board "favorite" long-answer topics:** (1) Lime route for citric acid + gypsum waste; (2) Protein A vs. mixed-mode resins; (3) mAb platform process flow + cycle time; (4) NPV + payback with PVAF table.
